CIDER.definition: An alcoholic drink made from fermented apple juice; in the US, unfermented apple juice.
CIDER.origin: Old French 'cidre', from Latin 'sicera', from Greek 'sikera', from Hebrew 'shekhar' meaning 'strong drink'
CIDER.evolution: A remarkably well-traveled word from Hebrew through Greek, Latin, and Old French; the US/UK sense split developed, with American 'cider' remaining non-alcoholic while British 'cider' fermented.
CIDER.joke: Cider is the honest drink — it advertises its ingredients right in the name.

CIGAR.definition: A cylinder of tobacco rolled in tobacco leaf for smoking.
CIGAR.origin: Spanish 'cigarro', possibly from Maya 'sikar' meaning 'to smoke rolled tobacco leaves'
CIGAR.evolution: From Spanish colonization of Mesoamerica; 'close but no cigar' (almost but not quite) comes from fairground prizes of cigars; Freud's famous disclaimer about cigars is the source of the 'sometimes a cigar' quote.
CIGAR.joke: Sometimes a cigar is just a cigar — and sometimes it's an occasion for an unasked-for lecture.

CINCH.definition: Something that is very easy; a girth for a saddle; to tighten or secure.
CINCH.evolution: From the saddle-girth sense through American cowboy culture; a cinched saddle is secure, so 'a cinch' for a certainty emerged in late 19th-century American slang.
CINCH.joke: It's a cinch — said everyone about something that turned out to be extremely not a cinch.

CIRCA.definition: Approximately; used before a date or figure to indicate it is approximate.
CIRCA.evolution: A Latin preposition used directly in English scholarly writing; commonly abbreviated to 'c.' or 'ca.' in historical contexts; rare in casual speech but essential in academic dating.
CIRCA.joke: Circa is the academic world's 'ish' — with better credentialing.

CIVIC.definition: Of or relating to a city or town, or the duties and rights of citizens.
CIVIC.evolution: From the Latin root that also gives 'civil', 'civilization', and 'city'; civic duty and civic pride are standard compounds emphasizing community responsibility.
CIVIC.joke: Civic pride is highest in the week before elections and the week after the road gets repaved.

CIVIL.definition: Of or relating to ordinary citizens; courteous and polite; not religious or military.
CIVIL.origin: Latin 'civilis' meaning 'of citizens, of public life'
CIVIL.evolution: From the same Latin root as 'civic'; 'civil war' (citizens fighting citizens) and 'civil engineering' (non-military public works) show its range; 'keep it civil' for polite discourse is a modern common use.
CIVIL.joke: Being civil costs nothing — which is exactly why it's in such short supply.

CLACK.definition: To make a sharp sound as of hard objects striking together; chatter or prattle noisily.
CLACK.origin: Imitative of the sound itself, related to 'clap' and 'click'
CLACK.evolution: An onomatopoeic word capturing the sound of hard surfaces meeting; the gabbling-speech sense extends the repeated-clicking idea to rapid, empty talk.
CLACK.joke: Her keyboard clacked — the sound of productivity, or the sound of vigorous online arguing, indistinguishable at this distance.

CLAIM.definition: An assertion that something is true; a demand for something as one's right; a piece of land staked out.
CLAIM.origin: Old French 'clamer' meaning 'to call out, claim', from Latin 'clamare' meaning 'to shout'
CLAIM.evolution: From the Latin shout to the legal demand; mining claims (staking land) developed in the gold rush era; 'claim to fame' is a relatively recent idiom for one's notable achievement.
CLAIM.joke: A claim without evidence is just a sentence with ambitions.

CLAMP.definition: A device used to hold things tightly together; to hold or grip firmly.
CLAMP.origin: Middle Dutch or Middle Low German 'klampe' meaning 'clamp, cramp'
CLAMP.evolution: From Low Dutch/German workshop terminology; the wheel clamp (boot) used to immobilize illegally parked cars is a 20th-century application of the same gripping concept.
CLAMP.joke: A clamp is just a very committed hug for two pieces of wood.

CLANG.definition: To make or cause to make a loud, resonant metallic sound.
CLANG.origin: Latin 'clangere' meaning 'to resound', imitative of the sound
CLANG.evolution: From Latin through its sound-capturing quality; onomatopoeic in origin; 'clanger' in British English means a blunder — like the clanging sound of a dropped instrument.
CLANG.joke: A clang is the sound of someone's plan meeting reality at high velocity.

CLANK.definition: To make a heavy metallic sound; such a sound.
CLANK.evolution: Slightly heavier and more irregular than 'clang'; 'clanking chains' is the quintessential ghost-story sound, so established it's become its own cliché.
CLANK.joke: Chains clank so you know the ghost is coming — ghosts are very considerate about the jump scare.

CLASH.definition: To come into conflict; to make a loud, harsh noise when meeting; (of colors) to look unpleasant together.
CLASH.evolution: The metallic-collision sound is original; the conflict sense followed naturally; the color 'clash' (pink and orange together) is a 20th-century fashion usage.
CLASH.joke: A clash of egos is just two monologues pretending to be a debate.

CLASP.definition: A device for fastening things together; to grasp tightly.
CLASP.origin: Middle English; origin uncertain, possibly related to 'clap' or Old English 'clyppan' meaning 'to embrace'
CLASP.evolution: Both the fastener and the grip sense have been in use since Middle English; 'a clasp of the hand' for a handshake is an older, more formal expression.
CLASP.joke: A clasp keeps things together — everything else is just hoping for the best.

CLASS.definition: A set of things sharing common characteristics; a social stratum; a lesson period; elegance.
CLASS.origin: Latin 'classis' meaning 'a class or division of the Roman people, fleet'
CLASS.evolution: From the Roman census divisions; educational class sense from 17th century; the elegant 'class act' and 'classy' as compliments are 20th-century American developments.
CLASS.joke: Class is what you have when you stop needing everyone to notice that you have it.

CLEAN.definition: Free from dirt or contamination; morally pure; complete or thorough.
CLEAN.evolution: A fundamental Old English adjective; the moral purity sense has been present since early Christian English; 'clean break', 'come clean', and 'clean slate' show its thoroughness connotation.
CLEAN.joke: A clean desk is the sign of an empty mind — or someone who knew company was coming.

CLEAR.definition: Easy to perceive or understand; free from obstruction or impurity; to remove obstructions.
CLEAR.origin: Old French 'cler', from Latin 'clarus' meaning 'clear, bright, renowned'
CLEAR.evolution: From Latin brightness through French; 'all clear' (signal of safety) is WWI military usage; 'clear the air' for resolving tension is a 19th-century idiom.
CLEAR.joke: Crystal clear is what every manager says and nobody achieves.

CLEAT.definition: A T-shaped or similar piece of metal or wood for fastening ropes or providing grip; a projection on the sole of a shoe.
CLEAT.origin: Middle English 'cleat', from Old English 'clēot' meaning 'wedge'
CLEAT.evolution: The Old English wedge became the nautical rope-fastening device; then the sports-shoe projection for grip on grass; all preserve the gripping/holding function.
CLEAT.joke: Cleats give athletes confidence that the ground is on their side — briefly.

CLEFT.definition: A split or division in something; a space or hollow made by splitting.
CLEFT.origin: Old English 'geclyft' meaning 'fissure', from 'clēofan' meaning 'to split'
CLEFT.evolution: From the Old English splitting verb; 'cleft palate' is the medical term for a congenital gap; 'cleft stick' (a forked dilemma) is an old idiom for being caught in two bad options.
CLEFT.joke: A cleft chin is the face's way of creating drama without committing to it.

CLERK.definition: An administrative employee dealing with records and correspondence; a sales assistant (American English).
CLERK.origin: Old English 'cleric', from Ecclesiastical Latin 'clericus' meaning 'a Christian minister'
CLERK.evolution: Medieval clerks were churchmen who could read and write; literacy was rare, so 'clerk' came to mean any administrative literate person; 'clerk' and 'cleric' are doublets.
CLERK.joke: A clerk files everything in a system only they understand — and considers that job security.

CLICK.definition: To make a short, sharp sound; to press a button on a computer mouse; to suddenly understand something.
CLICK.evolution: Onomatopoeic; the mouse-clicking sense is purely 20th-century; 'it clicked' for sudden understanding uses the idea of a mechanism engaging; 'click with someone' for rapport is informal 20th century.
CLICK.joke: Click here to proceed — and also to confirm you haven't actually read any of this.

CLIFF.definition: A steep rock face, typically at the edge of the sea.
CLIFF.evolution: A fundamental English geographical term; 'cliffhanger' (a suspenseful story stopping at a crisis point) comes from the serial melodramas of the 1870s where characters literally hung from cliffs.
CLIFF.joke: A cliffhanger ends where you least expect it — which is always exactly where you expected it.

CLIMB.definition: To go up or ascend, especially using the hands and feet; to increase gradually.
CLIMB.evolution: Stable Old English verb; 'social climber' for an ambitious person seeking higher status is a 19th-century metaphor; the financial/economic 'climb' follows the same upward trajectory.
CLIMB.joke: Climb every mountain — but maybe bring a map, because enthusiasm has limits.

CLING.definition: To hold on tightly to something; to resist change or separation.
CLING.evolution: From the Old English adhesion sense; 'clingfilm' (plastic wrap) is a 20th-century British term; 'clingy' as a personality descriptor for excessive attachment is informal 20th century.
CLING.joke: Clingfilm is the only material that sticks to itself but nothing you actually want it to stick to.

CLINK.definition: To make a light, sharp ringing sound; prison (slang).
CLINK.evolution: Onomatopoeic from Dutch; 'in the clink' for prison probably comes from a famous London prison called Clink; the glasses-touching toast sense is from the sound made.
CLINK.joke: Clink your glasses to celebrate — and clink your bars to reconsider.

CLOAK.definition: A sleeveless outer garment hanging loose from the shoulders; to disguise or conceal.
CLOAK.origin: Medieval Latin 'clocca' meaning 'bell' — from the bell-shaped garment
CLOAK.evolution: From the bell shape of a draped garment; the same Latin root gives 'clock' (bell that rings hours); 'cloak and dagger' for espionage comes from the romantic image of cloaked figures.
CLOAK.joke: A cloak of invisibility would be more useful if people could see when you're wearing it.

CLOCK.definition: An instrument for measuring time; to measure the speed of; to hit (informal).
CLOCK.origin: Medieval Latin 'clocca' meaning 'bell' — early clocks used bells to mark hours
CLOCK.evolution: From the bell that marks hours; 'clock' and 'cloak' are doublets from the same bell-shape root; 'beating the clock' (finishing before time expires) and 'around the clock' are 19th–20th century.
CLOCK.joke: The clock's whole job is to remind you that you're running late — and it's never wrong about it.

CLONE.definition: An organism genetically identical to another; a person or thing identical to another; to make an exact copy.
CLONE.origin: Greek 'klon' meaning 'twig, slip' — from horticultural grafting
CLONE.evolution: The botanical grafting sense came first; the genetic copying sense emerged with 20th-century biology; 'clone' for a software product copied from another is 1970s tech slang.
CLONE.joke: A clone is a great idea until you have to argue with yourself and lose.

CLOSE.definition: Near in space or time; having a strong relationship; stuffy and lacking fresh air.
CLOSE.origin: Old French 'clos', from Latin 'clausus' meaning 'shut, closed'
CLOSE.evolution: From the Latin for enclosed/shut; 'close call' (a narrow escape) and 'close-knit' (tightly bonded) use different aspects of nearness; the British 'close' for a cul-de-sac preserves the enclosed space.
CLOSE.joke: Close only counts in horseshoes, hand grenades, and 'I almost finished the report.'

CLOTH.definition: Woven or felted fabric; a piece of fabric for a specific purpose.
CLOTH.evolution: A core Old English word; 'the cloth' as a collective noun for the clergy preserves the sense of clerical garments; 'cut from the same cloth' means sharing the same nature.
CLOTH.joke: Cut from the same cloth — which explains why everyone at the family reunion is wearing vaguely similar opinions.

CLOUD.definition: A visible mass of water droplets in the sky; a state of gloom; computing infrastructure for remote data storage.
CLOUD.origin: Old English 'clud' meaning 'mass of rock' — clouds looked like rock masses floating
CLOUD.evolution: The Old English rock-mass metaphor became sky-water by 13th century; 'cloud nine' (extreme happiness) comes from the US Weather Bureau's cloud classification; cloud computing is a 21st-century metaphor.
CLOUD.joke: Every cloud has a silver lining — and every silver lining has a terms of service agreement.

CLOUT.definition: A heavy blow; influence or power, especially in politics.
CLOUT.origin: Old English 'clut' meaning 'piece of cloth or metal patch'
CLOUT.evolution: The cloth/metal patch sense is original; the heavy blow sense comes from hitting with something flat; 'pull clout' and 'wield clout' for influence come from the force metaphor; 'clout' on social media means influence.
CLOUT.joke: Clout is just what everyone pretends not to care about while actively accumulating it.

CLOVE.definition: The dried flower bud of a tropical tree, used as a spice; a single segment of a garlic bulb.
CLOVE.origin: Old French 'clou de girofle' meaning 'nail of clove tree' — from its nail-like shape
CLOVE.evolution: Named for the nail shape of the dried bud; the spice trade made cloves immensely valuable — they motivated much of the early European colonization of Southeast Asia.
CLOVE.joke: Cloves are the spice that say 'I was expensive enough to start a war over' while improving your mulled wine.

CLOWN.definition: A comic performer wearing bright clothes and makeup; a foolish person.
CLOWN.origin: Origin uncertain; possibly Low German 'cluns' or Frisian 'klönne' meaning 'clumsy fellow'
CLOWN.evolution: Originally meant a rustic or boor; the theatrical performer sense emerged in the 16th century; the modern painted-face circus clown is a Victorian development; 'clown' as an insult for a fool is relatively recent.
CLOWN.joke: A clown's real superpower is that everyone's uncomfortable but nobody wants to be the first to say so.

CLUCK.definition: To make the characteristic sound of a hen; to express concern fussily.
CLUCK.origin: Imitative of the sound a hen makes
CLUCK.evolution: Pure onomatopoeia; 'clucking about' for fussy over-concern uses the mother-hen behavior as metaphor; the dismissive 'dumb cluck' for a stupid person is 20th-century American slang.
CLUCK.joke: Hens cluck to communicate — and if you listen long enough, it sounds a lot like a committee meeting.

CLUED.definition: Informed; aware of the facts; 'clued in' or 'clued up' (British).
CLUED.origin: From 'clue' (a ball of yarn, then a guiding piece of information) plus past participle suffix
CLUED.evolution: 'Clue' comes from Old English 'cliewen' (ball of yarn), which is how Theseus found his way out of the labyrinth; 'clued up/in' for well-informed is mainly British informal usage.
CLUED.joke: Being clued in is great until you realize everyone else got a different memo.

CLUMP.definition: A compact mass or group; to walk heavily; to group things together.
CLUMP.origin: Middle Low German 'klumpe' meaning 'cluster of trees'
CLUMP.evolution: From the Dutch/German cluster sense; the heavy-walking sense is later, imitating the sound of clumping feet; 'clumpy' shoes (British) preserve both the mass and awkward sense.
CLUMP.joke: A clump of hair in the drain is just your body's way of itemizing its resignation letter.

CLUNG.definition: Past tense of 'cling'; held on tightly.
CLUNG.evolution: Irregular past tense of 'cling'; 'clung to hope' and 'clung to the wreckage' are both literal and metaphorical uses that have been common for centuries.
CLUNG.joke: She clung to her opinion like a barnacle to a ship's hull — impervious to scraping.

COACH.definition: A horse-drawn carriage; a sports trainer or instructor; a railway carriage; to train intensively.
COACH.origin: Hungarian 'kocsi' from 'Kocs', a Hungarian village known for its carriages
COACH.evolution: One of the few English words to come directly from Hungarian; a 'coach' in education (one who carries the student toward their goal) is a natural metaphor from the carriage sense.
COACH.joke: A coach knows exactly how to win — just not why they're not doing it themselves.

COAST.definition: The land near the sea; to move without effort, using momentum already gained.
COAST.origin: Old French 'coste', from Latin 'costa' meaning 'rib, side'
COAST.evolution: From the 'side/edge' of the land meeting the sea; 'coast is clear' (no danger visible) is a 16th-century nautical phrase; 'coasting' for effortless success borrows the downhill rolling sense.
COAST.joke: Coasting through life is fine until gravity reminds you there's always a bottom.

COBRA.definition: A large venomous snake native to Africa and Asia, able to spread its neck ribs to form a hood.
COBRA.origin: Portuguese 'cobra de capelo' meaning 'hooded snake', from Latin 'colubra' meaning 'snake'
COBRA.evolution: Shortened from the Portuguese descriptive phrase; became the English name for the hooded venomous snake; the Cobra sports car took its name from the snake's danger and speed association.
COBRA.joke: The cobra wears its own hood — nature's way of saying 'I dressed for the occasion.'

COCOA.definition: A powder made from roasted cacao seeds, used to make chocolate and cocoa drinks.
COCOA.origin: Alteration of 'cacao', influenced by Spanish 'coco' meaning 'skull' (from the cacao pod's shape)
COCOA.evolution: A corruption of 'cacao' that became standard; the hot drink sense became domestic and comforting while 'cacao' remained the botanical term; both words come from the same Nahuatl source.
COCOA.joke: Cocoa is the grown-up version of hot chocolate — same thing, more dignity in the name.

COLON.definition: A punctuation mark (:) used before a list or explanation; the large intestine.
COLON.origin: Latin 'colon', from Greek 'kolon' meaning 'limb, member, clause'
COLON.evolution: Both the grammatical and anatomical senses come from Greek for a 'member' or segment; the punctuation 'colon' marks a segment division in writing; the intestinal colon is a segment of the gut.
COLON.joke: A colon promises what follows is important: and then everything after it has to live up to that.

COLOR.definition: The property of objects reflecting light of particular wavelengths; a substance that changes color; liveliness or vividness.
COLOR.evolution: From Latin; the figurative 'local color' (vivid detail) and 'true colors' (real character) are 18th-century developments; 'to color' as to influence slightly is an older metaphorical use.
COLOR.joke: Seeing the world in color is a gift — arguing about which color it is, apparently mandatory.

COMET.definition: A celestial body with a bright tail of gas and dust, orbiting the sun in an elongated ellipse.
COMET.origin: Latin 'cometa', from Greek 'komētēs' meaning 'long-haired star', from 'komē' meaning 'hair'
COMET.evolution: The Greek 'long-haired' description refers to the comet's tail; ancient cultures interpreted comets as omens; Halley's comet is the most famous, named for Edmund Halley who calculated its orbit in 1705.
COMET.joke: A comet is the universe's reminder that everything is passing through — some things just take longer.

COMFY.definition: Comfortable (informal); giving or enjoying ease and relaxation.
COMFY.evolution: A colloquial shortening widely used in informal speech; 'comfy' carries a warmer, cozier connotation than 'comfortable', suggesting domestic ease rather than mere absence of discomfort.
COMFY.joke: Comfy is the state you achieve five minutes before you have to get up.

COMIC.definition: Causing laughter; relating to comedy; a comedian; a comic book.
COMIC.origin: Latin 'comicus', from Greek 'komikos', from 'komos' meaning 'revel, celebration'
COMIC.evolution: From the Greek festive celebration through the theatrical tradition of comedy; 'comic strip' and 'comic book' are 20th-century compounds; 'comic relief' is a literary technique name.
COMIC.joke: Comic timing is the art of being right — just slightly too late.

COMMA.definition: A punctuation mark (,) indicating a pause between parts of a sentence.
COMMA.origin: Latin 'comma', from Greek 'komma' meaning 'piece cut off', from 'koptein' meaning 'to cut'
COMMA.evolution: From the Greek cutting sense — a comma was originally a short clause cut from a sentence; the punctuation sense is from Aldus Manutius's 15th-century printing conventions; 'comma splice' is a modern grammar error name.
COMMA.joke: The Oxford comma, is a matter, of life, and death — or so its defenders insist.

CONCH.definition: A large spiral sea shell; the shellfish living in such a shell; its shell used as a horn.
CONCH.evolution: From Greek through Latin; the shell's use as a horn is ancient and appears in mythology (Triton's conch); 'Lord of the Flies' gave the conch symbolic weight as an emblem of order and democracy.
CONCH.joke: Whoever finds the conch gets to speak — which beats most meeting policies I've experienced.

CONDO.definition: A condominium; an apartment or housing unit owned by its occupant within a larger building.
CONDO.evolution: The legal form 'condominium' (joint ownership) dates to the 18th century in diplomacy (jointly controlled territory); the residential sense emerged in American law in the 1960s; 'condo' is the informal shortening.
CONDO.joke: A condo is a home where you own everything from the drywall inward and argue about everything else at quarterly meetings.

CONIC.definition: Relating to or resembling a cone; in mathematics, relating to conic sections (parabolas, ellipses, hyperbolas).
CONIC.origin: From Latin 'conicus', from Greek 'konikos', from 'konos' meaning 'cone'
CONIC.evolution: A technical mathematical term since the ancient Greek geometers; conic sections were studied by Apollonius of Perga around 200 BCE and remain fundamental to mathematics, optics, and astronomy.
CONIC.joke: A conic section walks into a bar — the parabola exits gracefully, the hyperbola never decides.

COPSE.origin: Old French 'copeiz' meaning 'cut timber', from 'couper' meaning 'to cut'
COPSE.evolution: From the practice of coppicing — cutting trees to the stump to encourage dense regrowth; a copse was originally managed woodland; the word now refers to any small thicket.
COPSE.joke: A copse is where the path turns ambiguous and the map starts lying to you.

CORAL.definition: A hard material formed from the skeletons of marine polyps; a reddish-pink color.
CORAL.origin: Old French 'coral', from Latin 'corallium', from Greek 'korallion'
CORAL.evolution: From Greek/Latin through French; coral reefs were recognized as animal structures only in the 18th century; the color 'coral' (warm pinkish-red) derives from the characteristic hue of Mediterranean red coral.
CORAL.joke: Coral is the ocean's apartment complex — built over millennia, currently declining in value.

CORER.definition: A tool for removing the core from fruit, especially apples.
CORER.origin: From 'core' (Old French 'cor', from Latin 'cor' meaning 'heart') plus '-er'
CORER.evolution: A direct derivation for the tool that removes the central part; 'core' for the essential center of something (apple core, core values) all share the heart metaphor.
CORER.joke: A corer is a tool with one job — and the apple never consents.

CORNY.definition: Trite or banal; using obvious or overused sentimental elements; extremely old-fashioned.
CORNY.origin: From 'corn' in American slang — rural, unsophisticated (from corn country)
CORNY.evolution: American slang from the idea of rural corn-country simplicity; 'corny' became the standard term for jokes or sentiments that are too obvious or clichéd; originally more critical, now often affectionate.
CORNY.joke: Corny jokes are the comedy equivalent of comfort food — you're embarrassed to admit how much you needed that.

COUCH.definition: A comfortable piece of upholstered furniture for lying on; to express in a particular way.
COUCH.origin: Old French 'couche' meaning 'a bed, layer', from 'coucher' meaning 'to lie down'
COUCH.evolution: From the lying-down sense; the psychiatrist's couch became iconic in 20th-century culture; 'couch potato' (idle TV watcher) is a 1980s American coinage; 'couched in diplomatic terms' uses the verbal sense.
COUCH.joke: The couch knows all your secrets — every snack, every nap, every spirited debate with a television.

COUGH.definition: To expel air from the lungs with a sudden sharp sound; such a sound.
COUGH.origin: Middle English 'coughen', related to Dutch 'kuchen', imitative of the sound
COUGH.evolution: Onomatopoeic in origin; 'cough up' (to produce money unwillingly) is an early 20th-century idiom using the reluctant-expulsion sense; 'cough medicine' and 'coughing fit' are standard compounds.
COUGH.joke: A polite cough is society's most deniable 'please stop talking.'

COULD.definition: Past tense of 'can'; expressing possibility, ability, or permission in past or conditional contexts.
COULD.origin: Old English 'cuthe' (past tense of 'cunnan' meaning 'to know, be able')
COULD.evolution: The silent 'l' was added by analogy with 'would' and 'should' in the 15th–16th centuries; 'could have' versus 'could of' is one of the most common written English errors.
COULD.joke: 'Could' is English's way of saying 'theoretically yes, but let's not get ahead of ourselves.'

COUNT.definition: To determine the total number of; to consider important; a nobleman of continental European rank.
COUNT.origin: Old French 'conter' meaning 'to count, reckon', from Latin 'computare'
COUNT.evolution: From the Latin computation through French; the nobleman 'count' (Latin 'comes' — a companion to the king) is an unrelated word that merged in spelling; 'Count Dracula' exploits the title's sinister sound.
COUNT.joke: Every vote counts — which is why so many people are surprised when they actually count them.

COUPE.definition: A car with a fixed roof and two doors; a type of iced dessert served in a glass.
COUPE.origin: French 'coupé' meaning 'cut', past participle of 'couper' meaning 'to cut'
COUPE.evolution: The carriage 'coupé' was a shorter version of a larger coach, 'cut' at the back; the automobile sense follows this truncated shape; the dessert 'coupe' is named for the glass bowl.
COUPE.joke: A coupe is a sports car for people who want to look adventurous but still have somewhere to put their umbrella.

COURT.definition: A tribunal of judges; the residence and retinue of a sovereign; an enclosed area for sport; to seek someone's favor or affection.
COURT.origin: Old French 'cort', from Latin 'cohors' meaning 'enclosure, yard, retinue'
COURT.evolution: From the enclosed yard; the royal court (enclosed residence), the legal court, and the sports court all share this enclosed-space origin; 'courting' (wooing) comes from seeking to impress at the royal court.
COURT.joke: Courting disaster is the only kind of courtship with a guaranteed outcome.

COVEN.definition: A group or gathering of witches.
COVEN.origin: Middle English, related to 'convent' from Latin 'conventus' meaning 'assembly'
COVEN.evolution: From the same root as 'convent'; originally any group meeting; became specifically associated with witches in the context of witch trials; the traditional thirteen-member coven is a 20th-century folk belief.
COVEN.joke: A coven is just a book club with better special effects.

COVER.definition: To place something over or in front of; to deal with a topic or report on it; an outer layer or binding.
COVER.origin: Old French 'covrir', from Latin 'cooperire' meaning 'to cover completely'
COVER.evolution: From the Latin comprehensive covering; 'cover story' (a magazine cover) and 'cover story' (a false alibi) are coincidentally parallel; 'undercover' and 'cover your tracks' show the deception extension.
COVER.joke: Cover your bases and your bases will cover you — eventually, reluctantly, at the last possible moment.

COVET.definition: To yearn to possess something belonging to someone else.
COVET.evolution: From the Latin desire (related to 'Cupid' and 'cupidity'); the biblical commandment 'thou shalt not covet' made the word strongly moral; 'covetous' carries a moral charge that 'desire' lacks.
COVET.joke: Covet thy neighbor's lawn — and then watch their HOA letters pile up.

COVEY.definition: A small group of birds, especially partridges; a group of people.
COVEY.origin: Old French 'covee' meaning 'a brood', from 'cover' meaning 'to sit on eggs'
COVEY.evolution: From the brooding/hatching sense — a covey was originally a batch of hatched birds; extended to small groups of people in informal use.
COVEY.joke: A covey of partridges is the collective noun nature invented specifically to win at Scrabble.

COWER.definition: To crouch down or shrink back in fear.
COWER.origin: Middle Low German 'kuren' meaning 'to lie in wait'
COWER.evolution: From the crouching/waiting sense in Low German; the fearful shrinking sense dominated in English; 'cowering' is strongly associated with being intimidated into a smaller physical presence.
COWER.joke: Cower is the instinct that survived — your ancestors cowered very well, which is why you're here.

COYLY.definition: In a reluctant or shy manner; with a pretended modesty or shyness.
COYLY.origin: From 'coy' (Old French 'coi', from Latin 'quietus' meaning 'quiet, still') plus '-ly'
COYLY.evolution: 'Coy' originally meant quiet or still; shifted to the affected shyness sense; 'coyly' carries the sense of deliberate self-presentation through apparent diffidence.
COYLY.joke: She smiled coyly — which is the smile that knows exactly what it's doing.

CRACK.definition: A narrow break or opening; a sharp explosive sound; an attempt; first-rate or excellent (adjective).
CRACK.evolution: The sound sense is original; cracks in material things followed; 'crack of dawn' (the first light sound of day breaking), 'crack shot', and 'crack cocaine' all extend the original sharpness.
CRACK.joke: A crack in the plan is just the plan letting the light in — or so the optimists say.

CRAFT.definition: Skill in making things by hand; cunning or guile; a boat, aircraft, or spacecraft.
CRAFT.origin: Old English 'cræft' meaning 'power, physical strength, skill'
CRAFT.evolution: The Old English strength-and-skill sense; 'crafty' came to mean cunningly skillful, then just cunning; the vessel sense ('watercraft', 'aircraft') is a late extension of the skill-in-making metaphor.
CRAFT.joke: Craft beer is what happens when someone's hobby gets a business license and a loyalty program.

CRAMP.definition: A painful involuntary contraction of a muscle; a tool for clamping things; to restrict movement.
CRAMP.origin: Middle Dutch or Middle Low German 'crampe' meaning 'hook, cramp iron'
CRAMP.evolution: From the hooked/gripping tool through the painful muscle grip; 'cramp someone's style' (to inhibit them) is a 20th-century idiom using the restrictive sense.
CRAMP.joke: A leg cramp at 3am is your calf's dramatic performance review — it waited all week for your attention.

CRANE.definition: A large wading bird with long legs and neck; a machine for lifting and moving heavy loads.
CRANE.origin: Old English 'cran', related to Greek 'geranos' and Latin 'grus'
CRANE.evolution: The bird name is original; the mechanical crane was named by the 14th century for its resemblance to the bird's long neck extended over the water; 'to crane one's neck' preserves the bird's distinctive posture.
CRANE.joke: The construction crane spends all day lifting heavy things and never gets credit — much like the people below it.

CRANK.definition: A part of an axle bent at right angles for converting motion; an eccentric person; to start a motor; bad-tempered (informal).
CRANK.origin: Old English 'cranc' related to 'cringle' — a bent ring
CRANK.evolution: The mechanical sense is original; the 'eccentric person' sense may come from the twisted shape (a twisted mind); 'crankshaft' keeps the mechanical meaning; 'get cranky' for bad-tempered is 19th-century American.
CRANK.joke: A crank call was only funny until caller ID — now it's just a data point.

CRASH.definition: A loud noise of something breaking; a violent collision; a sudden economic failure; to go to sleep suddenly.
CRASH.origin: Middle English; imitative of the sound
CRASH.evolution: Onomatopoeic origin; 'market crash' for economic collapse is 19th century; 'crash course' (intensive learning) and 'crash and burn' are 20th-century extensions; computer 'crash' is 1970s.
CRASH.joke: A crash course is just learning the hard way with extra steps removed.

CRASS.definition: Showing no intelligence or sensitivity; lacking all finesse or subtlety.
CRASS.evolution: From the Latin for physically thick — the metaphor of thickness for dullness or vulgarity; 'crass stupidity' and 'crass materialism' are standard compounds; stronger than 'vulgar', implying a complete absence of refinement.
CRASS.joke: Crass is what happens when subtlety took the day off and nobody covered the shift.

CRATE.definition: A slatted wooden or plastic container for transporting goods; an old dilapidated vehicle (informal).
CRATE.evolution: From Dutch trade; 'milk crate' became iconic in America; 'crate training' for dogs uses the enclosed container sense; the 'old crate' for a worn-out vehicle is 20th-century British slang.
CRATE.joke: A crate is what you put things in when 'box' doesn't feel official enough.

CRAVE.definition: To feel a powerful desire for something; to ask for earnestly.
CRAVE.origin: Old English 'crafian' meaning 'to demand as a right'
CRAVE.evolution: The Old English sense was more formal — demanding as a right; the intense desire sense is later; 'craving' is now strongest in contexts of food, substances, or emotional needs.
CRAVE.joke: Craving carbs at midnight is just your body demanding its rights — specifically its right to regret.

CRAWL.definition: To move on hands and knees; to move slowly; to be covered with crawling things.
CRAWL.evolution: From the clawing-movement sense; 'pub crawl' (visiting many bars) uses the slow-movement sense; 'crawling with' something uses the swarming sense; 'crawl space' is 20th-century American.
CRAWL.joke: Progress that moves at a crawl is still progress — just ask anyone who's ever installed a software update.

CRAZE.definition: An intense but short-lived fashion or enthusiasm.
CRAZE.origin: Probably from Old Norse 'krasa' meaning 'to crumble, shatter'
CRAZE.evolution: Originally meant to shatter or crack; the 'crackpot' sense of madness followed; the fad sense developed from the idea of being cracked by enthusiasm; 'crazed' preserves the cracked/fractured sense.
CRAZE.joke: Every craze is someone's best idea until the next craze arrives to replace it.

CRAZY.definition: Mentally unhinged; extremely enthusiastic; impractical or unwise; (informal) excellent.
CRAZY.origin: From 'craze' (to crack or shatter); 'full of cracks'
CRAZY.evolution: 'Full of cracks' became 'unsound in mind'; the enthusiastic sense ('crazy about music') is 19th century; the ironic 'crazy good' is 20th century; remarkable semantic range for a word meaning 'cracked'.
CRAZY.joke: Crazy is what sane people call a good idea before it works.

CREAK.definition: To make a high-pitched sound when pressure is applied; such a sound.
CREAK.origin: Middle English, imitative of the sound, related to 'crake'
CREAK.evolution: Onomatopoeic; creaking floorboards and doors are horror-film staples; 'creaky' for old or unsound extends the sound to any weakening structure.
CREAK.joke: A creaking floorboard is the house's way of complaining about your midnight snack habits.

CREAM.definition: The fatty part of milk that rises to the top; the best part of something; a cosmetic preparation; a pale yellowish-white color.
CREAM.origin: Old French 'cresme', blend of Late Latin 'chrisma' (holy oil) and Gaulish 'crama' (cream)
CREAM.evolution: A blend of two separate sources; 'the cream of the crop' for the best uses the rising-to-the-top metaphor; 'creamed' as a defeat sense is 20th-century American sports slang.
CREAM.joke: The cream rises to the top — along with everything else that doesn't sink immediately.

CREDO.definition: A statement of beliefs or aims guiding someone's actions; a creed.
CREDO.origin: Latin 'credo' meaning 'I believe', the first word of the Apostles' Creed
CREDO.evolution: Directly from the Latin first-person of 'credere' (to believe); the Apostles' Creed begins 'Credo in Deum'; extended to any personal or organizational statement of principles.
CREDO.joke: A personal credo is what you tell yourself on good days — the rest of the time, it's an aspiration.

CREED.definition: A system of belief, principles, or opinions; a formal statement of religious beliefs.
CREED.origin: Old English 'creda', from Latin 'credo' meaning 'I believe'
CREED.evolution: From the Latin first word of the Christian profession of faith; extended to any systematic set of beliefs — political, ethical, or philosophical creeds.
CREED.joke: A creed is the belief system you follow until something inconvenient is asked of you.

CREEK.definition: A small stream; an inlet or cove.
CREEK.evolution: Originally a coastal inlet; in American English, shifted to mean any small stream; 'up the creek' (in difficulties) came from the navigational hazard of stranded shallow water.
CREEK.joke: Up the creek without a paddle is just kayaking with extra existential dread.

CREEP.definition: To move slowly and carefully to avoid being noticed; to gradually spread; a detestable or unpleasant person.
CREEP.evolution: Stable Old English movement verb; 'creep up on' and 'it crept up on me' use the stealthy sense; 'mission creep' is Cold War military jargon for expanding objectives; 'creep' as an insult is 20th century.
CREEP.joke: Scope creep is how a weekend project becomes a two-year renovation.

CREME.definition: Cream; used in names of various sweet or creamy foods and cosmetics.
CREME.evolution: Borrowed directly from French to give food and cosmetic products a sophisticated air; 'crème de la crème' (cream of the cream) is the superlative French expression for the very best.
CREME.joke: Crème brûlée is just pudding that had the audacity to get a blowtorch.

CREPE.definition: A thin French pancake; a light, crinkled fabric; a type of rubber with a wrinkled texture.
CREPE.origin: French 'crêpe', from Old French 'crespe', from Latin 'crispus' meaning 'curled'
CREPE.evolution: The curled/crinkled sense applies to the fabric, the rubber, and (loosely) the thin pancake; 'crêpe paper' captures the crinkled texture; the pancake became a French cultural symbol internationally.
CREPE.joke: A crepe is a pancake that studied abroad and came back with opinions.

CREPT.definition: Past tense of 'creep'; moved slowly and carefully.
CREPT.evolution: Irregular past tense of 'creep'; 'the deadline crept up on me' perfectly captures the stealthy approach of things one should have seen coming.
CREPT.joke: Time crept forward — as if aware that urgency only made things worse.

CRESS.definition: A plant of the mustard family with small, peppery leaves used in salads.
CRESS.evolution: A culinary herb known since Old English; watercress grows in streams; garden cress grows quickly from seed; used in sandwiches and salads for its peppery bite.
CRESS.joke: Watercress is the vegetable that insists it counts as decoration and lunch simultaneously.

CREST.definition: The top of a hill or wave; a plume or tuft on an animal's head; a heraldic device above a coat of arms.
CREST.origin: Old French 'creste', from Latin 'crista' meaning 'crest, tuft'
CREST.evolution: From the Latin tuft/comb through the heraldic and natural world; 'on the crest of a wave' for peak success and 'crested' for decorated with a crest both use the topmost-point sense.
CREST.joke: Riding the crest of the wave is exhilarating right up until the wave decides it's done with you.

CRICK.definition: A painful stiffness in the neck or back; a small stream (dialectal).
CRICK.origin: Origin uncertain; possibly from Middle Dutch 'crick' meaning 'a stiff joint'
CRICK.evolution: The painful stiffness sense has been consistent; in American English 'crick' is also a dialectal term for a small creek, possibly a different word; 'crick in the neck' is the standard phrase.
CRICK.joke: A crick in the neck is your body's invoice for sleeping in an unsupported position.

CRIED.definition: Past tense of 'cry'; shed tears; shouted out.
CRIED.origin: Old French 'crier', from Latin 'quiritare' meaning 'to cry out'
CRIED.evolution: From the Roman public shouting sense through French; the shedding-tears sense is a secondary development; 'wolf was cried' preserves the alerting shout; 'she cried' now primarily means weeping.
CRIED.joke: I cried at a commercial once — twice — seven times. The algorithm knows my exact weakness.

CRIER.definition: A person who cries or weeps easily; a town crier who makes public announcements by shouting.
CRIER.origin: Old French 'crieur', from 'crier' meaning 'to cry out'
CRIER.evolution: Both senses coexist — the town crier (public announcer) is the professional, the tearful crier the informal person; the town crier's role became obsolete with print media and newspapers.
CRIER.joke: The town crier had the loudest news feed and no algorithm to suppress it.

CRIME.definition: An act punishable by law; an act widely condemned as wrong; a shame or a pity (informal).
CRIME.origin: Old French 'crime', from Latin 'crimen' meaning 'judgment, charge, crime'
CRIME.evolution: From the Latin legal judgment; the informal 'it's a crime' for something deplorable is a longstanding hyperbole; 'crime doesn't pay' is a 20th-century aphorism challenged by available evidence.
CRIME.joke: Crime doesn't pay — but apparently it does cover the cost of a very interesting documentary series.

CRIMP.definition: To compress into small folds or ridges; to cause a problem or restriction.
CRIMP.evolution: From the wrinkling/compression sense in Low German; the hair-crimping sense is the most common modern use; 'to put a crimp in' (to hinder) is an early 20th-century American expression.
CRIMP.joke: Crimped hair was the 1980s' way of asking 'what if texture was a full personality?'

CRISP.definition: Firm and dry; pleasantly cool and fresh; decisive and brisk; (British) a potato chip.
CRISP.evolution: From the curly/textured Latin sense through the dry-and-firm sense; 'crisp' morning air and crisp lettuce share the freshness quality; in British English, a 'crisp' is a potato chip (thin and crispy).
CRISP.joke: A crisp British morning and a crisp British potato chip share exactly one quality — they both require some adjustment.

CROAK.definition: To make a deep, harsh sound like a frog; to die (informal).
CROAK.origin: Imitative; related to Old English 'cracian' meaning 'to croak'
CROAK.evolution: Onomatopoeic frog sound; 'croak' for death probably comes from the death-rattle sound or from the ominous croaking of ravens; 'croaked' as slang for dying is 19th century.
CROAK.joke: A frog croaks all night and calls it music — and honestly, given the alternative meaning, points for optimism.

CROCK.definition: An earthenware pot or jar; an old, worn-out person or vehicle (informal); nonsense (informal).
CROCK.evolution: The pottery vessel sense is original; 'crockery' for dishes derives from it; 'a crock' for an old vehicle is from the broken-pottery sense; 'crock of gold' at the rainbow's end is Irish folklore.
CROCK.joke: A crock pot is patient and reliable — it will outlast your ambitions and possibly you.

CRONE.definition: An old woman who is considered ugly or bad-tempered; a wise woman in folklore.
CRONE.origin: Old French 'caroigne' meaning 'carcass, decrepit woman', related to 'carrion'
CRONE.evolution: Initially quite negative (like carrion); in modern neopagan and feminist discourse, 'crone' is being reclaimed as the third stage of the Triple Goddess — Maiden, Mother, Crone — honoring wisdom.
CRONE.joke: Crone wisdom is hard-won — every wrinkle is a chapter title.

CRONY.definition: A close friend or companion, especially one who is part of a social or professional group.
CRONY.evolution: From the Greek for long-standing; Cambridge slang at first; now predominantly used in 'cronyism' for favoritism toward friends in politics or business, giving the word a negative charge.
CRONY.joke: A crony is just a friend who got the contract — and the friend calls it loyalty.

CROOK.definition: A bent or curved thing; a person who is dishonest or criminal; a hooked staff.
CROOK.evolution: From the Norse hook through the shepherd's crook; 'by hook or by crook' (by any means) comes from medieval wood-gathering rights; the dishonest-person sense is from the crooked/devious metaphor.
CROOK.joke: A crook's career is full of twists and turns — structurally inevitable, really.

CROSS.definition: A mark or structure with two lines intersecting; a hybrid; angry or annoyed (adjective).
CROSS.origin: Old Norse 'kross', from Old Irish 'cros', from Latin 'crux'
CROSS.evolution: The Christian symbol gave the shape its name in English via Norse; 'cross-examination' and 'cross-purposes' use the 'going across' sense; 'cross' for angry (British) uses the 'opposed/contrary' sense.
CROSS.joke: Being cross is the British version of being furious — same intensity, better manners.

CROUP.definition: A childhood respiratory disease causing a harsh cough; the rump of a horse.
CROUP.origin: Scottish 'croup' meaning 'to speak hoarsely'; the horse sense from Old French 'croupe' meaning 'rump'
CROUP.evolution: Two separate words merged in spelling; the disease is named for its croaking cough; the horse's croup (hindquarters) is from Old French; both senses remain in current use.
CROUP.joke: Croup knows exactly how to wake parents at 3am — efficiently and memorably.

CROWD.definition: A large number of people gathered together; to fill or occupy too closely.
CROWD.evolution: From the pushing/pressing sense — a crowd presses together; 'crowdfunding' and 'crowd control' are 20th–21st century compounds; 'playing to the crowd' for populism is a 19th-century expression.
CROWD.joke: A crowd is a committee that formed spontaneously and has no chairperson, which is why it always ends in traffic.

CROWN.definition: A circular headpiece worn as a symbol of royal authority; the top of the head; to place a crown on.
CROWN.origin: Old French 'corone', from Latin 'corona' meaning 'garland, crown'
CROWN.evolution: From the Latin garland through the regal headpiece; the top-of-the-head sense, 'crown of a tooth', and the British coin 'crown' all use the circular-top metaphor; 'to crown it all' is an idiomatic climax.
CROWN.joke: The crown is the world's most expensive hat — and it comes with a lifetime subscription to obligation.

CRUDE.definition: In a natural or raw state; not refined or processed; lacking tact or sophistication.
CRUDE.evolution: From the Latin raw-and-bloody sense; 'crude oil' (unrefined petroleum) is the most literal current use; the vulgar/uncouth sense follows the unprocessed metaphor.
CRUDE.joke: Crude humor is just refined taste that skipped the refinery.

CRUEL.definition: Causing pain or suffering deliberately; devoid of compassion.
CRUEL.origin: Old French 'cruel', from Latin 'crudelis' meaning 'not easily moved by tears, hard-hearted'
CRUEL.evolution: From the Latin hard-heartedness; 'cruel to be kind' (causing short-term pain for long-term benefit) is from Shakespeare's Hamlet; 'cruelly' and 'cruelty' are standard derivations.
CRUEL.joke: Cruelly honest is what people call themselves when they want permission to be rude.

CRUMB.definition: A small fragment of bread, cake, or similar food; a contemptible person (informal).
CRUMB.origin: Old English 'cruma' meaning 'interior part of bread'
CRUMB.evolution: From the soft interior of bread (where crumbs form); the contemptible-person sense is 20th century American; 'leave a trail of crumbs' uses the Hansel and Gretel metaphor for clues.
CRUMB.joke: A crumb of comfort is what you offer when you've run out of the whole loaf.

CRUMP.definition: To make a heavy thudding sound; to crumple or cave in; a heavy explosive shell (WWII slang).
CRUMP.origin: Imitative of a heavy impact sound
CRUMP.evolution: Onomatopoeic for a heavy dull impact; 'crumple' and 'crump' share the same compressive sense; WWI/WWII soldiers used 'crump' for the sound of incoming shells.
CRUMP.joke: A crump in traffic means something structural gave up — either the road or your patience.

CRUSH.definition: A large crowd; a strong but often brief romantic interest; to press with great force.
CRUSH.origin: Old French 'croissir', related to 'crush', imitative of pressing sounds
CRUSH.evolution: The pressing/squeezing sense is original; the romantic infatuation sense is 19th-century American; 'orange crush' (a soft drink) and 'crush a goal' are 20th-century developments.
CRUSH.joke: A crush is the most inefficient form of cardio — elevated heart rate, zero movement.

CRUST.definition: The hard outer part of bread; the Earth's outer layer; an impertinent manner (informal).
CRUST.origin: Old French 'crouste', from Latin 'crusta' meaning 'rind, shell, crust'
CRUST.evolution: From the Latin shell/rind sense; the Earth's crust uses the same outer-layer metaphor; 'the upper crust' for the social elite comes from the best part of the bread being reserved for those of rank.
CRUST.joke: The upper crust gets the best bread — it's been that way since bread was invented.

CRYPT.definition: An underground chamber, especially one beneath a church used for burials.
CRYPT.origin: Latin 'crypta', from Greek 'kruptē' meaning 'hidden vault', from 'kruptein' meaning 'to hide'
CRYPT.evolution: From the Greek hiding/concealing sense; 'crypto-' (hidden) and 'cryptic' share this root; the modern 'cryptocurrency' uses the encryption/hidden sense; 'cryptography' is the science of hidden writing.
CRYPT.joke: A crypt is the original cold storage — reliable, secure, and very final.

CUBIC.definition: Having the shape of a cube; measured in cubic units (volume).
CUBIC.origin: From Latin 'cubicus', from Greek 'kubikos', from 'kubos' meaning 'cube'
CUBIC.evolution: A geometrical term from ancient Greek cube mathematics; 'cubic equation' (a polynomial of degree 3) and 'cubic meter/foot' (volume measurement) are the primary uses.
CUBIC.joke: A cubic measurement is the math world's way of saying 'now imagine this in three directions simultaneously.'

CUMIN.definition: A plant of the parsley family whose aromatic seeds are used as a spice.
CUMIN.origin: Old English 'cymen', from Latin 'cuminum', from Greek 'kyminon', probably from a Semitic source
CUMIN.evolution: One of the oldest known spices; mentioned in ancient Egyptian texts and the Bible; traveled from the Middle East through Greece and Rome into European cooking; essential in Mexican, Indian, and Middle Eastern cuisines.
CUMIN.joke: Cumin is the spice that makes everything taste vaguely intentional.

CURIO.definition: A rare, unusual, or intriguing object.
CURIO.evolution: A 19th-century shortening of 'curiosity' for small unusual objects; 'curio shops' and 'curio cabinets' became Victorian collections of unusual items; the 'cabinet of curiosities' is an early museum form.
CURIO.joke: A curio is an object that has no function beyond making visitors ask where you got it — and that's enough.

CURLY.definition: Having or forming curls; not straight.
CURLY.evolution: Derived from the curl or spiral shape; 'curly' hair and 'curly' brackets ({}) both use the spiraling form; 'Curly' as a nickname for someone with curly (or sometimes, ironically, no) hair is common.
CURLY.joke: Curly hair is nature's reminder that you're not in charge of how this day begins.

CURRY.definition: A dish of meat, vegetables, or fish cooked with a sauce of spices; to groom a horse.
CURRY.evolution: From Tamil through Portuguese trade; 'curry favor' is an unrelated phrase from Old French 'currier' (to groom, from 'currier' — to groom a horse) — the two curries are etymologically distinct.
CURRY.joke: Curry is the answer to the question 'what if spice were the point, not the accent?'

CURSE.definition: A solemn utterance invoking evil on someone; a cause of harm or misery; a swear word.
CURSE.origin: Old English 'curs' meaning 'a prayer for harm or misfortune'
CURSE.evolution: From the prayer-for-evil sense; 'the curse of' (the bane of) and 'the curse' (menstruation, in older usage) show its range; 'cuss' is an American dialectal form.
CURSE.joke: A curse is just a very targeted negative review — pre-digital social media.

CURVE.definition: A line or shape that bends smoothly without angles; to bend smoothly.
CURVE.evolution: From the Latin bent line; 'learning curve' (rate of skill acquisition) is a 20th-century business expression; 'throw a curve' (a curveball) comes from baseball; 'curve' for a road bend is standard.
CURVE.joke: A learning curve sounds gentle until you realize the steepness isn't a metaphor.

CURVY.definition: Having many curves; (of a person) having an attractively curved figure.
CURVY.evolution: A direct derivation; both geometric and bodily senses have been in use since the early 20th century; the aesthetic 'curvy' body sense became more common as body-positive language shifted.
CURVY.joke: Curvy roads are beautiful in landscapes and terrifying at 60 mph — context is everything.

CUTIE.definition: An attractive or endearing person or thing.
CUTIE.origin: From 'cute' (a shortening of 'acute' meaning sharp, clever) plus '-ie'
CUTIE.evolution: 'Cute' shortened from 'acute' (smart) in 18th-century English; the attractive sense followed in American English; 'cutie' is the affectionate diminutive, used both sincerely and condescendingly.
CUTIE.joke: A cutie can get away with almost anything — that's not the lesson we intended to teach, but here we are.

CYBER.definition: Relating to computers and the internet; relating to virtual reality and cyberpunk culture.
CYBER.evolution: 'Cybernetics' was coined by Norbert Wiener in 1948; 'cyber-' became a prefix for all internet-related terms in the 1990s; 'cyberspace' was coined by William Gibson in 1982; the prefix has been both mainstream and overused.
CYBER.joke: Cyber is the prefix that turns every crime into a more serious-sounding crime.

CYCLE.definition: A series of events regularly repeated; a bicycle or motorcycle; to ride a bicycle.
CYCLE.origin: French 'cycle', from Latin 'cyclus', from Greek 'kuklos' meaning 'circle, wheel'
CYCLE.evolution: From the Greek circle; 'cycle' and 'wheel' share the same Indo-European root; the bicycle sense is 19th century; 'news cycle', 'sleep cycle', and 'product lifecycle' are modern compounds.
CYCLE.joke: The news cycle spins so fast it's become its own centrifuge for separating signal from noise.

CYNIC.definition: A person who believes that people are motivated by self-interest; one who questions the sincerity of others.
CYNIC.origin: Latin 'cynicus', from Greek 'kunikos' meaning 'dog-like' — from the Cynic school of philosophy who lived like dogs
CYNIC.evolution: The Greek Cynics (like Diogenes) rejected social conventions and lived simply; their critics called them dog-like; 'cynic' shifted from this philosophical school to mean anyone doubting human goodness.
CYNIC.joke: A cynic is an optimist with experience — and a slightly worse posture.

DADDY.definition: An informal word for father; a person of dominance or authority in a particular group.
DADDY.origin: From 'dad' (Celtic origin, used by children) plus '-y'
DADDY.evolution: 'Dad' is attested from the 16th century, 'daddy' from the 17th; 'sugar daddy' (wealthy older man) is 20th-century American slang; 'big daddy' for an authority figure follows the same dominance extension.
DADDY.joke: Every daddy was once a person who had no idea what he was doing — some of them never stopped.

DAILY.definition: Done, produced, or occurring every day; relating to each day.
DAILY.evolution: A core time-measurement word; 'daily bread' from the Lord's Prayer preserves the most essential daily need; 'daily driver' for a car used every day is 20th-century automotive slang.
DAILY.joke: A daily habit is just doing something so often you forget you chose to start.

DAIRY.definition: A place for storing or processing milk and milk products; relating to milk production.
DAIRY.origin: Middle English 'deierie', from 'deie' meaning 'female servant, dairywoman', from Old English 'dæge'
DAIRY.evolution: From the woman who worked with dough and dairy; the farm/processing building sense narrowed the word; 'dairy-free' is a 20th-century compound reflecting dietary choice.
DAIRY.joke: A dairy farm is where 4am is considered a reasonable start time and nobody argues with it.

DAISY.definition: A small plant with white petals around a yellow center; a person or thing considered outstanding (informal).
DAISY.origin: Old English 'dægesēage' meaning 'day's eye' — the flower closes at night
DAISY.evolution: A beautiful compound — the flower that opens with daylight; 'daisy chain' for linking things together and 'pushing up daisies' for being dead are old idioms.
DAISY.joke: A daisy closes at night — finally, a flower with appropriate work-life boundaries.

DALLY.definition: To delay or act slowly; to have a casual, playful romantic interaction.
DALLY.origin: Old French 'dalier' meaning 'to chat, amuse oneself'
DALLY.evolution: The playful-chatting sense is original; the loitering sense developed from the idea of wasting time in idle pleasure; 'dalliance' for a casual romance preserves the flirtatious origin.
DALLY.joke: To dally is to turn procrastination into a social activity — very efficient use of wasted time.

DANCE.definition: To move rhythmically to music; a sequence of rhythmic movements to music.
DANCE.origin: Old French 'dancier', of uncertain origin; possibly from Old High German 'dansōn'
DANCE.evolution: Entered English from French; 'dance' as a metaphor for complex interaction ('a careful dance of negotiations') is a centuries-old pattern; 'dancing around' an issue is a clear extension.
DANCE.joke: Dance like nobody's watching — and then immediately wonder why nobody warned you they were recording.

DANDY.definition: A man who is excessively concerned with elegant appearance; first-rate or excellent (adjective).
DANDY.origin: Possibly a Scottish nickname for Andrew, or from 'Dandy' as a pet form of 'Jack-a-dandy'
DANDY.evolution: The fashion-obsessed man sense emerged in the 18th century; Beau Brummell is the archetypal dandy; the adjective 'just dandy' for fine came from the idea of being dressed and groomed to perfection.
DANDY.joke: A dandy considers getting dressed an art form — which is more defensible than it sounds at 7am.

DATUM.definition: A piece of information; a fact or statistic used for analysis (usually used in plural 'data').
DATUM.origin: Latin 'datum' meaning 'something given', past participle of 'dare' meaning 'to give'
DATUM.evolution: The Latin neuter past participle; 'data' is the plural but has become a mass noun in everyday English; in surveying, a 'datum' is a reference point for measurements.
DATUM.joke: A datum is just one lonely fact waiting for context to make it meaningful.

DAUNT.definition: To make someone feel intimidated or apprehensive; to discourage.
DAUNT.origin: Old French 'danter', from Latin 'domitare' meaning 'to tame'
DAUNT.evolution: From the taming/subduing sense; 'nothing daunted' (undeterred) is an archaic phrase preserving the subdued sense; 'daunting task' is the commonest modern use.
DAUNT.joke: A daunting task is just a normal task seen through the eyes of someone who hasn't started yet.

DEALT.definition: Past tense of 'deal'; distributed cards; traded; managed or coped with something.
DEALT.evolution: The dividing/sharing sense is original; 'deal' for a business transaction developed in the 15th century; 'dealt with' for handling a problem and 'dealt a blow' are figurative extensions.
DEALT.joke: History dealt the cards — most civilizations just played a very bad hand.

DEATH.definition: The end of life; the permanent cessation of all biological functions.
DEATH.evolution: One of the most stable words in English; personified as Death since medieval times; 'death toll', 'death trap', and 'putting to death' are standard compounds; 'death by a thousand cuts' is a Chinese execution metaphor.
DEATH.joke: Death and taxes are inevitable — but only one of them sends reminders.

DEBAR.definition: To officially exclude someone from a place or activity.
DEBAR.origin: From 'de-' (removal) plus 'bar' (to obstruct), from Old French 'barre'
DEBAR.evolution: A formal, legal word; debarment from professional practice (law, medicine) is the most common modern use; less common than 'bar from' in everyday speech but more precise.
DEBAR.joke: To be debarred is to have the door closed and the key explained in formal writing.

DEBIT.definition: An entry in an account recording a sum taken out; to record as an outgoing payment.
DEBIT.origin: Latin 'debitum' meaning 'what is owed', from 'debere' meaning 'to owe'
DEBIT.evolution: From the same Latin root as 'debt'; the accounting distinction between debit and credit (from Latin 'creditum') has been standard since medieval Italian banking; 'debit card' is a 1970s innovation.
DEBIT.joke: A debit is just money expressing its desire to leave you at the most inconvenient moment.

DEBUG.definition: To detect and remove errors from computer software or hardware.
DEBUG.evolution: 'Bug' for a technical defect was popularized by Grace Hopper who found a literal moth causing errors in an early computer in 1947; 'debug' followed naturally as the removal process.
DEBUG.joke: Debugging is twice as hard as writing code — which means if you wrote it cleverly, you'll never fix it.

DEBUT.definition: A person's first appearance in a role; the beginning of a career or the introduction of a product.
DEBUT.origin: French 'début' meaning 'a beginning', from 'débuter' meaning 'to lead off'
DEBUT.evolution: Directly from French theatrical and social context — a debutante's first formal introduction to society; extended to any first appearance or product launch.
DEBUT.joke: A debut is the one performance where your nerves are expected and your inexperience is charming.

DECAL.definition: A design or picture prepared for transfer from paper to another surface.
DECAL.origin: Short for 'decalcomania', from French 'décalcomanie', from 'décalquer' meaning 'to transfer a tracing'
DECAL.evolution: Shortened from the longer French term; 'decal' became the American standard while 'transfer' is the British equivalent; laptop stickers are the modern iteration of the century-old decal tradition.
DECAL.joke: A decal is how you express a personality commitment without the permanence of a tattoo.

DECAY.definition: To rot or cause to rot; to deteriorate in quality, power, or vitality.
DECAY.origin: Old French 'decair', from Latin 'decidere' meaning 'to fall off'
DECAY.evolution: From the falling-off sense; 'urban decay', 'moral decay', and 'radioactive decay' show the word's range from concrete to abstract to scientific; tooth decay is the most common household sense.
DECAY.joke: Decay is just nature's way of recycling — with excellent timing and no regard for your schedule.

DECOR.definition: The furnishing and decoration of a room; a style of decoration.
DECOR.origin: French 'décor', from 'décorer' meaning 'to decorate', from Latin 'decorare'
DECOR.evolution: Borrowed directly from French design/theatre vocabulary; 'interior decor' became a standard compound as interior design professionalized in the 20th century.
DECOR.joke: Decor is the art of making strangers feel judged before they've sat down.

DECOY.definition: An animal or object used to lure game or an enemy into a trap; a person used to mislead.
DECOY.origin: Dutch 'de kooi' meaning 'the cage', from 'kooi' meaning 'cage'
DECOY.evolution: From the Dutch duck-cage used in hunting; the live bird lured others into the cage; 'decoy' expanded to any form of strategic distraction used in hunting, warfare, and intelligence.
DECOY.joke: A decoy works perfectly right up until the moment everyone is looking at the wrong thing — which is most meetings.

DECRY.definition: To publicly denounce; to express strong disapproval of.
DECRY.origin: French 'décrier' meaning 'to cry down', from 'de-' plus 'crier' meaning 'to cry'
DECRY.evolution: From the French of crying out against something; a formal word for public disapproval; 'decry' is stronger than 'criticize' and implies public moral condemnation.
DECRY.joke: To decry something loudly ensures its audience triples — the first rule of effective outrage.

DEFER.definition: To put off to a later time; to yield to someone's opinion or wishes.
DEFER.origin: Two origins: deferring time is from Latin 'differre'; deferring to someone is from Latin 'deferre' meaning 'to carry away, refer'
DEFER.evolution: Two separate Latin roots merged in spelling; 'I defer to your expertise' and 'let's defer this to Tuesday' are grammatically related but etymologically distinct.
DEFER.joke: Defer is what you say when you want to say no but admire their confidence too much to commit.

DEIGN.definition: To do something that one considers beneath one's dignity; to condescend.
DEIGN.origin: Old French 'deignier', from Latin 'dignari' meaning 'to deem worthy'
DEIGN.evolution: From the Latin dignity; 'to deign to reply' carries unmistakable condescension; 'disdain' is a related word where the Latin dignity is negated (dis-dain).
DEIGN.joke: He deigned to acknowledge the memo — three weeks later, in passing, while looking at his phone.

DEITY.definition: A god or goddess; divine status, quality, or nature.
DEITY.origin: Old French 'deité', from Late Latin 'deitas', from 'deus' meaning 'god'
DEITY.evolution: From the Latin god through French theological vocabulary; 'deity' is more formal and cross-cultural than 'god'; 'deify' (to treat as a god) shares the root.
DEITY.joke: Every deity has worshippers who interpret their silence differently — usually in their own favor.

DELAY.definition: To make something late; to act slowly; an instance of being made late.
DELAY.origin: Old French 'delaier', from 'de-' plus 'laier' meaning 'to leave'
DELAY.evolution: From the leaving/putting off sense; 'delay of game' is sports terminology; 'delayed gratification' is a psychology concept from the Stanford marshmallow experiment (1970s).
DELAY.joke: A delay is just the universe adding buffer time before you have to deal with whatever comes next.

DELTA.definition: The fourth letter of the Greek alphabet; a triangular tract of land formed where a river meets the sea.
DELTA.evolution: The triangular shape of the letter matched the geography of river mouths (famously the Nile delta); used in mathematics for change, in aviation for the wing shape, and in phonetics.
DELTA.joke: Delta stands for change — which explains why airline passengers invoke it with such mixed feelings.

DELVE.definition: To research or investigate deeply; to reach inside something and search around.
DELVE.evolution: The literal digging sense has largely become obsolete; the figurative 'delve into' for thorough investigation is now the primary use; 'delver' for a miner is archaic.
DELVE.joke: To delve into a topic is to start with a question and end somewhere completely unexpected — usually at midnight.

DEMON.definition: An evil spirit; a person of great energy or enthusiasm; something destructive or tormenting.
DEMON.origin: Latin 'daemon', from Greek 'daimōn' meaning 'divine power, fortune, spirit'
DEMON.evolution: The Greek daimon was a neutral spiritual being; Christianity made it specifically evil; 'demon' for a person of terrifying energy ('she works like a demon') is a positive modern use; 'speed demon' preserves this.
DEMON.joke: A demon for detail is just a compliment wearing very dramatic clothing.

DEMUR.definition: To raise doubts or objections; a pause or hesitation.
DEMUR.origin: Old French 'demorer', from Latin 'demorari' meaning 'to linger, delay'
DEMUR.evolution: From the delaying sense through the objection sense; 'without demur' means without raising objections; used mainly in formal and legal contexts; 'demurral' is a rarely used noun form.
DEMUR.joke: To demur politely is to say no in a way that gives everyone time to pretend they didn't hear it.

DENIM.definition: A sturdy cotton fabric with a diagonal twill weave; jeans or clothing made from this fabric.
DENIM.origin: French 'serge de Nîmes' meaning 'serge from the city of Nîmes' — shortened to 'de Nîmes'
DENIM.evolution: Named for the French city where the fabric was made; Levi Strauss used it for gold-rush miners' trousers in 1873; denim jeans became a global symbol of casual culture in the 20th century.
DENIM.joke: Denim has survived every fashion era and outlasted every trend — it's the cockroach of textiles, and we mean that respectfully.

DENSE.definition: Closely compacted; (of a person) stupid or slow to understand; complex and not easy to understand.
DENSE.evolution: From the physical compactness; 'dense forest', 'dense prose', and 'he's a bit dense' all use the impenetrable metaphor; 'density' is the measurable form.
DENSE.joke: Dense prose is the written equivalent of a locked door — possible to get through, but nobody's happy about it.

DEPOT.definition: A place for storage; a bus or railway station; a military supply base.
DEPOT.evolution: From the French storage sense; American English added the railway station meaning (a place passengers deposit themselves); 'home depot' uses the storehouse sense for building supplies.
DEPOT.joke: A depot is where everything waits to go somewhere — occasionally including the people.

DEPTH.definition: The distance from top to bottom; the intensity of a quality; the deepest part.
DEPTH.evolution: Derived from the adjective 'deep'; 'depth of character', 'out of one's depth', and 'in depth' show the literal becoming figurative; 'depths of winter' and 'depth charge' use different aspects.
DEPTH.joke: Out of your depth is just in-the-deep-end re-framed by someone trying to be helpful.

DERBY.definition: An annual horse race; a sports event between rival local teams; a type of stiff felt hat.
DERBY.origin: Named after the 12th Earl of Derby, who founded the Epsom Derby horse race in 1780
DERBY.evolution: The Earl's famous horse race gave its name to all annual sports championships; the hat style (bowler in British English) was called a derby in America after becoming associated with horse racing fashion.
DERBY.joke: A derby is the event where everyone has strong opinions and only the horse doesn't.

DETER.definition: To discourage someone from doing something by instilling fear or doubt.
DETER.origin: Latin 'deterrere' meaning 'to frighten off', from 'de-' plus 'terrere' meaning 'to frighten'
DETER.evolution: From the Latin frightening-off sense; 'deterrent' (especially nuclear deterrent) became a Cold War term; the distinction between 'deter' and 'prevent' is that deterrence works through threat, prevention through action.
DETER.joke: A deterrent only works if the other person is weighing consequences — a small but important assumption.

DETOX.definition: A process of removing toxins from the body; abstaining from drugs, alcohol, or unhealthy foods.
DETOX.origin: Short for 'detoxification', from Latin 'de-' plus Greek 'toxikon' meaning 'poison for arrows'
DETOX.evolution: Medical detoxification programs started in the 20th century; the wellness-culture 'juice detox' and 'digital detox' are 21st-century extensions that the medical community views skeptically.
DETOX.joke: A digital detox is the modern ritual of putting down the phone and immediately wondering what you're missing on the phone.

DEUCE.definition: A score of 40-all in tennis, requiring two consecutive points to win; the playing card with two pips; the devil (archaic).
DEUCE.evolution: The number 'two' gives both the tennis score and the card sense; 'deuce' as a mild oath (short for 'devil') shares the two/evil association; 'what the deuce!' was a polite form of 'what the devil!'
DEUCE.joke: In tennis, deuce means neither player can admit defeat — politically, we call it a negotiation.

DEVIL.definition: The supreme spirit of evil in Abrahamic religions; a mischievous or unfortunate person; to prepare food with hot seasoning.
DEVIL.origin: Old English 'deofol', from Latin 'diabolus', from Greek 'diabolos' meaning 'slanderer, devil'
DEVIL.evolution: From the Greek slanderer through Latin theology to Old English; 'devil's advocate', 'devil in the details', and 'speak of the devil' are standard phrases; 'deviled eggs' uses the spicy preparation sense.
DEVIL.joke: The devil is in the details — which is why every contract has forty pages of them.

DIARY.definition: A daily record of events or private thoughts; a book with spaces for each day.
DIARY.origin: Latin 'diarium' meaning 'daily allowance' or 'daily record', from 'dies' meaning 'day'
DIARY.evolution: From the Latin daily allowance; Samuel Pepys's diary (1660–1669) established the personal record as a literary form; 'Dear Diary' became the classic opening phrase.
DIARY.joke: A diary is the one place you write the unfiltered truth — and then hide it from everyone who would understand.

DICEY.definition: Risky or unpredictable; uncertain as to outcome.
DICEY.origin: From 'dice' (plural of 'die') plus '-y' — from the gambling unpredictability of dice
DICEY.evolution: A 20th-century informal word; the gambling metaphor captures the randomness of uncertain outcomes; primarily British and Australian English in origin.
DICEY.joke: Dicey is how adults say 'this plan has significant variance' without updating the spreadsheet.

DIGIT.definition: Any numeral from 0 to 9; a finger or toe; a unit of measurement equal to the breadth of a finger.
DIGIT.evolution: The finger sense is original; numerals were called digits because early counting used fingers; 'digital' now means computerized, itself because computers use binary digit (bit) arithmetic.
DIGIT.joke: Your fingers are digits — and in the information age, they never stop working.

DILLY.definition: A person or thing that is remarkable or outstanding (informal, chiefly American).
DILLY.evolution: American English informal; 'a real dilly' means something genuinely impressive; 'dillydally' (to waste time) is a separate word of similar feel.
DILLY.joke: 'That's a real dilly' is what you say when you're too impressed to be specific about why.

DIMLY.definition: In a way that is not bright; vaguely or indistinctly.
DIMLY.evolution: A straightforward derivation; 'dimly aware' for vague consciousness and 'dimly lit' for poor lighting are the primary uses.
DIMLY.joke: Dimly remembered is the charity setting your brain applies to most of your 20s.

DINER.definition: A person who eats dinner; a small restaurant, typically American in style, serving simple food.
DINER.evolution: Originally meant a person who dines; the American diner (roadside restaurant often in a converted railroad car) became an iconic 20th-century institution; 'a real diner' is shorthand for unpretentious American food culture.
DINER.joke: A diner is where the coffee is bad, the portions are enormous, and somehow both of these are selling points.

DINGO.definition: A wild dog native to Australia.
DINGO.evolution: Borrowed from the indigenous Dharug people of coastal New South Wales; 'the dingo ate my baby' became one of the most infamous lines in Australian criminal history (1980) and popular culture.
DINGO.joke: The dingo is Australia's way of proving that even the dogs here are feral and confident about it.

DINGY.definition: Gloomy and drab; dirty-looking; dark and discolored.
DINGY.origin: Origin uncertain; possibly from Hindi 'dingā' meaning 'soiled'
DINGY.evolution: Possibly from Hindi through sailors' vocabulary; the drab, neglected appearance sense has been consistent; 'dingy' is stronger than 'dull' — it implies both darkness and a quality of neglect.
DINGY.joke: Dingy is what happens when no one loses things but nobody ever cleans anything either.

DIODE.definition: An electronic component that allows current to flow in only one direction.
DIODE.evolution: Named in 1919 by William Eccles; the two-path concept (two terminals, one-way flow) is captured in the name; LED (light-emitting diode) made diodes visible in everyday life.
DIODE.joke: A diode is the electronics world's one-way street — and unlike traffic, it's actually enforced.

DIRGE.definition: A lament for the dead; a mournful song or piece of music.
DIRGE.origin: From Latin 'dirige' meaning 'direct thou' — the first word of an antiphon in the Office for the Dead
DIRGE.evolution: From the liturgical Latin opening; the funeral-song sense has always been primary; figuratively, any slow or tedious piece of music is described as a dirge.
DIRGE.joke: A dirge is music's honest response to bad news — no silver lining, just good timing.

DIRTY.definition: Covered in dirt; morally wrong or dishonest; unpleasant.
DIRTY.evolution: From the Old Norse waste word; 'play dirty', 'dirty look', and 'dirty money' extend the soil sense to moral contamination; 'the dirty work' for unpleasant necessary tasks is a centuries-old idiom.
DIRTY.joke: Dirty laundry aired publicly remains dirty laundry — just with a larger audience.

DISCO.definition: A type of pop music with a regular beat; a nightclub playing this music; to dance to disco music.
DISCO.evolution: Shortened from 'discothèque', itself from 'disque' (record) plus 'bibliothèque' (library); the music genre peaked in the late 1970s; 'disco ball' and 'disco era' are the cultural shorthand.
DISCO.joke: Disco is what happens when music, fashion, and confidence all peak simultaneously and refuse to coordinate their timing.

DITCH.definition: A long, narrow channel dug in the ground; to abandon or get rid of something; to make an emergency landing on water.
DITCH.evolution: The drainage channel sense is original; 'ditch it' for discarding something is from the idea of dumping something in a ditch; 'last-ditch effort' comes from a final defensive position.
DITCH.joke: A last-ditch effort is called last-ditch because that's where it was found.

DITTO.definition: The same thing again; used to indicate repetition of a previous statement.
DITTO.origin: Italian dialect 'ditto' meaning 'said', from Latin 'dictum' — 'the aforesaid'
DITTO.evolution: From Italian bookkeeping shorthand; 'ditto marks' (〃) replaced rewriting the same entry; 'ditto' as agreement slang ('ditto!' for 'same here') is 20th century.
DITTO.joke: Ditto is what you say when you agree but can't be bothered to say why — a complete thought in two syllables.

DITTY.origin: Old French 'ditié' meaning 'composition', from Latin 'dictatum' meaning 'something dictated'
