FILER.evolution: The tool and the person share the name; 'nail filer' and 'tax filer' are common compounds; the document-filing sense comes from threading papers onto a wire or string.
FILER.joke: A filer knows exactly where everything is — in the very large pile labeled 'to file.'

FILET.definition: A boneless cut of meat or fish; a piece of net or lace worked in a square mesh.
FILET.origin: French 'filet' meaning 'thread, fillet', from 'fil' meaning 'thread', from Latin 'filum'
FILET.evolution: From the thread/string sense — the lace network; the boneless cut was named for being threaded or stripped of bone; 'filet mignon' is the French for 'dainty fillet' (the small, tender cut).
FILET.joke: Filet mignon is the steak that's so refined it refuses to be compared to other steaks in the same conversation.

FILLY.definition: A young female horse, especially one less than four years old.
FILLY.evolution: From Old Norse horse vocabulary; 'filly' in informal speech has sometimes referred to a young woman, a usage that has become outdated and is now considered offensive in most contexts.
FILLY.joke: A filly at the races is the one variable that makes every prediction simultaneously reasonable and wrong.

FILMY.definition: Resembling or covered with a thin film; hazy or gauzy.
FILMY.evolution: The thin membrane sense gives the hazy, gauze-like quality; 'filmy curtains' and 'filmy eyes' are standard uses; 'filmy layer' preserves the thin-coating sense.
FILMY.joke: Filmy is the texture of every curtain that provides the illusion of privacy without the reality of it.

FILTH.definition: Disgusting dirt; obscene or offensive material; morally corrupt behavior.
FILTH.evolution: From Old English moral and physical impurity; 'filthy rich' (extremely wealthy) uses the excess-disgust metaphor positively; 'filthy language' preserves the moral-pollution sense.
FILTH.joke: Filthy rich is proof that enough money turns any adjective into a compliment.

FINAL.definition: Coming at the end; conclusive and not subject to change; the last game in a competition.
FINAL.origin: Latin 'finalis' meaning 'of an end', from 'finis' meaning 'end'
FINAL.evolution: From the Latin ending; 'final answer' (Who Wants to Be a Millionaire) made the phrase carry game-show weight; 'finals' for university exams compress months of study into one existential afternoon.
FINAL.joke: 'Final answer' is what you say right before you change your answer.

FINCH.definition: A small to medium-sized songbird, typically with a short bill and colorful plumage.
FINCH.evolution: A stable Old English bird name; Darwin's finches (Galapagos, 1835) became iconic examples of adaptive radiation and natural selection; the house finch and goldfinch are common North American garden birds.
FINCH.joke: Darwin's finches changed science; your garden finches change nothing — and seem fine with that.

FINER.definition: Comparative of 'fine'; of higher quality; more delicate or subtle.
FINER.origin: Old French 'fin' meaning 'fine, refined', from Latin 'finis' meaning 'end, perfection'
FINER.evolution: The comparative of 'fine'; 'the finer things in life' is the aspirational phrase for luxury; 'finer points' for subtle details uses the delicacy sense; 'never finer' is a common hyperbolic praise.
FINER.joke: The finer things in life cost money, time, and patience — which is exactly why the rest of us enjoy talking about them.

FIRST.definition: Coming before all others in time or order; the most important; to do something for the first time.
FIRST.evolution: Superlative of 'fore'; 'first come, first served', 'first impressions', and 'at first' are standard idioms; 'first world problem' is a 21st-century self-deprecating phrase about minor inconveniences.
FIRST.joke: First impressions are formed in seven seconds — which means they're made before anyone says anything worth judging.

FISHY.definition: Smelling of fish; arousing suspicion; questionable.
FISHY.evolution: The smell-of-fish sense is original; 'something fishy' for something suspicious is a long-standing idiom — fish that smell are often past their best, suggesting decay and concealment.
FISHY.joke: Something fishy is usually exactly as suspicious as it smells — the nose knows.

FIXER.definition: A person who arranges things, especially informally or illegally; a photographic chemical that sets an image.
FIXER.evolution: The person-who-fixes sense is the base; the informal-arranger sense (a fixer for corrupt dealings) is 20th century; the photographic chemical 'fixer' (sodium thiosulfate) is also a 19th-century term.
FIXER.joke: A fixer fixes problems — which sounds like a compliment until you realize why the problems kept appearing.

FIZZY.definition: (Of a drink) containing bubbles of gas; sparkling; full of excited energy.
FIZZY.origin: From 'fizz' (imitative of the hissing sound) plus '-y'
FIZZY.evolution: 'Fizz' is onomatopoeic; 'fizzy drink' is the British English term for carbonated soft drink; 'fizzy personality' uses the bubbly-energy metaphor; champagne is the archetypal fizzy celebration drink.
FIZZY.joke: A fizzy drink is the only beverage that announces itself before you've opened the bottle.

FJORD.definition: A long, narrow deep inlet of the sea between high cliffs, especially in Norway.
FJORD.origin: Norwegian and Danish 'fjord', from Old Norse 'fjörðr'
FJORD.evolution: Borrowed directly from Norse; Norway has more fjords than any other country; 'fjord' famously appears on the deepest page of The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy as Earth's greatest achievement.
FJORD.joke: A fjord is what happens when a glacier leaves in a hurry and doesn't clean up.

FLACK.definition: A publicity agent; criticism or opposition; antiaircraft fire (variant of 'flak').
FLACK.origin: Acronym from German 'Fliegerabwehrkanone' (anti-aircraft gun); the PR sense came from the name Flack (a PR pioneer)
FLACK.evolution: Two words merged: 'flak' from German anti-aircraft guns (WWI) and 'flack' from Gene Flack, an early Hollywood publicist; 'take flack' for receiving criticism and 'flack' for a PR person coexist.
FLACK.joke: Taking flack is what happens when you say the thing everyone was thinking but nobody expected you to actually say.

FLAIL.definition: To wave or swing wildly; to struggle; a device for thrashing grain.
FLAIL.evolution: The threshing tool sense is original; the whip root gave 'flagellate'; 'flailing about' for uncoordinated struggling uses the uncontrolled-swing sense; the arms-flailing image became the standard panic gesture.
FLAIL.joke: Flailing is what ambition looks like before it finds its arms.

FLAIR.definition: A special or instinctive aptitude or ability; stylish elegance.
FLAIR.origin: French 'flair' meaning 'sense of smell, instinct', from 'flairer' meaning 'to smell'
FLAIR.evolution: From the French instinctive nose for something; 'a flair for languages' and 'dressed with flair' both use the natural-instinct and stylish sense; 'flair bartending' is a modern specialty.
FLAIR.joke: Flair is talent with better outfits.

FLAKE.definition: A small, flat, thin piece that has broken off; to break off in flakes; an unreliable person (informal).
FLAKE.evolution: From the Norse thin-slice sense; snowflakes, cornflakes, and paint flakes all preserve the thin-layer meaning; 'flake' for an unreliable person appeared in 20th-century American slang.
FLAKE.joke: A flake confirms plans and then disappears — nature's most elaborate form of ambiguity.

FLAKY.definition: Tending to come off in flakes; unreliable or unpredictable.
FLAKY.evolution: The physical sense (flaky pastry) and the personality sense (an unreliable person) both developed from the same base; 'flaky pastry' uses thin layers; 'flaky friend' uses the same fragmentation metaphor applied to commitments.
FLAKY.joke: Flaky pastry is a virtue; a flaky friend is a recurring disappointment — same word, very different pie.

FLAME.definition: A hot glowing body of burning gas; to burn with a bright fire; an angry online message.
FLAME.evolution: From the Latin fire; 'old flame' for a former lover preserves the passionate-fire metaphor; 'flame war' and 'flaming' for online hostility are 1980s internet coinages using fire as anger.
FLAME.joke: An old flame is warmly remembered and wisely left in the past — like most things that burn.

FLANK.definition: The side of an animal between ribs and hip; the side of a military formation; to be at the side of.
FLANK.origin: Old French 'flanc' meaning 'flank, side', of Germanic origin
FLANK.evolution: The body/military side sense has been stable; 'outflanked' for being strategically surprised from the side is standard military and business metaphor; the 'flank steak' preserves the anatomical sense.
FLANK.joke: Being outflanked in a meeting is just being surprised from a direction you forgot to monitor.

FLARE.definition: A sudden brief burst of flame; a signal device producing bright light; to spread outward.
FLARE.origin: Origin uncertain; possibly related to Norwegian 'flara' meaning 'to blaze'
FLARE.evolution: The sudden fire sense is original; 'flare-up' for a sudden recurrence of conflict or illness; flared trousers (bell-bottoms) use the widening sense; 'solar flare' is an astrophysical event.
FLARE.joke: A flare-up is what happens when a problem wasn't solved, just interrupted.

FLASH.definition: A sudden brief intense light; a sudden bright occurrence; a small amount of time.
FLASH.origin: Middle English, imitative of a sudden bright occurrence
FLASH.evolution: Onomatopoeic for sudden brightness; 'flash in the pan' (a brief success that fails) comes from flintlock muskets; 'flash mob', 'flash drive', and 'flash sale' are 21st-century coinages using the sudden/brief sense.
FLASH.joke: A flash sale creates urgency so effective that people buy things they don't need before they've finished deciding they don't need them.

FLASK.definition: A narrow-necked container for storing liquid; a vacuum flask; a hip flask for spirits.
FLASK.origin: Medieval Latin 'flasca, flasco' meaning 'cask'; origin uncertain
FLASK.evolution: The vessel shape is consistent across all uses; the thermos flask (vacuum flask, 1892, Dewar) preserves heat by vacuum; the hip flask (for discreet spirits consumption) became associated with prohibition-era smuggling.
FLASK.joke: A hip flask is optimism in metal form — the conviction that the occasion will require more than one glass.

FLECK.definition: A very small patch of color or light; a small particle.
FLECK.evolution: From Norse spotting; a 'fleck of paint', 'flecked with gold', and 'flecks of light' all use the small-patch sense; 'freckle' may share the same root.
FLECK.joke: A fleck of dust is the universe's reminder that cleanliness has a very short half-life.

FLEET.definition: A group of ships, aircraft, or vehicles; moving nimbly; passing quickly.
FLEET.origin: Old English 'flēot' meaning 'a float, ship, creek'
FLEET.evolution: From the floating/flowing sense; 'fleet of foot' for swift movement uses the water-flowing speed; a 'fleet' of vehicles uses the grouped-vessels sense; 'fleeting' for temporary shares the passing-quickly root.
FLEET.joke: A fleeting moment is exactly as long as it takes to appreciate it and regret not appreciating it sooner.

FLESH.definition: The soft substance of a body, especially the muscular parts; the body as opposed to the mind; the soft edible part of fruit.
FLESH.evolution: A fundamental Old English word; 'flesh and blood' for human nature and family ties; 'in the flesh' for physical presence versus virtual; 'press the flesh' for political handshaking is 20th-century American.
FLESH.joke: Meeting in the flesh is what we call human interaction ever since we invented alternatives to it.

FLICK.definition: To move or propel with a quick light movement; a light touch; a film (informal, British).
FLICK.origin: Imitative of a light striking sound
FLICK.evolution: Onomatopoeic; 'flick through' a book, 'the flicks' for cinema (British slang), and 'flick of a switch' show the quick-light-motion range; swiping a smartphone screen is the digital flick.
FLICK.joke: A flick of the wrist achieves things that an hour of planning only describes.

FLIER.definition: A person or thing that flies; a small advertising leaflet; a person who travels by plane regularly.
FLIER.evolution: Both the flying-thing and the advertising-paper sense have been in use since the late 19th century; the advertising 'flier/flyer' was distributed from balloons or by hand; frequent flier programs are 20th-century airline marketing.
FLIER.joke: A flier promises everything it says and delivers everything it withholds — the paper version of digital marketing.

FLING.definition: To throw forcefully; a short period of unrestrained activity; a brief sexual relationship.
FLING.evolution: From the Norse throwing/flogging sense; 'to have a fling' (a brief relationship) uses the unrestrained-activity sense from 'have a fling at something'; 'Highland fling' is a vigorous Scottish dance.
FLING.joke: A highland fling is the dance form that explains why kilts are pleated for maximum aerodynamics.

FLINT.definition: A hard grey rock that produces sparks when struck; a piece of this used to light fires.
FLINT.evolution: One of the most important materials in human prehistory; the flintlock firearm made flint crucial to warfare; 'flint-hearted' for unfeeling people uses the hardness; Flintstone is the cartoon's geological pun.
FLINT.joke: Flint made fire, then weapons, then a cartoon caveman — a complete arc of human ingenuity.

FLIRT.definition: To behave playfully romantically without serious intention; to briefly consider something.
FLIRT.origin: Origin uncertain; possibly from French 'fleureter' meaning 'to touch lightly, chat about'
FLIRT.evolution: The playful romantic sense and 'flirting with danger/disaster' both emerged in the 16th century; 'flirt with an idea' uses the non-committal engagement sense; both carry the sense of light, non-binding interaction.
FLIRT.joke: Flirting with disaster is what every interesting plan actually is before anyone calls it a strategy.

FLOAT.definition: To rest on or move on the surface of liquid; to move through air lightly; a decorated platform in a parade.
FLOAT.evolution: The buoyancy sense is original; 'float an idea' for proposing tentatively and 'float a stock' (IPO) are figurative extensions; a parade float is a wheeled decorated platform that 'floats' past the crowd.
FLOAT.joke: Floating an idea is the business version of trying to see if it sinks before you're on it.

FLOCK.definition: A number of birds, sheep, or goats; a congregation of people; to gather in a crowd.
FLOCK.origin: Old English 'flocc' meaning 'a band of people'
FLOCK.evolution: Originally a group of people; extended to sheep (a shepherd's flock) and then birds; 'birds of a feather flock together' is a 16th-century proverb; 'flock wallpaper' (textured) is a separate word from Old French.
FLOCK.joke: Birds of a feather flock together — which is why conference nametags were invented.

FLOOD.definition: A large overflow of water; an overwhelming quantity; to inundate.
FLOOD.origin: Old English 'flōd' meaning 'a flowing of water, flood'
FLOOD.evolution: A fundamental Old English water word; the biblical flood is the most famous reference; 'flood of calls', 'flood of emails', and 'flood the market' use the overwhelming-quantity metaphor.
FLOOD.joke: A flood of emails is the natural disaster that nobody declares a state of emergency for — yet.

FLOOR.definition: The lower surface of a room; a storey in a building; to baffle or stun completely.
FLOOR.evolution: The walking-surface sense is original; 'the floor' of a house represents the lowest level; 'floor someone' (to knock down or stun completely) and 'have the floor' (the right to speak) are figurative extensions.
FLOOR.joke: 'The floor is yours' means it's your turn — which is different from it being literally yours, though both require significant upkeep.

FLORA.definition: The plants of a particular region or period.
FLORA.origin: From Flora, the Roman goddess of flowers, from 'flos' meaning 'flower'
FLORA.evolution: Carl Linnaeus popularized its scientific use alongside 'fauna'; 'flora and fauna' became the standard natural-history pairing; 'floral' and 'florist' share the Latin root; 'Florence' means 'flowering'.
FLORA.joke: Flora is what scientists named plant life after the goddess of flowers — a rare case of taxonomy being romantic.

FLOSS.definition: Dental floss; soft silk thread; to use dental floss; a hip-hop dance move.
FLOSS.origin: From 'floss' meaning 'the rough silk on a cocoon', from French 'floche' meaning 'downy'
FLOSS.evolution: The soft-silk sense came first; dental floss (1815) named for its thread-like quality; the 'floss' dance move (2016) has no connection to dental hygiene except that both require a kind of practiced wrist action.
FLOSS.joke: Dental floss is the easiest health habit that almost nobody does consistently — and a dance move that proved otherwise.

FLOUR.definition: A powder made by grinding grain, used to make bread, cakes, and pasta.
FLOUR.origin: Middle English, same word as 'flower' — the finest part of the grain was called its 'flower'
FLOUR.evolution: 'Flour' and 'flower' were the same word until the 18th century; flour was the 'flower' (best part) of the wheat; the spelling diverged to distinguish the baking ingredient from the plant part.
FLOUR.joke: Flour and flower were once the same word — proof that language can split something delicate and something practical from the same source.

FLOUT.definition: To openly disregard a rule or convention.
FLOUT.origin: Possibly from Dutch 'fluiten' meaning 'to play the flute, hiss, jeer'
FLOUT.evolution: Often confused with 'flaunt' (to display ostentatiously); 'flout the rules' means to break them with contempt, while 'flaunt the rules' would mean to show them off, which is meaningless; the confusion is widespread.
FLOUT.joke: Flouting rules and flaunting rules are different — one takes the rules seriously enough to break them.

FLOWN.definition: Past participle of 'fly'; moved through the air; elapsed quickly.
FLOWN.evolution: 'Time has flown' is the most common figurative use; 'the bird has flown' for an escaped quarry is an old idiom; the irregular strong verb pattern (fly/flew/flown) is a hallmark of ancient Germanic roots.
FLOWN.joke: Time has flown — which is a charming way of saying it left without asking if you were ready.

FLUFF.definition: Soft fiber clusters; trivial or superficial content; to do something badly.
FLUFF.origin: Possibly from 'flue' meaning 'soft down'; related to Flemish 'vluwe'
FLUFF.evolution: The soft fiber sense is original; 'fluff' as trivial content (entertainment journalism) and 'to fluff a line' (to make an error while performing) are 20th-century uses; 'fluffed' shots are also photography errors.
FLUFF.joke: Fluff content is what you publish between the things that actually matter — and half the time it does better.

FLUID.definition: Able to flow like water; not fixed or stable; a liquid or gas.
FLUID.evolution: From the Latin flowing sense; 'fluid situation' for an unstable one and 'fluid movement' for graceful motion are common metaphors; 'gender fluid' is a 21st-century identity term using the flexible sense.
FLUID.joke: A fluid situation is stable enough to plan around but not stable enough to blame anyone for misplanning.

FLUKE.definition: An unlikely chance occurrence; a flatfish; the broad triangular part of an anchor; a parasitic worm.
FLUKE.origin: Several origins: the fish (Old English), the anchor (possibly same), the chance occurrence (19th century)
FLUKE.evolution: Multiple unrelated words converged; the 'lucky fluke' sense may come from billiards (an accidental successful shot); the flatfish and anchor-fluke are probably related by shape.
FLUKE.joke: A fluke success is both celebrated and held against you — the market is very efficient at irony.

FLUME.definition: An artificial channel for conveying water; a water slide at an amusement park.
FLUME.origin: Old French 'flum' meaning 'river', from Latin 'flumen' meaning 'river'
FLUME.evolution: From Latin river through Old French; the mining flume (for washing ore) is historical; the amusement park water slide sense is 20th century, named for its resemblance to the industrial channel.
FLUME.joke: A flume slide is the only engineering structure where screaming at the top is considered a positive review.

FLUNG.evolution: Irregular past tense of 'fling'; 'flung open' for doors and 'flung himself at' are standard idioms; the dramatic physicality of the word makes it a favorite in action writing.
FLUNG.joke: He flung the door open — a move that communication theory would describe as non-verbal and architecturally intensive.

FLUNK.definition: To fail an examination or course; to give a failing grade.
FLUNK.origin: American English, origin uncertain; possibly from 'funk' or dialectal 'flunk' meaning 'to back down'
FLUNK.evolution: American college slang from the early 19th century; 'flunk out' for failing to complete a course is the extended form; carries a sense of disappointed inevitability rather than dramatic failure.
FLUNK.joke: Flunking is what happens when the test finds out you and the syllabus were never properly introduced.

FLUSH.definition: Level with or even with a surface; (of a face) red; completely full of money; to clean with water.
FLUSH.evolution: Multiple senses converged; a flush toilet (1596, Sir John Harington) predates the flush financial sense; 'flush with success' uses both the red-faced excitement and the abundance sense; a flush in poker is all one suit.
FLUSH.joke: Being flush with cash is what happens right before you remember what you owe.

FLUTE.definition: A high-pitched woodwind instrument; a tall narrow champagne glass; a groove in a column.
FLUTE.origin: Old French 'flaüte', from Provençal 'flaüt', origin uncertain
FLUTE.evolution: The instrument sense is original; the architectural flute (groove in a column) named for the instrument's tubular shape; a champagne flute preserves the stem-column shape; 'fluted' for any grooved surface follows.
FLUTE.joke: A champagne flute is the architectural flute's more glamorous sibling — same shape, better occasion.

FLYER.definition: Alternative spelling of 'flier'; a person who flies; an advertising leaflet; a risky investment.
FLYER.evolution: Both 'flier' and 'flyer' are acceptable; 'take a flyer' (taking a financial risk) comes from speculative investment language; frequent flyer programs are a 1970s airline loyalty concept.
FLYER.joke: Taking a flyer on a startup is either vision or a donation to someone else's education.

FOAMY.definition: Full of or covered with foam; resembling foam.
FOAMY.evolution: The frothy/bubbly adjective from the Old English sea-foam sense; 'foamy latte' is a 21st-century compound that Chaucer would have found baffling but would probably have enjoyed.
FOAMY.joke: A foamy cappuccino is the coffee world's way of saying this will take longer to make and cost more — and it's correct about both.

FOCAL.definition: Relating to a focus or focal point; of central importance.
FOCAL.evolution: The Latin hearth-as-center-of-the-home gave 'focus'; 'focal point' in both photography and discussion uses the central-attention sense; 'focal length' is the optical measurement of lens magnification.
FOCAL.joke: A focal point is where all attention goes — which means whoever controls it controls the meeting.

FOCUS.definition: The center of interest or activity; the quality of producing clear images; to concentrate attention.
FOCUS.evolution: Johannes Kepler introduced the optical sense (1604), using the Latin hearth as the gathering point of light; all modern senses of focused attention derive from this optical metaphor.
FOCUS.joke: Focus is the productivity skill that everyone claims and the phone immediately refutes.

FOGGY.definition: Full of or covered with fog; unclear or vague.
FOGGY.origin: From 'fog' (possibly Scandinavian, related to Danish 'fog' meaning 'spray, shower') plus '-y'
FOGGY.evolution: The weather sense is primary; 'not the foggiest' (British for 'no idea at all') and 'foggy memory' use the obscured-visibility metaphor; 'foggy bottom' is the nickname for the US State Department.
FOGGY.joke: Not having the foggiest is nature's most accurate description of most opinions on most Mondays.

FOIST.definition: To impose an unwelcome thing on someone; to pass off as genuine.
FOIST.origin: Possibly from Dutch 'vuisten' meaning 'to take in the fist'
FOIST.evolution: From the sleight-of-hand cheating sense; 'foisting something off on someone' is the standard construction; implies both deception and the unwillingness of the recipient.
FOIST.joke: To foist is to volunteer someone else for the thing they didn't ask for — a classic management skill.

FOLIO.definition: A large sheet of paper folded once to make two leaves; a page number in a book; the size of such a paper.
FOLIO.origin: Latin 'folio' meaning 'a leaf of paper', ablative of 'folium'
FOLIO.evolution: From the leaf metaphor; Shakespeare's First Folio (1623) is the most famous; 'in folio' was the standard way to describe a large-format book; the word persists in publishing and archival contexts.
FOLIO.joke: Shakespeare's First Folio is proof that some things only become important after everyone involved has died.

FOLLY.definition: Lack of good sense; foolishness; a costly ornamental building; a failed or misguided project.
FOLLY.evolution: From the French fool/madness; 'Seward's Folly' (Alaska purchase, 1867) became famous as a mockery that became gold-bearing; architectural follies (ornamental structures with no practical use) became fashionable in 18th-century gardens.
FOLLY.joke: Every folly looks like vision until it looks like an ornamental tower in someone's garden.

FORAY.definition: A sudden attack into enemy territory; a brief but spirited attempt at something new.
FORAY.evolution: From the foraging raid sense; 'make a foray into' something new uses the bold-but-brief-venture sense; both military raids and culinary adventures share the same word.
FORAY.joke: A foray into cooking is when confidence outpaces skill and the smoke alarm becomes the recipe critic.

FORCE.definition: Strength or power exerted; an organized body of people; to make someone do something unwillingly.
FORCE.evolution: From the Latin strength; 'the Force' (Star Wars, 1977) elevated physics vocabulary to mythology; 'in force' (in large numbers), 'force majeure' (overwhelming event), and 'workforce' show its range.
FORCE.joke: 'May the force be with you' is the only physics lesson with a perfect box office record.

FORGE.definition: To make a copy of something fraudulently; to heat and shape metal; to move forward with determination; a furnace for metalworking.
FORGE.origin: Old French 'forge' meaning 'smithy', from Latin 'fabrica' meaning 'workshop'
FORGE.evolution: The metal-shaping workshop sense is original; 'forge a relationship' (to build) and 'forge a signature' (to fake) both use the crafting sense, one honest and one not.
FORGE.joke: To forge ahead and to forge a signature are the same word doing very different moral work.

FORGO.definition: To go without; to abstain from; to refrain from.
FORGO.origin: Old English 'forgān' meaning 'to pass over, abstain from'
FORGO.evolution: Often confused with 'forego' (to go before); 'forgo dessert' means to skip it; the prefix 'for-' implies going without rather than going before.
FORGO.joke: To forgo dessert is a moral stance that collapses under the right chocolate presentation.

FORTE.definition: A thing at which someone excels; the strongest part of a sword blade; loud (musical direction).
FORTE.origin: French 'fort' meaning 'strong', and Italian 'forte' meaning 'strong, loud'
FORTE.evolution: Two words merged: the English 'forte' (one's strength) is from French, and the musical direction 'forte' (loud) is from Italian; they're pronounced differently — 'fort' for the strength sense, 'for-tay' for music — though the latter has largely won.
FORTE.joke: Knowing someone's forte is 90% of managing them — and 100% of surviving family dinners.

FORTH.definition: Out from a starting point; forward; onward.
FORTH.evolution: 'Go forth' is a biblical command; 'back and forth', 'and so forth', and 'bring forth' preserve the directional sense; 'henceforth' (from this point forward) uses the forward-time sense.
FORTH.joke: Go forth and prosper — but bring an umbrella, because the weather also has its own plans.

FORTY.evolution: A fundamental number; 'forty winks' for a short nap and 'life begins at forty' are cultural clichés; the 'forty days and nights' of the biblical flood made forty synonymous with a long test.
FORTY.joke: Life begins at forty — which is what everyone tells you when you turn forty and aren't sure about it.

FORUM.definition: A meeting or medium for open discussion; an online discussion board; the public square of ancient Rome.
FORUM.evolution: The Roman forum was the center of public life; English borrowed it for any public meeting space; online forums (bulletin boards) are the digital descendants; 'forum shopping' is a legal term for choosing a favorable jurisdiction.
FORUM.joke: An online forum is the Roman forum with better reach and considerably worse moderation.

FOUND.definition: Past tense of 'find'; to establish or set up; to melt metal for casting.
FOUND.origin: Three origins: Old English 'findan' (find); Old French 'fonder' from Latin 'fundare' (establish); Old French 'fondre' from Latin 'fundere' (melt)
FOUND.evolution: Three distinct words merged in spelling; 'found a company', 'found a treasure', and 'found metal' are etymologically different; 'founding fathers' uses the establishment sense.
FOUND.joke: Found is a word doing three different jobs — finding, building, and melting — and everyone assumes they know which one you mean.

FOYER.definition: An entrance hall in a hotel or theater; a lobby.
FOYER.origin: French 'foyer' meaning 'hearth, home, place for warming oneself', from Latin 'focus' meaning 'hearth'
FOYER.evolution: From the backstage actors' waiting room in French theaters (the warming room near the stage); extended to the entrance hall of any large building; the Latin 'focus' (hearth as center) gives both 'foyer' and 'focus'.
FOYER.joke: A foyer is where you decide whether you're staying or whether you made a mistake about the address.

FRAIL.definition: Weak; easily damaged or broken; (of a person) physically weak.
FRAIL.evolution: From the Latin fragile/breakable; 'frail' implies both physical weakness and moral susceptibility in older usage ('frail of character'); 'fragile' and 'frail' are doublets from the same root.
FRAIL.joke: Frail is fragile with better vocabulary and slightly more dignity.

FRAME.definition: A rigid structure surrounding a picture or window; the underlying structure; to falsely incriminate someone.
FRAME.origin: Old English 'framian' meaning 'to benefit, progress', from 'fram' meaning 'forward'
FRAME.evolution: The forward-progress sense gave the structural sense; a house frame is the forward-structure that holds it; a 'frame-up' for a false incrimination uses the idea of constructing a false case around someone.
FRAME.joke: A frame-up is the one construction project where the goal is to ensure it falls on one specific person.

FRANK.definition: Open and honest in expression; free from pretense.
FRANK.origin: Old French 'franc' meaning 'free', from a Germanic root — the Franks were the free ruling people
FRANK.evolution: The Germanic Franks (Charlemagne's people) were the free citizens; 'to be frank' means to speak with the freedom of a free person; 'frankly' is now the word you say just before saying something someone doesn't want to hear.
FRANK.joke: 'Frankly speaking' is the verbal equivalent of putting on safety glasses — you know what's coming.

FRAUD.definition: Intentional deception for financial or personal gain; a person who makes false claims.
FRAUD.origin: Old French 'fraude', from Latin 'fraus' meaning 'deceit, injury'
FRAUD.evolution: From the Latin harm-through-deception; 'commit fraud', 'fraud prevention', and 'he's a fraud' cover the crime, the defense, and the character judgment; 'fraudulent' is the adjective.
FRAUD.joke: A fraud is someone who convinced you they were worth your trust before you had enough data — which is just early-stage venture capital.

FREAK.definition: A person who is considered unusual; an enthusiast; an abnormal occurrence; to become very disturbed.
FREAK.origin: Origin uncertain; possibly from Old English 'frician' meaning 'to dance'
FREAK.evolution: The dancing/whimsical sense may be original; the abnormality sense developed in the 19th-century freak show era; 'control freak', 'fitness freak', and 'freak accident' show its range from enthusiast to unusual event.
FREAK.joke: A control freak is just someone who noticed that leaving things to chance rarely improves them.

FREED.definition: Past tense of 'free'; released from captivity or restriction.
FREED.evolution: From the Old English love/free connection — to free was to hold dear; 'freed the slaves' (emancipation), 'freed from obligation', and 'freed up time' show the liberation range.
FREED.joke: Being freed from your calendar for a day is the closest modern luxury to actual emancipation.

FREER.definition: Comparative of 'free'; having more liberty or fewer constraints.
FREER.evolution: The comparative of 'free'; 'a freer society' and 'feel freer to speak' are standard uses; philosophers have debated whether being 'freer' is meaningfully different from being 'free', to diminishing audiences.
FREER.joke: A freer market is what everyone wants until it turns out the market has different opinions about what things should cost.

FRESH.definition: Not previously known; not processed or preserved; insolent (informal).
FRESH.evolution: The original sense was not-salty (fresh water vs. salt water); the 'new and unprocessed' sense followed; 'fresh' for insolence is American slang from the 19th century; 'start fresh' preserves the clean-slate sense.
FRESH.joke: A fresh start is just the moment before all the previous complications catch up with you again.

FRIAR.definition: A member of a religious order, especially a mendicant order such as the Franciscans or Dominicans.
FRIAR.evolution: From the Latin for brother; Friar Tuck (Robin Hood) and Friar Lawrence (Romeo and Juliet) are the most famous fictional friars; friars differ from monks in being active in the community rather than cloistered.
FRIAR.joke: A friar is a monk who got a mobile working arrangement approved.

FRIED.definition: Cooked in hot oil; exhausted (informal); drunk (slang).
FRIED.origin: From 'fry' (Old English 'frigan' meaning 'to fry') plus past participle suffix
FRIED.evolution: The cooking sense is original; 'fried' for exhaustion uses the burned-out metaphor; 'brain-fried' and 'completely fried' are informal compounds; the American South elevated frying to a culinary art form.
FRIED.joke: Fried food is delicious evidence that a hot bath solves most culinary problems.

FRILL.definition: A strip of pleated or ruffled fabric; an unnecessary extra feature or luxury.
FRILL.evolution: From the curled fabric; 'no frills' for basic without extras is a common descriptor for budget services; 'frilly' for excessively ornate uses the decorative excess sense.
FRILL.joke: No frills is what airlines call removing everything that made the experience not feel like a sardine can.

FRISK.definition: To search someone by running hands over their clothing; to skip or leap playfully.
FRISK.origin: Old French 'fresque' meaning 'lively, merry', from a Germanic root
FRISK.evolution: The lively-and-merry sense is original; the police-search sense is 18th century, using the quick-handling sense; 'frisky' for playful energy preserves the original lighthearted sense.
FRISK.joke: Being frisked is the airport's way of saying 'we trust you, but only about 73% of the way through this process.'

FRITZ.definition: On the fritz — not working properly (American informal).
FRITZ.origin: Origin disputed; possibly from 'Fritz' as a German personal name (contemptuous usage in WWI) or from 'frizzle/frizz'
FRITZ.evolution: American slang with an uncertain etymology; 'on the fritz' for malfunctioning has been in use since at least 1902; possibly wartime anti-German slang that outlasted its original context.
FRITZ.joke: On the fritz is what the manual calls 'consult a qualified technician' in a more honest dialect.

FROCK.definition: A dress; a monk's or priest's habit; to invest someone with priestly authority.
FROCK.evolution: The ecclesiastical garment sense is original; extended to women's dresses in the 18th century; 'defrock' (to remove priestly status) uses the robe metaphor; 'frock coat' is a 19th-century formal men's coat.
FROCK.joke: To defrock a priest is to remove the metaphorical garment of office — the actual clothes are theirs to keep.

FROND.definition: A large divided leaf of a palm, fern, or similar plant.
FROND.evolution: A botanical term from Latin; ferns and palms both have fronds; the word suggests lush tropical or prehistoric vegetation; 'fronded' is the rare adjective.
FROND.joke: A palm frond is proof that nature invented the fan long before anyone had to attend a meeting.

FRONT.definition: The forward-facing part; the most prominent position; a political movement used as a cover; weather system.
FRONT.evolution: From the Roman forehead/face; 'front line', 'front page', and 'cold front' use different aspects; a 'front' for illegal activity uses the facing/false-face sense; 'putting up a front' for maintaining appearances follows.
FRONT.joke: A weather front and a criminal front share one key feature — both bring trouble without adequate warning.

FROST.definition: Deposits of ice crystals formed by condensation below freezing; to cover with frost or icing.
FROST.evolution: A fundamental weather word; 'Jack Frost' personified cold weather since the 18th century; 'frosting' a cake uses the white-powdery resemblance; Robert Frost's poetry made the name synonymous with winter imagery.
FROST.joke: Jack Frost is the one weather phenomenon that got a charming name despite ruining windshields every morning.

FROTH.definition: A mass of small bubbles; insubstantial talk or activity; to bubble up.
FROTH.evolution: From Norse foam; 'frothy' for insubstantial content and 'frothing at the mouth' for extreme anger use different ends of the bubble spectrum; 'frothed milk' is a café compound.
FROTH.joke: Frothy content is like cappuccino foam — impressive to look at, but you're still hungry afterward.

FROWN.definition: To furrow one's brows to show displeasure; to look disapprovingly at.
FROWN.origin: Old French 'froignier' meaning 'to show displeasure', of uncertain origin
FROWN.evolution: From the displeasure expression; 'to frown upon' for disapproving of something is the standard idiom; 'frowned at' and 'frowned upon' are slightly different — one personal, one social.
FROWN.joke: Frowned upon is society's version of the warning sticker — technically advisory, socially binding.

FROZE.definition: Past tense of 'freeze'; became hardened by cold; became fixed or unable to move.
FROZE.evolution: Irregular past tense; 'froze in his tracks' for sudden paralysis and 'the screen froze' for software failure both use the inability-to-move sense.
FROZE.joke: The screen froze at the worst possible moment — which is the only moment screens ever choose.

FRUIT.definition: The sweet edible product of a plant; the result of work or activity.
FRUIT.origin: Old French 'fruit', from Latin 'fructus' meaning 'enjoyment, fruit of the earth'
FRUIT.evolution: From the Latin enjoyment/produce; 'the fruits of one's labor' uses the reward metaphor; 'bear fruit' for producing results is biblical; 'fruitful' for productive and 'fruitless' for unproductive are the standard antonyms.
FRUIT.joke: Fruitless effort is nature's way of saying the labor was perfectly fine — the tree just had other plans.

FUDGE.definition: A soft candy made from sugar and butter; to present inaccurately; to avoid making a clear decision.
FUDGE.origin: Origin uncertain; possibly from 'fadge' meaning 'to fit' or a personal name; the confection may be named separately
FUDGE.evolution: 'Fudge!' as an exclamation of disappointment predates the candy; 'to fudge' for falsifying data or evading issues is 19th century; the candy sense is 19th-century American; the meanings appear to be coincidentally related.
FUDGE.joke: Fudging the numbers is what happens when the numbers weren't prepared to support the conclusion.

FUGUE.definition: A polyphonic musical composition where a theme is introduced and developed by different voices; a state of confusion or amnesia.
FUGUE.origin: French 'fugue', from Italian 'fuga' meaning 'flight', from Latin 'fuga'
FUGUE.evolution: The musical form uses the 'flight' metaphor — themes pursue each other; Bach's fugues are the architectural standard; 'fugue state' (psychological dissociation) uses the mental-flight sense.
FUGUE.joke: A fugue state is the brain's way of solving the problem of being present by simply leaving.

FULLY.evolution: 'Fully aware', 'fully committed', and 'fully loaded' are common compounds; the word adds emphasis to completeness; 'fully operational' is the Star Wars Death Star's most unfortunate boast.
FULLY.joke: 'Fully committed' is what you say right before the project scope changes for the third time.

FUNGI.definition: Plural of fungus; organisms including mushrooms, molds, and yeasts.
FUNGI.evolution: Latin plural used directly; fungi are their own kingdom, distinct from plants and animals; 'fungicide' and 'fungal' are derivatives; mycology is the study of fungi; 'fun guy' is the obligatory pronunciation joke.
FUNGI.joke: Fungi are the original recyclers — they've been composting longer than it was fashionable.

FUNKY.definition: Strongly rhythmic and earthy in musical style; (informal) having a strong smell; unconventionally stylish.
FUNKY.origin: From 'funk' — originally meaning 'tobacco smoke or odor', possibly from French 'funquer' meaning 'to smoke'
FUNKY.evolution: 'Funk' started as a bad smell; in jazz, 'funky' meant a raw, soulful style (linking earthiness to music); James Brown and Sly Stone defined 'funky' as a groove genre; the style sense now often dominates the smell sense.
FUNKY.joke: Funky music is the one category where smelling slightly like the room is considered authentic.

FUNNY.definition: Causing laughter; strange or puzzling; not quite right.
FUNNY.evolution: From the amusing sense; the ambiguity between 'funny ha-ha' and 'funny peculiar' has been a joke setup since 1937 (Ian Hay); 'funny business' and 'something funny going on' use the suspicious sense.
FUNNY.joke: Funny is context-dependent — which is why the same joke works at the party and destroys the job interview.

FUROR.definition: An outbreak of public anger or excitement; a great stir.
FUROR.origin: Latin 'furor' meaning 'madness, rage', from 'furere' meaning 'to be mad'
FUROR.evolution: From the Latin madness/rage; 'a furor erupted' and 'cause a furor' are the standard constructions; more intense than 'controversy' and implies widespread public emotional response.
FUROR.joke: A furor subsides in about 72 hours — which is both the news cycle and the attention span.

FURRY.definition: Covered with fur; resembling fur; relating to anthropomorphic animal characters.
FURRY.evolution: The animal-fur sense is original; 'furry animals' and 'furry dice' are separate applications; 'furry' as a noun for someone interested in anthropomorphic animal characters is a 21st-century fandom term.
FURRY.joke: Furry dice are a driving accessory that say 'I have an interesting relationship with both luck and rear visibility.'

FUSSY.definition: Hard to please; showing excessive concern for small details; excessively elaborate.
FUSSY.evolution: 'Fuss' for needless worry appeared in the 18th century; 'fussy eater', 'fussy dresser', and 'fussy decor' all use the excessive-detail-attention sense; implies both difficulty and over-care.
FUSSY.joke: A fussy eater is someone whose palate is operating in a different time zone from the kitchen.

FUZZY.definition: Covered with or having a fluffy or frizzy texture; not clear or distinct; vague.
FUZZY.evolution: The soft-textured sense is original; 'fuzzy logic' (1965, Lotfi Zadeh) is a mathematical system dealing with degrees of truth rather than binary yes/no; 'fuzzy memory' uses the unclear sense.
FUZZY.joke: Fuzzy logic is the mathematical acknowledgment that most decisions are made in the grey zone between yes and no.

GAFFE.definition: A socially awkward blunder or embarrassing mistake, especially one made in public.
GAFFE.evolution: Originally referred to a boathook used to catch things; came to mean catching oneself in an embarrassing error.
GAFFE.joke: Making a gaffe at a formal dinner is just society's way of keeping you hooked.

GAILY.definition: In a cheerful, light-hearted, or showy manner.
GAILY.origin: Old French 'gai' (merry, joyful), of Germanic origin
GAILY.evolution: Retained its core sense of cheerfulness while also acquiring connotations of bright colors and festive display.
GAILY.joke: She dressed gaily for the funeral — nobody told her 'festive' had fine print.

GAMER.definition: A person who plays video games or participates in gaming culture.
GAMER.evolution: Expanded from general player of games to specifically describe video game enthusiasts as digital gaming became a distinct cultural phenomenon.
GAMER.joke: A gamer's diet: Red Bull, chips, and respawn points.

GAMMA.definition: The third letter of the Greek alphabet; also a unit of magnetic field strength.
GAMMA.evolution: Moved from the Greek alphabet into scientific notation as a symbol for radiation and electromagnetic phenomena.
GAMMA.joke: Gamma radiation: the only glow-up that turns you green and angry.

GAMUT.definition: The complete range or scope of something.
GAMUT.origin: Medieval Latin 'gamma ut', from 'gamma' (lowest note) + 'ut' (first note of the scale)
GAMUT.evolution: Originally a musical term for the full scale of notes; extended metaphorically to mean any complete range of anything.
GAMUT.joke: Run the gamut from A to Z — because sometimes the alphabet isn't enough drama.

GASSY.definition: Full of or producing gas; also informal for talkative or boastful.
GASSY.origin: Dutch 'gas' (coined by chemist van Helmont) + suffix -y
GASSY.evolution: Chemical term that found its way into casual speech to describe both physical gas and windy verbosity.
GASSY.joke: Gassy politicians: they emit hot air and nobody can pinpoint the source.

GAUDY.definition: Extravagantly bright or showy in a way considered tasteless.
GAUDY.origin: Possibly from Latin 'gaudium' (joy) or from 'gaude' (rejoice); also possibly from 'gaud' (trinket)
GAUDY.evolution: Shifted from simply 'joyful' to meaning excessively ornate or garish, reflecting changing tastes in what constituted appropriate display.
GAUDY.joke: Gaudy Christmas sweaters: joy translated directly into blinding wool.

GAUGE.definition: An instrument for measuring or a standard dimension or measurement.
GAUGE.origin: Old Northern French 'gauge' (measuring rod), of uncertain Germanic origin
GAUGE.evolution: From a physical measuring stick to any instrument of measurement; now also used metaphorically to mean assess or estimate.
GAUGE.joke: A good gauge of patience: assembling furniture without the instructions.

GAUNT.definition: Lean and haggard, especially as a result of suffering, hunger, or age.
GAUNT.origin: Middle English, possibly of Scandinavian origin; related to Norwegian 'gand' (thin stick)
GAUNT.evolution: Originally described extreme thinness; evolved to carry connotations of grimness and hardship beyond mere physical leanness.
GAUNT.joke: Gaunt: when your skeleton is showing off without your permission.

GAUZE.definition: A thin, transparent fabric with an open weave; also a fine mesh used in medical dressings.
GAUZE.origin: French 'gaze', possibly from Gaza (city in Palestine), a medieval textile center
GAUZE.evolution: Named after the city where it was originally produced; spread as a fabric term then expanded to any thin, mesh-like material.
GAUZE.joke: Gauze: so thin it's basically a fabric suggestion.

GAVEL.definition: A small mallet used by a judge or auctioneer to call for order or signal a decision.
GAVEL.origin: Origin uncertain; possibly related to Old English 'gafoluc' (tribute), suggesting authority
GAVEL.evolution: Emerged as a symbol of legal and auction authority; the act of gaveling became synonymous with finality and official decisions.
GAVEL.joke: The gavel: a tiny hammer with enormous authority issues.

GAWKY.definition: Nervously clumsy and awkward, especially in physical movement.
GAWKY.origin: Dialectal 'gawk' (fool, stare stupidly) + suffix -y; possibly from Old Norse 'gá' (heed)
GAWKY.evolution: From gawk (to stare foolishly) into an adjective describing the awkward physicality of someone who doesn't know where to put their limbs.
GAWKY.joke: Gawky teenagers: all elbows and no instruction manual.

GAYER.definition: Comparative form of gay; more joyful or more brightly colored.
GAYER.origin: Old French 'gai' (merry, joyful), of Germanic origin
GAYER.evolution: Comparative of gay in its original sense of joyful; the word's modern meanings added additional layers of comparison.
GAYER.joke: The gayer the party decorations, the less parking there will be.

GAYLY.definition: Variant spelling of gaily; in a cheerful or brightly decorated manner.
GAYLY.evolution: Variant of gaily that persisted in literary usage; both forms describe the same cheerful, festive quality.
GAYLY.joke: Gayly: the way autocorrect refuses to spell 'gaily' in a business email.

GAZER.definition: One who gazes; a person who stares intently or contemplatively.
GAZER.origin: Middle English 'gazen' (to stare) + agent suffix -er; possibly from Old Norse 'gá' (heed)
GAZER.evolution: From the verb gaze; used for stargazers and daydreamers alike — anyone whose eyes fix on something with intent wonder.
GAZER.joke: A gazer at an art museum: paying admission to look confused professionally.

GECKO.definition: A small, padded-footed lizard known for its ability to climb smooth surfaces and its vocalizations.
GECKO.origin: Malay 'gekok', an imitation of its call
GECKO.evolution: Borrowed from Malay onomatopoeia into English as a straightforward animal name; now also culturally associated with insurance commercials.
GECKO.joke: The gecko saves 15% on car insurance — and 100% on footwear.

GEEKY.definition: Characterized by obsessive enthusiasm for niche subjects, especially technology or science fiction.
GEEKY.origin: Carnival slang 'geek' (performer of bizarre acts) + suffix -y; origin obscure, possibly Low German 'geck' (fool)
GEEKY.evolution: Carnival freakshow term rehabilitated by computing culture; 'geeky' shifted from insulting to a badge of honor among tech enthusiasts.
GEEKY.joke: Geeky hobbies: where memorizing Star Wars lore is called 'studying history.'

GEESE.definition: Plural of goose; large waterfowl known for their aggressive territorial behavior.
GEESE.origin: Old English 'gēs', plural of 'gōs' (goose); from Proto-Germanic
GEESE.evolution: Maintained the ancient irregular plural form; geese retained their reputation as farm guardians and aggressive park inhabitants across millennia.
GEESE.joke: Geese: nature's bouncers, working parks for free and expecting nothing in return.

GENIE.definition: A spirit in Islamic mythology capable of granting wishes when summoned, typically from a bottle or lamp.
GENIE.evolution: Arabic folkloric concept entered European literature through Arabian Nights translations; popularized in Western culture through media adaptations.
GENIE.joke: A genie's fine print: unlimited wishes, limited reading of what 'unlimited' means.

GENRE.definition: A category of artistic, literary, or musical composition characterized by a particular style or form.
GENRE.evolution: Borrowed from French art criticism into general English usage; now applied to everything from novels to movies to music as a classification system.
GENRE.joke: Genre: the box they put art in so it can complain about being in a box.

GHOST.definition: The spirit of a dead person believed to appear to the living; also to suddenly cut off contact with someone.
GHOST.evolution: Originally meant 'breath, spirit, soul'; narrowed to supernatural apparition; in modern slang, 'to ghost' means to vanish from someone's life without explanation.
GHOST.joke: Ghosting someone: the digital age's way of haunting the living.

GHOUL.definition: An evil spirit or monster that feeds on corpses; a person morbidly interested in death or disaster.
GHOUL.origin: Arabic 'ghūl' (demon that robs graves), from 'ghāla' (to seize)
GHOUL.evolution: Arabic folkloric creature entered English through Arabian Nights; extended to describe anyone with an unhealthy fascination with death or tragedy.
GHOUL.joke: A ghoul's diet is technically paleo — very, very paleo.

GIANT.definition: A mythical being of great size and strength; any person or thing of extraordinary size.
GIANT.origin: Old French 'geant', from Latin 'gigas', from Greek 'gigas' (giant, one of the Gigantes)
GIANT.evolution: From Greek mythology's Gigantes who battled the gods; became a general term for anything oversized in both literal and figurative senses.
GIANT.joke: Giants: what happens when nature skips the fine-tuning stage.

GIDDY.definition: Having a sensation of whirling; light-headed or dizzy; also excitedly frivolous.
GIDDY.origin: Old English 'gydig' (insane, foolish), from Proto-Germanic *gudiga- (possessed by a god)
GIDDY.evolution: Originally meant divinely possessed or insane; softened over centuries to light-headed dizziness, then to excited frivolity.
GIDDY.joke: Giddy: when your joy goes full spin cycle.

GIPSY.definition: Variant spelling of Gypsy; a member of the Romani people; also a free-spirited, wandering person.
GIPSY.origin: Shortening of 'Egyptian', mistakenly thought to be the origin of the Romani people
GIPSY.evolution: Based on a misnomer that persisted for centuries; the Romani actually originated in northern India, not Egypt. Term now considered offensive in many contexts.
GIPSY.joke: Gipsy: proof that a geographic misunderstanding can stick around for 500 years.

GIRLY.definition: Characteristic of or appropriate to a girl; often used to describe things considered stereotypically feminine.
GIRLY.evolution: From a neutral descriptor to a sometimes diminutive or pejorative term; its cultural weight has shifted considerably with changing gender norms.
GIRLY.joke: Girly: the adjective that tells you more about the speaker than the subject.

GIRTH.definition: The measurement around something, especially a person's waist or a tree trunk; also a band under a horse's belly.
GIRTH.evolution: From a horse's belly-band to a general measurement term; retained its circular sense across both equestrian and anatomical usage.
GIRTH.joke: Girth: the polite word your tailor uses when measurements get personal.

GIVEN.definition: Specified or stated; also inclined or disposed toward something.
GIVEN.origin: Old English 'gifan' (to give); past participle used as adjective
GIVEN.evolution: The past participle of 'give' evolved into an adjective and preposition; 'given that' became a standard logical connector in argument.
GIVEN.joke: Given: math's way of saying 'just trust me on this one.'

GIVER.definition: A person who gives, donates, or bestows something.
GIVER.evolution: Simple agent noun from 'give'; remained straightforward in meaning while acquiring positive moral connotations of generosity.
GIVER.joke: A gift giver's nightmare: the receipt you definitely shouldn't need.

GLADE.definition: An open space in a forest or woodland, cleared or naturally treeless.
GLADE.origin: Middle English, possibly related to 'glad' (bright) or 'glide' (smooth area); exact origin uncertain
GLADE.evolution: Possibly from the brightness of open woodland clearings; became a standard poetic and literary term for peaceful forest openings.
GLADE.joke: A forest glade: where trees politely make room for your picnic.

GLAND.definition: An organ in the body that secretes substances for use in the body or for elimination.
GLAND.evolution: Named for the acorn-like shape of many glands; spread through anatomical vocabulary to describe any secretory organ.
GLAND.joke: Glands: the body's silent factories, working overtime so you can panic appropriately.

GLARE.definition: A fierce or angry stare; also intense dazzling light.
GLARE.origin: Middle Low German 'glaren' (to gleam, stare); related to Old English 'glær' (amber)
GLARE.evolution: Both meanings — angry stare and blinding light — trace to the idea of something unbearably intense; the eyes and the sun share the same glare.
GLARE.joke: The glare of a disappointed parent: 10,000 lumens of pure wordless judgment.

GLASS.definition: A hard, transparent material made by fusing silica; also a drinking vessel made of this material.
GLASS.origin: Old English 'glæs', from Proto-Germanic *glasaz; related to 'gleam'
GLASS.evolution: From the transparent material to the vessel to metaphors for fragility and clarity; 'glass ceiling' and 'glass half full' show its metaphoric range.
GLASS.joke: A glass half empty is just a pessimist doing portion control.

GLAZE.definition: To fit or cover with glass; to coat with a glossy substance; also said of eyes becoming unfocused.
GLAZE.origin: Middle English 'glasen' (to furnish with glass), from 'glas' (glass)
GLAZE.evolution: From installing glass windows to coating ceramics and pastries; the 'glazed eyes' metaphor captures the same smooth, unfocused quality.
GLAZE.joke: Glazed eyes during a meeting: your brain installing its screensaver.

GLEAM.definition: A faint or brief light; a glimmer of something abstract like hope or intelligence.
GLEAM.evolution: From physical brightness to metaphorical glimmers; 'a gleam in someone's eye' became shorthand for a nascent idea or mischievous intent.
GLEAM.joke: A gleam in your eye: the universe's tiniest flashlight for big ideas.

GLEAN.definition: To gather information or material bit by bit; originally to collect leftover crops after a harvest.
GLEAN.origin: Old French 'glener', from Late Latin 'glenare' (to glean); possibly of Celtic origin
GLEAN.evolution: Agricultural term for gathering fallen grain after reapers passed; extended to any careful collection of small pieces of knowledge or material.
GLEAN.joke: Gleaning wisdom from meetings: harvesting leftover sense after the agenda passes.

GLIDE.definition: To move smoothly and effortlessly; to fly without engine power using air currents.
GLIDE.evolution: From smooth physical movement to aeronautical flight; retained the sense of effortless, frictionless motion in both literal and figurative use.
GLIDE.joke: Gliding through life: the adult equivalent of sliding down a banister.

GLINT.definition: A small flash of light; a tiny sign of a quality or feeling.
GLINT.origin: Alteration of dialectal 'glent' (to glint); from Old Norse 'gletta' (to peep)
GLINT.evolution: From a brief flash of light to a metaphor for a tiny visible trace of personality or feeling; 'a glint of mischief' is a common figurative use.
GLINT.joke: A glint: the universe's way of teasing you with just enough light to stub your toe.

GLOAT.definition: To feel or express great self-satisfaction, especially at another's misfortune.
GLOAT.origin: Origin obscure; possibly from Old Norse 'glotta' (to grin scornfully)
GLOAT.evolution: Moved from general gazing with pleasure to specifically smug satisfaction over rivals' failures; gloating became a recognized social transgression.
GLOAT.joke: Gloating: the victory lap nobody asked you to do.

GLOBE.definition: A spherical object; the Earth; also a spherical map of the world.
GLOBE.origin: Latin 'globus' (sphere, ball), possibly related to 'glomus' (ball of yarn)
GLOBE.evolution: From a general sphere to specifically the Earth; 'global' extended to mean worldwide, and globalization carried this to planetary-scale interconnection.
GLOBE.joke: A globe: the only spinning thing in the classroom that was actually educational.

GLOOM.definition: Darkness or partial darkness; a feeling of depression or despondency.
GLOOM.origin: Middle Low German 'glum' (to look gloomy); or possibly from Old Norse 'glúmr' (murky)
GLOOM.evolution: Physical darkness and emotional darkness merged early; 'doom and gloom' became a fixed expression capturing both literal and existential darkness.
GLOOM.joke: Gloom: the aesthetic you didn't choose but your weather imposed anyway.

GLORY.definition: High renown or honor won by notable achievements; magnificence or great beauty.
GLORY.origin: Old French 'glorie', from Latin 'gloria' (fame, renown, praise)
GLORY.evolution: From Roman concepts of public honor to religious transcendence; 'glory' straddled military triumph and divine radiance across cultures.
GLORY.joke: Basking in glory: sunbathing in someone else's achievements since antiquity.

GLOSS.definition: A shiny appearance on a surface; also a brief explanation of a difficult word or expression.
GLOSS.origin: Two separate sources: 'gloss' (shine) from Old Norse 'glossi'; 'gloss' (explanation) from Greek 'glōssa' (tongue, language)
GLOSS.evolution: Two unrelated words merged in spelling; surface shine and explanatory notes share a name, both adding something on top of what's underneath.
GLOSS.joke: Glossing over problems: the professional name for a high-shine finish on bad news.

GLOVE.definition: A covering for the hand with separate parts for each finger.
GLOVE.origin: Old English 'glōf', from Proto-Germanic *galōfō; possibly from *ga- + *lōfō (palm of hand)
GLOVE.evolution: From simple hand coverings to symbols of challenge (throwing down the gauntlet) to specialized protective gear; gloves still carry ceremonial weight.
GLOVE.joke: The glove: humanity's way of saying 'I want to touch things but maintain deniability.'

GLYPH.definition: A carved or inscribed symbol or figure; in computing, a graphical symbol representing a character.
GLYPH.evolution: From physical carving in stone to digital typography; glyphs now include every tiny pictogram on your phone's emoji keyboard.
GLYPH.joke: A glyph: ancient graffiti that became a UNESCO heritage site.

GNASH.definition: To grind the teeth together, typically as a sign of anger or pain.
GNASH.origin: Middle English, possibly from Old Norse 'gnesta' (to gnash); imitative origin
GNASH.evolution: Onomatopoeic in feel; 'gnashing of teeth' became a biblical expression for anguish, cementing its association with extreme distress.
GNASH.joke: Gnashing teeth: when your frustration decides to express itself through dental fitness.

GNOME.definition: A small mythological creature that lives underground; also a brief pithy saying.
GNOME.origin: New Latin 'gnomus', coined by Paracelsus (16th century); possibly from Greek 'gnōmē' (thought, judgment)
GNOME.evolution: Coined by alchemist Paracelsus for earth elementals; later became garden ornaments and in computing, a desktop environment.
GNOME.joke: Garden gnomes: earth elementals who retired and got into property decoration.

GODLY.definition: Devoutly religious; conforming to the laws and wishes of a deity.
GODLY.evolution: Shifted from 'divine, resembling a god' to 'pious, following God's laws'; the humility of following rather than being became central.
GODLY.joke: Godly patience: the kind that only gets tested on your worst driving days.

GOING.definition: The act of moving or traveling; the condition of a surface for traveling over.
GOING.origin: Old English 'gān' (to go) + present participle suffix -ing
GOING.evolution: Present participle nominalized; 'the going gets tough' became proverbial, and 'going concern' entered business vocabulary for an active enterprise.
GOING.joke: 'The going gets tough' — as if the going had a choice in the matter.

GOLEM.definition: A creature in Jewish folklore animated from clay by mystical means; any artificially created being.
GOLEM.origin: Hebrew 'gōlem' (shapeless mass, embryo), from Talmudic literature
GOLEM.firstUsed: 16th century in Jewish tradition; wider use 19th century
GOLEM.evolution: From Talmudic philosophical concept to the Prague Golem legend; became a precursor metaphor for artificial life and robots in modern thought.
GOLEM.joke: The golem: the original automation solution, before anyone invented a bug-reporting system.

GOLLY.definition: A mild exclamation of surprise or wonder, used as a euphemism for 'God'.
GOLLY.origin: Euphemistic alteration of 'God'; one of many minced oaths in English
GOLLY.evolution: Developed as a polite substitute for taking the Lord's name in vain; fell out of fashionable use but persists as a quaint expression of innocent surprise.
GOLLY.joke: Golly: what you say when you're too surprised to swear but too old to say 'OMG.'

GONAD.definition: An organ that produces reproductive cells; a testis or ovary.
GONAD.evolution: Clinical anatomical term derived from Greek reproductive vocabulary; became the preferred technical term over cruder alternatives.
GONAD.joke: Gonads: biology's way of making reproduction sound like an exam topic.

GONER.definition: Informal for a person or thing that is doomed or beyond hope of saving.
GONER.origin: American English, from 'gone' (past) + agent suffix -er; mid-19th century slang
GONER.evolution: American slang that extended 'gone' — already on its way out — to describe someone who's definitively finished. Kept its casual fatalism.
GONER.joke: A goner: someone who hasn't been informed of their status yet.

GOODY.definition: A small treat or sweet; also an excessively virtuous or prim person.
GOODY.origin: Shortening of 'goodwife' (archaic title for a married woman) + diminutive suffix
GOODY.evolution: From an honorific for women to a term for small treats; also used ironically for sanctimoniously good people — a 'goody-goody.'
GOODY.joke: A goody-two-shoes: so moral they've trademarked both feet.

GOOEY.definition: Soft, sticky, and wet; also excessively sentimental.
GOOEY.origin: Possibly from 'goo' (sticky substance), origin uncertain; perhaps imitative
GOOEY.evolution: From describing sticky physical substances to emotional stickiness; 'gooey' sentiment drips the same way overcooked caramel does.
GOOEY.joke: Gooey: the texture of both fresh brownies and bad rom-coms.

GOOFY.definition: Foolish or harmlessly eccentric; silly in an endearing way.
GOOFY.origin: Dialectal 'goof' (fool, simpleton), possibly from dialectal English 'goff' (fool); origin uncertain
GOOFY.evolution: From 'goof' (fool) to 'goofy' as an affectionate descriptor; Disney's character Goofy cemented its endearing rather than insulting connotations.
GOOFY.joke: Goofy: the only Disney character whose personality is a full job description.

GOOSE.definition: A large waterfowl; also informal for a silly person or an unexpected poke.
GOOSE.origin: Old English 'gōs', from Proto-Germanic *gans-; cognate with Latin 'anser'
GOOSE.evolution: Ancient waterfowl name with extraordinary staying power; gave us 'goosebumps,' 'wild goose chase,' and the verb meaning to poke unexpectedly.
GOOSE.joke: A wild goose chase: the original pointless side quest, predating video games by centuries.

GORGE.definition: A narrow valley between hills or mountains with steep rocky walls; also to eat greedily.
GORGE.origin: Old French 'gorge' (throat, narrow passage), from Latin 'gurges' (whirlpool, abyss)
GORGE.evolution: From throat to narrow passage to canyon; the eating sense connects back to the throat — gorging fills the gorge.
GORGE.joke: Gorging at a buffet: the geological event your stomach didn't account for.

GOUGE.definition: To cut or hollow out with a chisel; also to swindle or overcharge.
GOUGE.origin: Old French 'gouge' (chisel), from Late Latin 'gubia', of Celtic origin
GOUGE.evolution: From physical carving to financial exploitation; price gouging uses the same metaphor — digging into someone's pocket with precision.
GOUGE.joke: Price gouging: when the market decides your wallet is a woodworking project.

GOURD.definition: A large fleshy fruit with a hard rind, including pumpkins, squashes, and calabashes.
GOURD.origin: Anglo-French 'gourde', from Old French 'cohorde', from Latin 'cucurbita' (gourd)
GOURD.evolution: From the vegetable to containers made from its dried shell; 'out of your gourd' entered slang as a colorful phrase for being crazy.
GOURD.joke: Out of your gourd: when your head decides to go freelance.

GRACE.definition: Smoothness and elegance of movement; also divine favor or the saying of a blessing before a meal.
GRACE.origin: Old French 'grace', from Latin 'gratia' (favor, charm), from 'gratus' (pleasing)
GRACE.evolution: From divine favor to physical elegance; grace bridged physical beauty and spiritual goodness, making 'graceful' the highest possible compliment.
GRACE.joke: Saying grace before a meal: thanking the divine before blaming the cook.

GRADE.definition: A level of quality or rank; a score given for academic work; a gradient or slope.
GRADE.evolution: From a step or degree to academic scores to road gradients; the Latin 'step' metaphor runs through all meanings — moving up or down by degrees.
GRADE.joke: Grading on a curve: hope dressed up in statistics.

GRAFT.definition: A piece of plant or tissue transplanted to grow in a new location; also corrupt practices for personal gain.
GRAFT.origin: Old French 'greffe' (stylus), from Latin 'graphium', from Greek 'graphion' (writing implement)
GRAFT.evolution: From horticultural transplanting to political corruption; both involve inserting something foreign into a system to make it 'grow' for personal benefit.
GRAFT.joke: Political graft: horticulture for people who prefer their gains in cash.

GRAIL.definition: The Holy Grail; any object of quest or goal pursued with great ambition.
GRAIL.origin: Old French 'graal' (large flat dish), from Medieval Latin 'gradalis'; possibly from Latin 'crater' (bowl)
GRAIL.evolution: A medieval dish became the sacred cup of the Last Supper in Arthurian legend; evolved to mean any ultimate, possibly unattainable goal.
GRAIL.joke: The Holy Grail: the original 'we'll find it eventually' company goal.

GRAIN.definition: A seed of a cereal plant; the texture of wood or stone; a unit of weight.
GRAIN.origin: Old French 'grain', from Latin 'granum' (seed, small particle)
GRAIN.evolution: From crop seeds to texture patterns to tiny measurements; 'against the grain' captures how texture metaphors extended to personality and preference.
GRAIN.joke: Taking things with a grain of salt: the condiment of healthy skepticism.

GRAND.definition: Magnificent and imposing in appearance; of the highest importance; a thousand dollars.
GRAND.evolution: From physical grandeur to cultural importance to slang for $1,000; the dollar amount borrowed the word's impressive scale for a round number.
GRAND.joke: A grand piano: music's way of reminding you that apartments have limits.

GRANT.definition: To give or bestow formally; to admit or acknowledge; a sum of money given for a purpose.
GRANT.origin: Old French 'granter' (to promise), from Vulgar Latin *credentare, from Latin 'credere' (to believe, trust)
GRANT.evolution: From acknowledging or agreeing to formally bestowing; 'taking for granted' shows how easy giving can slide into unappreciated routine.
GRANT.joke: Taking things for granted: the hobby you don't realize you have until they're gone.

GRAPE.definition: A small, round fruit growing in clusters on vines, used to make wine and juice.
GRAPE.origin: Old French 'grape' (bunch of grapes), from 'graper' (to harvest with a hook)
GRAPE.evolution: From the harvesting hook to the fruit itself; 'grapevine' became the metaphor for informal news networks — information spreading like vineyard runners.
GRAPE.joke: The grapevine: how rumors travel with zero nutritional value.

GRAPH.definition: A diagram showing the relationship between variables; a network of nodes and edges in mathematics.
GRAPH.evolution: From Greek writing to visual data representation; graphs became the universal language of showing 'it went up' without admitting what went down.
GRAPH.joke: A graph that goes up and to the right: the universal language of 'please invest.'

GRASP.definition: To take and hold firmly; to understand or comprehend.
GRASP.origin: Middle English 'graspen', from Proto-Germanic *graspōn; related to 'grope'
GRASP.evolution: Physical gripping extended to mental comprehension; 'grasp a concept' makes learning tactile, as if ideas were objects you could finally pick up.
GRASP.joke: Grasping at straws: the optimist's structural engineering solution.

GRASS.definition: Ground-covering plants with narrow leaves; also slang for cannabis; also to inform on someone.
GRASS.origin: Old English 'græs', from Proto-Germanic *grasam; related to 'green' and 'grow'
GRASS.evolution: Basic vegetation term that acquired drug slang and British underworld slang for informer; same word, wildly different social contexts.
GRASS.joke: The grass is always greener: your neighbor's lawn and your life choices, same problem.

GRATE.definition: To shred food by rubbing against a rough surface; to make an irritating scraping sound; to irritate.
GRATE.origin: Old French 'grater' (to scrape), of Germanic origin; related to Old High German 'krazzōn'
GRATE.evolution: Physical scraping extended to emotional irritation; a grating personality wears on you the same way a cheese grater wears on parmesan.
GRATE.joke: Some people grate on you like parmesan — in small doses, acceptable; in large, overwhelming.

GRAVE.definition: A hole dug for burial; also an adjective meaning serious or solemn.
GRAVE.origin: Old English 'græf' (grave, trench), from 'grafan' (to dig); the adjective from Latin 'gravis' (heavy, serious)
GRAVE.evolution: Noun and adjective are separate etymologies that converged; a grave matter has the gravity of a burial — serious, weighty, final.
GRAVE.joke: A grave situation: when even the pun potential feels inappropriate.

GRAVY.definition: Sauce made from meat juices; informal for extra benefit or profit obtained easily.
GRAVY.origin: Old French 'gravé' (seasoned dish), possibly misread from a manuscript; exact origin debated
GRAVY.evolution: From a seasoned meat dish to the sauce; 'gravy train' became the idiom for easy money — rich sauce flowing without effort.
GRAVY.joke: The gravy train: best travel experience, no ticket required, stops at all the good accounts.

GRAZE.definition: To feed on growing grass; to touch or scrape lightly; to browse casually.
GRAZE.origin: Old English 'grasian' (to feed on grass), from 'græs' (grass)
GRAZE.evolution: From animals eating grass to the light-touch scrape of a wound to browsing food or the internet; all involve light, passing contact.
GRAZE.joke: Grazing at a buffet: technically pastoral, spiritually chaotic.

GREAT.definition: Of an extent, amount, or intensity considerably above the normal; notably large or important.
GREAT.evolution: Originally meant 'large, coarse'; evolved to mean excellent or important; the word's casual overuse has diluted it — everything is 'great' until nothing truly is.
GREAT.joke: 'That's great': the response that commits to nothing and reassures nobody.

GREED.definition: Intense and selfish desire for something, especially wealth or power.
GREED.origin: Back-formation from 'greedy'; Old English 'grǣdig' (greedy), from Proto-Germanic *grēdagaz
GREED.evolution: Back-formed from the adjective 'greedy'; named as one of the Seven Deadly Sins, giving it theological weight beyond mere wanting.
GREED.joke: Greed: the only deadly sin that gets a documentary series instead of condemnation.

GREEN.definition: The color of grass; also inexperienced or naive; also environmentally conscious.
GREEN.origin: Old English 'grēne', from Proto-Germanic *grōnjaz; related to 'grass' and 'grow'
GREEN.evolution: Connected to grass and growth since Old English; 'green with envy' came from medieval theories that envy caused bile; 'going green' added environmental meaning.
GREEN.joke: Green with envy: when your feelings choose a color that photographs terribly.

GREET.definition: To address someone in a friendly way upon meeting; to receive or acknowledge.
GREET.origin: Old English 'grētan' (to approach, attack, weep), from Proto-Germanic *grōtijanan
GREET.evolution: Old English had several senses including 'to weep'; narrowed to the friendly acknowledgment sense; formal greetings became elaborate social rituals.
GREET.joke: Greeting cards: the industry that monetized the obligation to acknowledge people.

GRIEF.definition: Deep sorrow, especially that caused by bereavement; also trouble or problems.
GRIEF.origin: Old French 'grief' (harm, hardship), from 'grever' (to harm), from Latin 'gravare' (to burden)
GRIEF.evolution: From general burden or harm to specifically emotional sorrow; 'Good grief!' repurposed it as a mild exclamation — grief so commonplace it became punctuation.
GRIEF.joke: Good grief: when your feelings need an adjective before they can be expressed.

GRILL.definition: A device with parallel bars for cooking food over heat; also to question someone intensely.
GRILL.origin: French 'gril' (gridiron), from Old French 'graille', from Latin 'craticula' (small grating)
GRILL.evolution: From cooking grate to interrogation metaphor; being 'grilled' applies the heat of cooking to the heat of questioning — both leave marks.
GRILL.joke: Getting grilled at a job interview: technically a cooking method, definitely not a raise.

GRIME.definition: Dirt ingrained in a surface; also a genre of electronic music originating in East London.
GRIME.origin: Middle Low German or Middle Dutch 'grime' (soot, smear)
GRIME.evolution: Persistent ingrained dirt; later gave its name to UK grime music — raw, urban, refusing to be scrubbed clean — perfect etymological fit.
GRIME.joke: Grime music: proof that the best genre names are just honest about their aesthetic.

GRIMY.definition: Covered with or characterized by grime; dirty and ingrained with filth.
GRIMY.evolution: Simple derivation from grime; 'grimy' retained associations with urban industrial dirt — not just surface dust but deeply embedded filth.
GRIMY.joke: Grimy windows: the house's way of saying it's going for a 'moody' aesthetic.

GRIND.definition: To crush or reduce to small particles; to work hard at a monotonous task; the daily routine.
GRIND.evolution: From physical crushing to the metaphor of endless tedious work; 'the daily grind' captures how routine work wears people down like stone on stone.
GRIND.joke: The daily grind: coffee preparation and life philosophy, same process.

GRIPE.definition: To complain persistently about something; also sharp stomach pain.
GRIPE.origin: Old English 'grīpan' (to seize, grasp), from Proto-Germanic *grīpanan
GRIPE.evolution: From physically gripping to intestinal griping (cramping); the complaint sense followed — a gripe seizes on something like a cramp, refusing to let go.
GRIPE.joke: Chronic griping: when your complaints have developed a subscription model.

GROAN.definition: To make a deep inarticulate sound of pain, despair, or displeasure; to make a groan-worthy pun.
GROAN.origin: Old English 'grānian' (to groan, lament), from Proto-Germanic *grainōjanan
