SALSA.joke: Salsa: the only word where you can't tell if someone's about to eat or dance, and honestly either is fine.

SALTY.definition: Tasting of or containing salt; (informal) bitter or resentful after a loss or embarrassment.
SALTY.evolution: Originally purely gustatory; nautical slang extended it to describe an experienced sailor's character (tough, crude); modern slang uses it for bitter or offended reactions, especially online.
SALTY.joke: Being salty online is just the internet's way of preserving grievances for the long haul.

SALVE.definition: A soothing ointment for wounds or burns; to soothe or comfort.
SALVE.origin: Old English 'sealf' (ointment), from Proto-Germanic *salbō, related to Latin 'sebum' (tallow)
SALVE.evolution: Originally referred to a greasy healing ointment; metaphorically extended to anything that soothes emotional pain; 'to salve one's conscience' means to make oneself feel better about wrongdoing.
SALVE.joke: A salve for your conscience is cheaper than a therapist, but the FDA hasn't approved either.

SALVO.definition: A simultaneous discharge of artillery or firearms; a sudden, vigorous outburst of criticism or applause.
SALVO.origin: Italian 'salva' (salute, volley), from Latin 'salve' (be safe/healthy), imperative of 'salvere'
SALVO.evolution: Originally a ceremonial gun salute; extended to any simultaneous volley of shots in battle; figuratively used for any sudden coordinated outburst — of arguments, applause, or criticism.
SALVO.joke: The opening salvo: dramatic military term, terrifying debate tactic, great band name.

SANDY.definition: Covered with or consisting of sand; of a yellowish-brown color like sand.
SANDY.origin: Old English 'sandig', from 'sand' + '-ig' (adjectival suffix), from Proto-Germanic *samdaz
SANDY.evolution: Originally described a terrain or surface covered with sand; extended to describe the pale yellowish-brown color resembling sand, especially of hair; also a common given name.
SANDY.joke: Sandy hair: when your head wants beach vibes but your boss expects professionalism.

SANER.definition: Comparative of sane; more mentally sound or reasonable.
SANER.evolution: Comparative form of 'sane'; used to compare levels of mental stability or reasonableness; often used ironically to suggest some sanity is barely present.
SANER.joke: Saner is just 'sane' for people who need the comparison to feel better.

SAPPY.definition: Excessively sentimental or mawkish; full of sap (tree fluid).
SAPPY.origin: From 'sap' (tree fluid), Old English 'sæp', from Proto-Germanic *sapaz, possibly related to Latin 'sapere' (to taste)
SAPPY.evolution: Originally meant 'full of sap'; extended to describe something overly sweet or foolish (like tree sap is sticky-sweet); the sentimental meaning developed in American English in the 19th century.
SAPPY.joke: Sappy movies: engineered to make you cry, then feel embarrassed that you cried.

SASSY.definition: Lively, bold, and full of spirit; impudent or cheeky.
SASSY.origin: American English variant of 'saucy', from 'sauce' (impudence) via Old French
SASSY.evolution: American dialectal alteration of 'saucy'; originally meant impudent or impertinent; shifted to a more positive sense of spirited boldness and confident self-expression; widely used in popular culture.
SASSY.joke: Sassy: what polite people call you when they mean mouthy but can't argue with your points.

SATIN.definition: A smooth, glossy fabric with a weave producing a sheen; having a smooth, glossy finish.
SATIN.origin: Old French 'satin', from Arabic 'zaytūnī' (of Zaytun/Quanzhou, a Chinese city known for silk exports)
SATIN.evolution: Named after Zaytun, the medieval Arabic name for the Chinese port city of Quanzhou, a major silk trade center; the fabric's smooth, lustrous finish became associated with luxury and elegance.
SATIN.joke: Satin: when you want to feel fancy but can't afford silk — and can't pronounce 'sateen' correctly.

SATYR.definition: In Greek mythology, a woodland deity with a man's torso and a goat's legs; a lustful man.
SATYR.origin: Latin 'satyrus', from Greek 'satyros' (woodland deity), of uncertain pre-Greek origin
SATYR.evolution: From classical mythology; satyrs were companions of Dionysus known for drunkenness and lechery; gave rise to 'satyriasis' (excessive sexual desire in men) and 'satire' (possibly, though that etymology is disputed).
SATYR.joke: A satyr walks into a bar — goat legs, man torso, god of poor decisions.

SAUCE.definition: A liquid condiment served with food; (informal) alcoholic drink; impudence or cheekiness.
SAUCE.evolution: Originally meant a salted or seasoned liquid accompaniment to food; later used as slang for alcoholic drink ('on the sauce'); 'saucy' developed to mean impudent, then flirtatiously bold.
SAUCE.joke: Secret sauce: the one ingredient every restaurant claims but no one's ever actually identified.

SAUCY.definition: Impudent and cheeky; flirtatiously bold; resembling or covered with sauce.
SAUCY.evolution: Originally meant impertinent and forward; the connection between sauce (seasoning) and impudence reflects the idea that such behavior adds unwanted 'flavor' to interactions; now often means playfully bold or risqué.
SAUCY.joke: Saucy: the culinary compliment that became a personality trait.

SAUNA.definition: A small room used as a hot-air or steam bath for cleaning and relaxation.
SAUNA.origin: Finnish 'sauna' (bathhouse, bath), from Proto-Finnic *savna, related to 'savu' (smoke)
SAUNA.evolution: Borrowed directly from Finnish; saunas have been a central part of Finnish culture for millennia; the word entered English as the practice spread internationally; Finland has more saunas than cars.
SAUNA.joke: A sauna is just a hot room where Finns go to be themselves — quietly, naked, and sweating.

SAUTE.definition: To fry quickly in a little hot fat; a dish prepared by sautéing.
SAUTE.origin: French 'sauter' (to jump), from Latin 'saltare' (to leap), frequentative of 'salire' (to jump)
SAUTE.evolution: From French culinary vocabulary; the 'jumping' refers to the way food is tossed in a hot pan; introduced into English cooking with the spread of French cuisine in the 19th century.
SAUTE.joke: Sauté means the food jumps in the pan — or you panic and fling it at the ceiling.

SAVOR.definition: To taste or smell with great pleasure; to appreciate fully; a pleasant taste, smell, or quality.
SAVOR.origin: Old French 'savourer', from Late Latin 'saporare' (to season), from Latin 'sapor' (taste, flavor)
SAVOR.evolution: Originally purely sensory — to taste or smell something pleasurably; extended metaphorically to appreciate any experience fully; 'savoring the moment' became a common expression for mindful enjoyment.
SAVOR.joke: Savor your food, they say — hard to do when you've been staring at it since noon.

SAVOY.definition: A type of cabbage with wrinkled leaves; a region of southeastern France and northwestern Italy.
SAVOY.origin: From Savoy (Savoie), a historical region in the Alps; French 'Savoie', possibly from Latin 'Sapaudia'
SAVOY.evolution: Named after the Savoy region where the cabbage variety was cultivated; the Savoy Hotel in London and the Gilbert and Sullivan operas performed there are also named for this region.
SAVOY.joke: Savoy cabbage: the frilly one at the market you buy for aesthetic reasons and forget to eat.

SAVVY.definition: Having practical knowledge and understanding; shrewd; practical intelligence.
SAVVY.origin: Black Caribbean English and African-American Creole, from Spanish 'sabe usted?' (do you know?), from Latin 'sapere' (to know/taste)
SAVVY.evolution: Entered English from pidgin/creole languages used in Caribbean trade; the Spanish 'sabez' (do you know) was used as a question and adopted into English as a noun meaning 'understanding'; now widely used as an adjective.
SAVVY.joke: Tech-savvy: the polite way to say 'you once updated a driver without calling your nephew.'

SCALD.definition: To burn with hot liquid or steam; a burn caused by such hot liquid.
SCALD.origin: Old Northern French 'escalder', from Late Latin 'excaldare' (to wash in hot water), from 'ex-' + 'calidus' (hot)
SCALD.evolution: Originally meant to wash in very hot water, then to burn with hot liquid; 'scalding hot' became a common intensifier; used in brewing and food preparation (scalding milk).
SCALD.joke: Scalding coffee: the universe's way of punishing you for trying to function before 9 AM.

SCALE.definition: A graduated measuring device; the relative size of something; overlapping plates on fish or reptiles; to climb; to measure or adjust proportionally.
SCALE.origin: Multiple: measurement sense from Old Norse 'skál' (bowl of scales); fish scale from Old French 'escale'; climb from Latin 'scala' (ladder)
SCALE.evolution: Three distinct etymologies merged under one word; the weighing sense gave us 'tipping the scales'; the fish sense gave us 'to scale' (remove scales); the climbing sense gave us 'scaling mountains' and tech 'scalability'.
SCALE.joke: Scale: simultaneously how you measure fish and how you measure your startup's ambitions.

SCALP.definition: The skin covering the top of the head; to resell tickets at inflated prices.
SCALP.origin: Old Norse 'skalpr' (sheath, scabbard), possibly related to Middle Dutch 'schelpe' (shell); scalping tickets from the practice of taking scalps
SCALP.evolution: Originally referred to the skull or crown of the head; the practice of taking enemy scalps as war trophies led to the verb; 'ticket scalping' is a 20th-century American extension of the taking/profiting metaphor.
SCALP.joke: Ticket scalpers: taking the war trophy metaphor further than anyone intended.

SCALY.definition: Covered with or resembling scales; flaking or peeling.
SCALY.origin: From 'scale' (fish/reptile plate) + '-y'; Old French 'escale', from Frankish *skala (shell)
SCALY.evolution: Originally described the texture of fish or reptile skin; extended to describe any surface with overlapping or flaking layers; used medically to describe skin conditions.
SCALY.joke: My lizard is scaly and my moisturizer bill is zero — who's really winning here?

SCAMP.definition: A person who behaves mischievously; to do work hastily and carelessly.
SCAMP.origin: Probably from Dutch 'schampen' (to escape, slip away), related to 'scamper'
SCAMP.evolution: Originally meant a highway robber or rogue; softened to describe a mischievous rascal, often affectionately; the verb sense (to scamp work) means to rush it carelessly, as if skipping or escaping the effort.
SCAMP.joke: A scamp is a villain who aged well — still up to no good, but now it's charming.

SCANT.definition: Barely sufficient or adequate; to provide an insufficient amount of.
SCANT.origin: Old Norse 'skamt' (short, brief), neuter of 'skammr' (short, brief), from Proto-Germanic *skamma-
SCANT.evolution: Originally meant 'short' or 'insufficient'; 'scant attention' and 'scant resources' became fixed phrases; also used as a verb meaning to provide inadequate amounts or to stint.
SCANT.joke: Scant evidence: what lawyers call it when they have plenty but would rather you not notice.

SCARE.definition: To cause sudden fear; a sudden feeling of fright.
SCARE.origin: Old Norse 'skirra' (to frighten, avoid), from 'skjarr' (shy, timid), from Proto-Germanic *skerhwaz
SCARE.evolution: From Old Norse into northern English dialects; 'scarecrow' appeared in the 16th century; 'scare tactics' and 'scare quotes' are modern extensions; the noun sense emerged from the verb.
SCARE.joke: Scare quotes: when you're too polite to say 'that's not actually a thing' out loud.

SCARF.definition: A length of fabric worn around the neck; to eat quickly and greedily.
SCARF.origin: Neck covering: Old North French 'escarpe' (sash, sling); eating sense: alteration of 'scoff' (to eat greedily)
SCARF.evolution: Two unrelated words merged; the neck covering came from medieval sash-wearing; the eating sense is informal American English, likely from 'scoff' meaning to eat ravenously; both involve wrapping around or wolfing down.
SCARF.joke: To scarf your food while wearing a scarf — fashion and dining at maximum efficiency.

SCARY.evolution: Later formation from 'scare'; simpler and more colloquial than 'frightening' or 'terrifying'; widely used in informal speech and children's language to describe anything from mild unease to genuine terror.
SCARY.joke: Scary movies: where you pay money to feel afraid, then wonder why you can't sleep.

SCENE.definition: The place where an event occurs; a subdivision of a play or film; a display of emotions; a social sphere.
SCENE.origin: Latin 'scena', from Greek 'skēnē' (tent, stage, scene), from 'skia' (shadow)
SCENE.evolution: Originally the backdrop or stage of a theatrical performance; extended to any location of events; 'making a scene' means causing a public disturbance; 'the scene' (art scene, music scene) refers to a social milieu.
SCENE.joke: Making a scene: the theatrical tradition of turning personal problems into everyone's problem.

SCENT.definition: A distinctive, often pleasant smell; perfume; to detect by smell or recognize.
SCENT.origin: Old French 'sentir' (to sense, smell), from Latin 'sentire' (to feel, perceive); the 'c' was added in the 17th century by false analogy with words like 'scene'
SCENT.evolution: Originally 'sent' (the 'c' was added by analogy with Latin); used in hunting (following a scent trail); extended to perfume and any detectable smell; 'scent of danger' became a metaphorical expression.
SCENT.joke: The 'c' in scent is silent — much like the confidence of the person who added it in the 1600s.

SCION.definition: A descendant of a notable family; a young shoot or twig of a plant, especially for grafting.
SCION.origin: Old French 'sion, cion' (shoot, twig), from Frankish *sīđ (twig), related to 'sew' (to cut)
SCION.evolution: Originally referred to a plant cutting used for grafting; the 'offspring of a family' meaning emerged by analogy — a scion of a great family is a shoot from the main stem; both senses remain active.
SCION.joke: The scion of a dynasty and a grafted twig: both defined by what they're attached to.

SCOFF.definition: To speak scornfully; to mock or jeer; to eat greedily (British).
SCOFF.origin: Mockery sense: Old French 'escoffier'; food sense: dialectal English/Afrikaans 'schoff' (meal)
SCOFF.evolution: Two distinct words converged; the mockery sense comes from Old French; the eating sense comes via South African English from Dutch/Afrikaans; both ended up as 'scoff' in British English.
SCOFF.joke: To scoff at your food is both a critique and a serving suggestion.

SCOLD.definition: To rebuke angrily; a person who scolds habitually.
SCOLD.origin: Old Norse 'skáld' (poet, one who uses invective), via Middle English 'scolde'
SCOLD.evolution: Ironically from the Old Norse word for 'poet'; originally a 'scold' was someone who used verbal abuse and invective; 'common scold' was a legal category in medieval English law; now means to rebuke or nag.
SCOLD.joke: A scold was originally a poet — which says something about how audiences received early criticism.

SCONE.definition: A small, flat baked good made of flour and fat, associated with British afternoon tea.
SCONE.origin: Possibly from Dutch 'schoonbroot' (fine white bread), or Scots Gaelic 'sgonn' (shapeless mass), or named after Scone, a town in Scotland
SCONE.evolution: Etymology debated; became central to British afternoon tea culture in the 19th century; the great debate over whether to say 'scone' rhyming with 'gone' or 'bone' has divided the nation more than most political issues.
SCONE.joke: The great scone pronunciation war: proof that pastry can divide a nation more than politics.

SCOOP.definition: A deep bowl-shaped utensil for lifting; an exclusive news story; to lift or gather with a scoop.
SCOOP.origin: Middle Dutch or Middle Low German 'schōpe' (water vessel, bucket), from Proto-Germanic *skōpōn
SCOOP.evolution: Originally referred to a tool for bailing water; extended to any deep-spooned utensil; journalism adopted 'scoop' for an exclusive story in the 19th century — the reporter 'scoops up' the story before rivals.
SCOOP.joke: In news, a scoop is an exclusive. In ice cream, it's a serving. Both disappear quickly.

SCOPE.definition: The extent of subject matter covered; an instrument for viewing; opportunity; to assess.
SCOPE.origin: Italian 'scopo' (purpose, aim), from Greek 'skopos' (watcher, aim), from 'skopein' (to look at)
SCOPE.evolution: Originally meant 'purpose' or 'aim'; extended to the range or extent of something; scientific instruments ending in '-scope' (telescope, microscope) derive from the same Greek root; in project management, 'scope creep' is dreaded.
SCOPE.joke: Scope creep: when the project grows like a plant that wasn't supposed to be there but now owns the garden.

SCORE.definition: A numerical record of points; twenty; the music for a film or play; to achieve or obtain; to notch.
SCORE.origin: Old Norse 'skor' (notch, tally, twenty), from 'skora' (to notch), related to Proto-Germanic *skur-
SCORE.evolution: Originally a tally mark notched in wood (every 20 notches = a score); gave us the counting sense of twenty (Lincoln's 'four score'); extended to points in games; composers 'scoring' music records all the parts.
SCORE.joke: Score originally meant 'twenty notches on a stick' — the world's most depressing spreadsheet.

SCORN.definition: Contempt or disdain; to treat with contempt.
SCORN.origin: Old French 'escarn' (mockery, derision), from Frankish *skernjan (to mock), from Proto-Germanic *skern-
SCORN.evolution: From Old French via Frankish; 'scorned woman' became a literary trope (from Congreve's 'Heaven has no rage like love to hatred turned'); 'to scorn' means to reject with contempt.
SCORN.joke: Hell hath no fury like a woman scorned — or someone whose order was wrong at a café.

SCOUR.definition: To clean by rubbing vigorously; to search thoroughly.
SCOUR.origin: Middle Dutch or Middle Low German 'schūren' (to clean, rub), from Old French 'escurer', from Late Latin 'excurare' (to clean out)
SCOUR.evolution: Originally meant to clean by rubbing hard; extended to any thorough searching or sweeping through — 'scouring the internet' uses the same vigorous thoroughness metaphor as cleaning a pot.
SCOUR.joke: Scouring the internet for answers: the modern equivalent of scrubbing a pot — equally exhausting, rarely cleaner.

SCOUT.definition: A person sent ahead to gather information; to observe or search for; the scouting movement.
SCOUT.origin: Old French 'escouter' (to listen), from Latin 'auscultare' (to listen carefully)
SCOUT.evolution: Originally meant someone who listens for information; became a military term for an advance observer; talent scouts extended the meaning to searching for promising athletes or performers; the Boy Scout movement (1908) added a new dimension.
SCOUT.joke: A talent scout: someone paid to watch other people be talented and pretend they found them.

SCOWL.definition: To frown with displeasure or anger; an angry or sullen frown.
SCOWL.origin: Probably from Scandinavian, related to Danish 'skule' (to scowl, lurk), from Old Norse *skúla
SCOWL.evolution: Of uncertain origin, likely from Scandinavian; a scowl specifically implies visible displeasure through facial expression; distinguished from a frown by its active, directed quality of anger or disapproval.
SCOWL.joke: A scowl is just a frown that completed its training and got its certification.

SCRAM.definition: To leave hurriedly; the rapid shutdown of a nuclear reactor.
SCRAM.origin: American English slang, probably from 'scramble'; nuclear sense: backronym 'Safety Control Rod Axe Man' (disputed)
SCRAM.evolution: The 'leave quickly' sense emerged from American slang around the 1920s; the nuclear engineering term for emergency shutdown was retrofitted with an acronym explanation, though this etymology is disputed; both involve sudden, urgent action.
SCRAM.joke: Scram: valid instruction for a toddler, a burglar, or a nuclear reactor — context is everything.

SCRAP.definition: A small piece; discarded material; a fight; to discard or abandon.
SCRAP.origin: Old Norse 'skrap' (trifles, scraps), from 'skrapa' (to scrape, scratch)
SCRAP.evolution: Originally small pieces cut off; extended to discarded material in general; the 'fight' sense emerged in 19th-century slang, perhaps from the idea of scrapping over something; 'to scrap a plan' means to discard it.
SCRAP.joke: Scrap metal and scrap plans: both discarded when the original didn't work out.

SCREE.definition: A mass of small loose stones on a mountain slope; the slope itself.
SCREE.evolution: Geological and mountaineering term from Old Norse; describes the loose rock debris that accumulates at the base of cliffs and steep mountain slopes; walking on scree is notoriously difficult due to its shifting nature.
SCREE.joke: Scree: the mountain's way of saying 'this path is available, but I wouldn't recommend it.'

SCREW.definition: A cylinder with a spiral thread for fastening; a propeller; to fasten with a screw; to cheat.
SCREW.origin: Old French 'escroue' (nut, hole in a nut), from Latin 'scrofa' (sow) — the spiral groove resembled a pig's corkscrew tail
SCREW.evolution: The fastener was named for its resemblance to a pig's curly tail; became essential to mechanical engineering; 'Archimedes screw' is an ancient water-lifting device; colloquially 'screw' became a vulgar term and an expression of displeasure.
SCREW.joke: The screw was named after a pig's tail — which explains why nothing ever goes in straight.

SCRUB.definition: To clean by rubbing hard; to cancel; low-growing vegetation; an insignificant person.
SCRUB.origin: Low German or Dutch 'schrubben' (to scrub), related to 'shrub'
SCRUB.evolution: Originally to rub vigorously with a brush; extended to 'scrub a mission' (cancel a launch) in aerospace jargon; 'scrub' (brushwood) is a related word; the insult 'scrub' (insignificant person) comes from this sense of being low and rough.
SCRUB.joke: Scrub: simultaneously the cleanest and most dismissive word in the English language.

SCRUM.definition: A rugby formation where players pack tightly together; an agile software development methodology.
SCRUM.origin: Shortening of 'scrummage', variant of 'scrimmage', from Old French 'eskirmir' (to fight)
SCRUM.evolution: From the rugby formation where forwards pack together to contest possession; adopted wholesale by software developers in the 1990s as a framework for iterative development — the analogy being a team working closely together toward a goal.
SCRUM.joke: Scrum meetings: the software industry borrowed the name from rugby and forgot to borrow the exercise.

SCUBA.definition: An apparatus for breathing underwater consisting of a tank of compressed air.
SCUBA.origin: Acronym: Self-Contained Underwater Breathing Apparatus, coined by Christian Lambertsen in 1952
SCUBA.evolution: One of the most successful acronyms — so thoroughly adopted that most users don't recognize it as one; popularized by Jacques Cousteau's explorations; now used adjectivally ('scuba diving') and the full expansion is rarely invoked.
SCUBA.joke: Scuba is an acronym that became a word — the underwater version of a successful rebranding.

SEDAN.definition: A closed automobile with a fixed roof and separate trunk; an enclosed chair carried on poles.
SEDAN.origin: Possibly from Italian dialect 'sedere' (to sit), from Latin 'sedere'; or from Sedan, France (disputed)
SEDAN.evolution: The sedan chair was a one-person enclosed litter carried by servants; when automobiles developed enclosed body styles, 'sedan' was applied to distinguish them from open-top vehicles; the original carrying meaning is now largely forgotten.
SEDAN.joke: A sedan was originally a chair you paid people to carry — now you pay people to fix the car of the same name.

SEEDY.definition: Sordid or disreputable; shabby in appearance; unwell.
SEEDY.origin: From 'seed' + '-y'; Old English 'sæd' (seed) — figuratively 'gone to seed'
SEEDY.evolution: Originally 'gone to seed' described a plant that had bolted past its prime and become useless; 'seedy' extended this to describe anything past its best — a seedy neighborhood, a seedy hotel; 'feeling seedy' (unwell) follows the same decline metaphor.
SEEDY.joke: Seedy is just a plant metaphor for human decline — gone to seed, and not in a good harvest way.

SEGUE.definition: To move smoothly without interruption from one situation or topic to another; such a transition.
SEGUE.origin: Italian 'segue' (it follows), from 'seguire' (to follow), from Latin 'sequi' (to follow)
SEGUE.evolution: Originally a musical direction meaning 'continue without a break'; adopted into general usage to describe any smooth transition between topics or situations; frequently used (and overused) by radio DJs and podcast hosts.
SEGUE.joke: Segue: what podcasters say when they have no idea how to connect two unrelated topics.

SEIZE.definition: To take hold of suddenly or forcibly; to confiscate; to comprehend suddenly.
SEIZE.origin: Old French 'seisir' (to take possession of), from Frankish *sakjan (to sue for), from Proto-Germanic *sakaz (lawsuit)
SEIZE.evolution: Originally a legal term for taking formal possession of property; extended to forcible physical taking; 'seize the day' (carpe diem) became a cultural motto; engines 'seize' when parts bind from lack of lubrication.
SEIZE.joke: Seize the day — though technically the day belongs to whoever woke up first.

SEMEN.definition: The fluid containing spermatozoa discharged by the male reproductive organs.
SEMEN.origin: Latin 'semen' (seed), from Proto-Indo-European *sē- (to sow), related to 'sow' (plant seeds)
SEMEN.evolution: Directly from Latin 'seed'; the agricultural metaphor of sowing and reproduction was central to how ancient cultures conceptualized procreation; used in both biological and medical contexts.
SEMEN.joke: Latin for 'seed' — which explains why botanists and biologists occasionally confuse each other's papers.

SENSE.definition: Any of the faculties of sight, smell, hearing, taste, and touch; a feeling or awareness; sound practical judgment; to detect.
SENSE.origin: Latin 'sensus' (faculty of feeling), from 'sentire' (to feel, perceive)
SENSE.evolution: From Latin 'sentire' (to feel); encompasses all five physical senses plus the metaphysical sense of meaning or logic; 'common sense' emerged as a term for basic practical reasoning in the 16th century; 'to sense' danger uses the detection meaning.
SENSE.joke: Common sense: increasingly uncommon, yet still the most widely claimed personal asset.

SEPIA.definition: A reddish-brown color; a brown pigment from cuttlefish ink; old photographic printing in this tone.
SEPIA.origin: Latin 'sepia', from Greek 'sēpia' (cuttlefish), related to 'sēpein' (to cause to decay)
SEPIA.evolution: Named after the cuttlefish whose ink was used as a brown pigment in art; sepia-toned photographs became a symbol of the past; modern use of 'sepia filter' on photos invokes nostalgia.
SEPIA.joke: Sepia filter: the color of nostalgia, named after a cuttlefish that also thought everything was better in the old days.

SERIF.definition: A small line or stroke attached to the end of a letter in typography.
SERIF.origin: Probably Dutch 'schreef' (a scratch, line), from 'schrijven' (to write), from Latin 'scribere'
SERIF.evolution: Typography term for the small finishing strokes on letters; serifs likely originated from stone carving where a chisel would leave finishing marks; the serif vs. sans-serif debate has occupied designers for a century.
SERIF.joke: Serif vs. sans-serif: the design argument that turns graphic designers into philosophy professors.

SERUM.definition: The liquid component of blood; a medical or cosmetic preparation injected or applied to the skin.
SERUM.evolution: Originally the thin liquid that separates from clotted blood; adopted in medicine for antivenoms and vaccines; beauty industry adopted 'serum' in the late 20th century for concentrated skin treatments, trading on the medical authority of the term.
SERUM.joke: Serum: once it saved you from snakebite, now it's in your moisturizer at forty times the price.

SERVE.definition: To perform duties for; to provide with food or drink; to hit a ball to start play; a service in tennis.
SERVE.origin: Old French 'servir', from Latin 'servire' (to serve), from 'servus' (slave, servant)
SERVE.evolution: From Latin 'slave'; 'serve' covers an enormous range — serving food, serving a sentence, serving a ball in tennis, serving a court summons; in modern slang, 'serving looks' means displaying an impressive appearance.
SERVE.joke: Serving looks: the moment fashion became a court sport.

SETUP.definition: The way something is organized or arranged; the initial configuration of equipment or software; a trap.
SETUP.origin: From the verb phrase 'set up', from Old English 'settan' (to place) + 'up'
SETUP.evolution: Originally referred to the arrangement of printing type; extended to any initial configuration; in comedy, the setup is the premise before the punchline; a 'setup' can also mean being framed or tricked.
SETUP.joke: A setup in comedy leads to a punchline. A setup in IT leads to four hours on hold.

SEVEN.definition: The number 7; a group or set of seven.
SEVEN.origin: Old English 'seofon', from Proto-Germanic *sebun, from Proto-Indo-European *septm̥
SEVEN.evolution: Cognate across Indo-European languages (Latin 'septem', Greek 'hepta', Sanskrit 'sapta'); considered lucky in many Western cultures; numerologically significant (seven deadly sins, seven wonders, seven days, seven notes in a scale).
SEVEN.joke: Why is six afraid of seven? Because seven eight nine — the only math joke with a kill count.

SEVER.definition: To divide or cut by cutting; to end a relationship or connection abruptly.
SEVER.origin: Anglo-French 'severer', from Old French 'sevrer' (to separate), from Vulgar Latin *separare, from Latin 'separare'
SEVER.evolution: Originally meant to physically separate by cutting; extended to breaking ties, relationships, or diplomatic connections; 'severance pay' reflects the cutting of an employment relationship.
SEVER.joke: Severing ties: corporate euphemism for 'we cut the cord, here's a check, good luck.'

SEWER.definition: An underground channel for carrying waste water and sewage.
SEWER.origin: Old North French 'seuwiere' (channel from a fish pond), from Vulgar Latin *exaquaria (to drain water), from Latin 'ex-' + 'aqua' (water)
SEWER.evolution: Originally referred to a channel that drained fish ponds; extended to the underground drainage systems built in medieval and modern cities; modern sewer systems in London were built in the 1860s after the 'Great Stink' of 1858.
SEWER.joke: The sewer: civilization's most essential and least discussed infrastructure achievement.

SHACK.definition: A roughly built dwelling; to live together without being married (shack up).
SHACK.origin: Possibly from Mexican Spanish 'jacal' (hut), or back-formation from 'shackle', or from Nahuatl 'xacalli'
SHACK.evolution: Origin disputed; possibly from Nahuatl via Spanish; entered American English to describe rough frontier dwellings; 'shacking up' became slang for cohabiting without marriage in the 20th century.
SHACK.joke: Shack up: from frontier shelter to relationship status — the word did a lot of work.

SHADE.definition: Darkness caused by blocking light; a ghost; a slightly different color; to block light; to insult subtly.
SHADE.origin: Old English 'sceadu' (shadow, shade), from Proto-Germanic *skadwaz, from Proto-Indo-European *skot- (darkness)
SHADE.evolution: Originally darkness from blocked light; in LGBTQ+ ballroom culture, 'throwing shade' meant subtle public insults, which entered mainstream use; also used for color gradation; ghosts of the dead were called 'shades' in classical literature.
SHADE.joke: Throwing shade: ancient ballroom culture's gift to everyone who wanted to insult someone with plausible deniability.

SHADY.definition: Giving shade from sunlight; of questionable character; suspicious.
SHADY.evolution: Originally meant 'providing shade'; extended to mean 'unclear, not above board' — perhaps by the metaphor of being kept in the dark; now commonly means suspicious, dishonest, or disreputable.
SHADY.joke: A shady tree is a blessing. A shady deal is a lawsuit.

SHAFT.definition: A long, narrow part or beam; a mine tunnel; an elevator passage; to treat unfairly.
SHAFT.origin: Old English 'sceaft' (pole, staff, arrow), from Proto-Germanic *skaftaz, from Proto-Indo-European *skabh- (to support)
SHAFT.evolution: Originally a spear or arrow shaft; extended to any long narrow passage or structure (elevator shaft, mine shaft, drive shaft); the verb 'to shaft' (to cheat) emerged in the 20th century from slang.
SHAFT.joke: Getting the shaft: the rare workplace complaint with structural, mechanical, and moral dimensions.

SHAKE.definition: To move up and down or from side to side rapidly; a trembling movement; a milkshake.
SHAKE.origin: Old English 'scacan' (to shake, agitate, flee), from Proto-Germanic *skakanan
SHAKE.evolution: Old English word for rapid trembling movement; 'milkshake' (19th century) was originally an alcoholic drink; 'no great shakes' (not impressive) comes from dicing; 'fair shake' means equitable treatment.
SHAKE.joke: No great shakes: the verdict of a dice game that became the world's most modest understatement.

SHAKY.definition: Trembling or quivering; uncertain or unstable.
SHAKY.evolution: Derived from 'shake'; used for physical trembling (shaky hands) and figurative instability (shaky logic, shaky foundation); 'on shaky ground' is a common metaphorical phrase.
SHAKY.joke: Shaky evidence: what the prosecution presents and what the defense dreams of encountering.

SHALE.definition: A fine-grained sedimentary rock that splits easily into layers.
SHALE.origin: German 'Schale' (shell, layer, scale), from Old High German 'scala', related to English 'shell'
SHALE.evolution: Geological term for layered sedimentary rock; contains organic matter that can be processed into oil (oil shale); hydraulic fracturing ('fracking') of shale formations became controversial in the 21st century.
SHALE.joke: Shale: the rock that became an energy debate and a geological lesson simultaneously.

SHALL.definition: Used to indicate the future tense or strong intention; used in questions to offer or suggest.
SHALL.origin: Old English 'sceal' (I owe, I am obliged), from Proto-Germanic *skulaną (to owe, be obliged)
SHALL.evolution: Originally meant 'to owe' or 'to be obliged'; became a pure future marker; traditional English grammar distinguished 'shall' (first person future) from 'will' (first/second/third person intention), a distinction mostly lost in modern English.
SHALL.joke: 'Shall' vs 'will': the grammar war that consumed the 19th century and bored the 21st.

SHALT.definition: Archaic second-person singular past tense of 'shall'; used in solemn or religious contexts.
SHALT.origin: Old English 'scealt', second person singular of 'sceal' (I owe/must), from Proto-Germanic *skulaną
SHALT.evolution: The second-person singular form of 'shall'; now used almost exclusively in biblical or mock-archaic contexts ('Thou shalt not'); the Ten Commandments preserved this form in their authoritative translations.
SHALT.joke: Thou shalt: the grammatical form that survived solely because commandments needed to sound serious.

SHAME.definition: A painful feeling of humiliation from wrong or foolish behavior; a pity or unfortunate thing; to cause shame in.
SHAME.origin: Old English 'scamu, sceomu' (shame, disgrace), from Proto-Germanic *skamō, from Proto-Indo-European *kem- (to cover)
SHAME.evolution: The root idea involves covering — to be ashamed means wanting to hide; extended from personal dishonor to pity ('what a shame'); 'shaming' (body-shaming, slut-shaming) became a specific modern social phenomenon.
SHAME.joke: Shame and guilt: same neighborhood, different architecture — guilt is internal, shame requires an audience.

SHANK.definition: The lower leg; a shaft or stem; a cut of meat from the leg; to stab; to hit a ball badly.
SHANK.origin: Old English 'scanca' (shinbone, lower leg), from Proto-Germanic *skankaz
SHANK.evolution: Originally the lower part of the leg; extended to any long narrow shaft or stem; in cooking, the shank cut is from the leg of an animal; in golf, 'to shank' is to hit the ball off the hosel; prison slang for improvised knife.
SHANK.joke: Shank: from anatomical term to prison improvisation — the leg's long journey through language.

SHAPE.definition: The external form or appearance of something; to give form to; to influence the development of.
SHAPE.origin: Old English 'gesceap' (creation, form), related to 'scieppan' (to create, shape), from Proto-Germanic *skapjan
SHAPE.evolution: Old English word meaning 'creation' (cognate with Old Norse 'skap'); extended to any external form; 'to shape up' means to improve; 'in good shape' means healthy or functional; 'shape the future' is a common motivational phrase.
SHAPE.joke: Shape up or ship out: the motivational phrase that never specified which shape was acceptable.

SHARD.definition: A piece of broken pottery, glass, or similar material.
SHARD.origin: Old English 'sceard' (gap, notch, sherd), from Proto-Germanic *skardaz (notched, jagged)
SHARD.evolution: Originally 'sherd' (still used in archaeology for pottery fragments); 'shard' is an alternate form that became dominant; archaeological sites are dated by ceramic sherds; 'The Shard' skyscraper in London was named for its jagged profile.
SHARD.joke: The Shard skyscraper: named after broken pottery fragments, which is either ironic or very on-brand for London.

SHARE.definition: A portion belonging to one person; to divide and distribute; to use jointly; a unit of ownership in a company.
SHARE.origin: Old English 'scearu' (a cutting, division), from 'scieran' (to cut, shear), from Proto-Germanic *skar-
SHARE.evolution: Originally meant a cutting or division; extended to any portion allocated; financial 'shares' (stock certificates) emerged in the 17th century as companies divided ownership; social media 'sharing' became the dominant modern sense.
SHARE.joke: 'Please share' at the bottom of every social media post — the entire marketing budget in two words.

SHARK.definition: A large, predatory fish; a swindler; to swindle or hustle.
SHARK.origin: Possibly from German 'Schurke' (scoundrel, villain), or from Mayan 'xoc' (shark); etymology disputed
SHARK.evolution: The fish name appeared in English in the 1560s; the 'swindler' sense is nearly as old; both share the predatory quality; 'loan shark' and 'card shark' are compounds where the swindler sense dominates; 'pool shark' is exceptional skill.
SHARK.joke: Pool shark: the only kind of shark you hope to encounter in a dimly lit bar.

SHARP.definition: Having a cutting edge or point; keenly perceptive; a musical note raised by a semitone; exactly on time.
SHARP.origin: Old English 'scearp' (cutting, pointed, keen), from Proto-Germanic *skarpaz
SHARP.evolution: Originally meant having a cutting edge; extended to keenness of mind; in music, a sharp raises a note one semitone; 'sharp' as in 'ten o'clock sharp' means precisely; 'sharp-dressed' man became an idiom for stylish.
SHARP.joke: Arriving sharp: the temporal achievement of people who've been early their whole lives.

SHAVE.definition: To cut hair close to the skin; to cut thin slices; a close shave (narrow escape).
SHAVE.origin: Old English 'scafan' (to scrape, shave), from Proto-Germanic *skab-, from Proto-Indo-European *skabh- (to cut)
SHAVE.evolution: Originally meant to scrape; extended specifically to cutting facial hair; 'a close shave' began as a literal near-nick during shaving and became a metaphor for any narrow escape; 'razor-thin margin' is a related phrase.
SHAVE.joke: A close shave: simultaneously a barbershop milestone and the metric for catastrophes avoided.

SHAWL.definition: A large piece of fabric worn around the shoulders or head.
SHAWL.origin: Urdu/Persian 'shāl', from Persian 'Shāl' (possibly from Shaliat, a town in India)
SHAWL.evolution: Entered English from Urdu during the British colonial period in India; originally fine wool shawls from Kashmir became fashionable in Europe; the Paisley pattern (from Paisley, Scotland, where they were manufactured) is associated with the shawl.
SHAWL.joke: The shawl traveled from Kashmir to Scotland before anyone thought to put a fringe on it.

SHEAR.definition: To cut the fleece from a sheep; to cut with scissors or shears; force causing deformation.
SHEAR.origin: Old English 'scieran' (to cut, shear), from Proto-Germanic *sker-, from Proto-Indo-European *sker- (to cut)
SHEAR.evolution: The fundamental sense of cutting; used specifically for removing wool from sheep; engineering adopted 'shear force' to describe lateral stress; 'sheared off' describes something cut or broken away by force.
SHEAR.joke: Shear strength in engineering: named after sheep-shearing because stress is stress, regardless of species.

SHEEN.definition: A soft luster on a surface.
SHEEN.origin: Old English 'scīene' (beautiful, bright), from Proto-Germanic *skauniz (beautiful), related to 'show'
SHEEN.evolution: Old English 'sciene' meant 'beautiful'; narrowed to mean specifically a lustrous quality on surfaces; 'sheen' on hair, metal, or fabric implies a healthy, polished quality; the adjective 'sheeny' became archaic.
SHEEN.joke: Sheen: the word for 'beautiful' that survived only by narrowing to describe a surface quality.

SHEEP.definition: A domesticated ruminant animal valued for its wool and meat; a meek, easily led person.
SHEEP.origin: Old English 'scēap', from Proto-Germanic *skēpaz (sheep), of uncertain further origin
SHEEP.evolution: One of the oldest domesticated animals; the same word in singular and plural since Old English; metaphors for meekness and following abound (sheep vs. shepherd, black sheep, wolf in sheep's clothing).
SHEEP.joke: Counting sheep to fall asleep: the only livestock accounting that benefits both parties.

SHEER.definition: Nothing but; perpendicular; (of fabric) very thin; to swerve or deviate.
SHEER.origin: Two words: adjective from Middle Low German 'schir' (bright, clear); verb from Dutch 'scheren' (to cut away, separate)
SHEER.evolution: Two separate words merged; the adjective sense of 'utter, complete' ('sheer stupidity') emphasizes purity or entirety; the fabric sense comes from the clearness/transparency; the swerving verb is a navigation term.
SHEER.joke: Sheer cliff, sheer fabric, sheer audacity — one word for everything dangerously thin.

SHEET.definition: A large flat piece of fabric; a flat piece of paper; a rope controlling a sail.
SHEET.origin: Old English 'scēate' (lower corner of a sail), related to 'sceort' (short); paper sense later from 'scīete'
SHEET.evolution: Originally nautical — the sheet was a rope at the corner of a sail, not the sail itself; extended to bed linens and flat paper; 'three sheets to the wind' (very drunk) refers to three sheets (ropes) loose on a sailing ship, causing it to stagger.
SHEET.joke: Three sheets to the wind: sailors invented the best metaphors for intoxication.

SHEIK.definition: An Arab chief or leader; a term for an attractive, mysterious man (outdated).
SHEIK.origin: Arabic 'shaykh' (old man, elder, chief), from 'shakha' (to grow old)
SHEIK.evolution: From Arabic through multiple European languages; properly refers to a tribal or religious leader in Arab culture; in 1920s Western popular culture, 'sheik' became slang for an exotic, romantically compelling man (via Rudolph Valentino films).
SHEIK.joke: The 1920s used 'sheik' as a romantic compliment — Rudolph Valentino's whole career was etymology in motion.

SHELF.definition: A flat board fixed to a wall for storing objects; a ledge of rock.
SHELF.origin: Middle Low German 'schelf' (shelf, ledge), related to Old English 'scylfe' (shelf, ledge)
SHELF.evolution: From Low German mercantile vocabulary; 'shelved' (delayed indefinitely) comes from placing something on a shelf where it won't be touched; 'shelf life' (the period something remains usable) extended from food to careers and relationships.
SHELF.joke: Shelved: the fate of every ambitious project that becomes someone's good idea for next quarter.

SHELL.definition: The hard outer covering of eggs, nuts, and mollusks; a hollow projectile; to remove the outer covering; to bombard.
SHELL.origin: Old English 'scell, sciell' (shell, husk), from Proto-Germanic *skaljo, related to 'scale' and 'shale'
SHELL.evolution: The protective outer covering extended to anything hollow or enclosing; military shells (artillery projectiles) take their name from the hollow casing; 'shell shock' (now PTSD) emerged from WWI artillery warfare; computing 'shell' is an outer interface.
SHELL.joke: Shell company: the only business entity named after a protective covering that offers no protection.

SHIED.definition: Past tense of 'shy'; moved suddenly aside out of fright; threw with a sideways movement.
SHIED.origin: Old English 'scēoh' (shy, timid) + past tense; the throwing sense from Middle English 'shie' (to throw)
SHIED.evolution: Past tense of 'shy'; horses 'shy' when startled, shying away from perceived danger; the coin-throwing sense ('coconut shy') developed from fairground games; 'shied away from' became a common idiom for avoidance.
SHIED.joke: She shied away from the conversation the way a horse shies from its own shadow — dramatically.

SHIFT.definition: To move or change; a period of work; a woman's dress; a keyboard key.
SHIFT.origin: Old English 'sciftan' (to arrange, distribute, divide), from Proto-Germanic *skif- (to separate)
SHIFT.evolution: Originally meant to arrange or divide; extended to any change in position or condition; work 'shifts' (periods) come from the dividing sense; the keyboard Shift key changes the output; a 'shift dress' is a simple, straight garment.
SHIFT.joke: The Shift key: proving that pressing one key can completely change what everything else means.

SHINE.definition: To emit or reflect light; to be brilliant; to polish; brightness.
SHINE.origin: Old English 'scīnan' (to shine, be bright), from Proto-Germanic *skīnan
SHINE.evolution: Old English word for emitting light; 'rise and shine' became a morning exhortation; 'moonshine' and 'sunshine' are compounds; in American English, 'moonshine' became slang for illicit whiskey (made secretly by moonlight).
SHINE.joke: Rise and shine: the alarm clock's philosophical demand that you both get up and become luminous.

SHINY.definition: Reflecting light; bright and glossy; new and appealing.
SHINY.evolution: Straightforward derivation from 'shine'; in addition to describing reflective surfaces, 'shiny' colloquially describes anything new and attractive; 'shiny new toy' became a metaphor for distracting novelties.
SHINY.joke: Shiny new feature: tech's way of distracting you from the three old features that still don't work.

SHIRE.definition: A county in England; a rural area; a breed of large draft horse.
SHIRE.origin: Old English 'scīr' (official charge, administrative district), from Proto-Germanic *skīrō
SHIRE.evolution: Old English administrative term; counties ending in '-shire' preserve this word directly; Tolkien's 'Shire' in Lord of the Rings was modeled on the English countryside; the Shire horse breed was named for the shires where it was bred.
SHIRE.joke: Yorkshire, Lincolnshire, Tolkien-shire — the naming convention got progressively more ambitious.

SHIRK.definition: To avoid work or duty; to evade responsibility.
SHIRK.origin: Possibly from German 'Schurke' (villain, scoundrel), or from 'shark' (to swindle); exact origin disputed
SHIRK.evolution: Originally meant to practice fraud or to live by cheating; narrowed to specifically avoiding duties or responsibilities; 'shirking' one's duty became a military and civic charge; now used broadly for any avoidance of work.
SHIRK.joke: Shirking: the professional skill that's never listed on a resume but widely practiced.

SHIRT.definition: A garment for the upper body with a collar and sleeves.
SHIRT.origin: Old English 'scyrte' (skirt, short garment), from Proto-Germanic *skurtijō, from *skurtaz (short)
SHIRT.evolution: Related to 'short' and 'skirt' — all from the same root meaning 'cut short'; originally a short undergarment; 'to lose one's shirt' means to lose everything; 'keep your shirt on' means to remain calm.
SHIRT.joke: Shirt, skirt, and short all share a root — proof that English tailors were the original minimalists.

SHOAL.definition: A large number of fish swimming together; a shallow area of water; shallow.
SHOAL.origin: Two words: fish sense from Old English 'scolu' (troop, multitude); shallow sense from Old English 'sceald' (shallow)
SHOAL.evolution: Two distinct words that converged in spelling; a shoal of fish and a shoal of water share nothing etymologically; nautical charts mark shoals as navigation hazards; 'shoaling' in waves describes how they steepen as water shallows.
SHOAL.joke: A shoal of fish or a shoal of water: two ways to be in over your head at sea.

SHOCK.definition: A sudden disturbance to the emotions; an electric shock; a bunch of grain sheaves; to horrify or disturb.
SHOCK.origin: Three origins: emotional/electric from Dutch or Low German 'schokken' (to jolt); grain from Middle Dutch 'schok' (group of sixty)
SHOCK.evolution: Multiple etymologies merged; the jolting sense gave us electric shock and emotional shock; 'shock value' and 'culture shock' are 20th-century formations; a shock of hair describes a thick, bristly mass (possibly from the grain-sheaf meaning).
SHOCK.joke: Shock value: the artistic principle that declining it is always braver than using it.

SHONE.definition: Past tense and past participle of 'shine'; emitted or reflected light.
SHONE.origin: Old English 'scīnan' (to shine), past tense 'scān'; Old English period
SHONE.evolution: Strong past tense of 'shine'; irregular form retained while 'shined' (polished) developed as the regular alternative; 'the sun shone' versus 'he shined the shoes' — one irregular, one regular.
SHONE.joke: Shone vs. shined: one irregular verb somehow survived the great regularization and is very smug about it.

SHOOK.definition: Past tense of 'shake'; (slang) emotionally disturbed or distressed.
SHOOK.origin: Old English 'scacan' (to shake), past tense 'scōc'; Proto-Germanic *skakanan
SHOOK.evolution: Strong past tense of 'shake'; maintained its irregular form; in contemporary slang, 'shook' (or 'shooketh') means profoundly emotionally affected, as if literally shaken; widely used in online culture.
SHOOK.joke: I am shook: when regular past tense is insufficient for the emotional damage.

SHOOT.definition: To fire a projectile; to move rapidly; a new plant growth; a photography or filming session.
SHOOT.origin: Old English 'scēotan' (to shoot, rush), from Proto-Germanic *skeutaną
SHOOT.evolution: Old English word with many extensions; plant shoots grow rapidly upward; photography borrowed 'shoot' for capturing images; 'shooting the breeze' means casual conversation; 'shoot for the stars' became an aspirational phrase.
SHOOT.joke: Shoot for the moon: terrible ballistics advice that became humanity's most popular motivational poster.

SHORE.definition: The land along the edge of water; to prop up or support.
SHORE.origin: Two words: land sense from Middle Dutch 'schore' (bank, shore); prop sense from Middle Dutch 'schoren' (to prop up)
SHORE.evolution: Two separate Middle Dutch words; the land sense gave us shoreline, offshore, onshore; 'shore up' (to reinforce or support) is from the propping sense; both arrived in English during the medieval period of Dutch maritime trade.
SHORE.joke: Shore up your defenses: coastal geography as an action plan.

SHORN.definition: Past participle of 'shear'; having had wool or hair cut; deprived of power.
SHORN.origin: Old English 'scieran' (to cut, shear), past participle 'scoren'
SHORN.evolution: Past participle of 'shear'; used for sheared sheep and cut hair; figuratively, 'shorn of power' means stripped of authority; Samson shorn of his hair (and strength) is a famous biblical metaphor.
SHORN.joke: Shorn of power: when the haircut metaphor turns political and someone loses more than split ends.

SHORT.definition: Of small height or length; not reaching a target; a short film; a short-circuit; to short-circuit.
SHORT.origin: Old English 'scort' (short, not long), from Proto-Germanic *skurtaz, related to 'shirt' and 'skirt'
SHORT.evolution: From the same root as 'shirt' and 'skirt' (originally things cut short); in finance, 'shorting' a stock means betting on its decline; 'short-circuit' gave us the verb; 'short film,' 'short story' use the brevity meaning.
SHORT.joke: Short selling: betting something will fail, which is either pessimistic or just paying attention.

SHOUT.definition: To cry out loudly; a round of drinks bought for a group (Australian/NZ); a loud cry.
SHOUT.origin: Origin uncertain; possibly related to Old Norse 'skúta' (a taunt); perhaps from an unattested Old English form
SHOUT.evolution: Of uncertain origin; in Australian and New Zealand English, a 'shout' is a round of drinks — to shout your friends means to pay for their drinks; 'give someone a shout' means to contact them.
SHOUT.joke: It's my shout: the Australian way of announcing generosity before your wallet has agreed.

SHOVE.definition: To push roughly; to put somewhere carelessly; a rough push.
SHOVE.origin: Old English 'scūfan' (to push, thrust), from Proto-Germanic *skūb-, from Proto-Indo-European *skeubh- (to shove)
SHOVE.evolution: Old English word for pushing forcefully; 'shove off' became a dismissive farewell or an instruction to leave; 'shove it' became a vulgar expression of refusal; 'when push comes to shove' describes a situation reaching its critical point.
SHOVE.joke: When push comes to shove: the English idiom that carefully documents escalation.

SHOWN.definition: Past participle of 'show'; made visible or demonstrated.
SHOWN.origin: Old English 'scēawian' (to look at, show), from Proto-Germanic *skawwōjan
SHOWN.evolution: Past participle of 'show'; irregular form retained alongside the regular 'showed'; 'shown to the door' means politely expelled; 'shown up' means exposed or embarrassed; 'as shown above' is a staple of formal writing.
SHOWN.joke: Shown the door: the elegant euphemism for 'escorted out before security had to get involved.'

SHOWY.definition: Strikingly bright or elaborate; ostentatious; attracting attention.
SHOWY.origin: From 'show' + '-y'; Old English 'scēawian' (to look at, show)
SHOWY.evolution: Extended from 'show' to describe things that put themselves on display; 'showy' often implies more style than substance; botanical usage describes flowers that are visually spectacular; not always a compliment.
SHOWY.joke: Showy: the adjective that politely says 'too much' while technically being neutral.

SHREW.definition: A small mouselike insectivorous mammal; a scolding, nagging woman (archaic/offensive).
SHREW.origin: Old English 'scrēawa' (shrew-mouse), of uncertain origin; the woman-sense from the animal's alleged viciousness
SHREW.evolution: The animal shrew was believed to be venomous and bad-tempered in medieval times; by the 14th century the word transferred to a quarrelsome woman; Shakespeare's 'The Taming of the Shrew' made this usage famous; now recognized as sexist.
SHREW.joke: The shrew, a tiny insectivore unfairly maligned for centuries, would like an apology.

SHRUB.definition: A woody plant smaller than a tree with multiple stems; a drink made from fruit juice, sugar, and vinegar.
SHRUB.origin: Old English 'scrob, scrybb' (shrub, brushwood), from Proto-Germanic *skrub-, related to 'scrub'
SHRUB.evolution: Old English word for low-growing woody plants; 'shrubbery' became famous through Monty Python's Knights Who Say Ni; the drink sense ('shrub') is a revival of a colonial American acidulated beverage.
SHRUB.joke: We want a shrubbery! — the Monty Python demand that saved horticulture from obscurity.

SHRUG.definition: To raise and lower the shoulders to express uncertainty or indifference; the gesture itself; a small cardigan.
SHRUG.origin: Origin obscure; possibly from Scandinavian, related to Danish 'skrugge' (to shudder); attested 15th century
SHRUG.evolution: First appeared as a shudder or shiver; narrowed to the specific gesture of raising and dropping the shoulders; the emoji 🤷 became one of the most-used modern shrug equivalents; a 'shrug' cardigan is a garment that covers only the shoulders.
SHRUG.joke: The shrug: the only gesture that functions simultaneously as an answer, an apology, and a philosophy.

SHUCK.definition: To remove the outer covering of (oysters, corn, etc.); an outer shell or husk; an expression of disappointment.
SHUCK.origin: American English, of uncertain origin; possibly related to 'shock' (grain sheaves); attested 17th century
SHUCK.evolution: American English term for removing oyster shells or corn husks; 'aw shucks' became a mild exclamation of disappointment or embarrassment, particularly associated with folksy modesty; 'shucked' also means deceived (shucked and jived).
SHUCK.joke: Aw shucks: the only exclamation that apologizes for existing while still technically complaining.

SHUNT.definition: To push or pull aside; to divert to a different track; to redirect blood flow in medicine.
SHUNT.origin: Possibly from 'shun' + '-t' (frequentative suffix); origin uncertain; attested 15th century
SHUNT.evolution: Originally meant to shun or avoid; became a railway term for moving rolling stock to a side track; in medicine, a shunt is a tube that diverts body fluids; 'shunted aside' means to be disregarded or sidelined.
SHUNT.joke: Being shunted: the railway metaphor for being moved out of the way so the important trains can pass.

SHUSH.definition: To silence or quieten; an exclamation urging silence.
SHUSH.origin: Imitative/expressive formation from 'hush', reinforced with initial 'sh-' sound; early 20th century
SHUSH.evolution: Onomatopoeic creation; 'sh' is itself a sound urging silence; 'shush' intensifies 'hush'; widely used by librarians, parents, and cinema-goers; as a verb it means to actively quiet someone.
SHUSH.joke: Shush: the word that uses noise to demand the absence of noise.

SHYLY.definition: In a shy manner; timidly; with reserve.
SHYLY.evolution: Adverbial form of 'shy'; originally described horses that were easily startled; extended to people who are reserved or timid in social situations; 'shy away from' describes avoidance behavior.
SHYLY.joke: Shyly: the adverb of people who want to be noticed but only from across the room.

SIEGE.definition: A military operation surrounding a place to force surrender; a period of persistent attack.
SIEGE.origin: Old French 'siege' (seat, throne, siege), from Vulgar Latin *sedicum (sitting), from Latin 'sedere' (to sit)
SIEGE.evolution: Originally meant 'a sitting' — a siege is literally an army sitting around a fortification waiting it out; 'siege mentality' describes a defensive, us-against-them worldview; 'lay siege to' is both military and figurative.
SIEGE.joke: A siege is just patience as military strategy — extremely expensive, extremely boring patience.

SIEVE.definition: A utensil with a mesh for straining; a person who can't keep secrets; to pass through a sieve.
SIEVE.origin: Old English 'sife' (sieve), from Proto-Germanic *siβjōn, related to 'sift'
SIEVE.evolution: Old English kitchen implement; 'sieve-like memory' (unable to retain information) is a common metaphor; to 'sieve through' information means to filter out what's important; related to 'sift' which has the same root.
SIEVE.joke: A memory like a sieve: the only colander that stores excuses.

SIGHT.definition: The faculty of seeing; a thing seen; a device for aiming; to see or observe.
SIGHT.origin: Old English 'sihþ, gesiht' (vision, spectacle), from Proto-Germanic *sehwithō, from *sehwan (to see)
SIGHT.evolution: Old English word for the faculty and act of seeing; 'sight-reading' (reading music at first view); 'sight unseen' means buying without viewing first; sights on a firearm; tourist 'sights' are places worth seeing.
SIGHT.joke: Love at first sight: the romantic tradition of trusting vision over judgment.

SIGMA.definition: The eighteenth letter of the Greek alphabet (Σ, σ); a statistical symbol for standard deviation.
SIGMA.origin: Greek 'sigma', from Phoenician 'shin' (tooth/sun symbol), related to Hebrew 'shin'
SIGMA.evolution: Greek letter used in mathematics and statistics (standard deviation); in internet culture 'sigma male' emerged as a supposed social archetype above 'alpha'; 'six sigma' is a business quality management methodology.
SIGMA.joke: Sigma male: the archetype invented for people who found alpha too mainstream.

SILKY.definition: Of or resembling silk; smooth and soft; suavely persuasive.
SILKY.origin: From 'silk' + '-y'; Old English 'seoloc' (silk), probably from Latin 'sericum', from Greek 'sērikón'
SILKY.evolution: Derived from 'silk'; describes texture resembling silk fabric; extended to describe a smooth, ingratiating manner ('silky smooth voice'); 'silky terrier' is a breed; politicians with persuasive rhetoric may be described as silky.
SILKY.joke: Silky smooth: either the texture of fine fabric or the voice of someone selling you something.

SILLY.definition: Absurdly foolish; without reason or judgment; a silly person.
SILLY.origin: Old English 'sǣlig' (happy, blessed, lucky), from Proto-Germanic *sēlīgaz
SILLY.evolution: One of English's most dramatic semantic shifts: began as 'blessed, happy,' progressed through 'innocent, simple,' then 'pitiable,' then 'weak,' and finally 'foolish'; a 'silly season' is when news is slow and trivial stories dominate.
SILLY.joke: Silly started as 'blessed' — the word that fell from grace harder than almost any other.

SINCE.definition: From a past time until now; because; ago.
SINCE.origin: Middle English 'sithen, sithence' (afterwards, thereupon), from Old English 'siþþan' (after that), from 'sīþ' (after) + 'þan' (then)
SINCE.evolution: Originally a compound meaning 'after then'; contracted over time to 'since'; functions simultaneously as a temporal preposition, a causal conjunction, and an adverb; 'long since' means 'long ago'.
SINCE.joke: Since: three unrelated jobs in one word — time, cause, and duration all need the same preposition.

SINEW.definition: A piece of tough fibrous tissue connecting muscle to bone; physical strength and resilience.
SINEW.origin: Old English 'sinu, sionu' (sinew, tendon), from Proto-Germanic *sinwō, related to Old Norse 'sin'
SINEW.evolution: Old English anatomical term; 'sinews of war' became a classical metaphor for the financial resources needed to sustain military action; 'sinewy' describes both muscular physique and taut, resilient prose.
SINEW.joke: Sinews of war: the economist's metaphor, politely avoiding mention of who actually fights.

SINGE.definition: To burn the surface of slightly; a superficial burn.
SINGE.origin: Old English 'sengan' (to singe), from Proto-Germanic *sangwjan, causative of *singwan
SINGE.evolution: Old English word for surface burning; 'singed wings' became a metaphor for getting too close to something dangerous (Icarus again); Drake 'singeing the King of Spain's beard' was a famous Tudor military exploit.
SINGE.joke: Singe: the cooking term that separates confidence from catastrophe by about thirty seconds.

SIREN.definition: A device that makes a loud warning sound; in Greek mythology, a creature whose song lured sailors to their deaths; a dangerously attractive woman.
SIREN.origin: Old French 'sirene', from Late Latin 'sirena', from Greek 'seirēn' (a siren, enchantress), of unknown origin
SIREN.evolution: From Greek mythology (Odyssey); the piercing warning sound was likened to the mythological call; 'siren call' or 'siren song' describes an irresistible but dangerous temptation; extended to describe dangerously attractive people.
SIREN.joke: Siren call: mythological seduction that became the sound of emergency vehicles — equally urgent, different outcomes.

SISSY.definition: A person considered weak or cowardly; feminine or effeminate (often derogatory).
SISSY.evolution: Originally a pet form of 'sister'; became derogatory when applied to boys as implying feminine weakness; reflects the cultural stigma against qualities coded as feminine; its use as an insult has been widely challenged.
SISSY.joke: Sissy: what happens when 'sister' becomes an insult — revealing more about the insult-giver than the insulted.

SIXTH.definition: Constituting position six in a sequence; one of six equal parts.
SIXTH.origin: Old English 'siexta', from 'siex' (six) + '-ta' (ordinal suffix); from Proto-Germanic *sehstan
SIXTH.evolution: Ordinal of six; 'sixth sense' (extrasensory perception beyond the five known senses) became widely used in the 20th century; 'The Sixth Sense' film popularized 'I see dead people' as a cultural reference.
SIXTH.joke: Sixth sense: the supernatural ability that everyone claims, especially at traffic lights.

SIXTY.definition: The number 60; the years from 60 to 69 of a century or a person's life.
SIXTY.origin: Old English 'siextig', from 'siex' (six) + '-tig' (group of ten); from Proto-Germanic *sehs
SIXTY.evolution: Old English counting unit; 'the Sixties' (1960s) became synonymous with cultural revolution; 'sixty-four dollar question' (now sixty-four thousand) meant the crucial question; Babylonian mathematics used base-60 (hence 60 seconds, 60 minutes).
SIXTY.joke: We owe the Babylonians for sixty seconds and sixty minutes — the only ancient currency still in circulation.

SKATE.definition: A boot with a blade or wheels for moving on ice or a surface; a large flat sea fish; to glide on skates.
SKATE.origin: Dutch 'schaats' (stilt, skate), from Old French 'eschasse' (stilt); the fish from Old Norse 'skata'
SKATE.evolution: Two separate words: the ice/roller skate from Dutch; the flatfish from Norse; 'skating on thin ice' became a metaphor for risky behavior; 'skate park' emerged with 1970s skateboarding culture.
SKATE.joke: Skating on thin ice: the activity that gave us both a sport and a metaphor for poor decision-making.

SKIER.origin: From 'ski' + '-er'; 'ski' from Norwegian 'ski' (snowshoe, stick of wood), from Old Norse 'skíð' (stick of wood, snowshoe)
SKIER.evolution: Agent noun from 'ski'; skiing developed as transport in Scandinavia before becoming recreational sport; the 1924 Winter Olympics helped popularize skiing internationally; competitive skiing spawned multiple disciplines (slalom, downhill, cross-country).
SKIER.joke: A skier is someone who pays a lot to slide down a mountain then stand in line to do it again.

SKIFF.origin: Old French 'esquif', from Old Italian 'schifo', from Frankish or Old High German 'skif' (ship)
SKIFF.evolution: From Germanic through Italian and French; a small, light boat suitable for rivers and protected waters; used by fishermen and leisure rowers; 'in the same skiff' is an occasional variant of 'in the same boat'.
SKIFF.joke: A skiff is just a boat that knows its place — small enough to be modest, useful enough to matter.

SKILL.definition: The ability to do something well through training or experience.
SKILL.origin: Old Norse 'skil' (distinction, ability to discern), from 'skilja' (to separate, discern)
SKILL.evolution: From Old Norse via northern English; originally meant knowledge and understanding (the ability to discern); narrowed to practical ability; 'skill set' became business jargon in the 20th century; gaming adopted it for player abilities.
SKILL.joke: Skill set: the résumé word that turns hobbies into competitive advantages and lunch orders into 'stakeholder management.'

SKIMP.definition: To expend less time, money, or material on something than is needed; to be stingy.
SKIMP.origin: Origin uncertain; possibly from 'scrimp' with metathesis, or Scandinavian origin; attested 19th century
SKIMP.evolution: Of uncertain origin; possibly a variant of 'scrimp'; describes inadequate provision of resources; 'skimpy' (inadequate, barely covering) derived from this; both words suggest cutting corners or providing less than needed.
SKIMP.joke: Skimp on ingredients: the chef's equivalent of footnote-only documentation.

SKIRT.definition: A garment hanging from the waist; the edge or border of something; to go around the edge of; to avoid dealing with.
SKIRT.origin: Old Norse 'skyrta' (shirt), from Proto-Germanic *skurtijō (short garment), related to 'short' and 'shirt'
SKIRT.evolution: Related to 'shirt' and 'short' — originally a short garment; became the lower garment hanging from the waist; 'skirting the issue' (avoiding it) uses the 'going around the edge' sense; skirting boards go around the edge of a room.
SKIRT.joke: Skirting the issue: the grammatical equivalent of wearing something that doesn't fully commit to an answer.

SKULK.definition: To hide or move about stealthily; to lurk; a group of foxes.
SKULK.origin: Scandinavian origin, related to Danish 'skulke' (to shirk, lurk), Norwegian 'skulka'
SKULK.evolution: From Scandinavian; originally meant to shirk duty or hide to avoid work; extended to any stealthy lurking; a group of foxes is called a 'skulk' (reflecting the fox's reputation for cunning concealment).
SKULK.joke: A skulk of foxes: the collective noun that gave foxes their well-deserved reputation for suspicious behavior.

SKULL.definition: The bony framework of the head.
SKULL.origin: Middle English 'skulle', from Old Norse 'skulle' (skull), or Middle Dutch 'scul' (skull); exact origin uncertain
SKULL.evolution: Exact origin debated between Old Norse and Dutch; the skull-and-crossbones became the universal symbol for danger/poison; 'skull session' (informal planning meeting) emerged as American slang.
SKULL.joke: Skull and crossbones: the logo that predates modern warning labels by several centuries and is still the most effective.

SKUNK.definition: A black-and-white North American mammal that can spray foul-smelling liquid; to defeat completely.
SKUNK.evolution: From a Native American Algonquian language — one of many animals named by Indigenous peoples and adopted into English; 'skunked' means completely defeated; the plant 'skunk cabbage' emits a similar odor.
SKUNK.joke: The skunk's greatest contribution: proving that the best defense is making everything else defensive.

SLACK.definition: Not taut; lacking energy; the looser part of a rope; coal dust; to loosen; to be lazy.
SLACK.origin: Old English 'slæc' (slack, lax, careless), from Proto-Germanic *slakaz, from Proto-Indo-European *sleg- (to be slack)
SLACK.evolution: Old English word for looseness or laxness; 'cut someone slack' means to give allowances; 'take up the slack' means to compensate for unused capacity; Slack (the software) was named for its claim to reduce communication friction.
SLACK.joke: Cut me some slack: the only negotiation where loosening the rope is considered a favor.

SLAIN.definition: Past participle of 'slay'; having been killed.
SLAIN.origin: Old English 'slēan' (to strike, beat, kill), past participle 'slægen'; from Proto-Germanic *slahan
SLAIN.evolution: Strong past participle of 'slay'; retained from Old English; now used in formal, literary, or dramatic contexts; in contemporary slang, 'she slayed' (also 'slain') means impressive performance — killing it metaphorically.
SLAIN.joke: Slain: how epic fantasy describes deaths and how drag queens describe excellent performances — equally dramatic.

SLANG.definition: Informal words not considered part of standard vocabulary; to abuse verbally.
SLANG.origin: Origin disputed; possibly from 'sling' (to throw words), or Norwegian 'slengjeord' (slang word); attested 18th century
SLANG.evolution: Etymology still debated; originally referred specifically to thieves' cant and criminal argot; expanded to any informal non-standard vocabulary; 'to slang' means to insult verbally; slang has a social identity function.
SLANG.joke: Slang: officially not real English until enough people use it, at which point it becomes a dictionary entry.

SLANT.definition: A slope or incline; a particular point of view; to slope; to present with bias.
SLANT.origin: Scandinavian origin, related to Old Norse 'slenta' (to slide sideways), Norwegian 'slenta'
SLANT.evolution: From Scandinavian; originally described a slope or diagonal angle; extended to any bias or perspective; journalism 'slanting' a story means presenting it from a particular viewpoint; 'slant rhyme' is near-rhyme.
SLANT.joke: A news slant is just a slope that pretends to be objective while leaning in one direction.

SLASH.definition: To cut with a sweeping stroke; a cut or stroke; the symbol /; fan fiction involving romantic pairings.
SLASH.origin: Old French 'esclachier' (to break), or imitative of the sound; attested 14th century
SLASH.evolution: Originally the cut of a sword or whip; 'slash prices' means cut drastically; the forward-slash symbol (/) inherited the name; in fan culture, 'slash' fiction (Kirk/Spock) got its name from the punctuation mark pairing characters.
SLASH.joke: Slash fiction: where the forward-slash stopped being punctuation and became a shipping manifest.

SLATE.definition: A gray metamorphic rock; a writing tablet; a list of candidates; a grayish-blue color; to schedule; to criticize harshly.
SLATE.origin: Old French 'esclate' (split piece), from 'esclater' (to split, shatter), from Frankish *slitan
SLATE.evolution: From Old French; slates were used as reusable writing surfaces before paper was cheap; 'clean slate' means a fresh start; 'slated for' means scheduled; British 'slating' means harsh criticism; roofing slates are the split-layer rock.
SLATE.joke: Clean slate: the metaphor that ignores how long scrubbing actually takes.

SLAVE.definition: A person legally owned by another; one who works excessively; to work very hard.
SLAVE.origin: Old French 'esclave', from Medieval Latin 'sclavus' (Slav, slave) — so named because many Slavic people were enslaved during medieval conquests
SLAVE.evolution: Named after the Slavic peoples who were heavily enslaved in medieval Europe; the word encodes a historical atrocity; metaphorical uses ('slave to fashion,' 'wage slave') have been criticized for trivializing the reality of slavery.
SLAVE.joke: The word 'slave' encoding a whole people's suffering in etymology — a reminder that language carries history.

SLEEK.definition: Smooth and glossy; looking well-fed and healthy; elegant in design.
SLEEK.origin: Old Norse 'slíkr' (smooth), from Proto-Germanic *slīkaz, related to 'slick'
SLEEK.evolution: From Old Norse; related to 'slick'; 'sleek design' became a staple of product advertising; contrasted with 'slick' which gained connotations of insincerity; a sleek animal is healthy and well-groomed.
SLEEK.joke: Sleek design: what Apple calls it when they remove the ports.

SLEEP.definition: A natural periodic state of rest; to be in this state; sleep paralysis; sleeping bag.
SLEEP.evolution: Old English fundamental word; 'sleeping on it' means deferring a decision; 'sleep deprivation' became a modern health concern; 'sleeping partner' (silent partner) avoids active involvement; counting sheep to fall asleep is a folk remedy.
SLEEP.joke: Sleeping on a decision: the one time procrastination is actively encouraged by neuroscience.

SLEET.definition: Rain containing ice pellets; snow mixed with rain; to fall as sleet.
SLEET.origin: Middle English 'slete', from Middle Low German or Middle Dutch *slēte; exact origin uncertain
SLEET.evolution: Germanic weather term; sleet is meteorologically distinct from hail and snow; 'sleeting' describes the particular misery of frozen rain; often considered the worst weather because it's neither aesthetically interesting nor practically navigable.
SLEET.joke: Sleet: precipitation that couldn't commit to being either rain or snow, disappointing everyone.

SLEPT.definition: Past tense and past participle of 'sleep'; having been in a state of sleep.
SLEPT.origin: Old English 'slǣpan' (to sleep), past tense; from Proto-Germanic *slēpaną
SLEPT.evolution: Irregular past tense of 'sleep'; 'slept on' in modern slang means underappreciated or overlooked ('that album was slept on for years'); 'slept through' means missed entirely while unconscious.
SLEPT.joke: That album was slept on: modern slang that turned unconsciousness into a cultural oversight.

SLICE.definition: A thin flat piece cut from something; a portion; a spatula; to cut into slices; in golf/tennis, a shot that curves.
SLICE.origin: Old French 'esclice' (splinter, sliver), from 'esclicer' (to splinter), from Frankish *slītan (to slit)
SLICE.evolution: From Old French; 'slice of life' became a literary/cinematic genre term; a 'slice' of the profits is a share; in golf and tennis, a slicing shot curves away due to spin; the slice of bread (loaf already pre-cut) gave us 'best thing since sliced bread.'
SLICE.joke: Best thing since sliced bread: the benchmark of innovation that bread's PR team somehow won.

SLICK.definition: Smooth and glossy; clever but lacking integrity; an oil slick; a magazine with glossy paper; to make smooth.
SLICK.origin: Old Norse 'slíkr' (smooth), related to 'sleek'; or Old English *slician (to make smooth)
SLICK.evolution: Related to 'sleek' but developed negative connotations; an 'oil slick' is environmental; 'slick salesman' means superficially charming but untrustworthy; 'slick magazine' uses glossy paper; racing 'slick tires' are smooth for maximum contact.
SLICK.joke: Slick: the word where the same quality that's admirable in a magazine becomes suspicious in a salesman.

SLIDE.definition: To move smoothly over a surface; a playground slide; a presentation slide; a geological landslide; a music slide.
SLIDE.evolution: Old English movement word; 'slide into someone's DMs' became social media slang for initiating private contact; 'presentation slides' evolved from physical transparencies; 'letting it slide' means ignoring a transgression.
SLIDE.joke: Sliding into DMs: the social media era's version of passing notes in class.

SLIME.definition: A thick, slippery substance; to cover with slime.
SLIME.evolution: Old English word for viscous mud; 'slimeball' (an untrustworthy person) emerged in American slang; Nickelodeon's 'green slime' became a pop culture icon in the 1980s; DIY slime became a children's toy trend in the 2010s.
SLIME.joke: Slime: from primordial mud to Saturday morning TV to children's craft projects — quite the glow-up.

SLIMY.definition: Covered with or resembling slime; disgustingly dishonest or obsequious.
SLIMY.evolution: Extended from the physical to the moral; a 'slimy character' is one who is insincerely obsequious or morally reprehensible; the physical metaphor of something coated in viscous filth transferred perfectly to certain social behaviors.
SLIMY.joke: Slimy: the adjective for when slick goes too far and loses deniability.

SLING.definition: A device for hurling stones; a bandage to support an arm; to throw; a cocktail of spirits and water.
SLING.origin: Old Norse 'slyngva' (to sling, throw), related to Old English 'slingan' (to wind, twist)
SLING.evolution: From Old Norse; David's sling against Goliath is the biblical archetype; 'mud-slinging' (political attacks) extends the throwing metaphor; a Singapore Sling is a cocktail; a baby sling carries an infant; 'sling your hook' means to leave.
SLING.joke: Mud-slinging: the political tradition of weaponizing a humble farm tool.
