SLINK.definition: To move quietly and stealthily so as not to be seen or heard.
SLINK.origin: Old English 'slincan' (to creep, slink), from Proto-Germanic *slinkwanan
SLINK.evolution: Old English movement verb suggesting shame or stealth; 'slink away' implies shamefaced departure; 'slinky' (the toy) was named for its sinuous, sliding movement; 'slinky dress' describes a close-fitting garment.
SLINK.joke: Slinking out: the exit strategy of anyone who arrived with more confidence than they deserved.

SLOOP.definition: A single-masted sailing vessel with a fore-and-aft rig.
SLOOP.origin: Dutch 'sloep' (sloop, small boat), related to 'sluip' (narrow passage); attested 17th century
SLOOP.evolution: From Dutch nautical vocabulary; a sloop has one mast and is rigged fore-and-aft; the sloop-of-war was a small naval vessel; the word entered English during the period of Dutch maritime dominance.
SLOOP.joke: The sloop: the sporting car of sailing vessels — one mast, fast, and absolutely certain of itself.

SLOPE.definition: A surface with one end higher than the other; the degree of incline; to have a slope; (British slang) to sneak away.
SLOPE.origin: Possibly from Old English 'aslopen' (slipped away), past participle of 'aslupan'; or Middle Dutch 'sloopen'
SLOPE.evolution: Origins somewhat uncertain; used for inclined terrain and mathematical gradient; 'slippery slope' became a rhetorical term for chain-reaction arguments; British slang 'to slope off' means to leave quietly without being noticed.
SLOPE.joke: Slippery slope: the logical fallacy that's somehow still the most persuasive argument in most debates.

SLOSH.definition: To move liquid around in a container; to pour or splash carelessly; splashing sounds.
SLOSH.origin: Probably imitative/expressive; blend of 'slop' and 'wash' or 'slop' and 'squash'; attested 19th century
SLOSH.evolution: Onomatopoeic formation; describes the sound and movement of liquid in a container that isn't quite full; 'sloshed' became British slang for drunk (liquor sloshing around inside); vivid, informal word for careless liquid movement.
SLOSH.joke: Sloshed: the linguistic journey from messy liquids to personal incapacitation.

SLOTH.definition: Reluctance to work or exert oneself; the slow-moving mammal of Central and South America.
SLOTH.origin: Middle English 'slowthe', from Old English 'slǣwþ', from 'slǣw' (slow, sluggish)
SLOTH.evolution: From Old English; one of the seven deadly sins; the animal was named for embodying this sin (it moves extremely slowly); 'slothful' is the adjectival form; the animal's three-toed variety is famously the slowest mammal.
SLOTH.joke: The sloth: the only deadly sin that became a beloved animal mascot — and it is completely unapologetic.

SLUMP.definition: To sit or fall heavily; to undergo a sudden decline; a period of poor performance.
SLUMP.origin: Possibly from Low German 'slumpen' (to happen by chance), or Norwegian 'slumpa' (to fall); attested 17th century
SLUMP.evolution: Of Germanic origin; originally meant to plunge suddenly; extended to posture (slumping in a chair) and economic decline; sports 'slumps' are periods of underperformance; 'slumping economy' entered financial vocabulary in the 19th century.
SLUMP.joke: Economic slump: the word that politely avoids 'crash' while meaning exactly what you're afraid it means.

SLUNG.definition: Past tense and past participle of 'sling'; threw or hurled.
SLUNG.origin: Old Norse 'slyngva' (to sling), past tense; related to Old English 'slingan'
SLUNG.evolution: Irregular past tense of 'sling'; 'mud was slung' describes political attacks; 'hash-slinger' (short-order cook) gave rise to 'hash was slung'; 'slung low' describes something hung close to the ground.
SLUNG.joke: Mud-slinging: political discourse at its most honest, and most literally described.

SLUNK.definition: Past tense and past participle of 'slink'; moved stealthily.
SLUNK.evolution: Irregular past tense of 'slink'; retains the strong verb form; 'slunk away' implies shameful or secretive departure; dogs slunk with tail between legs became a visual shorthand for guilt or defeat.
SLUNK.joke: Slunk away: the past tense of every exit that should have been more graceful.

SLURP.definition: To eat or drink with a loud sucking sound; such a sound.
SLURP.origin: Dutch 'slurpen' (to sip, slurp), or Low German 'slurpen'; imitative formation
SLURP.evolution: Onomatopoeic and from Dutch; the sound itself defines the word; considered rude in Western table manners but a compliment in some Asian cultures where it indicates enjoyment of noodles or soup.
SLURP.joke: Slurping noodles: either an insult to your host or the highest compliment, depending entirely on the continent.

SLUSH.definition: Partially melted snow; liquid mud; sentimental material; to wash or splash with slush.
SLUSH.origin: Probably from Scandinavian; related to Norwegian 'slusk' (slushy), or imitative of the sound; attested 17th century
SLUSH.evolution: Of Scandinavian or imitative origin; 'slush fund' (a secret fund for bribes or illicit purposes) originally referred to money from selling ship's grease; 'slushy' describes both frozen beverages and sentimental romance.
SLUSH.joke: Slush fund: from ship grease to political bribery — the most liquid career transition in vocabulary.

SLYLY.definition: In a sly manner; cunningly; with secretive craftiness.
SLYLY.evolution: Adverbial form of 'sly'; from Old Norse meaning skilled or cunning; 'sly fox' became a stock idiom; 'on the sly' means secretly; slyness combines intelligence with deception in a way that 'cunning' doesn't fully capture.
SLYLY.joke: On the sly: doing things privately so that no one knows — except they always do.

SMACK.definition: To slap; a sharp blow; a loud kiss; to taste or smell of; heroin (slang); precisely/directly.
SMACK.origin: Multiple: slap sense from Low German 'smakken' or Dutch 'smakken'; taste sense from Old English 'smæc'
SMACK.evolution: Multiple etymologies converged; the taste/smell sense is Old English; the slapping sound is imitative; 'smack dab in the middle' means precisely; heroin slang 'smack' emerged in the 20th century; 'smack talk' means taunting speech.
SMACK.joke: Smack dab in the middle: the only measurement where both noise and precision are implied.

SMALL.definition: Of little size; of little importance; in a small way; the small of the back.
SMALL.origin: Old English 'smæl' (small, narrow, thin), from Proto-Germanic *smalaz
SMALL.evolution: Old English fundamental size word; 'small talk' (trivial conversation) emerged in the 18th century; 'small print' refers to fine details; 'small fry' means unimportant people; the small of the back is its narrowest part.
SMALL.joke: Small print: the font size that proves whether you read the contract or just trusted someone's smile.

SMART.definition: Intelligent; well-dressed; causing sharp pain; to feel sharp pain; a sharp stinging sensation.
SMART.evolution: Originally meant causing sharp pain (still survives in 'smarting from a rebuke'); shifted to mean clever in American English; 'smart casual' is a dress code that pleases no one; 'smartphone' co-opted the intelligence meaning.
SMART.joke: Smart casual: the dress code invented to confuse people who were managing just fine with formal or casual.

SMASH.definition: To break violently into pieces; a commercial or artistic success; a hard hit in sports.
SMASH.origin: Probably blend of 'smack' + 'mash'; or from Scandinavian; attested 18th century
SMASH.evolution: Possibly a blend or imitative formation; 'smash hit' emerged in entertainment to describe enormous commercial success; 'smashing' became British slang for excellent; in tennis/badminton, a smash is a powerful overhead shot.
SMASH.joke: Smash hit: when complete destruction becomes the highest compliment in show business.

SMEAR.definition: To spread a greasy or sticky substance over; to damage someone's reputation with false accusations.
SMEAR.origin: Old English 'smerian' (to anoint, smear), from Proto-Germanic *smarjan, from *smerwa (grease, fat)
SMEAR.evolution: Old English for spreading a fatty substance; 'smear campaign' (deliberate defamation) emerged in political contexts; a medical 'smear' (cervical smear) uses the literal spreading sense; 'smeared reputation' uses the dirty-coating metaphor.
SMEAR.joke: Smear campaign: when politics borrows from the kitchen and everyone ends up covered in something unpleasant.

SMELL.definition: To perceive by the nose; to have an odor; the faculty of olfaction; an odor.
SMELL.origin: Middle English 'smellen', of obscure origin; no Old English form found; possibly from Middle Dutch or Low German
SMELL.evolution: Of uncertain Germanic origin; no Old English record found; 'smell a rat' (to suspect deception) appeared in the 16th century; smells are uniquely powerful memory triggers (Proust's madeleine); 'something smells off' means suspicious.
SMELL.joke: Smell a rat: the phrase that assigned suspicion to rodents and they have never recovered their reputation.

SMELT.definition: Past tense of 'smell'; to extract metal from ore by heating; a small silvery fish.
SMELT.origin: Three origins: smell sense from Middle English 'smellen'; metal sense from Middle Dutch or Low German 'smelten'; fish from Old English 'smelt'
SMELT.evolution: Three words converged; the metallurgical sense (smelting iron ore) is economically critical; the fish (found in cold northern waters) is edible; past tense of 'smell' competes with 'smelled'; all three coexist in the same five letters.
SMELT.joke: Smelt: simultaneously a fish, a metal process, and what your nose did yesterday.

SMILE.definition: To form a pleased or friendly facial expression; such an expression.
SMILE.origin: Old Norse or Middle Low German; related to Old English 'smerian' (to laugh at); attested 14th century
SMILE.evolution: Of Scandinavian or Low German origin; 'smile' replaced Old English 'meartan' and 'smearcian'; the Mona Lisa's ambiguous smile has fascinated art analysts for five centuries; 'smile for the camera' became a reflexive social command.
SMILE.joke: The Mona Lisa's smile: the most analyzed facial expression in history, and she still isn't telling.

SMIRK.definition: To smile in a conceited, smug, or disrespectful way; such a smile.
SMIRK.evolution: Old English word that originally simply meant to smile; narrowed considerably to describe specifically a self-satisfied, knowing, or contemptuous smile; a smirk implies the smirker knows something you don't.
SMIRK.joke: A smirk is a smile that went to private school and came back thinking it was better than you.

SMITE.definition: To strike with a firm blow; to afflict; (archaic) to attack or destroy.
SMITE.origin: Old English 'smītan' (to smear, defile, strike), from Proto-Germanic *smītanan
SMITE.evolution: Old English word originally meaning to smear or defile; shifted to mean to strike hard; 'smitten' (the past participle) meaning struck by love became entirely positive; 'smite' retained the violent biblical connotation.
SMITE.joke: Smitten: the past participle that took a violent word and turned it into a description of falling in love.

SMITH.definition: A worker in metal; a craftsman (wordsmith, silversmith); to work metal.
SMITH.evolution: One of the oldest English occupational words; became one of the most common surnames through hereditary naming; compounds like 'blacksmith,' 'goldsmith,' 'wordsmith,' 'locksmith' show its productivity; 'smithy' is the workplace.
SMITH.joke: Wordsmith: the flattering implication that writing is as laborious and skilled as blacksmithing.

SMOCK.definition: A loose dress or overgarment worn to protect clothes; a type of embroidery; to gather fabric into a honeycomb pattern.
SMOCK.origin: Old English 'smocc' (undergarment), from Proto-Germanic *smugan (to creep into), related to 'snug'
SMOCK.evolution: Originally an undergarment that one crept into; became the loose outer garment worn by farm workers and craftspeople; 'smocking' (the embroidery technique) creates the gathered decorative panels associated with traditional children's clothing.
SMOCK.joke: Smock: from medieval undergarment to artist's studio standard — a garment that knows its purpose.

SMOKE.definition: The visible vapor from burning material; to emit smoke; to cure food with smoke; to smoke tobacco.
SMOKE.origin: Old English 'smoca' (smoke), from Proto-Germanic *smukaz, from *smaukan (to smoke)
SMOKE.evolution: Old English fundamental word; 'where there's smoke there's fire' became a proverb; smoking tobacco entered European culture after contact with the Americas; 'smoke and mirrors' means deceptive illusion; 'holy smoke' is an exclamation.
SMOKE.joke: Smoke and mirrors: the magician's toolkit that became every press secretary's job description.

SMOKY.definition: Full of or emitting smoke; having the taste or smell of smoke.
SMOKY.evolution: Extended from 'smoke'; 'smoky eye' (heavy eye makeup) became a beauty term; 'smoky flavor' is prized in barbecue and some whiskeys; 'smoky bar' described the pre-smoking-ban atmosphere of pubs.
SMOKY.joke: Smoky eye: the makeup trend that named itself after atmospheric pollution and called it glamorous.

SMOTE.definition: Past tense of 'smite'; struck with a firm blow; afflicted; attacked.
SMOTE.origin: Old English 'smītan' (to smear, strike), past tense 'smāt'
SMOTE.evolution: Strong past tense of 'smite'; retained from Old English; almost exclusively used in biblical or ironic/humorous contexts now; 'he was smote' appears in informal writing as a past-passive construction.
SMOTE.joke: He was smote: the grammatical construction that requires a distant or angry deity to even attempt.

SNACK.definition: A small amount of food eaten between meals; an attractive person (slang); to eat snacks.
SNACK.origin: Middle Dutch 'snacken' (to snap), related to 'snatch'; attested 17th century
SNACK.evolution: From Middle Dutch meaning to snap or bite; originally meant a hasty bite or small quantity; modern slang 'snack' meaning an attractive person emerged online — someone worth consuming attention on; 'snack bar' provides quick small meals.
SNACK.joke: Calling someone a snack: the food-obsessed era's most complex compliment.

SNAIL.definition: A slow-moving mollusk with a spiral shell; a slow person or thing.
SNAIL.evolution: Old English zoological term; 'snail mail' emerged with email as a retronym for postal mail; 'at a snail's pace' is a speed metaphor; 'snail's pace' appeared in literature by the 16th century; snails have been eaten as food (escargot) since antiquity.
SNAIL.joke: Snail mail: the insult that stuck so hard the postal service now uses it in marketing.

SNAKE.definition: A limbless reptile; a treacherous person; to move in a winding course; to drag or pull.
SNAKE.origin: Old English 'snaca' (snake), from Proto-Germanic *snakon, from *snakan (to crawl)
SNAKE.evolution: Old English word for the reptile; 'snake in the grass' (hidden enemy) became a proverb by the 17th century; 'snake oil' (fraudulent remedy) entered American English from traveling medicine shows; 'snaking through traffic' uses the winding movement.
SNAKE.joke: Snake oil: the original product that set the bar for supplement marketing and never lowered it.

SNAKY.evolution: Extended from 'snake'; describes physical resemblance to a serpent (winding roads) or moral qualities (treachery); also used for hair in medusa-like descriptions; less common than its base word but present in literary contexts.
SNAKY.joke: Snaky: when winding roads and untrustworthy people share an adjective — both best avoided in bad weather.

SNARE.definition: A trap for catching animals; a trap generally; the wire on a snare drum; to trap.
SNARE.evolution: From Old Norse; the snare drum gets its name from the wires stretched across its lower head that vibrate and create the characteristic rattling sound; metaphorically, anything that traps or ensnares is a snare.
SNARE.joke: Snare drum: the drum that borrowed its name from a trap and uses it to keep everything on edge.

SNARL.definition: To growl with bared teeth; to speak angrily; a tangle; a traffic jam.
SNARL.origin: Two sources: growl sense from 'snar' (to snarl), related to Low German 'snarren'; tangle sense from 'snare'
SNARL.evolution: The growling sense is imitative; the tangle sense is related to 'snare' (to catch in a tangle); 'traffic snarl' (jam) perfectly captures both: vehicles tangled and drivers snarling; the two meanings reinforce each other.
SNARL.joke: Traffic snarl: the word that captures both the obstruction and the emotional response simultaneously.

SNEAK.definition: To move quietly and secretly; a furtive person; done without warning.
SNEAK.origin: Probably from Old English *snīcan (to crawl), related to Old Norse 'snīkja' (to hanker after); attested 16th century
SNEAK.evolution: Of uncertain Old English or Scandinavian origin; 'sneak preview' (an unannounced showing) uses the adjective sense; 'sneak attack' (Pearl Harbor) became a specific military and political term; 'sneakers' were named for their quiet rubber soles.
SNEAK.joke: Sneakers were named for sneaking: the athletic shoe whose entire brand identity is based on being quiet.

SNEER.definition: To smile or speak in a contemptuous manner; an expression of contempt.
SNEER.origin: Origin uncertain; possibly from Low German or Danish 'snerre' (to snarl, sneer); attested 17th century
SNEER.evolution: Of Low German or Scandinavian origin; a sneer is specifically contemptuous, combining a facial expression with attitude; 'sneering at' something means dismissing it contemptuously; the sneer is distinct from the smirk by its more open scorn.
SNEER.joke: A sneer: the facial expression that communicates 'I've already written my review and it's one star.'

SNIDE.definition: Derogatory or mocking in an indirect way; counterfeit.
SNIDE.origin: Origin uncertain; possibly from German 'schneiden' (to cut), or Low German 'snid' (cut, swindle); attested 19th century
SNIDE.evolution: Of uncertain origin; originally meant counterfeit or inferior; shifted to describe indirectly cutting or mocking remarks; 'snide comment' became the standard phrase — the criticism is there, just plausibly deniable.
SNIDE.joke: Snide: the art of insults you can still claim were compliments if questioned.

SNIFF.definition: To draw air audibly through the nose; to detect by smell; an act of sniffing.
SNIFF.origin: Middle English 'sniffen', related to 'snivel'; imitative/expressive formation
SNIFF.evolution: Imitative word; 'sniff out' means to detect or discover; 'not to be sniffed at' (not to be dismissed) uses the nose as a metaphor for judgment; 'dog sniffing out drugs' extended the literal detection use.
SNIFF.joke: Not to be sniffed at: the idiom that used a nose as a quality-control instrument.

SNIPE.definition: A wading bird; to shoot from a hiding place; to make sniping remarks; to bid on an auction at the last second.
SNIPE.origin: Old Norse 'snīpa' (snipe, the bird); the verb from the bird's behavior as a difficult target to hit
SNIPE.evolution: Named after the bird, which was notoriously difficult to shoot due to its erratic flight; soldiers who could hunt snipe were skilled enough to shoot from hiding ('snipers'); online auction sniping uses the last-second hidden attack meaning.
SNIPE.joke: The sniper: a military role named after a bird that successfully avoided this exact situation.

SNOOP.definition: To investigate or look around furtively; a person who snoops.
SNOOP.origin: Dutch 'snoepen' (to eat on the sly, to sneak), related to 'snoep' (candy, sweets)
SNOOP.evolution: From Dutch via American English; originally meant to eat sweets secretly; extended to any furtive investigation; 'snoopy' (the adjective and the beagle) uses this; privacy advocates worry about government snooping.
SNOOP.joke: Snooping: from stealing candy to surveillance capitalism — the word scaled faster than the behavior.

SNORE.definition: To breathe noisily during sleep; the sound made.
SNORE.origin: Middle English 'snoren'; imitative/expressive formation related to 'snort'
SNORE.evolution: Imitative word for the vibrating sound of airway obstruction during sleep; 'snore fest' means a boring event; 'what a snore' is a dismissal; sleep studies now treat snoring as a medical indicator for apnea.
SNORE.joke: A snore: the only sound that's both a medical symptom and a film review.

SNORT.definition: To force air violently through the nose; to express scorn; to inhale a drug; a short drink of spirits.
SNORT.evolution: Imitative formation; horses snort; humans snort with laughter or derision; 'snorting' illicit substances uses the forced inhalation meaning; 'a snort of whisky' is an informal British measure; 'snorkel' is a related instrument.
SNORT.joke: Snort: the only verb that covers laughter, scorn, horses, and recreational chemistry.

SNOUT.definition: The projecting nose and mouth of an animal; a person's nose (informal); the nozzle of a device.
SNOUT.origin: Middle Low German or Middle Dutch 'snūt' (snout), from Proto-Germanic *snutaz
SNOUT.evolution: From Low German; specifically the projecting muzzle of animals like pigs and dogs; informally applied to human noses; extended to the nozzle of pipes or machines; 'snout to tail eating' (using the whole animal) became a culinary philosophy.
SNOUT.joke: Snout-to-tail cooking: the culinary trend that decided the whole animal deserved equal billing.

SNOWY.definition: Covered with or resembling snow; pure white.
SNOWY.evolution: Adjectival form of 'snow'; 'snowy white' describes purity; 'snowy owl' is a white Arctic bird; 'snowy reception' (on old TV sets) described static interference that resembled falling snow.
SNOWY.joke: Snowy TV reception: the meteorological metaphor that described the chaos of analog signal failure.

SNUCK.definition: Past tense and past participle of 'sneak'; moved quietly and secretly.
SNUCK.origin: American English dialectal past tense of 'sneak'; irregular form that originated in North American English
SNUCK.evolution: Non-standard past tense of 'sneak' that originated in American dialects; 'sneaked' is the traditional standard form, but 'snuck' has become widely accepted; linguists track its gradual absorption into standard American English.
SNUCK.joke: Snuck vs. sneaked: the irregular verb that snuck into standard English without anyone's permission.

SNUFF.definition: Powdered tobacco inhaled through the nose; to extinguish a candle; to sniff; (slang) to kill.
SNUFF.origin: Two sources: tobacco sense from Dutch 'snuif' (snuff), from 'snuiven' (to sniff); extinguish sense from 'snuff' (charred wick), Middle English
SNUFF.evolution: Two etymologies converged; snuff (tobacco) was fashionable among the 18th-century aristocracy; 'snuff out' (extinguish) came from pinching a candle wick; 'up to snuff' (meeting standards) comes from the snuff sense.
SNUFF.joke: Up to snuff: the quality standard that began as a nasal judgment and became universal.

SOAPY.definition: Containing or resembling soap; fawning or flattering; resembling a soap opera.
SOAPY.evolution: Extended from soap's physical properties; 'soapy' praise is excessively flattering (slippery and not quite genuine); 'soapy drama' is melodramatic like a soap opera; soap operas were named after the soap company sponsors of early radio serials.
SOAPY.joke: Soap opera: the genre named after a cleaning product sponsor that somehow became the blueprint for all drama.

SOBER.definition: Not affected by alcohol; serious and thoughtful; (of color) not bright; to make or become sober.
SOBER.origin: Old French 'sobre', from Latin 'sobrius' (not drunk, temperate), from 'se-' (without) + 'ebrius' (drunk)
SOBER.evolution: From Latin 'without drunkenness'; extended to any serious, measured state; 'sober second thought' means reconsidering with clear judgment; color 'sober' means understated; 'sobering' means shock that produces serious reflection.
SOBER.joke: Sober second thought: the upgrade your first thought needed but was too excited to accept.

SOGGY.definition: Wet and soft; saturated with water; (of weather) damp and overcast.
SOGGY.origin: Possibly from dialectal 'sog' (a swamp, bog), origin uncertain; or from Old Norse *soggr (wet, damp)
SOGGY.evolution: Of uncertain origin; 'soggy bottom' became famous as a Great British Bake Off verdict for a pastry case that hasn't cooked through; describes the particular misery of wet fabric, bread, or ground.
SOGGY.joke: Soggy bottom: the baking failure that a TV show turned into the most discussed pastry defect in history.

SOLAR.definition: Of or relating to the sun; powered by the sun.
SOLAR.origin: Latin 'solaris' (of the sun), from 'sol' (sun), from Proto-Indo-European *sóh₂wl̥
SOLAR.evolution: From Latin 'sol' (sun); the solar system was named once the heliocentric model was accepted; 'solar plexus' (the nerve network) was named for its radiating structure; solar energy became a major technological focus in the 20th century.
SOLAR.joke: Solar plexus: the body part named after the sun that you only think about when someone punches you there.

SOLID.definition: Firm, hard, and stable; not hollow; reliable; a substance in solid state; a favor.
SOLID.origin: Old French 'solide', from Latin 'solidus' (solid, dense, whole), from Proto-Indo-European *sol- (whole)
SOLID.evolution: From Latin 'solidus' which also became a gold coin (giving us 'soldier' and the French 'sou'); 'solid performance' means reliable; 'do me a solid' (do me a favor) is American slang from the mid-20th century.
SOLID.joke: Do me a solid: slang that turned the physical state of matter into a unit of social currency.

SOLVE.definition: To find an answer to a problem; to dissolve (archaic).
SOLVE.origin: Latin 'solvere' (to loosen, dissolve, release), from 'se-' (apart) + 'luere' (to loosen)
SOLVE.evolution: From Latin 'to loosen'; originally meant to dissolve (chemically related to 'solvent'); extended to untangling or resolving problems; 'solution' carries both the chemical and problem-solving senses.
SOLVE.joke: Solve and dissolve share a root: in chemistry and math, the answer is usually 'loosen something.'

SONAR.definition: A system using sound waves to detect underwater objects.
SONAR.origin: Acronym: SOund Navigation And Ranging, coined during World War II
SONAR.evolution: WWII military acronym; used to detect submarines and underwater obstacles; bats and dolphins use biological sonar (echolocation); 'sonar' became the generic term while 'radar' (radio waves) became its aerial equivalent.
SONAR.joke: Sonar: bats and dolphins had it first, and neither of them needed an acronym.

SONIC.definition: Of or relating to sound waves; at or near the speed of sound.
SONIC.origin: Latin 'sonus' (sound) + '-ic'; from Proto-Indo-European *swon- (to make a sound)
SONIC.evolution: From Latin 'sonus' (sound); 'sonic boom' describes the shockwave when an aircraft exceeds the speed of sound; 'Sonic the Hedgehog' appropriated the speed association; sonic weapons and sonic branding are modern applications.
SONIC.joke: Sonic boom: the sound that proves the plane arrived before the news that it was coming.

SOOTH.origin: Old English 'sōþ' (true, genuine), from Proto-Germanic *sanþaz, from Proto-Indo-European *sont- (being, existing)
SOOTH.evolution: Old English word for truth; now primarily archaic except in 'soothsayer' (one who speaks truths/predicts the future) and 'forsooth' (in truth — now used ironically); 'soothe' is related, as it meant 'to assert as true,' then 'to prove true,' then 'to comfort.'
SOOTH.joke: Forsooth: the Old English word for 'truly' that survived only for people doing theatrical impressions.

SOOTY.definition: Covered with or colored like soot; black.
SOOTY.evolution: Extended from soot (the black carbon from incomplete combustion); 'sooty' describes chimneys, industrial areas, and the coloring of certain animals; 'Sooty' is a famous British bear puppet, yellow but named ironically.
SOOTY.joke: Sooty: the British bear puppet that was yellow but named after the darkest substance — peak children's TV logic.

SORRY.definition: Feeling regret or distress; in a poor condition; an apology.
SORRY.origin: Old English 'sārig' (distressed, full of sorrow), from 'sār' (pain, sore) + '-ig' (adjectival suffix)
SORRY.evolution: Originally meant 'full of pain or distress'; shifted to expressing regret for one's actions; 'sorry state' (pitiful condition) retains the original distress meaning; 'I'm sorry' is both apology and expression of sympathy.
SORRY.joke: Sorry: the word that does double duty as both an apology and sympathy, confusing recipients since Old English.

SOUND.definition: Vibrations that travel through air to be heard; a narrow strait of water; to make a noise; healthy and undamaged; sensible.
SOUND.origin: Three words: noise sense from Old French 'son', from Latin 'sonus'; water sense from Old Norse 'sund' (swimming, strait); healthy sense from Old English 'gesund'
SOUND.evolution: Three etymologically distinct words; 'soundly' means thoroughly; 'sound argument' means logically valid; 'sound asleep' uses the healthy/complete sense; a geographical sound (Puget Sound) is a wide passage between islands.
SOUND.joke: Sound argument, sound asleep, sound of music — one word, three completely different jobs, no overtime.

SOUTH.definition: The direction toward the South Pole; the southern part of a region.
SOUTH.origin: Old English 'sūþ' (south), from Proto-Germanic *sunþaz, possibly from *sunnō (sun) — the direction of the sun
SOUTH.evolution: Old English directional term; possibly derived from 'sun' (the direction facing the sun in the Northern Hemisphere); 'go south' meaning to deteriorate emerged in American English in the 20th century; 'the Deep South' refers to specific American states.
SOUTH.joke: Go south: directional geography that became American slang for catastrophic deterioration.

SOWER.definition: A person who sows seeds; one who disseminates ideas or discord.
SOWER.origin: Old English 'sāwere', from 'sāwan' (to sow), from Proto-Germanic *sēaną
SOWER.evolution: Agent noun from 'sow' (to plant seeds); 'the parable of the sower' (biblical) made 'sower' a religious and literary figure; 'sowing discord' (causing division) uses the agricultural metaphor of planting harmful seeds.
SOWER.joke: Sowing discord: planting seeds of conflict and then wondering why the harvest is so unpleasant.

SPACE.definition: A continuous area; outer space; a blank portion; interval of time; to arrange with spaces.
SPACE.origin: Old French 'espace', from Latin 'spatium' (area, room, extent of time)
SPACE.evolution: From Latin 'spatium'; first covered physical and temporal extent; 'outer space' became the dominant use post-1950s; 'spacing out' means losing focus; 'personal space' became a psychology concept; typographical spacing is essential to communication.
SPACE.joke: Personal space: the invisible boundary that nobody respects until you specifically say it exists.

SPADE.definition: A tool for digging; a suit of playing cards; to dig with a spade.
SPADE.origin: Old English 'spadu, spædu' (spade), from Proto-Germanic *spadō; card suit from Italian 'spade' (swords)
SPADE.evolution: Two words: the digging tool is Old English; the playing card suit is from Italian 'spade' meaning swords (the suit symbol resembles a sword point); 'call a spade a spade' (to speak plainly) dates to classical Greek.
SPADE.joke: Call a spade a spade: the proverb that demands directness, which everyone agrees with in principle and ignores in practice.

SPANK.definition: To slap on the buttocks; to move briskly; a slap on the buttocks.
SPANK.origin: Probably imitative; related to Low German 'spanken' (to smack, move briskly); attested 18th century
SPANK.evolution: Probably imitative of a slapping sound; 'spanking new' (brand new) and 'at a spanking pace' use the 'brisk' sense; the punishment sense became increasingly controversial as attitudes toward corporal punishment changed.
SPANK.joke: Spanking new: the freshness idiom that somehow survived despite its more controversial meaning dominating.

SPARE.definition: Available but not needed; thin; to refrain from harming; to give; an extra item.
SPARE.origin: Old English 'sparian' (to refrain from using), from Proto-Germanic *sparōjan
SPARE.evolution: Old English word with many senses; 'spare tire' is both emergency automotive and informal for belly fat; 'going spare' (British) means becoming very angry or anxious; 'spare the rod, spoil the child' is a biblical proverb.
SPARE.joke: Spare tire: simultaneously the most useful item in your trunk and the least welcome around your waist.

SPARK.definition: A small fiery particle; a flash of light; a trace of a quality; to ignite; to trigger.
SPARK.evolution: Old English word for small flaming particles; 'spark plug' ignites engine cylinders; 'a spark of intelligence' uses the igniting metaphor; 'sparking joy' (Marie Kondo's concept) extended it to emotional triggers; 'sparks fly' suggests conflict or attraction.
SPARK.joke: Spark joy: two words that turned tidying up into a philosophy and made billions of people feel guilty about their socks.

SPASM.definition: A sudden involuntary muscular contraction; a sudden brief burst of activity or emotion.
SPASM.origin: Latin 'spasmus', from Greek 'spasmos' (spasm, convulsion), from 'spaein' (to pull, tear)
SPASM.evolution: From Greek medicine; originally purely physical; 'spasm of activity' extended the involuntary burst to behavior; 'spasmodic' (intermittent, fitful) uses the irregular nature of muscle spasms as a metaphor.
SPASM.joke: Spasmodic effort: doing the work in involuntary bursts and calling it a work style.

SPAWN.definition: To produce eggs or offspring; to produce in large numbers; fish or frog eggs; the origin of something.
SPAWN.origin: Anglo-French 'espaundre' (to shed roe), from Old French 'espandre' (to spread out), from Latin 'expandere'
SPAWN.evolution: Originally specifically about fish laying eggs; extended to any prolific production; gaming adopted 'spawn point' for where characters appear; 'spawn of' implies being the unpleasant offspring of something; 'mushroom spawn' is mycelium.
SPAWN.joke: Spawn point: where video games send you after death — the only respawn mechanic that doesn't come with a therapy bill.

SPEAK.definition: To say words; to communicate verbally; to know and use a language.
SPEAK.origin: Old English 'sprecan, specan' (to speak), from Proto-Germanic *sprekaną
SPEAK.evolution: Old English fundamental communication verb; 'speak volumes' means to communicate a great deal without words; 'speak truth to power' became a political idiom; 'speakeasy' (Prohibition-era illegal bar) required speaking quietly at the door.
SPEAK.joke: Speakeasy: the bar that required whispering, proving that the best places always involve a password.

SPEAR.definition: A long weapon with a pointed tip; to pierce with a spear or sharp object.
SPEAR.evolution: One of humanity's oldest weapons; 'spearhead' means to lead an initiative; 'spear fishing' uses the literal implement; 'spear of asparagus' or 'spear of broccoli' uses the pointed shape; Shakespeare's name may etymologically relate to 'spear.'
SPEAR.joke: Spearhead: the military metaphor that became every project manager's favorite verb.

SPECK.definition: A tiny spot or particle; to mark with specks.
SPECK.origin: Old English 'specca' (small spot), origin uncertain; possibly related to Low German 'speck' (spot, speck)
SPECK.evolution: Old English for a tiny particle or spot; 'not a speck of truth' uses the minimizing sense; 'specks' in vision (floaters) are medically monitored; 'bespeckled' is an older form of freckled.
SPECK.joke: Not a speck of truth: measuring dishonesty in particle sizes — and finding total deficiency.

SPEED.definition: The rate at which something moves; success (archaic); to move fast; to wish success.
SPEED.evolution: Originally meant success or prosperity; shifted to rapidity of movement; 'Godspeed' (a wish for safe travel) preserves the original blessing sense; 'speed dating' and 'speed reading' prefix the efficiency sense; amphetamines are called 'speed.'
SPEED.joke: Godspeed: the travel blessing that survived by being shouted at departing people before anyone could ask what it meant.

SPELL.definition: To write or name letters in order; a period of time; a magic formula; an indefinite influence.
SPELL.origin: Three sources: letter sense from Old French 'espeller'; time sense from Old English 'spell' (narrative, story); magic sense from Old English 'spell' (speech, incantation)
SPELL.evolution: Multiple Old English and French words merged; 'spelling bee' (19th century) made letter-ordering competitive; 'under a spell' uses the magic sense; 'a spell of bad weather' uses the time sense; 'spellbound' combines the magic and attention senses.
SPELL.joke: Spelling bee: the contest that weaponized letter-sequencing and gave introverts a competition to win.

SPELT.definition: A type of ancient wheat grain; past tense of 'spell' in British English.
SPELT.origin: Two sources: grain from Old English 'spelt', from Late Latin 'spelta'; past tense from Old French 'espeller'
SPELT.evolution: The ancient grain 'spelt' (Triticum spelta) predates modern wheat varieties; had a health food revival in the 21st century; the past tense 'spelt' (vs. 'spelled') is the British form; 'how it's spelt' signals British English immediately.
SPELT.joke: Spelt: the grain that outlived its own wheat variety, survived as a British past tense, and now appears on artisan bread menus.

SPEND.definition: To pay out money; to use up time or energy.
SPEND.origin: Old English 'spendan' (to spend, use up), from Latin 'expendere' (to weigh out, pay) or 'dispendere'
SPEND.evolution: From Latin via Old English; 'spendthrift' (a wasteful person) literally means one who spends in a way that requires new thrift; 'spending quality time' elevated the concept from finance to relationships.
SPEND.joke: Quality time: when spending was elevated from financial to emotional — and it still costs you.

SPENT.definition: Past tense of 'spend'; used up; exhausted.
SPENT.evolution: Past participle of 'spend'; 'spent force' describes something whose energy is exhausted; 'spent ammunition' is used up; 'I'm spent' colloquially means completely exhausted; the word captures total depletion.
SPENT.joke: Spent: the one-word self-assessment that covers financial, physical, and emotional bankruptcy simultaneously.

SPERM.origin: Old French 'sperme', from Late Latin 'sperma', from Greek 'sperma' (seed), from 'speirein' (to sow)
SPERM.evolution: From Greek 'seed'; the 'sperm whale' was named for the spermaceti (waxy substance) found in its head, which whalers mistook for reproductive fluid; van Leeuwenhoek first observed sperm cells under a microscope in 1677.
SPERM.joke: The sperm whale was named based on a misidentification that nobody corrected, and somehow the whale has to live with that.

SPICE.definition: An aromatic plant substance used to flavor food; to add interest or excitement to.
SPICE.origin: Old French 'espice', from Latin 'species' (sort, kind, appearance), from 'specere' (to look)
SPICE.evolution: From Latin 'species' (kinds of goods); the spice trade drove global exploration and colonization; 'Spice Girls' appropriated the word's variety and vitality; 'spicing things up' is a universal metaphor for adding excitement.
SPICE.joke: The spice trade drove centuries of exploration — proving humanity will go anywhere for better food.

SPICY.definition: Flavored with spice; pungently hot; (informal) edgy or controversial.
SPICY.evolution: Extended from actual spice flavor; 'spicy take' (controversial opinion) entered social media vocabulary; 'spicy' content means adult or edgy material; the word straddles culinary heat and social controversy.
SPICY.joke: Spicy take: internet vocabulary for 'I said something controversial and am blaming the grammar.'

SPIED.definition: Past tense of 'spy'; observed secretly; caught sight of.
SPIED.origin: Old French 'espier' (to spy), from Frankish *spehōn, from Proto-Germanic *spehōną
SPIED.evolution: Past tense of 'spy'; 'I spy with my little eye' is a children's game; espionage networks 'spied' during cold wars; 'spy who loved me' extended the term to romantic intrigue.
SPIED.joke: I spy with my little eye: a children's game that taught surveillance techniques through repetition.

SPIEL.definition: A long or fast talk intended to persuade; to give a spiel.
SPIEL.origin: German or Yiddish 'spiel' (play, game), from Middle High German 'spil' (game, play)
SPIEL.evolution: From German/Yiddish into American English via Jewish immigrant communities; originally a theatrical performance or game; narrowed to a prepared persuasive speech, typically from a salesperson or con artist; 'spieler' is the person delivering it.
SPIEL.joke: Spiel: when a presentation becomes a performance and the presenter forgot the distinction.

SPIKE.definition: A thin, pointed piece of metal or wood; to impale on a spike; to add alcohol to a drink; to reject a news story.
SPIKE.origin: Middle Dutch or Middle Low German 'spīker' (nail), from Proto-Germanic *spīkaz
SPIKE.evolution: From Germanic for a large nail; 'spiking the punch' means adding alcohol; in journalism, spiking a story means killing it (stories were literally impaled on a spike); a spike in data means a sharp rise; volleyball spikes.
SPIKE.joke: Spiking a story: journalism's most literal editing technique — physically stabbing the article you didn't want to run.

SPIKY.definition: Having many spikes; having a sharp, defensive manner.
SPIKY.evolution: Extended from physical spikes to personality; 'spiky personality' means easily offended and defensive; 'spiky hair' uses the shape metaphor; the word effectively conveys both literal and social impenetrability.
SPIKY.joke: Spiky personality: when your social defense mechanisms have become visible to the naked eye.

SPILL.definition: To cause liquid to flow over the edge; to reveal information; a fall from a horse; a thin strip for lighting fires.
SPILL.origin: Old English 'spillan' (to destroy, waste), from Proto-Germanic *spiljaną
SPILL.evolution: Originally meant to destroy or shed (blood); narrowed to liquid overflow; 'spill the tea' (share gossip) entered African-American vernacular English and spread online; a 'spill' from a horse is a tumble.
SPILL.joke: Spill the tea: the gossip idiom that elevated accidental liquid disasters to intentional information sharing.

SPILT.definition: Past tense and past participle of 'spill'; caused to overflow.
SPILT.origin: Old English 'spillan' (to destroy, waste), past tense
SPILT.evolution: Variant past tense of 'spill'; both 'spilled' and 'spilt' are acceptable; 'no use crying over spilt milk' (don't lament unchangeable past events) is one of English's most durable proverbs.
SPILT.joke: No use crying over spilt milk: the first mindfulness advice ever recorded, delivered by a pragmatic farmer.

SPINE.definition: The vertebral column; the sharp needle of a hedgehog or cactus; the back edge of a book; courage.
SPINE.origin: Latin 'spina' (thorn, spine, backbone), from Proto-Indo-European *spei- (sharp point)
SPINE.evolution: From Latin thorn/backbone; 'spineless' (lacking courage) uses the backbone metaphor; the spine of a book is its bound edge; 'spine-chilling' describes something that causes a sensation along the vertebral column; cactus and hedgehog spines are literal.
SPINE.joke: Spineless: the anatomical metaphor for cowardice that vertebrate chauvinism made universal.

SPINY.definition: Having spines or sharp projections; difficult to handle.
SPINY.evolution: Extended from spine; 'spiny' creatures (hedgehogs, urchins, cacti) use this defensively; 'spiny problem' means thorny or difficult to handle — the same metaphor as 'thorny.'
SPINY.joke: A spiny problem: when the issue has enough sharp edges that no one wants to pick it up.

SPIRE.definition: A tapering conical structure on a tower; a spiral; to rise or taper to a point.
SPIRE.origin: Old English 'spīr' (stalk, blade of grass, tapering body), from Proto-Germanic *spiraz
SPIRE.evolution: Originally a long, thin blade or stalk; extended to the tapering towers of churches; the spire became the dominant visual feature of medieval cities; 'aspire' (to direct one's hopes upward) comes from the same Latin root.
SPIRE.joke: Spire and aspire share a root: churches built pointing up so you'd look up and then aspire upward.

SPITE.definition: A desire to harm or annoy; to deliberately harm or annoy.
SPITE.origin: Shortened from 'despite', from Old French 'despit', from Latin 'despectus' (contempt), from 'despicere' (to look down on)
SPITE.evolution: Short form of 'despite'; originally meant contempt or scorn; 'in spite of' (contrary to) preserves the original adversarial sense; 'out of spite' describes doing something purely to cause harm; 'spiteful' is the adjectival form.
SPITE.joke: Spite: the emotion that asks 'is this worth it?' and answers 'yes, absolutely' every single time.

SPLAT.definition: To hit and flatten with a wet impact; the sound of such impact.
SPLAT.origin: Imitative/expressive; from the sound of something wet hitting a surface; attested 19th century
SPLAT.evolution: Onomatopoeic formation; the sound of a tomato hitting a wall; comic book uses 'SPLAT!' as a sound effect; 'splatter' extends the dispersal; 'splat' in skydiving slang is extremely morbid humor.
SPLAT.joke: SPLAT: the onomatopoeia that did everything action movies needed and still made the sound effects department redundant.

SPLIT.definition: To divide into parts; a division; a dessert of ice cream and fruit; a gymnastic position.
SPLIT.origin: Middle Dutch 'splitten' (to split, cleave), from Proto-Germanic *splaitan
SPLIT.evolution: From Middle Dutch; 'banana split' (the dessert) emerged in the 20th century; 'do the splits' is the gymnastic position; 'splitting headache' means severe; 'stock split' divides share value; 'split decision' in boxing is contested.
SPLIT.joke: Splitting the bill: the mathematical exercise that ends more friendships than the actual dinner.

SPOIL.definition: To damage; to become unfit for use; to over-indulge a child; goods taken by force.
SPOIL.origin: Old French 'espoillier' (to despoil), from Latin 'spoliare' (to strip, plunder), from 'spolium' (skin stripped from an animal)
SPOIL.evolution: Originally meant to plunder; 'spoils of war' preserves this; extended to corrupting or damaging; 'spoil a child' means to over-indulge; 'spoiler alert' warns readers about plot reveals; food 'spoils' when it becomes unsafe.
SPOIL.joke: Spoiler alert: the announcement that arrives just milliseconds after the information you were trying to avoid.

SPOKE.definition: A rod connecting hub to rim of a wheel; past tense of 'speak'.
SPOKE.origin: Two words: wheel sense from Old English 'spāca' (spoke of a wheel); past tense from Old English 'specan' (to speak)
SPOKE.evolution: Two Old English words coincide; 'put a spoke in someone's wheel' means to hinder them (inserting a stick to stop a wheel); the past tense 'spoke' is irregular; bicycle spokes provide structural support while minimizing weight.
SPOKE.joke: Put a spoke in the wheel: the sabotage idiom that requires you to carry a stick everywhere as a precaution.

SPOOF.definition: A parody or hoax; to parody or hoax; to falsify a signal or message.
SPOOF.origin: Invented by British comedian Arthur Roberts in 1884 as the name of a card game; etymology is intentionally obscure
SPOOF.evolution: Invented word with a known creator; spread from a game name to mean any hoax; extended to parody films and TV shows; cybersecurity 'spoofing' means falsifying the source of communications — the modern digital con.
SPOOF.joke: Spoof: invented as a meaningless word in 1884, now describing everything from SNL sketches to phishing emails.

SPOOK.definition: A ghost; a spy; to frighten; to become frightened.
SPOOK.origin: Dutch 'spook' (ghost, specter), from Middle Low German 'spōk'
SPOOK.evolution: From Dutch; originally just a ghost; CIA slang adopted 'spook' for intelligence agents (operating covertly like ghosts); 'spooked' means startled or frightened; horses that spook shy suddenly at perceived threats.
SPOOK.joke: Spook: the word that covers ghosts, CIA agents, and horses — all of whom are best handled with caution and no sudden movements.

SPOOL.definition: A cylinder on which something is wound; to wind onto a spool; to send data to a printer buffer.
SPOOL.origin: Old French 'espole' or Middle Dutch 'spoele' (spool, bobbin), of Germanic origin
SPOOL.evolution: From Old French/Dutch; computing 'spooling' (Simultaneous Peripheral Operations On-Line) manages print jobs in a queue buffer — an acronym backfitted to an existing word; thread and film spools preceded digital data spools.
SPOOL.joke: SPOOL: the acronym that used an existing word about thread to describe something that has nothing to do with thread.

SPOON.definition: A utensil with a bowl-shaped end; to transfer with a spoon; to lie in a curved position together.
SPOON.origin: Old English 'spōn' (chip of wood, splinter), from Proto-Germanic *spōnuz
SPOON.evolution: Originally a chip of wood or flat piece; became the eating utensil; 'born with a silver spoon' means born wealthy; 'spooning' (cuddling in aligned curved positions) emerged as slang; the 'spoon theory' of chronic illness explains limited energy reserves.
SPOON.joke: Spooning: when two people decide that parallel parking was the right analogy for intimacy.

SPORE.definition: A reproductive cell produced by fungi, algae, and plants that can grow into a new organism.
SPORE.origin: Greek 'spora' (seed, sowing), from 'speirein' (to sow), related to 'sperm'
SPORE.evolution: From Greek 'sowing'; botanists adopted it for the reproductive cells of non-flowering plants and fungi; 'sporadic' (occurring at irregular intervals) shares the root — scattered like spores.
SPORE.joke: Spore and sporadic share a root — scattered seeds and irregular events both refusing to follow a schedule.

SPORT.definition: An athletic activity; a person who accepts defeat graciously; to wear or display; to play.
SPORT.origin: Short for 'disport', from Old French 'desporter' (to carry away from work), from 'des-' + 'porter' (to carry)
SPORT.evolution: Contraction of 'disport' (to amuse oneself); 'good sport' means a gracious loser; 'sporting chance' is a fair opportunity; 'sportsmanship' codified behavioral expectations; 'sport' as a term of address is Australian/NZ familiarity.
SPORT.joke: Be a sport: the request that expects graciousness you may not currently be feeling.

SPOUT.definition: A tube or lip through which liquid pours; to send out liquid in a stream; to speak at length.
SPOUT.origin: Middle English 'spouten', from Middle Dutch 'spuiten' (to spout, squirt), related to 'spew'
SPOUT.evolution: From Middle Dutch; 'up the spout' (British) means ruined or pawned — goods sent via tube to a pawnbroker's upper floor; 'spouting off' means talking at length without restraint; a teapot spout became the standard reference.
SPOUT.joke: Up the spout: the British idiom whose plumbing metaphor landed so perfectly that everyone accepted 'ruined' for 'pawnshop elevator.'

SPRAY.definition: Liquid dispersed in fine droplets; a small branch with flowers; to scatter in fine droplets.
SPRAY.origin: Two words: liquid sense from Middle Dutch 'spraeyen' (to spray); plant sense from Old English *sprǣg (spray, twig)
SPRAY.evolution: Two unrelated words converged; the liquid sense describes atomized droplets; a spray of flowers is a small decorative branch; 'hairspray' and 'spray paint' are compounds; 'spray and pray' is informal for firing automatic weapons inaccurately.
SPRAY.joke: Spray and pray: the military technique that weaponizes optimism.

SPREE.definition: A lively, usually brief period of doing something without restraint.
SPREE.origin: Possibly from Scots/Irish 'spréidh' (cattle raid, booty), from Old Irish; or from French 'esprit' (spirit); origin uncertain
SPREE.evolution: Of uncertain origin; 'shopping spree,' 'spending spree,' 'killing spree' — the word amplifies both positive and negative activities; the implication is excess and lack of restraint; a 'crime spree' is a series of crimes in rapid succession.
SPREE.joke: Shopping spree and killing spree: the word 'spree' promises enthusiasm and does not specify what for.

SPRIG.definition: A small stem bearing leaves or flowers; to decorate with sprigs.
SPRIG.origin: Middle English 'sprigge', of Low German or Scandinavian origin; related to Low German 'sprick' (twig)
SPRIG.evolution: From Low German; used in cooking ('a sprig of thyme'), floral design, and heraldry; a 'sprig of holly' is a classic Christmas decorative element; 'besprigged' fabric has small floral patterns.
SPRIG.joke: A sprig of something: the recipe's polite way of saying 'a small amount that looks intentional.'

SPUNK.definition: Courage and determination; (British slang, vulgar) semen; tinder or touchwood.
SPUNK.origin: Scottish Gaelic 'spong' (tinder, touchwood, spark), related to Irish 'sponc'
SPUNK.evolution: From Gaelic 'tinder' (material that catches a spark); extended metaphorically to the spark of spirit and courage; British slang developed a vulgar sense; 'spunky' means spirited and courageous in American English.
SPUNK.joke: Spunky in America means courageous. In Britain it requires context. The word is genuinely brave.

SPURN.definition: To reject with contempt; to kick away.
SPURN.origin: Old English 'spurnan, spornan' (to kick, strike against), from Proto-Germanic *spurnan
SPURN.evolution: Originally meant to kick; extended to metaphorical rejection with contempt; 'spurned lover' became a literary archetype; the physical rejection (kicking away) and social rejection (dismissing contemptuously) merged.
SPURN.joke: Spurned: the past tense of rejection that implies a foot was involved, which somehow makes it worse.

SPURT.definition: To gush out in a sudden stream; a sudden burst of activity or speed.
SPURT.origin: Possibly from Old English *spryttan (to sprout), related to 'sprit'; or Low German 'sprutten'; attested 16th century
SPURT.evolution: Of uncertain Germanic origin; describes sudden forceful ejection of liquid; 'a spurt of productivity' uses the burst metaphor; 'final spurt' in a race is the last burst of speed; 'growth spurt' describes rapid physical development.
SPURT.joke: Growth spurt: when the body decides that gradual change is for people with a plan.

SQUAD.definition: A small group of people sharing a task; a sports team; a close group of friends.
SQUAD.origin: French 'escouade', from Spanish 'escuadra' or Italian 'squadra' (square), from Vulgar Latin *exquadrare (to make square)
SQUAD.evolution: Military term for the smallest tactical unit; extended to police and sports teams; 'squad goals' became social media shorthand for an enviable friend group; 'death squad' and 'fashion squad' show the word's extreme range.
SQUAD.joke: Squad goals: the moment military organization became a lifestyle aspiration on Instagram.

SQUAT.definition: To sit on one's heels; to occupy a building illegally; short and thickset; a weightlifting exercise.
SQUAT.origin: Old French 'esquatir' (to press flat), from Vulgar Latin *excoactire, from Latin 'coactus' (forced)
SQUAT.evolution: From Old French 'to press flat'; the physical position of crouching; 'squat' as an adjective means short and wide; squatting in empty buildings became a political and housing movement; the squat (exercise) builds leg strength.
SQUAT.joke: Squat: simultaneously a protest movement, a fitness routine, and a polite way to describe someone's proportions.

SQUIB.definition: A small firework; a short satirical piece; a damp squib (something disappointing).
SQUIB.origin: Origin uncertain; possibly imitative of the hissing-then-bang of a small firework; attested 16th century
SQUIB.evolution: Possibly imitative; a squib is a small firework or a short satirical writing piece; 'damp squib' (something that fails to impress) comes from a wet firework that fizzles rather than exploding — often mistakenly written as 'damp squid.'
SQUIB.joke: Damp squib: the firework metaphor for disappointment, routinely misspelled as a wet cephalopod.

STACK.definition: A pile of objects; a chimney stack; a large number; to pile up; to arrange cards fraudulently.
STACK.evolution: From Old Norse haystack; extended to any pile; computing adopted 'stack' for a data structure (LIFO); 'stacked' means both physically piled high and (slang) well-built; 'the deck is stacked against you' means conditions are unfair.
STACK.joke: Stack overflow: the programmer's error that became a website and an identity crisis for developers worldwide.

STAFF.definition: A long stick; employees of an organization; a musical notation system; to provide with staff.
STAFF.evolution: Originally a walking stick or weapon; 'staff' as employees derives from a military sense of the stick as a symbol of authority; the musical staff is the five lines on which notes are written; 'understaffed' means lacking sufficient employees.
STAFF.joke: Staff meeting: the gathering of employees named after a stick — which explains the energy level.

STAGE.definition: A raised platform for performance; a phase in a process; a stagecoach stop; to perform or produce; to arrange deceptively.
STAGE.origin: Old French 'estage' (floor, story, position), from Vulgar Latin *staticum (position), from Latin 'stare' (to stand)
STAGE.evolution: From Latin 'to stand'; originally a floor or story; became the theatrical platform; 'stage fright' is performance anxiety; 'life is a stage' (Shakespeare) made the metaphor universal; 'staged photo' means artificially arranged.
STAGE.joke: Life is a stage and we are merely players — Shakespeare's way of saying nobody's taking responsibility.

STAID.origin: Past participle of archaic 'stay' (to restrain), from Old French 'estayer' (to prop up)
STAID.evolution: Originally past participle of 'stay' (restrained); became an adjective meaning settled, sober, and dull; 'staid institution' implies it hasn't changed in decades; the word itself sounds appropriately dull.
STAID.joke: Staid: a word so thoroughly respectable that it sounds exactly like what it means.

STAIN.definition: A discoloration or spot; a cause of disgrace; to discolor; to tint wood or glass.
STAIN.origin: Short for 'distain', from Old French 'desteindre' (to tinge, discolor), from Latin 'dis-' + 'tingere' (to dye)
STAIN.evolution: Shortened from 'distain'; both physical marks and moral blemishes; 'stained glass' uses controlled coloring; 'staining' biological specimens helps microscopy; 'without a stain on his character' means an untarnished reputation.
STAIN.joke: Stained glass: when stains become art — the church's way of making the best of spilled something.

STAIR.definition: A single step of a staircase; stairs (plural): a set of steps between floors.
STAIR.origin: Old English 'stǣger' (staircase), from Proto-Germanic *staigri, from *steigan (to climb)
STAIR.evolution: Old English word for ascending; 'staircase' and 'stairway' are compounds; 'stairway to heaven' (Zeppelin and biblical) uses the metaphorical ascent; 'up the stairs' and 'downstairs/upstairs' indicate floor levels.
STAIR.joke: Stairway to heaven: either Led Zeppelin's best song or the theological justification for multi-story buildings.

STAKE.definition: A strong pointed post; money wagered; a share in a business; to support with stakes; to risk.
STAKE.evolution: Originally a pointed post; execution by burning at the stake; 'stakes are high' means the risk/reward is great; 'stake a claim' means to assert ownership (from gold mining); 'stakeholder' emerged as business jargon.
STAKE.joke: Stakeholder: the gold rush term that became every consultant's favorite noun without gaining any of the gold.

STALE.definition: No longer fresh; no longer new or interesting; to make or become stale.
STALE.origin: Possibly from Old French 'estale' (standing still), or from Middle Dutch/Low German 'stāl' (stagnant); attested 14th century
STALE.evolution: Of uncertain origin; 'stale bread' and 'stale air' are primary uses; 'stale joke' means one heard too many times; in chess, 'stalemate' (a draw where a player has no legal moves) is from the same root.
STALE.joke: Stalemate: when stale bread somehow described a chess draw and everyone accepted it.

STALK.definition: The main stem of a plant; to pursue obsessively; to walk in a stiff, dignified manner.
STALK.origin: Plant sense from Middle English 'stalke', related to Old English 'stæl' (stalk); pursuit sense may derive from hunting 'stalking'
STALK.evolution: Plant and hunting senses from related Middle English roots; hunting stalking meant quiet, methodical pursuit; the obsessive following of a person became 'stalking' as a legal concept in the 1990s; 'stalk' someone on social media is now routine.
STALK.joke: Stalking someone on Instagram: hunting metaphor, plant biology, and a privacy law, all in one swipe.

STALL.definition: A compartment in a stable; a market booth; to stop running; to delay or obstruct.
STALL.origin: Old English 'steall' (stall, standing place), from Proto-Germanic *stallaz
STALL.evolution: Originally a standing place for animals; extended to market stalls; 'stall for time' means to delay; an aircraft 'stalls' when it loses lift; an engine 'stalls' when it stops; 'installed' (put in a stall) is the origin of modern 'install.'
STALL.joke: Install and stall share a root: the history of software installation confirmed the relationship.

STAMP.definition: To bring the foot down heavily; a postage stamp; an official mark; a characteristic impression.
STAMP.origin: Old English *stampian (to stamp, pound), from Proto-Germanic *stampōjan
STAMP.evolution: Old English for pounding motion; postage stamps (Penny Black, 1840) transformed communication; 'rubber stamp' means to approve automatically without scrutiny; 'stamped out' means eliminated; 'leaving one's stamp' means having a lasting influence.
STAMP.joke: Rubber stamp: the office supply that became the metaphor for decisions made without thinking.

STAND.definition: To be in an upright position; to withstand; a halt; a position or opinion; a frame for holding things.
STAND.evolution: Old English fundamental positional verb; 'take a stand' means to adopt a firm position; one-night stand (brief romantic encounter) emerged in American English; a 'stand' of trees is a group; 'last stand' is a final defense.
STAND.joke: One-night stand: the furniture metaphor that became the most optimistic rebranding in relationship terminology.

STANK.definition: Past tense of 'stink'; emitted a strong unpleasant odor.
STANK.origin: Old English 'stincan' (to emit a smell), past tense 'stanc'; from Proto-Germanic *stenkwanan
STANK.evolution: Strong past tense of 'stink'; 'stank' vs. 'stunk' — both are acceptable but 'stank' is the older strong form; colloquially, 'it stank' describes poor performance; 'stank face' is slang for a grimace indicating something is good (like a bass line).
STANK.joke: Stank face: the facial expression that means something is so good it smells bad — music theory's most confusing compliment.

STARE.definition: To look fixedly with wide-open eyes; such a gaze.
STARE.origin: Old English 'starian' (to stare), from Proto-Germanic *starōjan, related to 'stark'
STARE.evolution: Old English for intense gazing; 'stare into the abyss' (from Nietzsche) became a cultural reference for confronting existential truth; 'staring contest' is a game; 'it's staring you in the face' means it's obvious.
STARE.joke: Stare into the abyss and it stares back: Nietzsche's warning that the internet would later prove empirically.

STARK.definition: Severe and unadorned; complete or sheer; stiff or rigid.
STARK.origin: Old English 'stearc' (stiff, strong, severe), from Proto-Germanic *starkaz
STARK.evolution: Old English for rigid or strong; 'stark naked' uses the 'complete/bare' sense; 'stark raving mad' emphasizes absolute madness; 'a stark landscape' has austere, unadorned quality; the Stark family name in Game of Thrones evokes this severity.
STARK.joke: Stark raving mad: the only mental health assessment delivered entirely in superlatives.

START.definition: To begin; to cause to operate; to move suddenly; a beginning; a sudden involuntary movement.
START.origin: Old English 'styrtan' (to leap up), from Proto-Germanic *sturtjan
START.evolution: Originally meant a sudden leap or jump; extended to beginning any action; 'head start' means an advantage from starting earlier; 'starter' covers both beginnings (starter home) and ignition; 'false start' is premature beginning.
START.joke: Head start: the competitive advantage of getting there before anyone announced there would be a race.

STASH.definition: To store secretly; a secret store.
STASH.origin: Origin uncertain; possibly blend of 'store' and 'cache', or from 'stave' and 'dash'; attested 18th century
STASH.evolution: Of uncertain origin; possibly British criminal slang; 'emergency stash' and 'snack stash' are benign; 'cash stash' and 'drug stash' are less so; the word implies secrecy and private hoarding.
STASH.joke: Secret stash: redundant by definition, yet everyone announces having one when they find it.

STATE.definition: A condition; a nation or political entity; a grand ceremony; to express in words; provided by the government.
STATE.origin: Old French 'estat', from Latin 'status' (condition, standing), from 'stare' (to stand)
STATE.evolution: From Latin 'status' (standing); 'state of the art' means the highest current development; 'state funeral' uses the grand ceremony sense; 'head of state' vs. 'head of government' is a constitutional distinction; 'stating the obvious' is a common complaint.
STATE.joke: State the obvious: the instruction that assumes shared observation but rarely confirms it.

STAVE.definition: A strong stick or staff; a curved strip of wood forming a barrel; a musical staff; to break a hole in; to avert.
STAVE.evolution: Back-formed from the plural of 'staff'; barrel staves are the curved wooden strips that form the sides; a musical stave has five lines; 'stave off' (to ward off) uses the stick-wielding sense of keeping something at bay.
STAVE.joke: Stave off disaster: keeping catastrophe away with the determination of someone swinging a wooden plank.

STEAD.definition: The place or role that someone or something should occupy (used in 'instead' and 'steadfast').
STEAD.origin: Old English 'stede' (place, position), from Proto-Germanic *stadiz, from *standan (to stand)
STEAD.evolution: Old English word for 'place'; rarely used alone now, but survives in 'instead' (in the place of), 'steadfast' (fixed in place), 'homestead,' 'bedstead,' and 'farmstead'; 'stand in good stead' means to be of advantage.
STEAD.joke: Stead: the word that gave up independent existence to prop up 'instead' and 'steadfast' indefinitely.

STEAK.definition: A thick slice of meat or fish for grilling or frying.
STEAK.origin: Old Norse 'steik' (roast, meat on a spit), from 'steikja' (to roast on a spit)
STEAK.evolution: From Old Norse; steak cuts (filet, ribeye, sirloin) have their own hierarchies and price points; 'steak and chips' is British comfort food; 'well-done steak' is considered a culinary crime by some; steakhouses became the power dining venue of choice.
STEAK.joke: Well-done steak: the one order that makes a chef question their career choices.

STEAL.definition: To take something without permission; to move stealthily; a bargain (informal).
STEAL.evolution: Old English fundamental word; 'steal the show' means to outperform everyone else; 'steal a march' means to gain an advantage secretly; 'it's a steal' (a bargain) ironically uses the theft metaphor for a good deal.
STEAL.joke: It's a steal: the one context where theft is the compliment the seller intended.

STEAM.definition: Water vapor; energy or momentum; to cook with steam; to move with power; to be very angry.
STEAM.evolution: Old English for vapor; 'steam engine' powered the Industrial Revolution; 'let off steam' means to release tension; 'full steam ahead' means proceed with maximum effort; 'steaming mad' uses the hot, pressurized anger metaphor.
STEAM.joke: Let off steam: the Industrial Revolution's gift to anger management — find a valve and use it.

STEED.definition: A horse, especially a spirited one (literary/archaic).
STEED.origin: Old English 'stēda' (stallion, stud horse), from Proto-Germanic *stōdijōn, from *stōdaz (stud)
STEED.evolution: Old English for a stallion or quality horse; 'steed' is now used primarily in literary, historical, or humorous contexts; medieval knights rode noble steeds; the word implies a noble, powerful horse rather than any horse.
STEED.joke: Trusty steed: the horse's job title in medieval literature — still awaiting formal HR recognition.

STEEL.definition: A hard, strong alloy of iron and carbon; to prepare oneself mentally.
STEEL.evolution: Old English metal; 'steel yourself' means to mentally prepare for something difficult; 'nerves of steel' means extreme calmness under pressure; 'steel trap mind' describes sharp intelligence; 'Pittsburgh Steelers' and 'steel industry' shaped industrial identity.
STEEL.joke: Nerves of steel: the compliment for people who function in situations where most collapse.

STEEP.definition: Having a sharp incline; (of prices) excessive; to soak in liquid; to pervade thoroughly.
STEEP.origin: Two words: adjective from Old English 'stēap' (high, deep); verb from Middle English 'stepen', related to Swedish 'stöpa'
STEEP.evolution: Two different words; 'steeped in history' uses the soaking metaphor to mean thoroughly imbued; 'that's a bit steep' (British) means the price is too high; steep learning curve now paradoxically means difficult, despite steepness indicating quick learning.
STEEP.joke: Steep learning curve: paradoxically means difficult when steep should mean fast — nobody checked the graph.

STEER.definition: To guide the direction of; a piece of advice; a young male bovine.
STEER.origin: Two words: guide sense from Old English 'stieran' (to steer); bovine from Old English 'stēor' (young ox)
STEER.evolution: Two Old English words; the vehicle steering sense gave us 'steering wheel' and 'steer clear of'; 'to steer someone in the right direction' is advisory guidance; the castrated male bovine is a steer (as opposed to a bull).
STEER.joke: Steer clear: navigational advice that somehow became universally applicable to situations with no vehicle involved.

STEIN.definition: A large earthenware beer mug, typically German.
STEIN.origin: German 'Stein' (stone), short for 'Steinzeugkrug' (stoneware jug); named for the stone or ceramic material
STEIN.evolution: From German 'stone'; steins are associated with Bavarian beer culture and Oktoberfest; often have hinged lids (originally to keep insects out); German steins can hold 1 liter; became a symbol of German drinking culture worldwide.
STEIN.joke: A stein: the cup that asks 'would you like more?' before you've finished answering the first question.

STERN.definition: Serious and unrelenting; strict; the rear part of a ship.
STERN.origin: Two words: character sense from Old English 'styrne' (stern, strict); nautical sense from Old Norse 'stjórn' (steering)
STERN.evolution: Two Old English/Norse words; the 'stern of a ship' is the back (steering end); 'stern look' and 'stern words' use the serious/unyielding sense; 'sternly' often precedes a reprimand; a 'stern face' is set and unsmiling.
STERN.joke: Stern face: the expression that communicates 'I am both the back of this ship and the one steering it.'

STICK.definition: A thin piece of wood; to attach or remain fixed; a poke with a pointed object; to perplex.
STICK.origin: Old English 'sticca' (stick, peg), from Proto-Germanic *stikkan; related to Old English 'stician' (to stick)
STICK.evolution: Old English for a thin piece of wood; 'stick to your guns' means persist in your position; 'the wrong end of the stick' means misunderstanding; 'stick in the mud' is an unadventurous person; 'get the stick' in British English means to be reprimanded.
STICK.joke: Wrong end of the stick: the misunderstanding metaphor that proves all tools can be mishandled.

STIFF.definition: Not easily bent; formal and constrained; (slang) a corpse; to cheat out of a tip; to fail.
STIFF.evolution: Old English for rigid; 'stiff upper lip' (British composure under pressure) became a national stereotype; 'stiff drink' is a strong alcoholic drink; 'working stiff' means an ordinary employee; to 'stiff someone' means to cheat them of payment.
STIFF.joke: Stiff upper lip: the British strategy of treating dignity as an alternative to acknowledging feelings.

STILL.definition: Not moving or making a sound; continuing; even; a distilling apparatus; to make calm.
STILL.evolution: Old English for quiet and motionless; 'still waters run deep' is a proverb about quiet people having hidden depths; a 'still' life in art depicts inanimate objects; a distilling 'still' makes spirits; 'still going strong' uses the continuation sense.
STILL.joke: Still waters run deep: the compliment given to quiet people that nobody asked them to verify.

STILT.definition: A long pole on which a person walks above the ground; a pole supporting a structure above water.
STILT.origin: Middle Low German or Middle Dutch 'stelte' (stilt, crutch), from Proto-Germanic *steltijō
STILT.evolution: From Low German/Dutch; stilts raise a person or structure above ground level; 'stilted language' (pompous, formal) uses the raised, artificial elevation as a metaphor; stilt houses are practical in flood-prone areas.
STILT.joke: Stilted speech: prose so elevated above its subject matter that communication can no longer make eye contact.

STING.definition: To wound with a stinger; to cause sharp pain; a sharp pain; a swindle operation.
STING.origin: Old English 'stingan' (to sting, stab), from Proto-Germanic *stingwan
STING.evolution: Old English for sharp wounding; 'sting operation' (undercover police operation) emerged in American English; 'the sting' is a classic film about a con; 'stinging criticism' uses the sharp pain metaphor; bees, jellyfish, and harsh words all sting.
STING.joke: Sting operation: when law enforcement borrowed from entomology and film noir simultaneously.

STINK.definition: To emit a strong unpleasant smell; to be very bad; a strong unpleasant smell; a fuss.
STINK.origin: Old English 'stincan' (to emit a smell), from Proto-Germanic *stenkwanan
STINK.evolution: Old English olfactory word; 'raise a stink' means to cause a commotion (transferring smell to noise); 'this stinks' means something is terrible; 'stinkbug' and 'stinkweed' use literal odor; 'stink bomb' is both a prank device and a failed political grenade.
STINK.joke: Raise a stink: the protest metaphor that hopes smell has the political power of a march.

STINT.definition: A person's fixed period of work; to restrict to a small allowance; a small sandpiper.
STINT.origin: Old English 'styntan' (to blunt, stop short), from Proto-Germanic *stuntijan
STINT.evolution: Originally meant to make blunt or stop short; extended to limiting or restricting; 'don't stint on quality' means don't cut corners; a 'stint' as a period of work uses the 'fixed amount' sense; the tiny wading bird is also named 'stint.'
STINT.joke: Don't stint on quality: the instruction everyone agrees with and everyone ignores when the budget arrives.

STOCK.definition: A supply kept for use; shares in a company; the handle of a gun; farm animals; to keep in supply; standard or routine.
STOCK.origin: Old English 'stocc' (trunk of a tree, post, stock), from Proto-Germanic *stukkaz
STOCK.evolution: Originally a tree trunk or post; extended to a supply stored like goods in a storehouse; financial 'stock' (shares) and 'livestock' both developed from the stored supply sense; 'stock response' means a predictable, routine one.
STOCK.joke: Stock response: the phrase you give before actually thinking — the intellectual equivalent of a pre-packaged answer.

STOIC.definition: Enduring hardship without showing feelings; a person who shows such endurance; a follower of Stoicism.
STOIC.origin: Latin 'stoicus', from Greek 'stōikos' (of the Stoa), from 'stoa' (porch) — Zeno taught at the Stoa Poikile in Athens
STOIC.evolution: From the Stoa Poikile (painted porch) where Zeno of Citium taught around 300 BCE; Stoicism emphasized virtue, reason, and indifference to external events; 'stoic' as an adjective describes someone enduring hardship without complaint.
STOIC.joke: Stoic philosophy: named after a porch. Zeno chose the most inconspicuous classroom in history.

STOKE.definition: To tend and add fuel to a fire; to encourage or incite; to be excited (slang).
STOKE.origin: Middle Dutch 'stoken' (to poke a fire), from 'stoc' (stick, post), related to 'stock'
STOKE.evolution: From Middle Dutch for fire-tending; a 'stoker' maintains furnaces on ships; 'stoke fear' or 'stoke enthusiasm' uses the fire-feeding metaphor; 'stoked' (excited) is surfing and skateboarding slang from the 1960s.
STOKE.joke: Stoked: when surfer culture borrowed from industrial furnace maintenance to describe excitement.

STOLE.definition: A long scarf or ecclesiastical vestment; past tense of 'steal'.
STOLE.origin: Two words: garment from Latin 'stola', from Greek 'stolē' (garment); past tense from Old English 'stelan'
STOLE.evolution: Two unrelated words; the vestment 'stole' is worn by priests and bishops; 'she stole my heart' uses the past tense of stealing metaphorically for love; both words occasionally surprise people with their homophone status.
STOLE.joke: He stole her heart and wore it as a stole — a romance and a fashion crisis simultaneously.

STOMP.definition: To tread heavily and noisily; a heavy noisy tread; a lively dance.
STOMP.origin: Variant of 'stamp', from Old English *stampian (to stamp, pound), influenced by dialectal forms
STOMP.evolution: Dialectal variant of 'stamp'; 'stomp' conveys more weight and noise; 'stomp around' means to walk heavily in anger; jazz 'stomps' are lively, vigorous compositions; 'Stomp' the percussion show uses found objects as instruments.
STOMP.joke: Jazz stomp: when musicians decided that feet were an underutilized percussion section.

STONE.definition: A piece of rock; a hard seed pit; a unit of weight (14 pounds); to throw stones at; completely (stone cold).
STONE.evolution: Old English fundamental word; 'leave no stone unturned' means to be exhaustively thorough; 'stone cold' means completely cold; British unit of weight (14 lbs); 'stoned' (under the influence of drugs) emerged in 20th-century American slang.
STONE.joke: Leave no stone unturned: the thoroughness mandate that nobody applies to their actual housekeeping.

STONY.definition: Full of or covered with stones; (of a look) cold and unfeeling.
STONY.evolution: Extended from physical stone; 'stony silence' and 'stony glare' use the hard, cold quality of stone as metaphors for emotional coldness; 'stony broke' (British, completely penniless) uses 'stony' as an intensifier.
STONY.joke: Stony broke: when broke needed a geological intensifier to communicate proper severity.

STOOD.definition: Past tense and past participle of 'stand'; was in an upright position.
STOOD.origin: Old English 'standan' (to stand), past tense 'stōd'; from Proto-Germanic *standanan
