STOOD.evolution: Strong past tense of 'stand'; 'stood up' means either raised to a standing position or failed to keep an appointment; 'where I stood' means one's position or opinion; 'couldn't stand it' uses the physical posture as a tolerance metaphor.
STOOD.joke: Being stood up: when 'to stand' and 'to abandon' became the same past tense experience.

STOOL.definition: A seat without a back or arms; feces; an informer (slang).
STOOL.origin: Old English 'stōl' (seat, throne), from Proto-Germanic *stōlaz, from *stō- (to stand)
STOOL.evolution: Old English for a seat; the toilet/feces sense developed from 'close stool' (a chamber pot with a seat); 'stool pigeon' (informer) comes from a tied pigeon used as a decoy; 'bar stool' is the modern backless seat.
STOOL.joke: Stool pigeon: the informant whose name came from a decoy bird, proving etymology was already suspicious of collaborators.

STOOP.definition: To bend one's body forward; to condescend to do something beneath one's dignity; a porch or small platform at a front door.
STOOP.origin: Two words: bend sense from Old English 'stūpian' (to stoop, bend); American porch sense from Dutch 'stoep' (front step, pavement)
STOOP.evolution: Two words merged; the front step 'stoop' came to America with Dutch settlers in New York; 'stoop to' means to lower one's standards; 'stoop-shouldered' describes permanently bent posture; 'stoop culture' in New York means socializing on front steps.
STOOP.joke: Stoop to their level: the social descent that requires bending down to do it properly.

STORE.definition: A place where goods are sold; a supply kept for future use; to keep for future use.
STORE.origin: Old French 'estor' (supply, provisions), from 'estorer' (to build, restore), from Latin 'instaurare' (to renew)
STORE.evolution: From Latin 'to renew/restore'; originally a supply of provisions; extended to the building where goods are sold; 'in store for you' means awaiting you; 'set great store by' means to value highly; computer storage inherited the keeping sense.
STORE.joke: In store for you: the future's delivery notification with zero tracking information.

STORK.definition: A large wading bird associated in folklore with delivering babies.
STORK.origin: Old English 'storc' (stork), from Proto-Germanic *sturkaz, possibly related to 'stark' (stiff) due to its rigid walking
STORK.evolution: Old English word; the stork-delivers-babies myth dates to European folklore where storks nested on chimneys; babies were believed to be brought from swamps or heaven; the myth became a polite explanation for childbirth before modern child education.
STORK.joke: The stork myth: the bird that Europe used as a deflection from explaining reproduction for several centuries.

STORM.definition: A violent atmospheric disturbance; an angry outburst; to attack with overwhelming force; to move angrily.
STORM.evolution: Old English weather word; 'brainstorm' (a spontaneous group ideation session) uses the mental weather metaphor; 'storm the castle' is military; 'storm in a teacup' (British) means an exaggerated fuss about a trivial matter; 'take by storm' means to overwhelm.
STORM.joke: Storm in a teacup: the British idiom that measures political crises by beverage container.

STORY.definition: An account of events; a narrative; a floor of a building; a piece of journalism.
STORY.origin: Two words: narrative from Old French 'estoire', from Latin 'historia'; floor of building from Medieval Latin 'historia' (a tier of windows with painted scenes)
STORY.evolution: Both meanings from Latin 'historia'; building 'stories' (floors) come from rows of windows with painted narrative scenes; 'story' in journalism is any article; 'storey' (British spelling for floor) distinguishes the building sense.
STORY.joke: A building story: when painted window scenes on each floor gave architecture a literary dimension.

STOUT.definition: Having a strong, heavy build; strong and determined; a dark, strong beer.
STOUT.origin: Old French 'estout' (bold, proud), from Frankish *stolt (bold, proud), related to Middle Dutch 'stout'
STOUT.evolution: Originally meant brave and bold; shifted to mean physically substantial; the dark beer 'stout' is short for 'stout porter' (a strong porter ale); 'stout defense' retains the brave sense while 'stout figure' uses the physical one.
STOUT.joke: Stout: the adjective that means both brave and stocky — once one, now often confused for the other.

STOVE.definition: An apparatus for cooking food or heating a room.
STOVE.origin: Middle Dutch or Middle Low German 'stove' (heated room, sweating room), from Proto-Germanic *stubōn
STOVE.evolution: Originally a heated room or steam bath; narrowed to the heating/cooking device; 'over a hot stove' became a cliché for domestic labor; the stove's design evolved from wood to coal to gas to electric to induction.
STOVE.joke: Slaving over a hot stove: the culinary martyrdom phrase that cookbooks have been quietly retiring.

STRAP.definition: A strip of leather or other material used to fasten, hold, or carry something; to fasten with a strap.
STRAP.origin: Dialectal form of 'strop' (a strip), from Old French 'estrope', from Latin 'stroppus', from Greek 'strophos' (twisted band)
STRAP.evolution: From a Greek twisted band; 'bootstraps' gave us 'to pull yourself up by your bootstraps' (achieve through individual effort — originally meant as impossible); 'strapped for cash' means short of money; 'strapped in' means secured.
STRAP.joke: Pull yourself up by your bootstraps: the motivational phrase that was literally impossible and became figuratively expected.

STRAW.definition: Dried stalks of grain; a thin tube for drinking; the last straw (the final problem).
STRAW.origin: Old English 'strēaw' (straw), from Proto-Germanic *strawą, from *strewaz (to strew)
STRAW.evolution: Old English agricultural word; 'last straw' (from 'the straw that broke the camel's back') means the final problem that causes a breakdown; 'straw man' is a weak argument constructed to be easily defeated; drinking straws were invented in the 19th century.
STRAW.joke: The last straw: the camel's contribution to conflict resolution theory — incremental loading has limits.

STRAY.definition: To wander from the correct path; an animal that has strayed; occurring at random.
STRAY.origin: Old French 'estraier' (to wander), from Vulgar Latin *extravagare, from Latin 'extra' + 'vagari' (to wander)
STRAY.evolution: From Old French wandering; 'stray bullet' means one that misses its target; 'stray thought' is an unintended mental tangent; 'stray animal' is lost or abandoned; 'going astray' means following the wrong path morally or physically.
STRAY.joke: Stray bullet: the projectile that proved physics doesn't care about your intended destination.

STRIP.definition: To remove covering from; a long narrow piece; a comic strip; a striptease.
STRIP.origin: Old English 'strȳpan, bestrȳpan' (to plunder), from Proto-Germanic *straupjan, related to 'strafe'
STRIP.evolution: Old English for removing and plundering; 'comic strip' (sequential cartoon panels in a newspaper) emerged in the late 19th century; 'airstrip' is a narrow landing area; 'strip mall' is a row of shops with parking; striptease extended the removal sense.
STRIP.joke: Comic strip: the newspaper innovation that made plundering fun and family-friendly.

STRUT.definition: To walk with a stiff, erect, and conceited gait; a supporting bar or framework element.
STRUT.origin: Two words: walk sense from Low German 'strutten' (to be stiff); structural sense from Middle Low German 'strutte'
STRUT.evolution: Two related Low German words; 'strut your stuff' means to display one's abilities or assets confidently; in aviation and construction, a strut provides structural rigidity; the peacock strut and the confident human swagger share the same verb.
STRUT.joke: Strut your stuff: the instruction that simultaneously means both 'be confident' and 'walk like you know it.'

STUCK.definition: Past tense of 'stick'; unable to move or progress; fixed in an undesirable situation.
STUCK.origin: Old English 'stician' (to pierce, stick), past participle; from Proto-Germanic *stikōjan
STUCK.evolution: Past participle of 'stick'; 'stuck in a rut' means unable to change a repetitive pattern; 'stuck up' means conceited (as if a nose raised high); 'stuck on' means infatuated; 'getting stuck in' (British) means beginning energetically.
STUCK.joke: Stuck in a rut: the achievement of making the same bad decision so consistently it became infrastructure.

STUDY.definition: The devotion of time to learning; a room for reading and writing; to learn about; an artistic preparatory sketch.
STUDY.origin: Old French 'estudier', from Medieval Latin 'studiare', from Latin 'studium' (eagerness, zeal, study)
STUDY.evolution: From Latin 'zeal'; a 'study' in music is a piece designed to develop technique; 'in a brown study' (deep in thought) uses the idea of absorbed mental activity; artistic studies are preparatory works for larger compositions.
STUDY.joke: In a brown study: the archaic phrase for deep thought that convinced nobody the color was relevant.

STUFF.definition: Material or things; personal possessions; to fill tightly; the substance of something.
STUFF.origin: Old French 'estoffe' (material, stuff), from 'estoffer' (to furnish), from Greek 'stuphein' (to tighten)
STUFF.evolution: From Old French for material; 'know your stuff' means to be well-informed; 'stuffed animal' is taxidermied or a plush toy; 'stuffed shirt' is a pompous person; 'stuff happens' is the polite version of a more profane sentiment.
STUFF.joke: Stuffed shirt: the insult that required creating a garment to contain the pomposity.

STUMP.definition: The base of a felled tree; a wicket in cricket; to baffle; to travel making political speeches.
STUMP.origin: Middle Low German 'stump' (stump, stub), from Proto-Germanic *stumpaz
STUMP.evolution: From Low German for a tree stub; 'on the stump' (political campaigning) comes from speakers standing on tree stumps to address crowds; 'stumped' means baffled; in cricket, to 'stump' a batsman is to get them out while they're out of their crease.
STUMP.joke: On the stump: political campaigns named after improvised furniture that nobody would otherwise choose to stand on.

STUNG.definition: Past tense and past participle of 'sting'; pierced or hurt.
STUNG.origin: Old English 'stingan' (to sting), past tense 'stang'; from Proto-Germanic *stingwan
STUNG.evolution: Strong past tense of 'sting'; 'stung by criticism' uses the sharp pain metaphor; 'stung for money' means overcharged; 'stung into action' means provoked into response; the irregular form retains the old strong verb pattern.
STUNG.joke: Stung by criticism: the metaphor that gives words the same grade as wasps.

STUNK.definition: Past participle of 'stink'; emitted a strong unpleasant smell; was very bad.
STUNK.origin: Old English 'stincan' (to emit a smell), past participle; from Proto-Germanic *stenkwanan
STUNK.evolution: Past participle of 'stink'; used in perfect aspect ('it has stunk'); 'that whole affair stunk' evaluates performance; competes with 'stank' as the simple past; the irregular form survived the regularization pressure.
STUNK.joke: It stunk: the three-word review that is simultaneously olfactory and critical.

STUNT.definition: A spectacular or dangerous feat; a trick to attract attention; to impede growth.
STUNT.origin: Two words: feat sense from American English dialect, possibly Norwegian 'stunt' (short, dwarfed); growth sense from Old English 'stunt' (foolish, short-witted)
STUNT.evolution: Two unrelated words; the 'daring stunt' emerged in late 19th-century American circus/film culture; 'stunt double' performs dangerous sequences; 'pull a stunt' means a trick or scheme; 'stunted growth' means checked or impaired development.
STUNT.joke: Stunt double: the person who does the dangerous part while the star perfects their post-stunt expression.

STYLE.definition: A manner of doing something; elegance; a person's distinctive manner; to design or arrange.
STYLE.origin: Old French 'stile', from Latin 'stilus' (pointed instrument for writing, writing style)
STYLE.evolution: From Latin writing stylus; extended from how one writes to how one does anything; 'style icon' emerged with celebrity culture; 'cramp someone's style' means to restrict their expression; 'hairstyle' uses the form-giving sense.
STYLE.joke: Cramp your style: the concern that someone else's presence is competing with your personal brand.

SUAVE.definition: Charming, confident, and elegant in manner.
SUAVE.origin: French 'suave', from Latin 'suavis' (sweet, pleasant), from Proto-Indo-European *swehd- (sweet)
SUAVE.evolution: From Latin 'sweet/pleasant'; in English, acquired the meaning of smooth social grace and charm; 'suavely' handling a situation means navigating it with effortless elegance; often associated with Bond-like sophistication.
SUAVE.joke: Suave: the compliment that means 'suspiciously comfortable in all social situations.'

SUGAR.definition: A sweet crystalline substance; a term of endearment; to coat with sugar; to make more palatable.
SUGAR.origin: Old French 'sukere', from Medieval Latin 'succarum', from Arabic 'sukkar', from Sanskrit 'śarkarā' (gravel, sugar)
SUGAR.evolution: Long etymological journey from Sanskrit through Arabic to English; 'sugar-coat' means to make unpleasant things seem nicer; 'sugar' as a euphemism replaces a profanity; 'sugar daddy/mommy' is modern slang; 'pure sugar' means excessive sweetness.
SUGAR.joke: Sugar-coating: humanity's commitment to making unpleasant truths taste better since the Bronze Age.

SUING.definition: Present participle of 'sue'; taking legal action against someone.
SUING.origin: Old French 'sivre, suer' (to follow), from Vulgar Latin *sequere, from Latin 'sequi' (to follow)
SUING.evolution: From following (Latin 'sequi'); originally meant to follow or pursue; narrowed to legal pursuit; 'sue' also means to petition or appeal; the 'following' etymology resonates — a lawsuit follows someone until settled.
SUING.joke: Sue: to follow someone so persistently that you need a court to make them stop.

SUITE.definition: A set of rooms; a piece of music in several movements; a set of matching furniture or software.
SUITE.origin: French 'suite' (following), from Old French 'siute', from 'sieute' (following), from Vulgar Latin *sequita, from Latin 'sequi' (to follow)
SUITE.evolution: From French 'following'; a suite of rooms follows one after another; a musical suite is movements that follow each other; software suites (Microsoft Office) bundle related programs; 'hotel suite' implies luxury.
SUITE.joke: Software suite: the bundled programs that follow you around asking if you've tried the cloud version.

SULKY.definition: Moody and resentfully withdrawn; a light, two-wheeled cart for one person.
SULKY.origin: Two words: moody sense from uncertain origin, possibly from Old English 'solcen' (sluggish); vehicle possibly from 'sulky' meaning the driver prefers to ride alone
SULKY.evolution: The moody sense came first; the racing sulky (used in harness racing) may have been named because the driver sits alone, unsociably; 'sulking' is the action form; a sulky child withdraws into resentful silence.
SULKY.joke: The racing sulky: a vehicle named after the mood of a person who refuses to share the carriage.

SULLY.definition: To damage or spoil the purity or integrity of; to tarnish.
SULLY.origin: Possibly from Old French 'souiller' (to soil, make dirty), from 'sol' (soil, ground); attested 16th century
SULLY.evolution: From Old French; 'to sully one's reputation' means to damage it; 'unsullied' means untarnished or pure; the word suggests a subtle contamination rather than outright destruction.
SULLY.joke: Sully: the word for reputation damage that sounds too genteel for how permanently effective it actually is.

SUMAC.definition: A shrub whose dried berries are used as a tart spice; some varieties are poisonous.
SUMAC.origin: Old French 'sumac', from Medieval Latin 'sumach', from Arabic 'summāq', from Aramaic 'summaq' (dark red)
SUMAC.evolution: From Aramaic and Arabic via medieval trade routes; Middle Eastern and Mediterranean cuisine uses sumac as a tangy, dark-red spice; poison sumac is distinct from culinary sumac; 'sumac' also appears in fall foliage due to its brilliant red coloring.
SUMAC.joke: Sumac: the spice hiding in plain sight on Middle Eastern menus while the poisonous variety gets all the warnings.

SUNNY.definition: Bright with sunlight; cheerful and happy.
SUNNY.evolution: Extended from sunlight to describe an optimistic personality; 'sunny side up' (eggs) describes yolks up without flipping; 'sunny disposition' became a positive character descriptor; 'sunny side of the street' is both literal and metaphorical.
SUNNY.joke: Sunny side up: the egg order that doubles as a life philosophy for mornings you're not quite ready to commit to.

SUPER.definition: Very good; a superintendent; a prefix meaning above or beyond.
SUPER.evolution: Latin prefix adopted into English; 'super' as a standalone adjective for excellent is 19th-century informal usage; 'Superman' (1938) made the prefix heroic; 'supermarket,' 'supernatural,' 'superintendent' all use the above/beyond sense.
SUPER.joke: Super: the Latin for 'above' that became the building superintendent, the grocery store, and an adjective for amazing.

SURER.definition: Comparative of 'sure'; more certain or reliable.
SURER.origin: Old French 'sur', from Latin 'securus' (secure, without care), from 'se-' + 'cura' (care)
SURER.evolution: Comparative form of 'sure'; 'surer footing' means more stable ground; used in expressions of increased certainty or reliability; 'the surest way' is the most certain method.
SURER.joke: Surer footing: what you need when the ground you were standing on turned out to be a financial metaphor.

SURGE.definition: A sudden powerful forward movement; a sudden increase; to move with a powerful rush.
SURGE.origin: Old French 'surgir' (to rise), from Latin 'surgere' (to rise, spring up), from 'sub-' + 'regere' (to rule, direct)
SURGE.evolution: From Latin 'to rise'; 'power surge' is a sudden increase in electrical current; 'surge in demand' describes rapid market increase; 'troop surge' was a military deployment increase; 'surge pricing' adjusts prices to demand peaks.
SURGE.joke: Surge pricing: when a power surge in demand becomes a power surge in your bill.

SURLY.origin: Earlier 'sirly' (lordly, imperious), from 'sir'; probably influenced by 'churlish'
SURLY.evolution: Evolved from 'sirly' (imperious, haughty like a lord); the pronunciation shift and meaning shift occurred over centuries; now means bad-tempered rather than haughty; 'surly cashier' is a customer service standard.
SURLY.joke: Surly: started as 'lordly' and arrived at 'hostile' — the complete opposite of its origin.

SUSHI.definition: A Japanese dish of cold rice with fish, seafood, or vegetables.
SUSHI.origin: Japanese 'sushi' (it is sour), from 'su' (vinegar) + 'shi' (past tense verb ending) — referring to the vinegared rice
SUSHI.evolution: From Japanese; originally a method of preserving fish in fermented rice for months; evolved into the fresh rice-and-topping dish; became globally popular in the late 20th century; 'sushi grade' (safe for raw consumption) became a food safety standard.
SUSHI.joke: Sushi means 'it is sour' — the name describing the rice while the raw fish remains diplomatically unnamed.

SWAMI.definition: A Hindu religious teacher; a person who claims supernatural insight.
SWAMI.evolution: From Sanskrit via Bengali; used as a title for Hindu holy men and spiritual leaders; in Western popular culture, 'swami' came to represent anyone claiming mystical or predictive powers; often used humorously.
SWAMI.joke: Swami: the mystical title that Hollywood gave to everyone in a turban who claimed to predict anything.

SWAMP.definition: An area of low-lying ground, often with standing water; to overwhelm with too much of something.
SWAMP.origin: Possibly from Low German 'swamp' (sponge, fungus), or Old English *swamp; related to Swedish 'svamp' (sponge)
SWAMP.evolution: Of Low German or Scandinavian origin; 'swamped with work' means overwhelmed; 'drain the swamp' became a political metaphor for eliminating corruption; ecologically, swamps are biodiverse wetland habitats.
SWAMP.joke: Drain the swamp: the political slogan that ignored every ecologist who explained swamps are good, actually.

SWARM.definition: A large group of insects; a moving crowd; to move in large numbers.
SWARM.origin: Old English 'swearm' (swarm, crowd), from Proto-Germanic *swarmaz, possibly from *swer- (to buzz)
SWARM.evolution: Old English word for insect groups; 'swarm intelligence' (collective behavior in AI) was modeled on insects; 'swarmed with tourists' uses the insect metaphor for overwhelming crowds; drone 'swarms' are a military technology.
SWARM.joke: Swarm intelligence: when we decided bees were smarter collectively than most management teams individually.

SWASH.definition: To move with a splashing sound; the rush of water on a beach; swagger.
SWASH.evolution: Imitative of splashing sounds; 'swashbuckler' (a daring adventurer) combines 'swash' (strike a shield) with 'buckler' (a small shield) — describing sword-fighters who made flashy moves; the archetype became synonymous with pirate/adventure stories.
SWASH.joke: Swashbuckler: the swordsman who defined a genre by aggressively hitting their own shield.

SWATH.definition: A strip of mown grass or grain; a broad strip or area.
SWATH.origin: Old English 'swæþ, swaðu' (track, trace, swath), from Proto-Germanic *swaþō
SWATH.evolution: Old English agricultural term for the width of a scythe's stroke; 'cut a swath through' means to move through creating destruction or impressive effect; 'a wide swath' describes a broad impact; still used in agriculture and figuratively.
SWATH.joke: Cut a swath: when agricultural mowing became the metaphor for impressive and destructive progress.

SWEAR.definition: To make a solemn statement; to use profanity; to take an oath.
SWEAR.origin: Old English 'swerian' (to take an oath), from Proto-Germanic *swarjaną, from *swarą (answer, oath)
SWEAR.evolution: Old English for oath-taking; the solemn legal oath and casual profanity both derive from the emphatic assertion; 'I swear by it' means I strongly recommend it; 'swear word' emerged when certain words became too strong for polite society.
SWEAR.joke: Swear words: vocabulary that started as the most serious oath and graduated to stubbed toe relief.

SWEAT.definition: Moisture from skin during exertion or heat; to perspire; to work hard; to be anxious.
SWEAT.origin: Old English 'swætan' (to sweat), from 'swāt' (sweat), from Proto-Germanic *swaitaz
SWEAT.evolution: Old English for perspiration; 'sweat equity' (contribution through labor rather than money) emerged in business/real estate; 'no sweat' means no problem; 'sweating it out' means enduring stress; the fashion 'sweatshirt' and 'sweatpants' named for the activity.
SWEAT.joke: Sweat equity: the business term that turned physical labor into a financial instrument without changing the smell.

SWEEP.definition: To clean with a brush; to move swiftly; to win completely; a chimney sweep; a comprehensive movement.
SWEEP.origin: Old English 'swāpan' (to sweep, dash), from Proto-Germanic *swaipanan
SWEEP.evolution: Old English for brushing movement; 'sweep the board' means to win everything; 'chimney sweep' (Mary Poppins's Bert) is a specific occupation; 'sweeping changes' means comprehensive reform; 'under the carpet' (hiding problems) is the opposite of sweeping.
SWEEP.joke: Sweeping it under the rug: the one sweep job where the dirt stays — performance art pretending to be cleaning.

SWEET.definition: Having a pleasant sugary taste; kind and pleasant; a sweet-tasting food; a term of endearment.
SWEET.evolution: Old English fundamental taste word; extended to personality ('sweet person'), music ('sweet melody'), and colloquial approval ('sweet!'); 'bittersweet' describes mixed emotions; 'sweet nothings' are affectionate whispers; 'sweetheart' compounds both.
SWEET.joke: Sweet nothings: the affectionate term for whispered endearments that have calorie counts despite the name.

SWELL.definition: To increase in size; a long wave; a fashionable person; excellent (dated informal).
SWELL.evolution: Old English for growth; ocean 'swells' are long rolling waves; 'swell' as a term for excellent was popular 1920s-1940s American slang; 'swelling with pride' uses the inflation metaphor; 'a swell person' means a fine fellow.
SWELL.joke: Swell: the 1940s exclamation of approval that got swallowed by history and left only the wave behind.

SWEPT.definition: Past tense and past participle of 'sweep'; cleaned with a brush; moved swiftly.
SWEPT.origin: Old English 'swāpan' (to sweep), past tense; from Proto-Germanic *swaipanan
SWEPT.evolution: Strong past tense of 'sweep'; 'swept off one's feet' means overwhelmed by romantic attraction; 'swept under the rug' means hidden or ignored; 'swept away' means overwhelmed by emotion or carried by water.
SWEPT.joke: Swept off your feet: the romantic ideal that confused velocity with affection.

SWIFT.definition: Moving at great speed; a fast-flying bird related to the swallow.
SWIFT.origin: Old English 'swift' (moving quickly), from Proto-Germanic *swiftaz, from *swīban (to sweep, move quickly)
SWIFT.evolution: Old English for speed; the swift (bird) is one of the fastest birds in level flight; Jonathan Swift chose the name as an ironic pen name (author of Gulliver's Travels); Taylor Swift's name continues the tradition of swiftness in unexpected contexts.
SWIFT.joke: Taylor Swift and Jonathan Swift: separated by three centuries, united by the irony of having speed as a surname.

SWILL.definition: Liquid food for animals; inferior drink; to wash out with water; to drink greedily.
SWILL.origin: Old English 'swilian' (to wash out), from Proto-Germanic *swaljaną
SWILL.evolution: Old English for washing out; extended to the liquid refuse fed to pigs; 'swill' as bad-quality drink is from the pig-slop association; 'swilling drinks' means drinking greedily; the word carries unavoidable barnyard associations.
SWILL.joke: Swill: the word that starts at the pig farm and ends at the bad bar, without improving the journey.

SWINE.definition: A pig; an unpleasant or contemptible person.
SWINE.evolution: Old English for pig; 'casting pearls before swine' (Matthew 7:6) means offering something valuable to those who won't appreciate it; 'swine flu' (influenza A H1N1) was named for its suspected porcine origin; the insult sense is from the pig's reputation.
SWINE.joke: Pearls before swine: the biblical idiom that insulted pigs on behalf of everyone who'd been underappreciated.

SWING.definition: To move back and forth; to hit with a sweeping motion; a seat hung on ropes; jazz music with a strong rhythm.
SWING.origin: Old English 'swingan' (to beat, strike), from Proto-Germanic *swingwanan
SWING.evolution: Old English for striking motion; 'in full swing' means at peak activity; 'swing a deal' means to negotiate successfully; jazz 'swing' emerged in the 1930s; 'swing state' (pivotal in elections) uses the back-and-forth metaphor.
SWING.joke: Swing state: where political outcomes depend on voters who keep changing their minds like a child's swing.

SWIRL.definition: To move in a twisting or spiraling pattern; such a movement.
SWIRL.origin: Possibly from Low German or Dutch 'swirren' (to whirl), or Middle Low German 'swirbelen'; attested 15th century
SWIRL.evolution: Of Low German or Dutch origin; 'a swirl of controversy' uses the turbulent movement metaphor; 'swirl' patterns appear in art, pastry decoration, and weather systems; the ice cream swirl pattern became a dessert design staple.
SWIRL.joke: A swirl of controversy: atmospheric science confirming that disagreements do indeed rotate.

SWISH.definition: To move with a hissing or rushing sound; such a sound; fashionable and elegant (British).
SWISH.origin: Imitative/expressive; the sound of something moving through air; attested 18th century
SWISH.evolution: Onomatopoeic for a rushing sound; in basketball, a 'swish' is a shot that passes through the net without touching the rim; 'swish' as an adjective meaning smart/fashionable is British slang; the sound remains the primary sense.
SWISH.joke: Basketball swish: the one athletic achievement with an official sound effect named for how satisfying it is.

SWOON.definition: To faint; to be overcome with admiration or delight; a fainting spell.
SWOON.origin: Middle English 'swown, swounen', from Old English 'geswōgen' (in a faint), past participle of *swōgan
SWOON.evolution: Old English for falling unconscious; Victorian women famously 'swooned' (fainted) which was partly cultural performance; 'swoon over' someone means to be overwhelmed with admiration; social media 'swoon' is enthusiastic appreciation.
SWOON.joke: Victorian swooning: a fainting tradition so theatrical it required its own furniture (the fainting couch).

SWOOP.definition: To move rapidly downward through the air; a sweeping downward movement.
SWOOP.origin: Possibly from Old English 'swāpan' (to sweep), influenced by dialectal forms; attested 16th century
SWOOP.evolution: From Old English sweeping motion; 'at one fell swoop' (in a single decisive action) comes from Shakespeare's Macbeth where 'fell' means fierce; birds of prey swoop; police 'swoop' in a raid; 'swoop in and save the day' is rescue-narrative language.
SWOOP.joke: At one fell swoop: Shakespeare's description of a sudden attack that became a synonym for multitasking.

SWORD.definition: A weapon with a long metal blade and a handle.
SWORD.evolution: Old English for the bladed weapon; 'sword of Damocles' (hanging threat) comes from classical Greek; 'live by the sword, die by the sword' is biblical; swordsmanship became an art form; 'swordfish' is named for its elongated bill.
SWORD.joke: The sword of Damocles: ancient Greece's warning about success-induced anxiety, delivered via precarious catering.

SWORE.definition: Past tense of 'swear'; made a solemn statement or oath; used profanity.
SWORE.origin: Old English 'swerian' (to swear), past tense 'swōr'; from Proto-Germanic *swarjaną
SWORE.evolution: Strong past tense of 'swear'; 'swore by it' means relied on or strongly recommended it; 'swore an oath' is formal; 'swore under oath' is legal; the contrast between sacred oath and casual profanity both use the same past tense.
SWORE.joke: Swore by it: the endorsement that sounds legal even when it's just someone recommending a kitchen gadget.

SWORN.definition: Past participle of 'swear'; bound by an oath; describing a formal commitment.
SWORN.origin: Old English 'swerian' (to swear), past participle; from Proto-Germanic *swarjaną
SWORN.evolution: Past participle of 'swear'; 'sworn enemies' means declared and committed adversaries; 'sworn testimony' is given under oath; 'sworn in' means formally inaugurated into office; 'sworn statement' carries legal weight.
SWORN.joke: Sworn enemies: the formal designation for rivalries that have passed the registration process.

SWUNG.definition: Past tense and past participle of 'swing'; moved back and forth.
SWUNG.origin: Old English 'swingan' (to swing), past tense 'swang'; irregular form 'swung' later dominant
SWUNG.evolution: Past tense of 'swing'; 'swung into action' means began acting energetically; 'the vote swung' means changed direction; 'the door swung open' is a thriller staple; the irregular form replaced earlier 'swang.'
SWUNG.joke: The door swung open: the thriller genre's most dependable three words before something goes wrong.

SYNOD.definition: An assembly of church officials to discuss and decide matters of doctrine.
SYNOD.origin: Late Latin 'synodus', from Greek 'synodos' (meeting), from 'syn-' (together) + 'hodos' (way, journey)
SYNOD.evolution: From Greek 'meeting on the way'; the term for ecclesiastical councils; 'General Synod' is the governing body of the Church of England; synods have historically made consequential decisions about theology and church organization.
SYNOD.joke: Synod: a meeting named after a journey, for people who believe the destination is already decided.

SYRUP.definition: A thick sweet liquid made by dissolving sugar in water; a medicinal liquid.
SYRUP.origin: Medieval Latin 'sirupus', from Arabic 'sharāb' (beverage, syrup), from 'shariba' (to drink)
SYRUP.evolution: From Arabic through medieval trade; 'syrupy' describes both thick sweet consistency and excessively sentimental material; maple syrup and corn syrup became American staples; cough syrups disguise medication in sweet liquids.
SYRUP.joke: Syrupy prose: the literary criticism that uses sugar consistency as the benchmark for excessive sentimentality.

TABBY.definition: A domestic cat with a striped or brindled coat; a silk fabric with a wavy pattern; a gossiping woman (archaic).
TABBY.origin: From 'Tabby', a pet form of 'Tabitha'; also from Arabic 'attabi' (a type of striped silk from Al-Attabiyya, Baghdad)
TABBY.evolution: Multiple origins converged; the silk fabric came from Baghdad via Arabic trade; the cat's name came partly from the fabric's resemblance to cat stripes and partly from Tabitha as a cat's name; the gossiping sense came from tabby as an old woman's name.
TABBY.joke: Tabby cat: named after a Baghdad silk pattern and a popular old woman's name — simultaneously fashion and biography.

TABLE.definition: A flat surface supported by legs; a presentation of data in rows; to postpone discussion; to present for consideration.
TABLE.origin: Old English 'tabule', from Latin 'tabula' (board, writing tablet, list)
TABLE.evolution: From Latin 'board'; 'table a motion' means opposite things in British and American English (propose vs. postpone); 'turn the tables' means to reverse a situation; 'under the table' means illegally or secretly; multiplication tables extend the data sense.
TABLE.joke: 'Table it' means to discuss it in Britain and to abandon it in America — one phrase, two continents, zero shared meaning.

TABOO.definition: A social or religious custom prohibiting certain actions; prohibited; to prohibit.
TABOO.evolution: Introduced to English by Captain Cook after his Pacific voyages; one of the first words borrowed directly from a Polynesian language; Freud used 'taboo' in his psychological theories; the board game Taboo uses the word's prohibition sense.
TABOO.joke: Taboo: the Polynesian concept that Captain Cook imported to explain everything Europeans found awkward to discuss.

TACIT.definition: Understood or implied without being stated explicitly.
TACIT.origin: Latin 'tacitus' (silent), past participle of 'tacere' (to be silent), from Proto-Indo-European *tak- (to be silent)
TACIT.evolution: From Latin 'silent'; 'tacit agreement' is implied consent; 'tacit approval' is given without a word; 'tacit knowledge' (Polanyi's concept) is knowledge you cannot fully articulate; the word itself is quietly influential.
TACIT.joke: Tacit agreement: the contract everyone signed without reading, which is somehow legally and socially binding.

TACKY.definition: Slightly sticky; in poor taste; cheap-looking.
TACKY.origin: Two senses: sticky from 'tack' (a fastening) + '-y'; tasteless possibly from a dialectal meaning of 'tack' as inferior things
TACKY.evolution: The sticky sense came first (wet varnish, adhesive); the tasteless/cheap sense followed in American English; 'tacky souvenir' and 'tacky decoration' are the primary modern uses; 'that's tacky' is a mild social criticism.
TACKY.joke: Tacky: the aesthetic judgment that starts with unset varnish and ends with novelty decorations.

TAFFY.definition: A chewy candy made by pulling boiled sugar; (informal) flattery.
TAFFY.origin: American English; possibly from Welsh 'taffy' (a Welsh person, also cheap or tawdry); or possibly from 'toffee'
TAFFY.evolution: Possibly from Welsh slang; the pulled candy is a North American confection; saltwater taffy is associated with Atlantic City boardwalks; 'taffy' meaning flattery uses the sweetness and sticky quality as a metaphor.
TAFFY.joke: Saltwater taffy: the candy that sounds like a flavor description of seawater, which it mostly isn't.

TAINT.definition: To contaminate or corrupt slightly; a trace of contamination or corruption.
TAINT.origin: Old French 'teint' (tinted, colored), from Latin 'tinctus' (dyed), from 'tingere' (to dye, taint)
TAINT.evolution: Originally meant to dye or color; shifted to contamination and moral corruption; 'tainted evidence' is inadmissible; 'tainted reputation' suggests compromised integrity; 'tainted love' became a song title extending moral contamination to romance.
TAINT.joke: Tainted love: when contamination metaphors infiltrated romance and nobody filed a complaint.

TAKEN.definition: Past participle of 'take'; seized or occupied; captivated; in a relationship.
TAKEN.origin: Old Norse 'taka' (to take, grasp), past participle; replaced Old English 'niman'
TAKEN.evolution: Past participle of 'take'; 'taken' as an adjective for 'in a relationship' is a colloquial use; 'taken aback' means surprised; 'taken for granted' means not properly appreciated; the Liam Neeson film series made 'taken' synonymous with specific skills.
TAKEN.joke: Taken: the relationship status that also describes movie premises and the plot of every Liam Neeson thriller.

TAKER.definition: A person who takes something; someone who accepts an offer.
TAKER.evolution: Agent noun of 'take'; 'any takers?' means who accepts the offer; 'risk-taker' describes someone willing to take chances; 'tax-taker' is a political epithet; in commerce, 'a taker' accepts the offered price.
TAKER.joke: Any takers? The question that tests whether your offer is actually as good as you think it is.

TALLY.definition: A running total; a stick on which scores were notched; to count; to correspond or match.
TALLY.origin: Old French 'taille' (notch, tally), from 'tailler' (to cut), from Vulgar Latin *taliare, from Latin 'talea' (rod, stick)
TALLY.evolution: From notched sticks used for counting before widespread literacy; medieval tally sticks were split so both parties kept half; 'tally ho!' is a fox-hunting exclamation when the fox is spotted; 'these figures don't tally' means they don't match.
TALLY.joke: Tally-ho: the fox hunting announcement that made spotting an animal into a grammar lesson.

TALON.definition: A claw of a bird of prey; a similar projecting hook.
TALON.origin: Old French 'talon' (heel, spur), from Latin 'talus' (ankle bone, heel)
TALON.evolution: From Latin ankle/heel; the hooked claws of eagles and hawks were compared to a heel spur; 'in the talons of' means in the grasp of; the word maintains an association with predatory birds.
TALON.joke: In the talons of: the metaphor reserved for predators, corporations, and landlords who've read too much Dickens.

TAMER.definition: A person who tames wild animals; comparative of 'tame': more subdued or docile.
TAMER.origin: From 'tame' + '-er'; Old English 'tam' (tame, domesticated), from Proto-Germanic *tamaz
TAMER.evolution: Two related words; a lion tamer is a specific circus profession; 'tamer' as comparative means less wild or exciting; 'a tamer version' of something has been made more acceptable; Shakespeare's 'Taming of the Shrew' uses the verb form.
TAMER.joke: A tamer version: the diplomatic description for content that had all the interesting parts removed.

TANGO.definition: A dance of Latin American origin; the letter T in the NATO phonetic alphabet; to dance the tango; to involve (it takes two to tango).
TANGO.origin: American Spanish 'tango' (a particular drum-dance), possibly from Niger-Congo languages via African slaves in Argentina
TANGO.evolution: Possibly of African origin via Argentina; developed in Buenos Aires in the late 19th century; 'it takes two to tango' became a proverb about mutual responsibility; the NATO 'Tango' is used for the letter T in radio communications.
TANGO.joke: It takes two to tango: the dance proverb for assigning responsibility to both parties involved in any mess.

TANGY.definition: Having a sharp, piquant flavor or smell.
TANGY.origin: From 'tang' (a sharp flavor), possibly from Old Norse 'tangi' (a sharp point), or imitative of a sharp taste; attested 19th century
TANGY.evolution: From 'tang' describing a sharp, biting flavor; 'tangy' is positive in food contexts; 'a tang of the sea' is olfactory; the word effectively conveys the puckering quality of citrus or fermented foods.
TANGY.joke: Tangy: the taste descriptor that describes pleasure through controlled discomfort.

TAPER.definition: A slender candle; to become gradually narrower; to gradually decrease.
TAPER.origin: Old English 'tapur, taper' (wax candle), possibly from Coptic 'taper' (wick), or from Latin 'papyrus' (the rush used for wicks)
TAPER.evolution: Originally a thin wax candle; extended to any narrowing shape; medical 'tapering' means gradually reducing a medication dose; 'tapering off' describes a gradual decline; the shape (wide at base, narrow at top) became a geometric term.
TAPER.joke: Tapering off: the gradual reduction strategy that promises slow change and rarely delivers on the speed.

TAPIR.definition: A large nocturnal mammal of tropical America and Southeast Asia, related to the rhinoceros and horse.
TAPIR.origin: Portuguese 'tapir', from Tupi 'tapyra' (large herbivore), an indigenous Brazilian name
TAPIR.evolution: From Tupi via Portuguese; the tapir is a living fossil whose relatives date back millions of years; its prehensile snout makes it distinctive; despite odd appearances, it is related to horses and rhinos.
TAPIR.joke: The tapir: the animal evolution apparently designed by committee with leftover horse, rhino, and elephant parts.

TARDY.definition: Delaying or delayed beyond the expected or proper time; late.
TARDY.origin: Old French 'tardif', from Vulgar Latin *tardivus, from Latin 'tardus' (slow, sluggish)
TARDY.evolution: From Latin 'slow'; 'tardy student' is a school-report standard; 'tardy response' is diplomatic criticism for being slow to reply; 'retard' shares the root; the word is slightly formal, suggesting a measured disapproval of lateness.
TARDY.joke: Tardy: the polite word for late that schools use to make being on time feel like an achievement.

TAROT.definition: A pack of 78 playing cards used for divination and fortune-telling.
TAROT.origin: French 'tarot', from Italian 'tarocchi' (plural), of unknown ultimate origin; possibly from Arabic 'turuq' (ways) or possibly from the city of Taro in Italy
TAROT.evolution: Of disputed origin; originally a playing card game in northern Italy (15th century); adopted for fortune-telling purposes in the 18th century; the major arcana (22 cards) became symbols of psychological archetypes for Carl Jung.
TAROT.joke: Tarot: the playing card game that got reassigned to fortune-telling and never looked back.

TASTE.definition: The faculty of perceiving flavor; a small sample; aesthetic judgment; to sample food.
TASTE.origin: Old French 'taster' (to touch, feel, taste), from Vulgar Latin *taxitare, frequentative of Latin 'taxare' (to assess, touch)
TASTE.evolution: From Latin assessment/touching; extended from sensory to aesthetic ('good taste'); 'taste-maker' influences cultural preferences; 'a taste of things to come' uses sampling as a previewing metaphor; 'in good/bad taste' is social judgment.
TASTE.joke: Good taste: the aesthetic standard that has been wrong about important things in every decade except the current one.

TASTY.definition: Having a pleasant flavor; (British slang) attractive.
TASTY.origin: From 'taste' + '-y'; Old French 'taster' (to taste), from Vulgar Latin *taxitare
TASTY.evolution: Extended from 'taste'; 'a tasty morsel' is both food and people; British slang 'tasty' for attractive uses the desirability metaphor; 'tasty bits' in information means the most interesting parts; the word generally implies pleasurable quality.
TASTY.joke: Tasty: the food adjective that British English promoted to general attractiveness without issuing a warning.

TATTY.definition: Worn and shabby; of inferior quality.
TATTY.origin: Possibly from 'tat' (cheap goods), possibly of Low German origin; attested 19th century
TATTY.evolution: Of uncertain origin; British English term for shabby or worn-out; 'tatty old thing' is affectionate or dismissive; 'tattered' is a related form; 'tat' (cheap, trashy goods) may be the base; contrasted with 'posh' in British social vocabulary.
TATTY.joke: Tatty but loved: the British assessment of antiques worth keeping despite their obvious structural concerns.

TAUNT.definition: To provoke with taunting words; a provocative remark.
TAUNT.origin: Old French 'tanter' or 'tencer' (to tempt, provoke), from Latin 'temptare' (to attempt, touch); or from 'tant pour tant' (so much for so much)
TAUNT.evolution: From Old French; 'taunt' implies goading specifically designed to provoke; Monty Python's 'I taunt you a second time!' became a cultural reference for elaborate verbal provocation; 'taunting' is against the rules in many sports for exactly this reason.
TAUNT.joke: I taunt you a second time: Monty Python's contribution to the literature of verbal escalation protocols.

TAWNY.definition: Of an orange-brown or yellowish-brown color.
TAWNY.origin: Old French 'tanné' (tanned), from 'tan' (tanbark), from Medieval Latin 'tannum' (tanbark, oak bark for tanning)
TAWNY.evolution: From tanned leather color; describes the warm brown of lions, owls ('tawny owl'), and port wines ('tawny port'); heraldry uses 'tawny' as a color; the word evokes warmth and autumn more than standard 'brown' does.
TAWNY.joke: Tawny owl: named for its color, which is the most dignified thing the owl got from the naming committee.

TEACH.definition: To impart knowledge or skill; to cause someone to learn.
TEACH.origin: Old English 'tǣcan' (to show, point out, teach), from Proto-Germanic *taikijan, from *taikną (sign, token)
TEACH.evolution: Old English for showing and pointing out; 'that'll teach you' is mock-severity; 'you can't teach an old dog new tricks' became a proverb about resistance to change; teacher-student dynamics appear in most human cultures.
TEACH.joke: You can't teach an old dog new tricks: the proverb that dogs have been diplomatically declining to dispute.

TEARY.definition: Inclined to cry; filled with tears.
TEARY.origin: From 'tear' (teardrop) + '-y'; Old English 'tēar' (tear, teardrop), from Proto-Germanic *tawraz
TEARY.evolution: Extended from 'tear' (the teardrop); 'teary-eyed' describes someone on the verge of crying; 'getting teary' is becoming emotional; 'teary goodbye' is an emotionally laden farewell; the word sits between 'tearful' and 'weepy.'
TEARY.joke: Teary goodbye: the farewell subgenre where nobody is quite ready to stop hugging.

TEASE.definition: To make fun of or attempt to provoke; to disentangle fibers; a person or thing that teases.
TEASE.origin: Old English 'tǣsan' (to pull apart, comb), from Proto-Germanic *taisjan
TEASE.evolution: Originally meant to pull apart fibers (teasing wool); extended to pulling at someone emotionally; 'tease' for hair means to backcomb; 'teaser trailer' shows just enough to provoke interest; 'such a tease' describes provocative withholding.
TEASE.joke: Teaser trailer: the marketing strategy named after emotional provocation and committed to delivering on neither.

TEDDY.definition: A soft toy bear; a woman's one-piece undergarment.
TEDDY.origin: From 'Teddy', nickname for Theodore Roosevelt; reportedly named after a bear he declined to shoot on a 1902 hunt
TEDDY.evolution: Named after Theodore ('Teddy') Roosevelt when a cartoon depicted him refusing to shoot a tied-up bear; toy manufacturers Michtom and Steiff independently created soft bear toys; 'teddy bear' became universally associated with childhood comfort.
TEDDY.joke: Teddy bear: named after a president's moment of mercy — the most influential hunting decision in toy history.

TEETH.definition: Plural of tooth; the hard calcified structures in the mouth; effectiveness or force.
TEETH.evolution: Old English with irregular plural; 'teething problems' (early difficulties) uses the infant's painful tooth emergence; 'show your teeth' means to demonstrate aggression; 'armed to the teeth' means heavily equipped; 'getting teeth into' means engaging deeply.
TEETH.joke: Teething problems: the pain of a baby's first teeth, repurposed to apologize for every flawed product launch.

TEMPO.definition: The speed at which a piece of music is played; the speed or pace of something.
TEMPO.origin: Italian 'tempo' (time), from Latin 'tempus' (time), from Proto-Indo-European *temp- (to stretch)
TEMPO.evolution: From Italian/Latin 'time'; musical tempo is marked by Italian terms (allegro, andante, presto); 'the tempo of city life' extends the musical sense to any pace; sports 'tempo' describes the pace of play; business leaders 'set the tempo.'
TEMPO.joke: Set the tempo: the one instruction where music, sports, and corporate meetings are completely interchangeable.

TENET.definition: A principle or belief held by a person or organization.
TENET.origin: Latin 'tenet' (he/she holds), third person singular present of 'tenere' (to hold)
TENET.evolution: Directly from Latin 'he holds'; a tenet is a position one holds firmly; religious tenets are fundamental beliefs; 'core tenets' of a philosophy are its essential principles; the 2020 Christopher Nolan film borrowed the word for time inversion.
TENET.joke: Tenet: a word meaning 'what you hold' that a Christopher Nolan film made confusing in both directions.

TENOR.definition: A singing voice between baritone and alto; the general meaning or character of something.
TENOR.origin: Old French 'tenor', from Italian 'tenore', from Medieval Latin 'tenor' (continuous holding), from Latin 'tenere' (to hold)
TENOR.evolution: From Latin 'holding'; in medieval polyphony, the tenor voice 'held' the main melody while others sang around it; extended to the general character or drift of something ('the tenor of his remarks'); the highest male chest voice.
TENOR.joke: The tenor of a conversation: the voice that holds the melody even when everyone else is improvising.

TENSE.definition: Stretched tight; anxious; to make or become tense; the grammatical expression of time.
TENSE.origin: Two words: physical/emotional sense from Latin 'tensus' (stretched), from 'tendere' (to stretch); grammatical sense from Old French 'tens', from Latin 'tempus' (time)
TENSE.evolution: Two Latin words converged; the stretched/anxious sense came from the physical sensation of tightness; grammatical tense expresses time relationships; 'tense situation' and 'past tense' are unrelated etymologically.
TENSE.joke: Past tense: the grammatical tool for discussing anxious moments after they've become slightly less terrifying.

TENTH.definition: Constituting number ten in a sequence; one of ten equal parts.
TENTH.origin: Old English 'tēoþa' (tenth), from 'tīen' (ten) + '-þa' (ordinal suffix); from Proto-Germanic *tehundaz
TENTH.evolution: Ordinal of ten; 'tithe' (one-tenth of income given to the church) shares this root; 'tenth of a second' is now a meaningful sports measurement; Beethoven's intended but unfinished Tenth Symphony is a musicological discussion point.
TENTH.joke: Beethoven's Tenth: the symphony that doesn't exist but has been discussed by musicologists more than most symphonies that do.

TEPEE.definition: A cone-shaped tent made of animal skins or canvas, traditionally used by some Native American peoples.
TEPEE.evolution: From Lakota; also spelled tipi or teepee; the portable structure suited the nomadic lifestyle of Great Plains peoples who followed bison herds; the design is remarkably effective at ventilation and heat management.
TEPEE.joke: Tepee: the structure that solved portability, ventilation, and weather resistance before those were considered design challenges.

TEPID.definition: Only slightly warm; showing little enthusiasm.
TEPID.origin: Latin 'tepidus' (moderately warm), from 'tepere' (to be lukewarm), from Proto-Indo-European *tep- (to be warm)
TEPID.evolution: From Latin for mildly warm; 'tepid response' means unenthusiastic; 'tepid water' is between cold and warm — the worst of both; 'lukewarm' is its near-synonym; both suggest insufficient commitment to a temperature or an opinion.
TEPID.joke: Tepid applause: the ovation that says 'I am present and technically clapping' and nothing more.

TERRA.definition: Earth or land (used in scientific and place-name compounds).
TERRA.origin: Latin 'terra' (earth, land, country), from Proto-Indo-European *ters- (to dry)
TERRA.evolution: From Latin earth/land; 'terra firma' (dry land) contrasts with sea; 'terra incognita' (unknown territory) appears on old maps; 'terracotta' is fired earth-clay; 'terrarium' is an earth container; 'Mediterranean' means 'middle of the earth.'
TERRA.joke: Terra incognita: cartography's most honest admission — here there are dragons, or at least we haven't checked.

TERSE.definition: Sparing in the use of words; abruptly brief.
TERSE.origin: Latin 'tersus' (wiped clean, neat), past participle of 'tergere' (to wipe, rub clean)
TERSE.evolution: From Latin 'wiped clean'; originally meant polished or neat in expression; narrowed to mean briefly expressed, often abruptly so; 'terse reply' implies the responder could say more but chose not to; the word is appropriately short.
TERSE.joke: A terse reply: the communication equivalent of paying exactly the minimum required and not a syllable more.

TESTY.origin: Old French 'testu' (headstrong), from 'teste' (head), from Latin 'testa' (pot, skull) — referring to being 'heady'
TESTY.evolution: From Old French 'headstrong' (led by the head rather than reason); shifted to mean irritable and quick to take offense; 'a testy exchange' describes a peevish, fractious conversation; 'getting testy' means becoming irritated.
TESTY.joke: Testy: from 'headstrong' to 'irritable' — the word evolved by getting on its own nerves.

THANK.definition: To express gratitude; an expression of gratitude.
THANK.origin: Old English 'þancian' (to thank), from 'þanc' (thought, goodwill, grace), from Proto-Germanic *thankaz
THANK.evolution: Old English word related to 'think' — a thank originally was a good thought directed at someone; 'thanks' is plural but used as singular; 'no thanks' as a refusal is strikingly ambiguous; 'thanksgiving' institutionalized gratitude as a national event.
THANK.joke: Thank and think share a root: gratitude began as a directed thought, which explains why it requires effort.

THEFT.origin: Old English 'þēofþ, þīefþ' (theft), from 'þēof' (thief) + '-þ' (abstract noun suffix); from Proto-Germanic *þeubithō
THEFT.evolution: Old English legal and moral term; 'grand theft' (major theft) and 'petty theft' distinguish by value; 'intellectual theft' extends to ideas; 'identity theft' is a modern crime category; 'theft of time' is a management accusation.
THEFT.joke: Identity theft: the crime that stole the concept of 'you' and then tried to apply for a car loan.

THEIR.definition: Possessive form of 'they'; belonging to them; used as a gender-neutral singular possessive.
THEIR.origin: Old Norse 'þeira' (of them), which replaced Old English 'hiera' (their) in Middle English
THEIR.evolution: Replaced Old English form via Norse influence; 'their' vs. 'there' vs. 'they're' is a persistent source of written errors; singular 'their' (referring to one person of unspecified gender) has been used since the 14th century — Shakespeare used it.
THEIR.joke: Singular 'their': the grammatical solution to gender-neutral pronouns that Shakespeare was already using 400 years ago.

THEME.definition: A subject of a speech or piece of writing; a recurring musical phrase; the underlying idea; to give a theme to.
THEME.origin: Old French 'teme', from Latin 'thema', from Greek 'thema' (proposition, deposit), from 'tithenai' (to place)
THEME.evolution: From Greek 'what is placed'; a musical theme recurs and develops; 'theme park' organizes entertainment around a central concept; 'thesis' and 'theme' share Greek roots; 'themed party' applies the organizing concept to social events.
THEME.joke: Theme park: the entertainment venue that made narrative coherence into a ticket price.

THERE.definition: In, at, or to that place; used to introduce a sentence; an expression of comfort.
THERE.origin: Old English 'þǣr' (there, in that place), from Proto-Germanic *þar-
THERE.evolution: Old English fundamental locative; 'there, there' as comfort is a reduplication; existential 'there is/are' introduces new subjects; 'been there, done that' became a dismissive phrase; 'over there' and 'right there' specify distance.
THERE.joke: There, there: the comfort phrase that admits the problem exists but declines to solve it.

THESE.definition: Plural of 'this'; referring to things close to the speaker.
THESE.origin: Old English 'þēs' (these), plural of 'þes' (this); from Proto-Germanic *þa-
THESE.evolution: Old English demonstrative plural; 'these days' means 'nowadays'; 'these things happen' is a resignation expression; fundamental to pointing out nearby objects or concepts; demonstratives ('this/that/these/those') form a spatial grid in language.
THESE.joke: These days: the phrase that signals a mild complaint about contemporary life is incoming.

THETA.definition: The eighth letter of the Greek alphabet (Θ, θ); used in mathematics and physics.
THETA.origin: Greek 'thēta', from Phoenician 'teth' (a Phoenician letter), related to Hebrew 'tet'
THETA.evolution: Greek letter used extensively in mathematics (angle variables) and physics; 'theta waves' are brain waves associated with drowsiness; in options trading, theta measures time decay; the 'th' sound in English comes from the theta phoneme.
THETA.joke: Theta: the Greek letter that got imported to describe angles, brain states, and how options lose value with time.

THICK.definition: Having a large distance between opposite surfaces; dense; (informal) stupid; in the middle of.
THICK.evolution: Old English for density/depth; 'thick and thin' means through all conditions; 'thick as thieves' means very close friends; 'thick skin' means emotional resilience; 'in the thick of it' means in the busiest part; British 'thick' for stupid is colloquial.
THICK.joke: Thick as thieves: the friendship metaphor that decided density was the best indicator of loyalty.

THIEF.evolution: Old English fundamental legal term; 'time is a thief' is a philosophical lament; 'opportunity makes a thief' is a proverb about circumstance; 'thief in the night' describes an unexpected arrival; 'identity thief' is a modern crime category.
THIEF.joke: Time is a thief: the metaphor that blames the calendar for all personal losses and never files a police report.

THIGH.definition: The part of the human leg between the hip and the knee.
THIGH.evolution: Old English anatomical term; 'high-thigh' boots extend above the knee; chicken thigh vs. breast is a culinary divide; the thigh is one of the body's largest muscle groups; 'to smite hip and thigh' is biblical for total defeat.
THIGH.joke: Smite hip and thigh: the biblical phrase for thorough defeat that survived by being so specific it can't be misunderstood.

THING.definition: An object, event, or concept; a matter; a situation; an obsession or concern.
THING.origin: Old English 'þing' (meeting, assembly, thing), from Proto-Germanic *þingą (assembly, judicial meeting)
THING.evolution: Originally an assembly or legal meeting; shifted to mean any object or matter; the Althing (Icelandic parliament, founded 930 CE) preserves the original meaning; 'having a thing for' someone means attraction; 'it's a thing' validates modern phenomena.
THING.joke: It's a thing: the contemporary validation system that requires critical mass before granting legitimacy.

THINK.definition: To have a thought or opinion; to use one's mind; to consider.
THINK.origin: Old English 'þencan' (to think, conceive), from Proto-Germanic *þankijanan, related to 'thank'
THINK.evolution: Old English fundamental cognitive verb; 'think tank' emerged in the 20th century for policy research groups; 'think outside the box' became an overused corporate call to creativity; 'overthinking' is a modern psychological concept.
THINK.joke: Think outside the box: the instruction delivered inside every PowerPoint presentation box, without apparent irony.

THIRD.definition: Constituting number three in a sequence; one of three equal parts.
THIRD.evolution: Ordinal of three; 'third wheel' (an unwanted extra in a couple's outing) uses the bicycle metaphor; 'Third World' (now developing nations) was Cold War terminology for non-aligned countries; 'third time lucky' is a perseverance proverb.
THIRD.joke: Third wheel: the bicycle metaphor for social superfluity — present, functional, but nobody's first choice.

THONG.definition: A strip of leather; a sandal fastened by a strap between the toes; minimal underwear.
THONG.origin: Old English 'þwang' (thong, strip of leather), from Proto-Germanic *þwangaz
THONG.evolution: Old English strip of leather used for binding; in Australian and New Zealand English, 'thongs' are flip-flop sandals; the underwear sense developed in American English in the late 20th century, causing international confusion.
THONG.joke: Thong: the word that means sandal in Australia and underwear in America — choose your audience carefully before speaking.

THORN.definition: A sharp pointed projection on a plant; a source of trouble or pain; the Old English letter þ.
THORN.evolution: Old English; the letter thorn (þ) represented the 'th' sound; when printing presses replaced hand-copying, the thorn was replaced by 'y' (similar shape), giving us 'ye olde' which was actually pronounced 'the olde'; 'thorn in one's side' means a persistent irritant.
THORN.joke: Ye Olde: the thorn letter þ being misread as 'y' by typesetters — the most successful typographical error in pub naming history.

THOSE.definition: Plural of 'that'; referring to things at a distance from the speaker.
THOSE.origin: Old English 'þās' (those), from Proto-Germanic *þa-; gradually displaced by this demonstrative form
THOSE.evolution: Old English demonstrative plural for distant things; 'those were the days' is a nostalgic phrase; 'those people' can be exclusive or dismissive depending on context; 'one of those things' accepts unavoidable situations with resignation.
THOSE.joke: Those were the days: the nostalgic claim that history was uniformly better before now.

THREE.definition: The number 3; a group of three.
THREE.origin: Old English 'þrīe, þrēo, þrī' (three), from Proto-Germanic *þrijiz, from Proto-Indo-European *tréyes
THREE.evolution: Cogante across Indo-European languages; three is considered a magic number in folklore, literature, and religion; 'three-act structure' organizes most storytelling; 'rule of three' in rhetoric creates rhythm; 'thrice' (three times) is its archaic adverb.
THREE.joke: Three: the number that folklore, religion, and Hollywood screenwriting all independently agreed was the optimal quantity.

THREW.definition: Past tense of 'throw'; propelled through the air with force.
THREW.origin: Old English 'þrāwan' (to throw, twist), past tense 'þrēow'; from Proto-Germanic *þrēwaną
THREW.evolution: Strong past tense of 'throw'; 'threw a curveball' means surprised or complicated things; 'threw me off' means confused or disoriented; 'threw caution to the wind' means acted recklessly; irregular form maintained through heavy usage.
THREW.joke: Threw caution to the wind: the idiom for recklessness that successfully identifies what you shouldn't do with caution.

THROB.definition: To beat or pulse with a strong, regular rhythm; such a beat.
THROB.origin: Middle English 'throbben', possibly imitative; or from Old English *þrobbian; exact origin uncertain
THROB.evolution: Possibly imitative of a pulsing sound; 'throbbing headache' describes a painful pulsating sensation; 'the music throbbed' describes deep bass frequencies; 'hearthrob' (romantically attractive person) dates to the 19th century.
THROB.joke: Heartthrob: the celebrity whose appeal is measured in cardiovascular palpitations per appearance.

THROW.definition: To propel through the air; a piece of decorative fabric; a distance something is thrown.
THROW.origin: Old English 'þrāwan' (to throw, twist, turn), from Proto-Germanic *þrēwaną
THROW.evolution: Old English for propelling; 'throw a party' means to organize one; 'throw in the towel' (boxing — conceding defeat) became a general metaphor; 'throw shade' means subtle public insult; 'don't throw good money after bad' is investment advice.
THROW.joke: Throw in the towel: the boxing concession that became the diplomatic surrender for every other kind of defeat.

THRUM.definition: To make a continuous rhythmic humming sound; to play a stringed instrument rhythmically; such a sound.
THRUM.origin: Possibly from Old English *þrum (edge, end), or imitative; related to Middle High German 'drumen'
THRUM.evolution: Possibly imitative; the continuous vibration sound of engines, bees, or plucked strings; 'the city thrummed with activity' uses the vibrant energy metaphor; 'thrumming guitar strings' describes rhythmic strumming.
THRUM.joke: Thrum: the word for low continuous sound that is itself onomatopoetically quite satisfying to say aloud.

THUMB.definition: The short thick first digit of the human hand; to request a lift by gesturing; to leaf through pages.
THUMB.evolution: Old English word; 'thumbs up/down' gesture originates from Roman gladiatorial combat (though the actual practice is disputed); 'Tom Thumb' is the tiny folk hero; 'thumb through a book' means to browse quickly; 'under someone's thumb' means controlled by them.
THUMB.joke: Under someone's thumb: when control is so complete that it fits under the smallest finger.

THUMP.definition: To hit heavily and loudly; a heavy dull blow.
THUMP.origin: Imitative/expressive; the sound of a heavy blow; possibly from Low German 'thumpen'; attested 16th century
THUMP.evolution: Onomatopoeic; 'thumper' is the rabbit in Bambi; 'heart was thumping' describes anxious cardiac awareness; 'thumping majority' (British) means a large electoral victory; 'to give someone a thumping' means to beat them thoroughly.
THUMP.joke: Thumping majority: when winning by a large margin requires the word for hitting something very hard.

THYME.definition: An aromatic herb used in cooking and medicine.
THYME.origin: Old French 'thym', from Latin 'thymum', from Greek 'thymon' (thyme), from 'thyein' (to burn as a sacrifice, make smoke)
THYME.evolution: From Greek sacrifice/smoke — thyme was burned as an offering and fumigant; the silent 'h' was added in the 16th century to align with Greek spelling; thyme is essential in bouquet garni; bees that feed on thyme produce distinctively flavored honey.
THYME.joke: Thyme: spelled with a silent 'h' since the 16th century, which is either respectful of Greek or deeply inconsiderate.

TIARA.definition: A jeweled ornamental band worn on the head; the Pope's triple crown.
TIARA.origin: Latin 'tiara', from Greek 'tiara' (a Persian headdress), of Persian origin
TIARA.evolution: From Persian royal headgear through Greek and Latin; the Pope's tiara (triple crown) was last worn in 1964; the modern tiara as formal female headwear developed in the 19th century; now associated with weddings, proms, and pageants.
TIARA.joke: Tiara: the Persian royal headpiece that descended through Greek, Latin, and eventually the prom queen circuit.

TIBIA.definition: The inner and typically larger of the two bones of the lower leg.
TIBIA.origin: Latin 'tibia' (shinbone, also a flute or pipe made from bone)
TIBIA.evolution: From Latin — the same word means both shinbone and the flute instrument made from leg bones; the tibia is the larger of the two lower leg bones (with the fibula); 'tibial' refers to this bone; injuries to the tibia are especially painful.
TIBIA.joke: The tibia is also a flute in Latin — which explains why leg injuries always sound so dramatic.

TIDAL.definition: Relating to or affected by tides; resembling the ebb and flow of tides.
TIDAL.origin: From 'tide' + '-al'; Old English 'tīd' (time, season, tide), from Proto-Germanic *tīdiz
TIDAL.evolution: Extended from 'tide'; 'tidal wave' was the original term for tsunami before 'tsunami' replaced it in scientific usage; 'tidal bore' is a wave that travels upstream; 'tidal power' harvests sea energy; 'tidal shift' describes significant change.
TIDAL.joke: Tidal wave: the scientifically imprecise term that refuses to retire despite tsunami's perfectly good candidacy.

TIGER.definition: A large wild cat with orange and black stripes; a fierce or energetic person.
TIGER.origin: Old French 'tigre', from Latin 'tigris', from Greek 'tigris', from Persian 'tigra' (sharp, pointed), or from an Avestan source
TIGER.evolution: Possibly from Persian 'sharp' or from a pre-Indo-European source; 'paper tiger' (Mao Zedong's phrase) means something that looks threatening but is harmless; 'tiger economy' describes rapidly developing Asian economies; 'eye of the tiger' is motivational.
TIGER.joke: Paper tiger: the diplomatic metaphor for anything that looks scary until you apply any pressure whatsoever.

TIGHT.definition: Fixed firmly; close-fitting; (informal) drunk; stingy; in a difficult situation.
TIGHT.evolution: From Old Norse; 'tight ship' means efficiently run organization; 'tightfisted' means miserly; 'in a tight spot' means in difficulty; 'tight' for drunk is colloquial; 'sit tight' means wait without moving; 'airtight alibi' uses the waterproof sense.
TIGHT.joke: Running a tight ship: the naval metaphor for an organization where nothing leaks, including information.

TILDE.definition: The diacritical mark ~ used over letters in Spanish and Portuguese; used in various notations.
TILDE.evolution: From Latin 'title'; used in Spanish to mark the palatal nasal (ñ); in mathematics and logic, represents 'approximately' or 'similar to'; in computing used in URLs, file paths, and regular expressions; in texting, used for sarcastic tone.
TILDE.joke: Tilde in texting: the punctuation that was a Spanish letter until it became the mark for sarcasm~

TIMER.definition: A device that measures or records a period of time; one who times an event.
TIMER.origin: From 'time' + '-er'; Old English 'tīma' (time, period), from Proto-Germanic *tīmô
TIMER.evolution: Agent noun from 'time'; kitchen timers became ubiquitous; 'on a timer' means set to activate at a specific time; 'bomb timer' appears in thriller plots; 'timer running out' became a sports metaphor for urgency.
TIMER.joke: Timer running out: the phrase that creates urgency in sports, cooking, and every heist film ever made.

TIMID.definition: Showing a lack of courage or confidence; easily frightened.
TIMID.origin: Latin 'timidus' (fearful, timid), from 'timere' (to fear), from Proto-Indo-European *twei- (to be afraid)
TIMID.evolution: From Latin 'to fear'; 'timidity' is the noun form; 'timid investor' describes risk-averse financial behavior; 'a timid knock' is barely audible; the word is gentler than 'cowardly,' suggesting shyness rather than failure of nerve.
TIMID.joke: Timid knock: the sound of someone who isn't quite sure they want the door to open.

TIPSY.definition: Slightly drunk; inclined to tilt or be unsteady.
TIPSY.origin: From 'tipsy' (unstable), from 'tip' (to lean over, tilt) + '-sy' (adjectival suffix); attested 16th century
TIPSY.evolution: From physical unsteadiness; a 'tipsy' person tilts the way an unbalanced object tips; positioned between sober and drunk on the intoxication scale; 'tipsy cake' is an English dessert soaked in alcohol; the word maintains the physical instability metaphor.
TIPSY.joke: Tipsy: the stage of intoxication where you're still making promises you'll remember forgetting tomorrow.

TITAN.definition: A person or thing of great size, strength, or importance; in Greek mythology, one of the pre-Olympian gods.
TITAN.origin: Greek 'Titān', one of the race of elder gods; of uncertain pre-Greek origin, possibly from a root meaning 'to stretch'
TITAN.evolution: From Greek mythology; the Titans were the older generation of gods overthrown by the Olympians; 'Titanic' derives from them (as does the unfortunate ship); Saturn's largest moon is Titan; 'industry titan' uses the giant-of-power meaning.
TITAN.joke: Titanic: the ship named for being giant, which proved that naming something for its size doesn't help with icebergs.

TITHE.definition: One tenth of annual income, traditionally taken as a tax for the Church; to pay or take a tithe.
TITHE.origin: Old English 'tēoþa' (tenth), from 'tīen' (ten); the same root as 'tenth'
TITHE.evolution: Old English; the Church required one-tenth of income as a tax for centuries in Europe; 'tithing' is also a historical administrative unit; modern churches that practice tithing ask for 10% of income; the mathematical exactness made evasion obvious.
TITHE.joke: Tithe: the original subscription model — ten percent of everything, auto-renewed annually by canon law.

TITLE.definition: A name describing a rank, function, or achievement; the name of a book or film; legal ownership; to give a title to.
TITLE.origin: Old French 'title', from Latin 'titulus' (inscription, label, title of honor)
TITLE.evolution: From Latin inscription; 'title deed' proves property ownership; 'job title' signals organizational rank; 'title track' of an album shares the album's name; 'championship title' is the prize in competition; 'titular' means in name only.
TITLE.joke: Titular: the modifier that acknowledges a title while suggesting the role behind it is mostly decorative.

TOAST.definition: Sliced bread browned by heat; a call to drink in honor of someone; to brown by heat; to honor with a toast.
TOAST.origin: Old French 'toster' (to roast, grill), from Vulgar Latin *tostare, from Latin 'tostus' (roasted); toast as a drink tribute because spiced toast was added to wine
TOAST.evolution: Bread toast and drinking toast share an origin; wine was drunk with a piece of spiced toast floating in it; 'toasting' someone transferred the flavor-giving to the person being honored; 'you're toast' (you're done for) is informal American slang.
TOAST.joke: You're toast: the threat that made breakfast food into the vocabulary of doom.

TODAY.definition: The present day; on or during this day.
TODAY.origin: Old English 'tōdæg' (today, on this day), from 'tō' (to, at) + 'dæg' (day); from Proto-Germanic
TODAY.evolution: Old English compound; 'today's news, tomorrow's fish and chips' reflects information's ephemerality; 'no time like today' is a procrastination antidote; 'live in today' is mindfulness advice; 'today' contrasts with 'yesterday' and 'tomorrow' in most reflective thought.
TODAY.joke: Today: the day that is always arriving and immediately becoming yesterday, yet somehow still impossible to use efficiently.

TODDY.definition: A drink made with spirits and hot water; the sap of some palm trees.
TODDY.origin: Hindi 'tārī' (palm tree sap, palm wine), from Sanskrit 'tāla' (palmyra palm); entered English through colonial India
TODDY.evolution: From Hindi/Sanskrit via colonial trade; originally the fermented sap of the palm; adapted to a hot whiskey drink; 'hot toddy' became a standard cold/flu remedy in Anglo-American folk medicine; a 'toddy stick' is a cocktail muddler.
TODDY.joke: Hot toddy: the remedy that manages to be simultaneously medicinal and enjoyable — always a suspicious combination.

TOKEN.definition: A thing serving as a sign of a feeling; a small coin substitute; a nominal gesture; only for show.
TOKEN.origin: Old English 'tācen' (sign, symbol, mark), from Proto-Germanic *taikną
TOKEN.evolution: Old English for a sign or symbol; 'token gesture' means symbolic action without real substance; 'tokenism' means symbolic inclusion without meaningful representation; 'book token' is a gift voucher; arcade tokens substitute for money.
TOKEN.joke: Token gesture: the acknowledgment that something should be done, done in the minimum possible way.

TONAL.definition: Of or relating to tone or tonality; relating to the music system of keys.
TONAL.origin: From 'tone' + '-al'; Latin 'tonus', from Greek 'tonos' (tone, tension), from 'teinein' (to stretch)
TONAL.evolution: From 'tone'; 'tonal music' uses a home key (as opposed to atonal music); 'tonal language' uses pitch to distinguish meaning (Mandarin, Yoruba); 'tonal quality' describes the character of a sound or color palette.
TONAL.joke: Tonal music vs. atonal: the debate that made 'having a home key' controversial in the 20th century.

TONGA.definition: A light two-wheeled horse-drawn carriage used in South Asia; also the island nation in the Pacific.
TONGA.origin: Hindi 'ṭāṃgā' (a type of horse-drawn vehicle); the country name Tonga is from the Tongan language meaning 'south'
TONGA.evolution: Two distinct words; the South Asian tonga is a colonial-era transport still used in some areas; the Pacific nation Tonga was first contacted by Europeans in the 17th century and joined the United Nations in 1999.
TONGA.joke: Tonga: simultaneously a horse carriage and a Pacific nation — context ensures you arrive at the right destination.

TONIC.definition: A medicinal substance giving strength or energy; tonic water; the first note of a musical scale; invigorating.
TONIC.origin: Greek 'tonikos' (of tone, stretching), from 'tonos' (tone, tension), from 'teinein' (to stretch)
TONIC.evolution: From Greek tone/tension; Victorian patent medicines were 'tonics'; tonic water contained quinine (anti-malarial) making gin and tonic a colonial health drink; in music, the tonic is the root note; 'tonic' also means invigorating.
TONIC.joke: Gin and tonic: the colonial medicine that survived the dissolution of empire by tasting good.

TOOTH.definition: A hard calcified structure in the jaw; a projecting part; a taste or liking.
TOOTH.origin: Old English 'tōþ' (tooth), from Proto-Germanic *tanþuz, from Proto-Indo-European *dent-
TOOTH.evolution: Old English; 'fight tooth and nail' means with every available means; 'long in the tooth' (old) comes from how horses' gums recede with age; 'sweet tooth' means a preference for sugar; 'toothless law' has no enforcement power.
TOOTH.joke: Long in the tooth: the horse-dentistry metaphor for age that humans have been applying to themselves for centuries.

TOPAZ.origin: Old French 'topace', from Latin 'topazus', from Greek 'topazos', possibly from Sanskrit 'tapas' (heat, fire) or from a Red Sea island called Topazios
TOPAZ.evolution: Etymology disputed; possibly from a legendary island in the Red Sea; the November birthstone; topaz occurs in various colors (yellow, blue, pink) though golden yellow is most associated with the name; 'smoky topaz' is actually a different mineral.
TOPAZ.joke: Smoky topaz: the jewel with an identity problem — named topaz, actually quartz, rebranded successfully anyway.

TOPIC.definition: A subject dealt with in discussion, study, or writing.
TOPIC.origin: Medieval Latin 'topica', from Greek 'topika' (topics), title of Aristotle's treatise, from 'topos' (place, topic)
TOPIC.evolution: From Aristotle's 'Topics' which organized arguments by 'places' (common forms of argument); extended from rhetorical categories to any subject of discussion; 'off-topic' means straying from the subject; 'topic sentence' leads a paragraph.
TOPIC.joke: Topic sentence: the sentence Aristotle probably imagined when he organized arguments by physical place.
