VOTER.joke: Swing voter: the electoral equivalent of the tie-breaker who didn't know they had this much power.

VOUCH.definition: To assert or confirm as a result of one's own experience; to act as a guarantor.
VOUCH.origin: Old French 'voucher' (to call, summon as witness), from Latin 'vocare' (to call)
VOUCH.evolution: From Latin 'to call'; 'to vouch for someone' means to guarantee their character; 'voucher' (a document granting entitlement) derives from this; 'I can vouch for it' is a personal endorsement; the legal sense of summoning a witness persists.
VOUCH.joke: Vouching for someone: the personal guarantee system that existed before references were a formal requirement.

VOWEL.definition: A speech sound produced with an open vocal tract; the letters a, e, i, o, u.
VOWEL.origin: Old French 'vouel', from Latin 'vocalis (littera)' (vocal letter), from 'vox' (voice)
VOWEL.evolution: From Latin 'voice'; vowels are produced without complete obstruction of the airstream; Welsh has almost no vowels in writing ('cwm' is a valley); Wheel of Fortune contestants buy vowels; 'Aeiou' was sometimes used as a motto by the Habsburg dynasty.
VOWEL.joke: Buy a vowel: Wheel of Fortune's reminder that even in linguistics, some letters cost more than others.

VYING.definition: Present participle of 'vie'; competing eagerly with someone.
VYING.origin: From 'vie', possibly from Old French 'envier' (to invite, challenge), from Latin 'invitare' (to invite)
VYING.evolution: Present participle of 'vie'; 'vying for attention' means competing for notice; 'vying for the title' means competing for championship; 'vying interests' are competing concerns; the word implies active competition between roughly matched parties.
VYING.joke: Vying for attention: the competitive format that social media platforms turned into an industry.

WACKY.definition: Funny or amusing in a slightly odd way; crazy.
WACKY.origin: Possibly from 'whack' (a blow) + '-y'; the idea of being hit on the head; attested 19th century
WACKY.evolution: Of uncertain origin; possibly from being 'whacked' (hit on the head, hence crazy); 'wacky races' became a cartoon name; 'wacky tobaccy' is slang for marijuana; the word combines mild craziness with endearing charm.
WACKY.joke: Wacky: the adjective for behavior that's too endearing to be alarming but too odd to be fully expected.

WAFER.definition: A thin, crisp biscuit or candy; a thin disc used in electronics; the communion bread.
WAFER.origin: Anglo-French 'wafre', from Middle Dutch 'wafel' (waffle, wafer), from Middle Low German 'wāfel'
WAFER.evolution: From Dutch/Low German; communion wafers represent the host in Christian rites; ice cream wafers and chocolate wafers are culinary; silicon wafers are the substrates for computer chips; 'wafer-thin' became the standard for extreme thinness.
WAFER.joke: Wafer-thin: the thickness standard that Monty Python elevated to mortal threat, forever changing our relationship with mint consumption.

WAGER.definition: A bet; to risk money on an outcome.
WAGER.origin: Anglo-French 'wageure', from Old North French 'wagier' (to pledge), from 'wage' (pledge, wage), from Frankish *waddi
WAGER.evolution: From Frankish pledge; related to 'wage' (both involve pledging something); 'wager of battle' was medieval trial by combat; 'I'll wager' is a confident assertion; Pascal's Wager is the philosophical argument for believing in God as a rational bet.
WAGER.joke: Pascal's Wager: the philosopher's gambling advice applied to theology, where the expected value calculation works out to 'just believe.'

WAGON.definition: A vehicle with four wheels pulled by horses; a railway freight car; 'on the wagon' means abstaining from alcohol.
WAGON.origin: Dutch 'wagen' (wagon, carriage), from Middle Dutch 'wagen', from Proto-Germanic *wagnaz
WAGON.evolution: From Dutch; 'on the wagon' (abstaining from alcohol) comes from water wagons used to wet down dirt roads — a reformed drinker would only drink water-wagon water; 'wagon train' was the pioneer transport; 'paddy wagon' is a police van.
WAGON.joke: On the wagon: the sobriety metaphor that asks you to imagine your drinking problem as a horse-drawn road-watering vehicle.

WAIST.definition: The part of the human body between the ribs and hips; the narrow middle part of something.
WAIST.origin: Old English 'wæst' (growth, size), from Proto-Germanic *wahstuz, from *wahsją (to grow)
WAIST.evolution: Old English for growth/size; 'waistline' measures the narrowest part of the torso; 'waistband' is the band at the top of trousers; 'waistcoat' (vest) covers the waist area; 'waspwaist' describes an extremely narrow waist.
WAIST.joke: Waistline: the anatomical measurement that appears on every clothing tag and features prominently in every January gym commercial.

WAIVE.definition: To refrain from insisting on or applying; to abandon a right or claim.
WAIVE.origin: Anglo-French 'weyver', from Old French 'gaiver' (to abandon, relinquish), from Old Norse 'veifa' (to oscillate, wave aside)
WAIVE.evolution: From Old Norse via Anglo-French; 'waive the fee' means to not charge; 'waive your right' means to surrender it voluntarily; 'waiver' is a signed document giving up a right; 'waiver wire' in fantasy sports is where unclaimed players sit.
WAIVE.joke: Sign the waiver: the document that summarizes all the ways something could go wrong before you do it anyway.

WALTZ.definition: A ballroom dance in triple time; music for this dance; to move in a casual, carefree way.
WALTZ.origin: German 'Walzer', from 'walzen' (to roll, turn, dance the waltz), from Old High German 'walzan' (to roll)
WALTZ.evolution: From German rolling/turning; considered scandalously intimate when introduced (close dancing position); 'waltz in' means to arrive casually and confidently; 'waltz off with' means to take something easily; Strauss waltzes defined 19th-century Vienna.
WALTZ.joke: Waltz in: when the dance step became the verb for arriving with unearned confidence.

WARTY.evolution: Extended from 'wart'; Oliver Cromwell's instruction to his portrait painter ('paint me warts and all') gave us the phrase 'warts and all' — depicting someone with all their flaws; 'warty toad' is the typical zoological description.
WARTY.joke: Warts and all: Oliver Cromwell's demand for honest portraiture — the most consequential portrait instruction in English history.

WASTE.definition: To use carelessly or extravagantly; material that is no longer useful; uninhabited land.
WASTE.origin: Old North French 'waster', from Latin 'vastare' (to lay waste), from 'vastus' (empty, desolate)
WASTE.evolution: From Latin emptiness/desolation; 'waste not, want not' is a frugality proverb; 'waistline' is unrelated; 'toxic waste' is hazardous industrial byproduct; 'lay waste to' means to destroy completely; 'a waste of time' is the universal frustrated assessment.
WASTE.joke: Waste not, want not: the proverb that has been losing the argument to convenience packaging since the 1950s.

WATCH.definition: To observe attentively; a timepiece worn on the wrist; a period of keeping guard.
WATCH.origin: Old English 'wæccan' (to watch, be awake), from Proto-Germanic *wakjaną, related to 'wake'
WATCH.evolution: Old English for wakefulness and observation; 'neighborhood watch' is communal surveillance; 'watch your step' is both literal and figurative advice; a wristwatch uses the observation metaphor for timekeeping; 'on watch' is a naval/military guard duty.
WATCH.joke: Watch your step: the single most useful phrase that works equally well as safety advice and a general life philosophy.

WATER.definition: The clear liquid essential for life; to pour water on; to dilute.
WATER.origin: Old English 'wæter' (water), from Proto-Germanic *watōr, from Proto-Indo-European *wódr̥
WATER.evolution: One of the oldest English words; the same Proto-Indo-European root gave Latin 'unda' (wave) and Greek 'hydōr'; 'watered-down' means diluted or weakened; 'water under the bridge' means past issues; 'water cooler conversation' is office small talk.
WATER.joke: Water cooler conversation: the informal discussions that shaped office culture before Slack could do it more efficiently.

WAVER.definition: To move in a fluttering or quivering manner; to be indecisive; to become unsteady.
WAVER.origin: Old Norse 'vafra' (to waver, hover), related to Old English 'wæfre' (restless, wavering)
WAVER.evolution: From Old Norse hovering motion; 'waver in one's resolve' means to become uncertain; 'wavering support' describes unreliable backing; 'waver between options' is indecision; the word captures the visual quality of something unstable.
WAVER.joke: Wavering support: the political term for allies who are with you right up until it's inconvenient.

WAXEN.definition: Made of or covered with wax; having a pale, smooth appearance like wax.
WAXEN.origin: Old English 'wæxen' (made of wax), from 'weax' (wax), from Proto-Germanic *wahsan
WAXEN.evolution: From 'wax'; 'waxen complexion' describes pallor associated with illness or death; waxwork figures (Madame Tussauds) are described as having waxen qualities when lifelike; the adjective implies both the material and the uncanny stillness.
WAXEN.joke: Waxen complexion: the literary description that confirms your doctor appointment was well-timed.

WEARY.definition: Feeling or showing tiredness; to make or become weary.
WEARY.origin: Old English 'wērig' (weary, exhausted), from Proto-Germanic *wōrīgaz, possibly from *wōraz (confused)
WEARY.evolution: Old English for exhaustion; 'world-weary' describes cynical disenchantment from too much experience; 'weary traveler' is a literary archetype; 'wearing on someone' (irritating) is a modern phrase from the wearing/weary root; 'grow weary of' means to tire of.
WEARY.joke: World-weary: the exhaustion that comes not from work but from paying attention for too long.

WEAVE.definition: To interlace threads to make fabric; to move in and out; an act of weaving; a hairstyle extension.
WEAVE.evolution: Old English textile technique; 'weave through traffic' uses the interlacing movement; 'weave a narrative' means to construct a complex story; hair 'weave' is an extension of natural hair; 'web' and 'weave' share a root.
WEAVE.joke: Weave a narrative: storytelling as a textile art — because all the best stories have threads you can follow and some you can't.

WEDGE.definition: A triangular piece for splitting; something causing division; to force into a space; a wedge salad.
WEDGE.evolution: Old English for the splitting tool; 'thin end of the wedge' means a seemingly small change that leads to larger ones; 'wedge issue' (a divisive political issue) uses the splitting metaphor; 'wedge heels' are platform shoes with a triangular sole.
WEDGE.joke: Thin end of the wedge: the phrase that has identified the beginning of every slippery slope since the 14th century.

WEEDY.definition: Full of weeds; (informal) thin and weak in body.
WEEDY.evolution: Extended from 'weed'; a 'weedy garden' is overgrown; a 'weedy person' is thin and lacking physical strength (like a weed that's tall but spindly); 'seedy' and 'weedy' both use botanical metaphors for undesirable qualities.
WEEDY.joke: Weedy: the botanical insult for spindly people, delivered by a plant that survives everything being against it.

WEIGH.definition: To measure the weight of; to consider carefully; to have a specified weight.
WEIGH.origin: Old English 'wegan' (to carry, move, weigh), from Proto-Germanic *weganą
WEIGH.evolution: Old English for carrying/weighing; 'weigh anchor' means to raise an anchor (originally to weigh it); 'weigh one's options' means to consider carefully; 'weigh in' is both a boxing weight check and an opinion contribution; 'weigh heavily' describes a burden on conscience.
WEIGH.joke: Weigh anchor: the naval term for lifting something to leave, because departure required heavy lifting before engines.

WEIRD.definition: Strange and unusual; supernatural fate (archaic); a person's destiny.
WEIRD.origin: Old English 'wyrd' (fate, destiny), from Proto-Germanic *wurdiz, from *werþaną (to become, happen)
WEIRD.evolution: Old English for fate/destiny; the 'weird sisters' in Macbeth were the Fates; shifted from supernatural to simply strange; 'weird' as an adjective meaning strange is relatively recent (19th century); 'weird flex but okay' is internet slang for displaying a strange personal trait.
WEIRD.joke: Weird: started as 'the Fates' in Shakespeare, ended as the adjective for your coworker who brings fish for lunch.

WELCH.definition: To fail to honor a debt or obligation (variant spelling of 'welsh').
WELCH.origin: Possibly from 'Welsh' — derogatory slang from old British prejudice; exact origin disputed
WELCH.evolution: The etymology is disputed but likely derogatory toward the Welsh people; both 'welch' and 'welsh' are considered offensive by many; the Welsh government and many style guides advise against using 'welsh' as a verb; the word persists in some contexts.
WELCH.joke: Welch/welsh: the verb that borrowed a nationality as an insult — the Welsh have been diplomatically pointing this out ever since.

WELSH.definition: Relating to Wales or its people; the Celtic language of Wales; to fail to honor a commitment (offensive).
WELSH.origin: Old English 'Wīelisc, Wēlisc' (Welsh, foreign, Celtic), from 'Wealh' (Welshman, foreigner, slave), from Proto-Germanic *walhaz (Roman, Celt)
WELSH.evolution: Old English for 'foreigner'; *walhaz also gave us 'walnut' (foreign nut) and 'Wallace'; 'Welsh rarebit' is a cheese on toast dish; Welsh is one of the oldest surviving European languages; the verbal use is considered offensive by Welsh people and linguists.
WELSH.joke: Welsh rarebit: cheese on toast with a name that has confused menus and diners equally for three centuries.

WENCH.definition: A young woman (archaic/humorous); a female servant (historical).
WENCH.origin: Middle English 'wenche', from Old English 'wencle' (girl, child), from Proto-Germanic *wankil- (unstable, weak)
WENCH.evolution: Old English for a female child; became a term for a female servant or common woman; acquired disreputable connotations over centuries; now used in historical fiction, Renaissance faires, and mock-archaic contexts; 'serving wench' is the stock period drama character.
WENCH.joke: Serving wench: the historical role that period dramas cast with modern irony and period costume.

WHACK.definition: To strike with a sharp blow; such a blow; a share; out of whack means not functioning properly.
WHACK.origin: Possibly imitative of the sound; or from 'thwack'; attested 18th century
WHACK.evolution: Possibly imitative; 'out of whack' means misaligned or malfunctioning; 'whacked out' means exhausted or intoxicated; in Mafia slang, 'to whack' means to kill; 'take a whack at' means to attempt; 'whack-a-mole' is the carnival game and policy metaphor.
WHACK.joke: Whack-a-mole: the carnival game that became political science's most accurate description of regulatory enforcement.

WHALE.definition: A large marine mammal; to hunt whales; to beat vigorously; a high-spending gambler or customer.
WHALE.evolution: Old English marine mammal; 'having a whale of a time' means having tremendous fun; 'whale' in casino/gaming contexts is a very high spender; 'whale watching' is wildlife tourism; Moby Dick made 'great white whale' a metaphor for an obsessive goal.
WHALE.joke: A whale of a time: when the largest animal in the ocean became the benchmark for enjoying yourself.

WHARF.definition: A structure built on the shore for loading and unloading ships.
WHARF.origin: Old English 'hwearf' (embankment, wharf), from Proto-Germanic *hwarbaz (a turning), from *hwarbōją (to turn)
WHARF.evolution: Old English for an embankment or turning place for boats; wharves became commercial hubs in port cities; 'wharf rat' is both a real rat and slang for a petty criminal; many city waterfronts have been redeveloped as 'wharf' districts for tourism.
WHARF.joke: Wharf rat: the only species that gets to be both a real animal and a Victorian insult without choosing.

WHEAT.definition: A cereal grain widely cultivated for food; the plant producing this grain.
WHEAT.evolution: From 'white' — wheat flour is lighter than other grains; one of the first cultivated crops (~10,000 BCE); 'separating wheat from chaff' means distinguishing valuable from worthless; gluten-free diets prompted a renewed examination of wheat's role in health.
WHEAT.joke: Separate the wheat from the chaff: agricultural quality control that became the metaphor for all sorting decisions.

WHEEL.definition: A circular object rotating on an axle; to push on wheels; to turn; to deal or scheme.
WHEEL.origin: Old English 'hwēol, hwēogol' (wheel), from Proto-Germanic *hwehlan, from Proto-Indo-European *kwekwlo- (wheel, circle)
WHEEL.evolution: One of humanity's key inventions (c.3500 BCE); 'reinvent the wheel' means to redo existing work pointlessly; 'wheel and deal' means to engage in complex transactions; 'fifth wheel' is superfluous; 'wheel of fortune' gives fortune its visual metaphor.
WHEEL.joke: Reinventing the wheel: the only innovation that's automatically criticized before anyone has seen the new wheel.

WHELP.definition: A puppy or young animal; an impudent young man; to give birth to puppies.
WHELP.evolution: Old English for a young dog; extended to other animals' young; 'a cheeky whelp' is a mildly derogatory term for an impudent young man; increasingly archaic except in historical and fantasy fiction contexts.
WHELP.joke: Cheeky whelp: the insult that sounds like you time-traveled from the 1400s but still got your point across.

WHERE.definition: In or at what place; in what situation; used to introduce a clause.
WHERE.origin: Old English 'hwǣr' (where), from Proto-Germanic *hwar, from Proto-Indo-European *kwor- (where)
WHERE.evolution: Old English fundamental locative; 'where there's a will, there's a way' is a perseverance proverb; 'where it's at' means the place of action or relevance; 'where do we go from here?' is the question after crisis; 'whereabouts' is one's location.
WHERE.joke: Where there's a will there's a way: the proverb that doubles as estate planning advice and motivational speaking.

WHICH.definition: Used to ask for information about one or more people or things from a group.
WHICH.origin: Old English 'hwilc' (which, of what kind), from Proto-Germanic *hwilīkaz, from *hwaz (who) + *-līkaz (like)
WHICH.evolution: Old English interrogative and relative pronoun; 'which is which' asks for distinction between two things; 'that vs. which' is a grammar controversy (restrictive vs. non-restrictive clauses); 'which way' means what direction.
WHICH.joke: That vs. which: the grammar dispute that style guides have been resolving and re-resolving since punctuation was invented.

WHIFF.definition: A brief smell or puff of air; to smell; to miss a ball completely in baseball/golf.
WHIFF.origin: Imitative/expressive; the sound and feeling of a brief puff; attested 16th century
WHIFF.evolution: Onomatopoeic; 'a whiff of scandal' means a hint of impropriety; 'whiff of danger' suggests a faint sense of threat; in sports, 'to whiff' means to miss completely; 'not a whiff' means no indication at all; the word suggests the briefest detection.
WHIFF.joke: A whiff of scandal: politics measured in olfactory units — the first indication before the smell becomes undeniable.

WHILE.definition: A period of time; during the time that; to pass time pleasantly.
WHILE.origin: Old English 'hwīl' (period of time, while), from Proto-Germanic *hwīlō, from Proto-Indo-European *kwi- (rest, calm)
WHILE.evolution: Old English for a period of rest or time; 'a while' vs. 'awhile' is an ongoing spelling issue; 'worth your while' means worth the time invested; 'while away the time' means to pass time pleasantly; 'once in a while' means occasionally.
WHILE.joke: Worth your while: the time investment assessment that should come with a projected ROI.

WHINE.definition: To make a high-pitched complaining cry; to complain in a feeble way; such a sound.
WHINE.origin: Old English 'hwīnan' (to make a whirring sound), from Proto-Germanic *hwīnaną
WHINE.evolution: Old English for a high-pitched sound; extended to complaining speech; 'stop whining' is the universal dismissal of complaints; 'engine whine' is mechanical; 'wind whine' is atmospheric; the vocal and mechanical meanings share the high-pitched quality.
WHINE.joke: Stop whining: the instruction that ends no whining but does confirm the listener has heard enough.

WHINY.definition: Given to whining; making a whining sound.
WHINY.origin: From 'whine' + '-y'; Old English 'hwīnan' (to make a whirring sound)
WHINY.evolution: Extended from 'whine'; 'whiny child' is the stock parenting challenge; 'whiny voice' describes a particular vocal quality; the word manages to sound like what it describes — there is something inherently irritating about it.
WHINY.joke: Whiny: the adjective that sounds like the behavior it describes — which is either brilliant design or unfortunate coincidence.

WHIRL.definition: To move rapidly in a circle; a rapid rotation; a busy or confused situation.
WHIRL.origin: Old Norse 'hvirfla' (to turn around), from Proto-Germanic *hwerflan
WHIRL.evolution: From Old Norse; 'whirlpool' is a rotating body of water; 'whirlwind' is a rotating column of air; 'whirlwind romance' is brief and intense; 'give it a whirl' means to try; 'my head is in a whirl' means confusion from overwhelming input.
WHIRL.joke: Whirlwind romance: the courtship measured in rotational speed — intense, brief, and leaves debris.

WHISK.definition: To move or take quickly; a kitchen utensil for beating; to beat or stir rapidly.
WHISK.origin: Old Norse 'viska' (to twist, a wisp), related to 'wisp'; attested 15th century
WHISK.evolution: From Old Norse twisting/wisp; a kitchen whisk beats air into mixtures; 'whisked away' means removed quickly; 'whisker' (facial hair) shares the root — both are wispy/sweeping; 'whisk a broom' means to sweep briefly.
WHISK.joke: Whisked away: culinary action turned romantic metaphor — when beating became departing in a hurry.

WHITE.definition: Of the color of snow; relating to a racial group; a white piece in chess.
WHITE.origin: Old English 'hwīt' (white), from Proto-Germanic *hwītaz, from Proto-Indo-European *kweid- (to shine, white)
WHITE.evolution: Old English color word; 'white lie' is a harmless falsehood; 'white flag' signals surrender; 'white noise' is random sound masking other sounds; 'white elephant' is an unwanted costly gift; 'white collar' describes office workers.
WHITE.joke: White elephant gift: an object so unwanted it needed its own gift exchange category with its own mythology.

WHOLE.definition: All of; not broken or damaged; a thing complete in itself.
WHOLE.origin: Old English 'hāl' (whole, healthy, uninjured), from Proto-Germanic *hailaz
WHOLE.evolution: Old English for health and completeness; 'whole' and 'healthy' share a root; 'hale and hearty' and 'whole' are cognates; 'on the whole' means generally; 'whole nine yards' means everything; 'wholly' and 'holy' are related.
WHOLE.joke: The whole nine yards: nine yards of what is still vigorously disputed by historians who clearly have time to spare.

WHOOP.definition: To shout for joy or excitement; a shout of this kind.
WHOOP.origin: Old French 'houper' (to shout a war cry), imitative of the sound; attested 15th century
WHOOP.evolution: From Old French imitative; 'whooping cough' produces a characteristic whooping sound when inhaling; 'whoop it up' means to celebrate loudly; 'whoops' is an exclamation for a mistake; 'big whoop' is sarcastic dismissal.
WHOOP.joke: Big whoop: the sarcastic diminishment of celebrations that probably deserved at least a moderate whoop.

WHOSE.definition: Belonging to which person; of which.
WHOSE.origin: Old English 'hwæs' (whose), genitive of 'hwā' (who); from Proto-Germanic *hwaz
WHOSE.evolution: Old English genitive of 'who'; 'whose' vs. 'who's' is a common spelling error; 'whose side are you on?' questions loyalty; 'whose idea was this?' assigns responsibility; the word can refer to people or things ('the book whose cover...').
WHOSE.joke: Whose vs. who's: the apostrophe debate that grammar teachers have been losing since the invention of the apostrophe.

WIDEN.origin: From 'wide' + '-en' (causative suffix); Old English 'wīd' (wide, broad), from Proto-Germanic *wīdaz
WIDEN.evolution: Causative of 'wide'; 'widen the gap' means to increase a disparity; 'widening one's horizons' means expanding one's range of experience; 'widening a road' is infrastructure; 'eyes widened in surprise' is a physical description of shock.
WIDEN.joke: Widen your horizons: the advice that turns a metaphorical landscape into a personal development plan.

WIDER.definition: Comparative of 'wide'; having greater width; more comprehensive.
WIDER.origin: Old English 'wīd' (wide), comparative form; from Proto-Germanic *wīdaz
WIDER.evolution: Comparative of 'wide'; 'wider context' means the broader surrounding situation; 'wider audience' means more people; 'give a wider berth' means to give more space; the comparative form often implies the need to consider more than is immediately obvious.
WIDER.joke: Wider context: the thing experts ask for when the current context is generating the wrong conclusions.

WIDOW.definition: A woman whose husband has died; to make someone a widow.
WIDOW.origin: Old English 'widewe' (widow), from Proto-Germanic *widuwō, from Proto-Indo-European *widʰéweh₂ (empty, separated)
WIDOW.evolution: From Proto-Indo-European 'separated/empty'; 'widow's peak' (hairline forming a V) was believed to predict early widowhood; 'grass widow' is a woman whose husband is temporarily absent; 'widow's walk' is a coastal roof platform for watching for ships.
WIDOW.joke: Widow's peak: the hairline that medieval people turned into a mortality prediction system — because 15th-century hairstyling was high stakes.

WIDTH.definition: The measurement from side to side.
WIDTH.origin: Old English 'wīdþu' (width, breadth), from 'wīd' (wide) + '-þu' (abstract suffix); from Proto-Germanic *wīdaz
WIDTH.evolution: Old English abstract from 'wide'; width is one of the three dimensions; 'bandwidth' in networking describes data-carrying capacity; 'full width' means spanning entirely; 'at arm's width' describes a short distance; measurements replaced 'wideness' in most contexts.
WIDTH.joke: Bandwidth: the network measurement named after width that became a metaphor for every human's capacity for tasks.

WIELD.definition: To hold and use a weapon or tool; to have and exercise power.
WIELD.origin: Old English 'wieldan, gewieldan' (to control, possess), from Proto-Germanic *waldijaną
WIELD.evolution: Old English for control/possession; 'wield power' means to exercise authority; 'wield influence' means to use one's position effectively; 'wield a pen' means to write; the word implies both possession and active use.
WIELD.joke: Wield a pen: the phrase that makes writing sound like it requires a weapon license.

WIGHT.definition: A person (archaic); a supernatural creature in modern fantasy.
WIGHT.origin: Old English 'wiht' (creature, thing, person), from Proto-Germanic *wihtiz
WIGHT.evolution: Old English general word for a creature or person; now almost entirely archaic except in fantasy literature and games; 'Game of Thrones' wights are undead creatures; Tolkien's Barrow-wights are malevolent spirits; the Isle of Wight preserves the name geographically.
WIGHT.joke: Wight: the Old English word for 'person' that survived by becoming undead in fantasy literature.

WILLY.definition: A pet name for William; the penis (informal/childish); the heebie-jeebies (give the willies).
WILLY.origin: Diminutive of William, from Old German 'Willahelm' (will + helmet); 'willies' of unknown origin
WILLY.evolution: Pet form of William; 'willy-nilly' means haphazardly or whether one wants to or not (from 'will he, nill he' — will or won't); 'free willy' has both innocent and double meanings; 'the willies' (a sense of unease) has unknown etymology.
WILLY.joke: Willy-nilly: the adverb for haphazardness that nobody realized was asking a question about free will.

WIMPY.origin: From 'wimp' + '-y'; 'wimp' possibly from 'whimper' or from J. Wellington Wimpy (the Popeye character)
WIMPY.evolution: Possibly from the Popeye character J. Wellington Wimpy, who was timid and food-obsessed; 'wimp out' means to fail to do something through cowardice; the Wimpy hamburger chain in Britain was named for the character; the adjective 'wimpy' is now standard for cowardly behavior.
WIMPY.joke: Wimpy: the burger chain named after a cowardly cartoon character — which is either brave branding or complete self-awareness.

WINCE.definition: To make a slight involuntary movement in response to pain; such a movement.
WINCE.origin: Old French 'wencir, guenchir' (to avoid, turn aside), from Frankish *wankjan (to waver)
WINCE.evolution: From Old French avoiding/turning; a wince is smaller than a flinch; 'cringe-worthy' and 'wince-inducing' overlap; 'wince at the memory' describes retroactive embarrassment; the word captures the involuntary nature of the response.
WINCE.joke: Wince at a memory: the involuntary response to past mistakes that proves the body keeps score.

WINCH.definition: A hauling or lifting device using a rope wound around a drum; to haul with a winch.
WINCH.origin: Old English 'wince' (pulley, roller), from Proto-Germanic *winkjan (to be crooked, turn aside)
WINCH.evolution: Old English for a turning/winding device; 'winch someone up' means to hoist them; mountain rescue, construction, and maritime operations use winches; 'hand winch' requires manual winding; the mechanical principle is a wheel and axle variation.
WINCH.joke: Winch vs. wince: two words that are one letter apart and equally useful in situations involving heavy lifting and bad memories.

WINDY.definition: Characterized by strong winds; using too many words; passing gas (informal).
WINDY.origin: From 'wind' + '-y'; Old English 'wind' (wind, breath), from Proto-Germanic *windaz
WINDY.evolution: Extended from wind; 'a windy day' is meteorological; 'windy speech' means verbose; 'windy city' is Chicago's nickname (though actually from political boasting, not weather); the informal 'windy' for flatulent is a childhood staple.
WINDY.joke: Windy city: Chicago's nickname came from politicians' rhetoric, not the lake wind — though both remain significant.

WISER.definition: Comparative of 'wise'; having more wisdom or good judgment.
WISER.origin: Old English 'wīs' (wise, knowing), comparative form; from Proto-Germanic *wīsaz
WISER.evolution: Comparative of 'wise'; 'none the wiser' means no more informed than before; 'sadder but wiser' means having learned through painful experience; 'older and wiser' is the optimistic theory of aging; 'being the wiser for it' means having gained wisdom.
WISER.joke: None the wiser: the outcome of explanations so thorough they left the listener exactly as confused as before.

WISPY.definition: Thin and delicate; of a strand of hair or smoke.
WISPY.origin: From 'wisp' + '-y'; 'wisp' from Middle English, possibly from Old Norse 'visk' (a wisp), or Proto-Germanic *wispaz
WISPY.evolution: Extended from 'wisp' (a small bundle of straw or hair); 'wispy cloud' is thin and feathery; 'wispy hair' is fine and sparse; 'will-o'-the-wisp' is a ghostly light over marshes (and a metaphor for elusive things); the word suggests fragility and insubstantiality.
WISPY.joke: Will-o'-the-wisp: the marsh ghost that became the metaphor for anything impossible to grasp, which marsh ghosts generally are.

WITCH.definition: A woman thought to have magic powers; to use witchcraft; to enchant.
WITCH.origin: Old English 'wicce' (female witch), from 'wicca' (male witch, sorcerer), from Proto-Germanic *wikkjaną
WITCH.evolution: Old English for a magic practitioner; 'witch hunt' was literal (Salem, 1692) and became a metaphor for persecution of dissenters; 'witch doctor' is now considered offensive; modern Wicca (pagan religion) revived the positive use; 'bewitching' means enchanting.
WITCH.joke: Witch hunt: from Salem's hysteria to political metaphor — the journey was unfortunately direct.

WITTY.definition: Showing quick and inventive verbal humor.
WITTY.origin: Old English 'wittig' (intelligent, knowing), from 'witt' (understanding, reason), from Proto-Germanic *witjaz
WITTY.evolution: Old English for intelligent; narrowed to verbal cleverness; 'witty repartee' is quick, clever response; 'half-wit' is the insult for limited intelligence; 'outwit' means to be cleverer than; 'wit' as intelligence is now archaic except in 'half-wit' and 'dim-wit.'
WITTY.joke: Half-wit: the insult that accidentally implies the person has completed 50% of the intelligence requirements.

WOKEN.definition: Past participle of 'wake'; having been roused from sleep.
WOKEN.origin: Old English 'wacan' (to wake, be awake), past participle form; from Proto-Germanic *wakaną
WOKEN.evolution: Past participle of 'wake'; 'woke' (socially aware) emerged from African-American Vernacular English in the early 21st century; 'woken up' means both literally aroused from sleep and metaphorically made aware; the political use of 'woke' has become highly contested.
WOKEN.joke: Woke: the verb for waking up that became both a political compliment and insult, simultaneously, in the same decade.

WOMAN.origin: Old English 'wīfmann' (woman), from 'wīf' (woman, wife) + 'mann' (person, human being)
WOMAN.evolution: Old English compound of 'female person'; 'womankind' refers to women collectively; 'womanly' describes qualities associated with women; 'womanhood' is the state of being a woman; 'woman up' emerged as a parallel to 'man up'; the etymology's 'mann' (person) shows that 'man' once meant human, not male.
WOMAN.joke: Woman: from 'wife-person' in Old English — proving that etymology has always had an awkward relationship with gender.

WOMEN.evolution: Irregular plural of 'woman'; the vowel change (woman/women) is an Old English holdover; 'women's rights' as a political movement began formally in the 19th century; 'women in STEM' is a contemporary equity discussion; 'women of the world' is a dignified collective address.
WOMEN.joke: Woman/women: the irregular plural that refuses to follow rules, which seems appropriate.

WOODY.definition: Having the texture or consistency of wood; of or resembling a plant with a woody stem.
WOODY.origin: From 'wood' + '-y'; Old English 'wudu' (wood, forest), from Proto-Germanic *widuz
WOODY.evolution: Extended from 'wood'; 'woody plant' has a hardened lignified stem (trees and shrubs vs. herbaceous plants); 'woody fragrance' describes earthy, timber-like scent notes in perfumery; Woody is also a common given name (Woody Allen, Woody Guthrie, Woody from Toy Story).
WOODY.joke: Woody: the name that Pixar assigned to a cowboy and somehow everything after that worked out fine.

WOOER.definition: A person who seeks the love of another; a suitor.
WOOER.origin: From 'woo' + '-er'; Old English 'wōgian' (to court, woo), of uncertain origin
WOOER.evolution: Agent noun from 'woo'; a wooer is a suitor; 'wooing' is the process of courtship; 'win-and-woo' describes romantic pursuit; the word is somewhat archaic but still used in historical, literary, and humorous contexts.
WOOER.joke: Wooer: the dedicated romantic who makes courtship sound like a formal occupation requiring a job title.

WOOLY.definition: Made of or resembling wool; (of thinking) vague and confused.
WOOLY.evolution: Extended from wool; 'woolly thinking' means imprecise and confused reasoning; 'wild and woolly' (especially 'Wild West') suggests rough and unrefined; a 'woolly mammoth' is the extinct ice-age elephant; sheep's wool as a metaphor for confused thought is apt.
WOOLY.joke: Woolly thinking: when mental clarity gets compared to fiber texture — soft, warm, and structurally unreliable.

WOOZY.origin: Possibly from 'oozy' (soft, muddy), with initial 'w' added; or from dialectal 'wooze' (to ooze); origin uncertain
WOOZY.evolution: Of uncertain origin; describes the dizzy, unsteady feeling from illness, drink, or spinning; 'feeling woozy' is mild to moderate disorientation; the word's soft phonetic quality captures the wobbly sensation it describes.
WOOZY.joke: Woozy: the word that sounds exactly like how you feel when you say it after the third glass.

WORDY.origin: From 'word' + '-y'; Old English 'word' (word, speech), from Proto-Germanic *wurdan
WORDY.evolution: Extended from 'word'; 'wordy explanation' uses too many words; 'verbose' is its formal synonym; 'wordy' carries a slight judgment of inefficiency; an entire dictionary entry could be considered 'wordy' from the right perspective.
WORDY.joke: Wordy: the one adjective that can be demonstrated by explaining what it means in more words than necessary.

WORLD.definition: The earth; all people; a particular sphere of activity.
WORLD.origin: Old English 'woruld, weorold' (world, age of man), from Proto-Germanic *weraldiz, from *wer (man) + *aldiz (age)
WORLD.evolution: Old English 'age of man'; 'world-class' means of the highest international standard; 'not of this world' means extraordinary; 'the world is your oyster' (Shakespeare) means opportunities abound; 'old world' and 'new world' divided hemispheres after 1492.
WORLD.joke: The world is your oyster: Shakespeare's promise of unlimited opportunity, delivered via a bivalve.

WORRY.definition: To feel anxious; to cause anxiety; a state of anxiety; to bite or attack repeatedly.
WORRY.origin: Old English 'wyrgan' (to strangle, choke), from Proto-Germanic *wurgjan
WORRY.evolution: Originally meant to choke or strangle; extended to harassing and biting (a dog 'worrying' sheep); shifted to mental anxiety; 'worry beads' provide tactile distraction from anxiety; 'not to worry' is British reassurance; 'worry wart' is a habitual worrier.
WORRY.joke: Worry: started as strangling and arrived at existential concern — the linguistic journey of a very stressed word.

WORSE.definition: Comparative of 'bad'; of a more serious, severe, or unpleasant nature.
WORSE.origin: Old English 'wyrsa, wiersa' (worse), from Proto-Germanic *wersizōn, from *wersa- (confused, mixed up)
WORSE.evolution: Old English comparative of 'bad'; 'for better or worse' is a wedding vow clause; 'could be worse' is the optimism of minimal standards; 'worse for wear' means damaged or tired; 'go from bad to worse' describes deterioration.
WORSE.joke: Could be worse: the British national philosophy, delivered as both reassurance and a warning.

WORST.definition: Superlative of 'bad'; most bad, severe, or unpleasant; to defeat or outdo.
WORST.origin: Old English 'wyrst, wierst' (worst), from Proto-Germanic *wersista-, superlative of *wersa-
WORST.evolution: Old English superlative of 'bad'; 'if worst comes to worst' means in the most extreme case; 'at worst' means in the worst scenario; to 'worst' someone means to defeat them; 'world's worst' is a hyperbolic criticism.
WORST.joke: If worst comes to worst: the idiom that prepares you for a situation where worst has already arrived and brought a friend.

WORTH.definition: The level of value or importance; the value equivalent to; deserving of.
WORTH.evolution: Old English value word; 'net worth' is total financial value; 'worth your salt' means deserving your salary (Roman soldiers were paid in salt); 'for what it's worth' offers information without claiming value; 'self-worth' is one's sense of personal value.
WORTH.joke: Worth your salt: the salary metaphor from when Romans paid in sodium — now you need to justify your paycheck differently.

WOULD.definition: Past tense of 'will'; used to express a polite request; used to describe repeated past actions.
WOULD.origin: Old English 'wolde' (wished, wanted), past tense of 'willan' (to will, wish); from Proto-Germanic *weldō
WOULD.evolution: Old English past of 'will'; 'would you mind' is a polite request form; 'would that it were' is a formal wish; 'wooden it' (wouldn't it) is dialectal; 'would-be' describes aspiration without achievement; 'as it would seem' is a hedging phrase.
WOULD.joke: Would you mind? The polite request that means 'please do this' but frames it as if you have an option.

WOUND.definition: An injury to the body; to injure; past tense of 'wind' (to coil).
WOUND.origin: Two words: injury sense from Old English 'wund' (wound), from Proto-Germanic *wundō; coil sense from Old English 'windan' (to wind), past tense 'wand'
WOUND.evolution: Two Old English words with identical spelling in modern English; 'licking one's wounds' means recovering from defeat; 'salt in the wound' means adding insult to injury; 'wound up' (tense) uses the coil sense; the pronunciation difference ('wound' vs. 'wound') is a classic English ambiguity.
WOUND.joke: Wound: the word that is pronounced two different ways depending on whether you're injured or coiled, proving English is committed to confusion.

WOVEN.definition: Past participle of 'weave'; made by interlacing threads.
WOVEN.origin: Old English 'wefan' (to weave), past participle 'wofen'; from Proto-Germanic *webanan
WOVEN.evolution: Past participle of 'weave'; 'woven into the fabric of' means deeply integrated; 'a tightly woven narrative' describes a carefully constructed story; 'hand-woven' distinguishes artisanal from machine production; woven textiles are among humanity's oldest technologies.
WOVEN.joke: Woven into the fabric of society: the phrase that confused textile manufacturing with sociological observation.

WRACK.definition: Seaweed cast ashore; wreckage; to cause destruction; 'wrack and ruin.'
WRACK.origin: Middle Dutch or Low German 'wrak' (wreckage), related to Old English 'wræc' (misery, punishment)
WRACK.evolution: From Dutch wreckage; 'wrack and ruin' means total destruction; 'nerve-wracking' (also 'racking') describes extreme stress; 'wrack your brain' means to think very hard; the 'rack' in 'nerve-racking' and 'wracking' are often confused.
WRACK.joke: Nerve-wracking: the stress descriptor that is correctly spelled either way, which is itself nerve-wracking.

WRATH.origin: Old English 'wrǣþþu' (wrath, anger), from 'wrāþ' (angry), from Proto-Germanic *wraiþaz
WRATH.evolution: Old English for intense anger; one of the Seven Deadly Sins; 'Grapes of Wrath' (Steinbeck) used the biblical phrase; 'wrath of God' implies divine punishment; 'wrathful' is the adjectival form; 'wrath' is more elevated and intense than ordinary anger.
WRATH.joke: Grapes of Wrath: John Steinbeck borrowed the biblical wrath and stored it in a vineyard, where it has been fermenting ever since.

WREAK.definition: To cause damage or harm; to inflict something harmful.
WREAK.origin: Old English 'wrecan' (to avenge, punish, drive out), from Proto-Germanic *wrekaną
WREAK.evolution: Old English for vengeance/punishment; 'wreak havoc' means to cause chaos; 'wreak vengeance' means to take revenge; almost exclusively used in 'wreak havoc' now; 'wreaked' is the past tense though 'wrought' is sometimes incorrectly used instead.
WREAK.joke: Wreak havoc: the two words that travel together so exclusively they've essentially formed a compound noun.

WRECK.definition: The destruction of a vehicle or vessel; the remains of a destroyed thing; a person in a bad state; to destroy.
WRECK.origin: Anglo-French 'wrec', from Old Norse 'rek' (wreckage, driftwood), from 'reka' (to drive, cast up)
WRECK.evolution: From Old Norse via Anglo-French; 'train wreck' and 'car wreck' are literal; 'nervous wreck' is a person in extreme stress; 'hot mess' replaced 'wreck' in some contemporary uses; 'wrecking ball' is both a demolition tool and a Miley Cyrus song.
WRECK.joke: Nervous wreck: the one time demolition imagery gets applied to personal mental states without anyone calling a contractor.

WREST.definition: To forcibly take from someone; to obtain by effort.
WREST.origin: Old English 'wrǣstan' (to wrest, twist), from Proto-Germanic *wraistjan
WREST.evolution: Old English for twisting/taking by force; 'wrest control' means to seize authority; 'wrest from someone's grasp' is the physical metaphor; 'wrestle' is related — wrestling involves grappling and wresting; the word implies force and difficulty.
WREST.joke: Wrest control: the political acquisition method that skipped the election and went straight to the verb.

WRING.definition: To squeeze and twist; to extract by squeezing; to distress.
WRING.origin: Old English 'wringan' (to press, squeeze), from Proto-Germanic *wrengwaną
WRING.evolution: Old English for squeezing/twisting; 'wring one's hands' means to show distress through this gesture; 'wring out a wet cloth' is literal; 'wring every last drop from' means to extract maximum value; 'wringing wet' means completely saturated.
WRING.joke: Wring your hands: the ancient distress gesture that modern drama confirmed is still the default setting for visible worry.

WRIST.definition: The joint connecting the hand to the forearm.
WRIST.origin: Old English 'wrist' (wrist), from Proto-Germanic *wristiz, related to 'wrest' and 'writhe'
WRIST.evolution: Old English anatomical term related to twisting; 'wristwatch' wears a watch at the wrist; 'slap on the wrist' means a mild punishment; 'wrist pin' is a mechanical connector; 'limp wrist' became a stereotype; wrist injuries are common in sports.
WRIST.joke: Slap on the wrist: the punishment so mild it needed an anatomical location to indicate how little it meant.

WRITE.definition: To mark letters or words on a surface; to compose text; to fill in a form.
WRITE.origin: Old English 'wrītan' (to scratch, incise, write), from Proto-Germanic *wrītanan (to tear, scratch)
WRITE.evolution: Old English for scratching/inscribing; 'the writing is on the wall' (from the biblical handwriting in Belshazzar's feast) means impending doom; 'write off' means to cancel or dismiss; 'write-up' is a written review; 'ghost-writing' is anonymous authorship.
WRITE.joke: The writing on the wall: the biblical forewarning that proved handwriting analysis was invented by prophets, not detectives.

WRONG.definition: Not correct; unjust; the state of being wrong; to treat unjustly.
WRONG.evolution: From Old Norse 'crooked'; 'wrongful' describes legally improper acts; 'wrong-footed' means caught off-guard; 'wrong side of the tracks' is a social metaphor; 'two wrongs don't make a right' is a moral maxim; 'wronged' means treated unjustly.
WRONG.joke: Two wrongs don't make a right: the moral arithmetic that most people agree with and then test thoroughly anyway.

WROTE.definition: Past tense of 'write'; marked letters or words; composed text.
WROTE.origin: Old English 'wrītan' (to write), past tense 'wrāt'; from Proto-Germanic *wrītanan
WROTE.evolution: Strong past tense of 'write'; 'she wrote the book on it' means she is the ultimate authority; 'as I wrote earlier' refers back to previous text; 'what's written is written' accepts the unalterable; literary history is defined by who wrote what when.
WROTE.joke: She wrote the book on it: the ultimate expertise claim — authorship as credential in a world full of Wikipedia.

WRUNG.definition: Past tense and past participle of 'wring'; squeezed and twisted.
WRUNG.origin: Old English 'wringan' (to wring), past participle; from Proto-Germanic *wrengwaną
WRUNG.evolution: Strong past participle of 'wring'; 'wrung dry' means completely depleted; 'wrung out' means exhausted or completely dried; 'heart-wrung' is an archaic word for deeply distressed; the physical squeezing metaphor extends perfectly to emotional depletion.
WRUNG.joke: Wrung out: when being completely exhausted required a laundry metaphor to capture the full extent.

WRYLY.definition: In a wry manner; with dry, ironic humor.
WRYLY.origin: From 'wry' + '-ly'; 'wry' from Old English 'wrīgian' (to go, strive toward), later 'to twist'
WRYLY.evolution: From 'wry' (twisted); a wry expression is the facial version; 'wryly observed' describes dry, ironic commentary; 'wry smile' turns the mouth slightly as if commenting on the absurdity of things; the word itself has a slight twist in it.
WRYLY.joke: Smiled wryly: the facial expression of someone who sees the joke in the situation but isn't quite ready to call it funny.

YACHT.definition: A medium-sized sailing or powered boat; to sail in a yacht.
YACHT.origin: Dutch 'jacht' (a fast ship), from 'jagen' (to hunt, chase), from Middle Dutch 'jachte'
YACHT.evolution: From Dutch hunting/chasing ship; originally fast patrol boats; became leisure vessels of the wealthy; 'yacht club' is exclusive recreation; 'superyacht' is the extreme luxury end; the word entered English directly from Dutch naval vocabulary.
YACHT.joke: Yacht: if you have to ask what it costs, you can't afford the docking fees, let alone the yacht.

YEARN.definition: To have an intense feeling of longing for something.
YEARN.origin: Old English 'giernan, geornan' (to yearn, desire), from Proto-Germanic *gernō
YEARN.evolution: Old English for desire/longing; 'yearn for' describes intense nostalgic or romantic longing; 'yearning' is the noun form; the word implies a deep, often unmet desire; it sits emotionally between 'want' and 'ache.'
YEARN.joke: Yearn: the emotional intensity level between 'want' and 'pine' on the desire scale that English needed a word for.

YEAST.definition: A microscopic fungus used in fermentation of bread and alcohol; a similar substance.
YEAST.origin: Old English 'giest, gyst' (yeast, froth), from Proto-Germanic *jestaz, from *jesan (to ferment, boil)
YEAST.evolution: Old English for fermentation froth; yeast is essential to bread-making and brewing; single-celled fungi that convert sugars to carbon dioxide and alcohol; 'active yeast' in baking; 'yeast infection' is a medical condition; Louis Pasteur proved yeast was alive in 1857.
YEAST.joke: Yeast: the microscopic fungus that has been making bread rise and wine ferment since before humans fully understood what it was.

YIELD.definition: To produce or provide; to give way to demands; a production output; a return on investment.
YIELD.origin: Old English 'gieldan' (to pay, reward, give), from Proto-Germanic *geldan
YIELD.evolution: Old English for payment/reward; 'yield to temptation' means to give in; 'crop yield' is agricultural production; 'bond yield' is financial return; 'yield sign' in traffic means give way; 'yield results' means to produce outcomes.
YIELD.joke: Yield sign: the traffic control that asks drivers to give way, which they interpret as a suggestion.

YOUNG.definition: Having lived for a short time; characteristic of youth; the offspring of animals.
YOUNG.origin: Old English 'geong' (young), from Proto-Germanic *jungaz, from Proto-Indo-European *h₂yuh₁n-ḱo- (young)
YOUNG.evolution: Old English fundamental age adjective; cognate with Latin 'iuvenis' (young, juvenile); 'young at heart' describes youthful spirit regardless of age; 'the young and the restless' is both a soap opera and a demographic; 'forever young' is the impossible aspiration.
YOUNG.joke: Young at heart: the aspirational condition that allows older people to claim youth as a metaphorical address.

YOUTH.definition: The period between childhood and adulthood; the qualities of being young; young people collectively.
YOUTH.origin: Old English 'geoguþ' (youth, young people), from Proto-Germanic *jugunþi-, from *jungaz (young)
YOUTH.evolution: Old English abstract of 'young'; 'fountain of youth' is the legendary source of immortal youth; 'wasted on the young' completes Shaw's observation about youth; 'misspent youth' is a retrospective regret; 'youth hostel' provides accommodation for young travelers.
YOUTH.joke: Youth is wasted on the young: the only complaint about being young that older people deliver with complete confidence in their own timing.

ZEBRA.definition: An African mammal with distinctive black and white stripes; a referee (sporting slang).
ZEBRA.origin: Italian or Portuguese 'zebra', possibly from Congolese 'zebra' or from Latin 'equiferus' (wild horse); exact origin uncertain
ZEBRA.evolution: Possibly from Congo via Portuguese exploration; zebras are closely related to horses; 'zebra crossing' is a pedestrian crossing with alternating stripes; 'when you hear hoofbeats, think horses, not zebras' is a medical aphorism about common diagnoses.
ZEBRA.joke: Think horses, not zebras: the medical school advice that has been failing every student who sees something genuinely unusual.

ZESTY.definition: Having a strong, pleasant flavor; full of enthusiasm and energy.
ZESTY.origin: From 'zest' + '-y'; 'zest' from French 'zeste' (lemon peel used to flavor), of unknown origin
ZESTY.evolution: From French lemon peel; 'zest for life' means enthusiastic engagement; 'zesty flavor' combines tang and aromatics; 'lemon zest' is the outermost layer of peel; the word suggests vitality and lively flavor; frequently overused in food marketing.
ZESTY.joke: Zest for life: when lemon peel became the metaphor for enthusiasm, and nobody thought it was strange.

ZONAL.definition: Relating to or divided into zones.
ZONAL.origin: From 'zone' + '-al'; Latin 'zona' (girdle, zone), from Greek 'zōnē' (belt, girdle), from 'zōnnynai' (to gird)
ZONAL.evolution: From Latin girdle/belt; the earth's climate zones are zonal; 'zonal defense' in sports covers designated areas; 'zonal marking' assigns defenders to zones rather than players; urban planning uses zonal categories; time zones are a global organizational system.
ZONAL.joke: Zonal defense: when basketball borrowed the word for climate classification to describe not following a specific person.
